# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
> *"Define a hybrid PET/CT system. List any three clinical advantages of integrating PET and CT into a single gantry."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hybrid PET/CT system is a medical imaging device that combines a Positron Emission Tomography scanner and an X-ray Computed Tomography scanner within a single gantry, sharing a common patient couch and workstation, enabling co-registered anatomical (CT) and functional (PET) imaging in one session.

Three clinical advantages (1 mark each):
1. **Precise anatomical localization** of metabolic abnormalities detected on PET.
2. **CT-based attenuation correction** of PET data, improving quantification accuracy and shortening scan time.
3. **Single-session workflow** — reduced patient discomfort, lower chance of motion between scans, and improved throughput.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
> *"What is the role of the Hounsfield Unit (HU) in the CT-based attenuation correction workflow of a hybrid PET/CT?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is the voxel intensity scale produced by the CT reconstruction, defined relative to water (HU = 0). In the PET/CT hybrid workflow, the HU of each voxel is **converted to a 511 keV linear attenuation coefficient (μ₅₁₁)** using a vendor-specific bilinear scaling law. This 511 keV μ-map is then forward-projected along each PET line of response to compute the attenuation correction factors (ACFs) used in PET image reconstruction. (3 marks — full credit requires the *energy conversion* to be explicitly mentioned.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option A)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
> *"(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a hybrid PET/CT system. Highlight the role of the common patient couch and the image fusion workstation. (7 marks)"*
> *"(b) Derive the conversion from CT Hounsfield Units to a 511 keV linear attenuation map. Why is this conversion necessary, and what is the role of the bilinear scaling model? (7 marks)"*

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A hybrid PET/CT integrates a CT scanner (anatomical) and a PET scanner (functional/metabolic) within a single gantry, sharing a common patient couch and a unified image workstation. The system allows near-simultaneous acquisition and pixel-accurate co-registration.

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. Expected textual elements:

| Block | Function (1 mark each) |
|-------|------------------------|
| CT gantry + detector array | X-ray transmission acquisition, generates raw sinograms |
| CT reconstruction | Filtered back-projection or iterative, outputs HU map |
| PET ring + coincidence processor | Detects 511 keV photon pairs, generates list-mode or sinogram data |
| PET reconstruction with AC | OSEM/TOF with CT-derived attenuation map |
| Image fusion workstation | Co-registers CT and PET volumes, computes SUV, displays side-by-side and colorwash |
| Common couch | Sub-mm indexing, single positioning for both modalities |

**Role of common couch (2 marks):**
The patient is positioned once on a precisely encoded couch. The same anatomical coordinate frame is used by both scanners, eliminating the need for retrospective software registration and ensuring voxel-level alignment. The couch typically has a positioning accuracy of **±0.25 mm** or better.

**Role of fusion workstation (1 mark):**
Combines the two volumes into a single dataset, computes SUV, and provides clinician-facing visualisation (fused overlay, MIP — Maximum Intensity Projection).

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Why the conversion is necessary (2 marks):**
The CT X-ray beam has an effective energy of about 70–80 keV (for a 120 kVp tube), but PET annihilation photons are at **511 keV**. The mass attenuation coefficient of tissue varies strongly with energy (approximately as $1/E^3$ in the photoelectric-dominated regime for high-Z materials), so a 70 keV μ-map cannot be directly used for 511 keV correction. A scaling conversion is therefore mandatory.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from the Hounsfield Unit definition:

$$
\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}}(E_{\text{CT}}) - \mu_{\text{water}}(E_{\text{CT}})}{\mu_{\text{water}}(E_{\text{CT}})}
$$

Solving for $\mu_{\text{tissue}}(E_{\text{CT}})$:

$$
\mu_{\text{tissue}}(E_{\text{CT}}) = \mu_{\text{water}}(E_{\text{CT}}) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mathrm{HU}}{1000}\right)
$$

A single multiplicative energy-scaling factor $k(E_{\text{CT}} \to E_{511})$ converts this to the 511 keV value:

$$
\mu_{\text{tissue}}(E_{511}) = k \cdot \mu_{\text{water}}(E_{\text{CT}}) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mathrm{HU}}{1000}\right)
$$

Because the energy dependence of attenuation is **Z-dependent** (photoelectric effect scales as $Z^{3-4}$), a single linear scaling factor is insufficient across the full HU range (air → fat → soft tissue → bone). A **bilinear scaling** is therefore used:

$$
\mu_{511} =
\begin{cases}
a_L \cdot \mathrm{HU} + b_L, & \mathrm{HU} \leq \mathrm{HU}_{\text{break}} \\
a_H \cdot \mathrm{HU} + b_H, & \mathrm{HU} > \mathrm{HU}_{\text{break}}
\end{cases}
$$

The break point (typically **+300 HU**) separates the soft-tissue-dominant and bone-dominant regimes. The constants $a_L, b_L, a_H, b_H$ are vendor-calibrated to minimise bias in the resulting 511 keV μ-map.

**Final step (1 mark):**
The μ-map is forward-projected along each PET line of response to yield the ACF for that LOR:

$$
\mathrm{ACF} = \exp\left( \int_{\text{LOR}} \mu_{511}(s) \, ds \right)
$$

These ACFs are applied in the OSEM reconstruction of the PET image.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option B)

### Question B (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
> *"(a) Explain the principle of coincidence detection in PET. How does the coincidence timing window affect true, scatter, and random coincidence rates? (7 marks)"*
> *"(b) A 70 kg patient is administered 370 MBq of ¹⁸F-FDG. After a 60-minute uptake period, the average activity concentration in a tumor region is 15 kBq/mL. Given $t_{1/2}(^{18}\mathrm{F}) = 109.8$ min, compute the SUV (body weight) of the tumor. Comment on the diagnostic implication. (7 marks)"*

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Coincidence detection principle (3 marks):**
When a positron emitted by the radiotracer (e.g., ¹⁸F) annihilates with a nearby electron, two 511 keV photons are emitted in **approximately opposite directions** (180°, modulo a small non-collinearity of $\pm 0.25°$). The PET detector ring registers these photons in opposing detectors within a narrow time window — the **coincidence window** (typically **6–12 ns**). The line joining the two detectors is the **line of response (LOR)**. By collecting millions of LORs and using tomographic reconstruction (FBP or OSEM), a 3-D map of tracer concentration is computed.

**Coincidence types and timing window (4 marks):**
- **True coincidences** ($T$): Both photons originate from the same annihilation, no prior interaction, no time correlation with another event. Useful signal.
- **Scatter coincidences** ($S$): One or both photons Compton-scattered in tissue before detection; the LOR is mis-positioned, reducing contrast.
- **Random (accidental) coincidences** ($R$): Two unrelated photons from different annihilations arrive within the timing window by chance. $R \propto \Delta t \cdot A^2$, where $\Delta t$ is the coincidence window and $A$ is the activity.

**Effect of narrowing the timing window:**
- **True rate $T$** is approximately preserved (most true events fall well within the window).
- **Random rate $R$** decreases **linearly** with $\Delta t$ — narrower windows suppress randoms.
- **Scatter rate $S$** is largely unaffected (it depends on Compton kinematics, not timing).
- Therefore, narrowing the window improves the **Noise Equivalent Count Rate** $\mathrm{NECR} = T^2/(T+S+2R)$ and image SNR. Modern **Time-of-Flight (TOF)** systems achieve coincidence windows as low as **~300–500 ps**, dramatically suppressing randoms.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 70$ kg, $A_{\text{inj}} = 370$ MBq, $t_{\text{up}} = 60$ min, $C_{\text{measured}} = 15$ kBq/mL, $t_{1/2} = 109.8$ min.

**Step 1 — Decay constant (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.6931}{109.8} = 6.313 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Decay-correct image activity to injection time (2 marks):**

$$
C_0 = C_{\text{measured}} \cdot e^{+\lambda t} = 15 \cdot e^{+(6.313 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 60}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
\lambda t = 6.313 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 0.3788
$$

Compute the exponential:

$$
e^{0.3788} = 1.4603
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_0 = 15 \times 1.4603 = 21.90 \text{ kBq/mL}
$$

**Step 3 — Injected dose per unit body weight (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{A_{\text{inj}}}{m} = \frac{370 \text{ MBq}}{70 \text{ kg}} = 5.286 \text{ MBq/kg} = 5285.7 \text{ kBq/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — SUV calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\mathrm{SUV}_{bw} = \frac{C_0}{A_{\text{inj}}/m} = \frac{21.90 \text{ kBq/mL}}{5285.7 \text{ kBq/kg}}
$$

Since 1 mL of water ≈ 1 g, we may write kBq/g in the denominator for unitless SUV:

$$
\mathrm{SUV}_{bw} = \frac{21.90}{5.2857} \approx 4.14
$$

**Step 5 — Diagnostic comment (1 mark):**
An SUV of **~4.14** is well above the conventional malignancy threshold of **SUV ≈ 2.5** for ¹⁸F-FDG in many solid tumors (e.g., lung, breast, colorectal). This is suggestive of **highly metabolically active malignant tissue**, warranting further investigation (biopsy, follow-up imaging, or treatment response assessment per PERCIST criteria).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Hybrid PET/CT Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting decay correction in SUV** — examiners will deduct 2–3 marks for an SUV computed from $C_{\text{measured}}$ without correcting to injection time. Always check the problem for the post-injection scan time.
> 2. **Stating "CT at 511 keV"** — CT is at the X-ray tube kVp (~70–100 keV effective). The *energy conversion* is the entire intellectual content of CT-based AC.
> 3. **Confusing random and scatter coincidences** — randoms depend on timing window; scatter depends on Compton interaction geometry. They are NOT the same thing.
> 4. **Omitting the bilinear scaling rationale** — a single linear scaling from HU to μ₅₁₁ is **wrong**; the photoelectric-vs-Compton transition near bone requires piecewise treatment.
> 5. **Drawing PET/CT diagrams as two disconnected boxes** — a hybrid system must be shown with a **common couch** and **shared data path**; otherwise the "hybrid" concept is not demonstrated.
> 6. **Mixing up SUV units** — SUV is **dimensionless**. If the calculation produces kg/mL or mL/kg, you have a units error.
> 7. **Skipping SUV comment** — even a single sentence on diagnostic interpretation (malignant threshold, PERCIST) earns the conclusion mark.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Hybrid PET/CT Systems**

- **Definition:** Integrated PET + CT scanner, single gantry, common couch, one workstation.
- **Clinical driver:** Localisation of metabolic abnormalities using CT anatomy.
- **First commercial system:** Discovery LS (2001), by Townsend and Nutt.
- **Acquisition order:** CT first (motion-free, AC ready), then PET.
- **Coincidence principle:** Two 511 keV photons in opposing detectors within **6–12 ns** window.
- **Coincidence types:** True (T), Scatter (S), Random (R) — only T is ideal signal.
- **NECR formula:** $\mathrm{NECR} = T^2 / (T + S + 2R)$ — narrows with shorter coincidence window.
- **Attenuation correction:** CT HU → 511 keV μ-map via **bilinear scaling** → forward projection → ACF per LOR.
- **HU definition:** $\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot (\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}})/\mu_{\text{water}}$; water = 0, air = −1000.
- **Bilinear break point:** typically **+300 HU** (soft tissue ↔ bone transition).
- **SUV formula:** $\mathrm{SUV}_{bw} = C_{\text{image}} / (\mathrm{ID}/\mathrm{BW})$ — dimensionless, threshold ~**2.5** for FDG malignancy.
- **SUV requires decay correction** to injection time using $\lambda = 0.6931 / t_{1/2}$.
- **¹⁸F half-life:** **109.8 min** — most commonly used PET radionuclide.
- **Modern PET crystals:** LYSO (preferred), LSO, BGO, GSO.
- **Modern photodetectors:** SiPMs (silicon photomultipliers) replacing PMTs in digital systems.
- **TOF advantage:** Coincidence timing resolution of **300–500 ps** improves SNR by factor $\propto D / \Delta x$ (large-patient gain).
- **PSF modelling:** Improves spatial resolution and SUV accuracy at lesion edges.
- **Bed overlap:** **15–25 %** to maintain uniform axial sensitivity.
- **CT effective dose for AC:** typically **5–20 mSv** whole-body — must be tracked.
- **SUV normalisation choices:** body weight (BW), lean body mass (LBM), body surface area (BSA).
- **Applications:** Oncology (dominant), cardiology, neurology, infection, RT planning.
- **PET/CT vs PET/MR:** PET/CT excels at AC accuracy and speed; PET/MR excels at soft-tissue contrast and zero-ionising AC.
- **Workstation output:** SUV map, MIP (Maximum Intensity Projection), fused colorwash, side-by-side review.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PET/MRI Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PET/MRI Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**PET/MRI** is a *hybrid imaging modality* that physically integrates a **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** subsystem with a **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** subsystem within a single gantry, enabling **simultaneous (or near-simultaneous) acquisition** of *metabolic/functional* information from PET and *anatomical/soft-tissue* information from MRI from the same anatomical region, in the same physiological state, and during the same imaging session.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A PET/MRI system is a *multimodal fusion imager* in which the PET detector ring is engineered to operate **inside or adjacent to** the high-static-field (typically **1.5 T** to **3 T**) MRI bore, preserving the MRI's main magnetic field ($B_0$), gradient fields, and radiofrequency (RF) environment while detecting 511 keV annihilation gamma photons.

The two modalities are fundamentally complementary:

| Modality | Strength | Information Type |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **PET** | Picomolar sensitivity to tracer concentration | Functional / Metabolic |
| **MRI** | Millimeter soft-tissue contrast resolution | Anatomical / Structural |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Two-Eyed Camera":**
> Imagine photographing a patient with **two cameras mounted on the same rig**: one camera (PET) sees only the *heat* of a fire inside a dark room (metabolic hotspots from a tumor), while the other camera (MRI) sees the *furniture layout* of the room (organs, muscles, vessels) in great detail. Used together, you instantly know **both** *where the fire is* AND *what furniture is around it* — without the patient moving between cameras. This is the clinical promise of PET/MRI: a single "snapshot" of metabolism **superimposed** on soft-tissue anatomy.

A second analogy helps with the physics: think of PET as a *Geiger counter for sugar molecules* (it detects the FDG tracer), and MRI as a *super-detailed X-ray that uses magnets instead of X-rays*. PET answers *"Is the tissue hungry for sugar?"* while MRI answers *"What does the tissue look like?"*

## 1.3 Why Hybrid PET/MRI Exists — The Clinical Motivation

PET/CT (introduced clinically in the early 2000s) revolutionized oncology but has limitations:
- **CT uses ionizing X-rays** (additional radiation dose).
- **CT has poor soft-tissue contrast** compared to MRI.
- **MRI is radiation-free** and is the **gold standard** for brain, spine, musculoskeletal, and pelvic soft-tissue imaging.

Combining PET with MRI therefore provides:
1. **Zero additional ionizing radiation** from the anatomical side (MRI replaces CT).
2. **Superior soft-tissue localization** of PET hot spots.
3. **Simultaneous dynamic imaging** — both modalities capture the same physiological instant, critical for moving organs (heart, lung, bowel) and for kinetic tracer studies.
4. **Multiparametric functional MRI** (fMRI, DTI, perfusion, spectroscopy) can be acquired *concurrently* with PET — something CT cannot provide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **MRI Field Strength ($B_0$):** typically **1.5 T** or **3 T** (clinical); up to **7 T** (research).
> - **511 keV** — energy of each annihilation photon ($E_\gamma = m_e c^2 + E_{\beta^+}$ residual $\approx 511$ keV).
> - **Larmor Frequency at 3 T:** $\omega_0/2\pi \approx 42.58 \times 3 \approx 127.74$ **MHz** (proton).
> - **PET Coincidence Window:** typically **$\tau_c \approx 6$ to $12$ ns**.
> - **PET Spatial Resolution (clinical):** **4 to 5 mm** full-width at half-maximum (FWHM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Block 1 — PET Subsystem Recap

A positron-emitting radiotracer (e.g., $^{18}$F-FDG, $^{68}$Ga-PSMA) decays by $\beta^+$ emission:

$$^{18}_{9}\text{F} \;\rightarrow\; ^{18}_{8}\text{O} \;+\; e^{+} \;+\; \nu_e$$

The emitted **positron ($e^+$)** travels a short distance (the **positron range**, typically **< 1 mm** for $^{18}$F), then **annihilates** with a nearby electron, producing **two 511 keV photons** emitted at approximately **180° apart** (back-to-back, by conservation of momentum, in the center-of-mass frame).

These two photons strike **opposing detectors** in the PET ring within a small **coincidence time window** $\tau_c$. The line connecting the two detectors is the **line of response (LOR)** — the PET system's fundamental geometric element.

### 2.1.1 Annihilation Energy

The rest-mass energy of the electron/positron pair converts into two gamma photons:

$$E_\gamma = m_e c^2 = (9.109 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg})(3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})^2 \approx 511\,\text{keV}$$

### 2.1.2 Coincidence Detection Logic

If photon 1 is detected at time $t_1$ and photon 2 at $t_2$:

$$\Delta t = \vert t_1 - t_2 \vert \;\leq\; \tau_c$$

then a *true coincidence* is recorded. A **time-of-flight (TOF)** PET additionally measures $\Delta t$ to estimate the annihilation point along the LOR:

$$x_{\text{annihilation}} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light. Modern TOF-PET achieves coincidence timing resolution of **~ 200–400 ps**, yielding a TOF spatial localization of **~ 3–6 cm** along the LOR.

## 2.2 Building Block 2 — MRI Subsystem Recap

MRI exploits the **nuclear magnetic resonance** of hydrogen nuclei (protons, $^{1}$H) abundant in biological tissue water and fat.

### 2.2.1 Larmor Precession Frequency

A proton in static field $B_0$ precesses at the Larmor frequency:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma \, B_0$$

where $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58$ **MHz/T** is the **gyromagnetic ratio of $^{1}$H**. At $B_0 = 3$ T: $\omega_0/2\pi \approx 127.74$ **MHz**.

### 2.2.2 Relaxation Times

After RF excitation at $\omega_0$, the net magnetization recovers via:
- **T1 (longitudinal/spin-lattice) recovery:** $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$
- **T2 (transverse/spin-spin) decay:** $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0)\, e^{-t/T_2}$
- **T2\*** includes static-field inhomogeneities: $\dfrac{1}{T_2^*} = \dfrac{1}{T_2} + \dfrac{1}{T_2'}$

The MRI signal (free induction decay, FID) is Fourier-transformed to fill **k-space**, then inverse-transformed to form the image.

## 2.3 The Engineering Challenge — Why Combining PET and MRI is Hard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MRI bore is filled with three hostile environments for a PET detector:
> 1. **Static field $B_0$ (1.5–7 T)** — affects the trajectory of secondary electrons in photodetectors.
> 2. **Gradient fields ($\sim$ 10–50 mT/m, slewing at $\sim$ 200 T/m/s)** — induce eddy currents and forces on conductive components.
> 3. **RF pulses (tens of kHz bandwidth around 128 MHz at 3 T)** — inject electromagnetic interference (EMI) into PET electronics.

Conventional **PMT (photomultiplier tube)** detectors are unusable inside MRI because:
- PMTs contain **ferromagnetic dynodes** (Ni/Be) and exhibit strong gain modulation in $B_0$.
- They pick up MRI's RF pulses as direct electrical interference.

**Solution used in modern PET/MRI:** Replace PMTs with **solid-state photosensors** that are MRI-compatible:

| Sensor Type | MRI Compatibility | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| **APD** (Avalanche Photodiode) | Good | Lower gain (~10–100) |
| **SiPM** (Silicon Photomultiplier) | Excellent | High gain ($10^5$–$10^6$), compact, fast, used in modern Siemens mMR and GE SIGNA PET/MR |

## 2.4 Two Architectural Approaches

1. **Sequential (tandem) PET/MRI** — patient is moved between separate PET and MRI scanners on a common bed. Easier engineering; loses true simultaneity.
2. **Simultaneous (fully integrated) PET/MRI** — PET ring sits *inside* the MRI bore, with the patient lying in a common bore. Both modalities acquire data at the same instant. Examples: **Siemens Biograph mMR** (3 T MRI + PET), **GE SIGNA PET/MR** (3 T MRI + TOF PET).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests the **attenuation correction (AC)** problem. In PET/CT, CT provides a direct map of X-ray attenuation at 511 keV (via bilinear scaling). In PET/MRI, MRI provides **proton density / T1-weighted** images that are **NOT** a direct map of 511 keV attenuation. **MR-based AC (MRAC)** uses segmentation (e.g., into air, lung, fat, soft tissue, bone) plus atlas-based or deep-learning methods to synthesize a pseudo-CT ($\mu$-map).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_\gamma = m_e c^2 \approx 511$ keV | Energy of each annihilation photon |
| 2 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$, with $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T | Larmor (precession) frequency of $^{1}$H |
| 3 | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Longitudinal (T1) recovery |
| 4 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0)\, e^{-t/T_2}$ | Transverse (T2) decay |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T_2^*} = \dfrac{1}{T_2} + \dfrac{1}{T_2'}$ | T2* with field inhomogeneity |
| 6 | $x_{\text{TOF}} = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2}$ | TOF-PET localization along LOR |
| 7 | $\Delta t = \vert t_1 - t_2 \vert \le \tau_c$ | Coincidence condition; $\tau_c$ typically 6–12 ns |
| 8 | $\dot{B}(t) = \dfrac{dB}{dt} \le 200$ T/m/s (typical) | Gradient slew-rate limit (safety) |
| 9 | $S = k \cdot \Delta B^2$ | Approx. RF eddy-current loss in conductor (Poynting) |
| 10 | $\mu_{511\,\text{keV}} = f(\rho, Z_{\text{eff}})$ | Linear attenuation coeff. at 511 keV — needed for AC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to remember:** $B_0$ in Tesla (T); $\gamma$ in rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹; $\omega_0$ in rad/s (or $f_0 = \omega_0/2\pi$ in Hz); $T_1, T_2$ in milliseconds (ms); energy in keV or J ($1$ eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J); positron range in mm.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oncology:** Combined FDG-PET + whole-body MRI for tumor staging (especially prostate, breast, liver).
- **Neurology:** Simultaneous fMRI + PET receptor studies (e.g., dopamine, serotonin) for psychiatric and neurodegenerative research.
- **Cardiology:** Cardiac PET/MRI fuses perfusion/metabolism data with MR cine, T1/T2 mapping.
- **Pediatrics:** Eliminating CT dose is critical — PET/MRI is preferred for children.
- **Drug development:** Pharmacokinetic PET + structural MRI in preclinical research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annihilation Photon Energy

**Step 1 — Identify the rest mass.** Both the positron ($e^+$) and the electron ($e^-$) have identical rest mass:

$$m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rest-mass energy formula.** At annihilation, the entire rest-mass energy of the two-particle system is converted to electromagnetic radiation. For a two-photon annihilation at rest (net momentum $\approx 0$):

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2 m_e c^2$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two photons by momentum conservation.** Because the system is at rest, the photons are emitted back-to-back (collinear, opposite direction). By symmetry, each photon carries half the total energy:

$$E_\gamma = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{2} = m_e c^2$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_\gamma &= (9.109 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg}) \times (2.998 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})^2 \\
&= 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 8.988 \times 10^{16}\;\text{J} \\
&= 8.187 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{J} \\
&= \frac{8.187 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}\;\text{eV} \\
&\approx 5.11 \times 10^5\;\text{eV} \\
&= 511\;\text{keV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each annihilation gamma photon carries an energy of **511 keV** — this is the universal signature of positron-electron annihilation used in every clinical PET scanner.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Larmor Frequency at 3 T

**Step 1 — Use the gyromagnetic ratio of $^{1}$H.**

$$\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.576\,\text{MHz/T}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the static field strength $B_0 = 3$ T.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \gamma_n \cdot B_0 \\
&= 42.576\,\text{MHz/T} \times 3\,\text{T} \\
&= 127.728\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular frequency.**

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 127.728 \times 10^6 \approx 8.025 \times 10^8\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Engineering implication:** The MRI RF transmit/receive coils must resonate at ~ 127.73 MHz at 3 T. The PET electronics must be RF-shielded to reject interference at this exact frequency.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TOF-PET Spatial Localization

**Step 1 — Define the time difference.** Photon 1 arrives at detector A at time $t_1$; photon 2 arrives at detector B at time $t_2$:

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$$

**Step 2 — Compute photon 1 travel distance.** The annihilation point $x$ (measured from detector A along the LOR) emits photon 1 traveling at $c$:

$$d_1 = c \cdot t_1 = c \cdot \frac{x}{c} = x$$

**Step 3 — Compute photon 2 travel distance.** The LOR has total length $L$; photon 2 travels the remainder:

$$d_2 = c \cdot t_2 = L - x$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_2 - t_1 &= \frac{L - x}{c} - \frac{x}{c} = \frac{L - 2x}{c} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \Delta t &= \frac{L - 2x}{c} \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2x &= L - c\,\Delta t \\
\Rightarrow \quad x &= \frac{L - c\,\Delta t}{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad x_{\text{annihilation}} &= \frac{L}{2} - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, with $\Delta t$ measured relative to the LOR midpoint, the simple form $x = c\,\Delta t/2$ is used (with sign convention).

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** For a timing resolution of $\Delta t = 300$ ps = $3 \times 10^{-10}$ s:

$$x = \frac{(3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})(3 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s})}{2} = \frac{0.09\,\text{m}}{2} = 0.045\,\text{m} = 4.5\,\text{cm}$$

So a 300 ps coincidence timer localizes the annihilation to within **± 4.5 cm** along the LOR — enough to substantially improve signal-to-noise ratio in large patients.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MRI Signal Equation (Simplified)

The magnitude of the acquired MR signal from a voxel of spin density $\rho$, after excitation flip angle $\alpha$ and relaxation during echo time TE and repetition time TR, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\propto \rho \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-TR/T_1}}{1 - \cos\alpha \cdot e^{-TR/T_1}} \cdot e^{-TE/T_2^*}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- $\rho$ — proton density factor (tissue contrast base).
- $\sin\alpha$ — initial transverse magnetization after flip.
- $\dfrac{1 - e^{-TR/T_1}}{1 - \cos\alpha \, e^{-TR/T_1}}$ — steady-state T1 saturation correction (Ernst angle regime).
- $e^{-TE/T_2^*}$ — T2* decay during readout.

**Engineering use:** In PET/MRI, the same scan session can produce *multiple contrasts* (T1w, T2w, diffusion, perfusion) — the anatomical map used for **MR-based attenuation correction** is typically a **UTE** (ultrashort-echo-time) or **Dixon** sequence because it captures signal from bone (short-T2* tissue) and separates fat/water — both critical for AC.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — PET vs MRI vs PET/MRI

| Parameter | PET (Standalone) | MRI (Standalone) | PET/MRI (Hybrid) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Signal source | 511 keV annihilation photons | $^{1}$H precession in $B_0$ | Both, simultaneously |
| Spatial resolution | 4–5 mm FWHM | 0.5–2 mm | PET limited by detector; MRI unchanged |
| Soft-tissue contrast | Poor (functional only) | Excellent | Excellent (MRI) + functional (PET) |
| Ionizing radiation | Yes ($\sim$ 5–25 mSv tracer dose) | **None** | Only tracer dose (lower than PET/CT) |
| Acquisition time | 15–30 min per bed | 30–60 min per region | Comparable or shorter (parallel) |
| Attenuation correction | Transmission/segmented | Not directly used for AC; **MRAC synthesized** | MRAC: Dixon, UTE, or deep-learning $\mu$-map |
| Hardware in MRI bore | N/A | N/A | **SiPM-based PET ring** + RF shielding |
| Key clinical use | Oncology, neurology | Soft-tissue, MSK, neuro | Oncology + soft-tissue staging, pediatric, cardiac |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated PET/MRI System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Patient on common table] --> R[RF Body Coil 127.7 MHz at 3T]
    P --> PET[PET Detector Ring inside MRI bore]
    PET --> SIPM[SiPM Photosensors MRI compatible]
    SIPM --> COINC[Coincidence Processor tau c equals 6 to 10 ns]
    COINC --> TOF[TOF Localizer c Delta t over 2]
    TOF --> IMGRECON1[List Mode PET Reconstruction]
    R --> GRAD[X Y Z Gradient Coils slew 200 T per m per s]
    GRAD --> KSP[k space Sampling]
    KSP --> FFT[2D or 3D Inverse Fourier Transform]
    FFT --> IMGRECON2[MRI Image Reconstruction]
    IMGRECON1 --> FUSE[Image Fusion and Registration]
    IMGRECON2 --> FUSE
    FUSE --> MRAC[MR based Attenuation Correction Dixon UTE]
    MRAC --> FUSE
    FUSE --> DISPLAY[Hybrid PET or MRI Display for Radiologist]
    SHIELD[RF Shielding Faraday cage around PET electronics] -.-> PET
    SHIELD -.-> SIPM
```

## 4.2 Data-Flow Diagram — MR-Based Attenuation Correction (MRAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acquire Dixon T1w MRI Sequence] --> B[Segment into Air Lung Fat Soft Tissue Bone via Atlas or UTE]
    B --> C[Assign Linear Attenuation Coefficients at 511 keV per tissue class]
    C --> D[Generate pseudo CT mu map]
    D --> E[Forward Project mu map along LORs]
    E --> F[PET Attenuation Correction Factors ACF per LOR]
    F --> G[Apply to PET Sinogram]
    G --> H[Reconstruct Corrected PET Image]
    H --> I[Fuse with MRI for Final Hybrid Image]
```

## 4.3 Subsystem Interaction — Engineering Compatibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MRI_Bore
        B0[Static Field B0 1.5 to 3 T]
        GRAD[Gradient Coils]
        RF[RF Transmit Receive 128 MHz]
    end
    subgraph PET_Ring
        SCINT[LYSO Scintillator Crystals]
        SIPM2[SiPM Photosensors]
        ELEC[Front End Electronics]
    end
    B0 -- compatible --> SIPM2
    GRAD -- eddy currents --> SCINT
    RF -- EMI interference --> ELEC
    SIPM2 --> COINC2[Coincidence and TOF Processor outside Faraday shield]
    ELEC --> COINC2
    COINC2 --> OUT[PET List Mode Data to Reconstruction Computer]
    B0 --> OUT2[MRI k space Data to Reconstruction]
    GRAD --> OUT2
    RF --> OUT2
    OUT --> MERGE[Hybrid Image Fusion]
    OUT2 --> MERGE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**
> *Define a hybrid PET/MRI system. Why is the combination clinically superior to standalone PET or MRI?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hybrid PET/MRI system integrates a positron emission tomography (PET) detector ring and a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanner within a single gantry, allowing *simultaneous* acquisition of functional/metabolic (PET) and anatomical/soft-tissue (MRI) information from the same anatomical region in the same physiological state. **(1 mark)**. It is clinically superior because **(1 mark each, total 2 marks):**
1. MRI provides superior soft-tissue contrast compared to CT (used in PET/CT), enabling better anatomical localization of PET hot spots — especially in brain, prostate, liver, and musculoskeletal regions.
2. MRI is radiation-free, eliminating the additional X-ray dose of CT; hence preferred in pediatric and repeat-scan patients.
3. True simultaneity enables dynamic studies (e.g., cardiac, kinetic tracer studies) where organ motion or rapid physiology would otherwise mis-register the two modalities.

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**
> *Explain why conventional photomultiplier tubes (PMTs) cannot be used in the PET subsystem of an integrated PET/MRI scanner. What is the alternative?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Conventional PMTs contain **ferromagnetic dynode materials (Ni/Be)** and **exhibit strong gain modulation in high static magnetic field $B_0$** (1.5 T – 3 T) — the magnetic field deflects the secondary electrons between dynodes, destroying the gain and noise performance **(1 mark)**. Additionally, the long anode leads of PMTs act as antennas that pick up the MRI's **RF pulses (e.g., 128 MHz at 3 T)**, causing severe electromagnetic interference (EMI) that masks the 511 keV coincidence signals **(1 mark)**. The modern alternative is the **Silicon Photomultiplier (SiPM)** — a solid-state, MRI-compatible photosensor with high internal gain ($10^5$ – $10^6$), compact form factor, and intrinsic immunity to magnetic fields **(1 mark)**.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **confuse Larmor angular frequency $\omega_0$ with cyclic frequency $f_0 = \omega_0/2\pi$** and write incorrect units (rad/s vs Hz). Watch this in numerical questions.
> - In TOF-PET derivations, students often forget the **factor of 2** (photon travels half the LOR distance). Always re-derive carefully.
> - For MRAC, do not say "MRI directly gives attenuation at 511 keV." It does **NOT** — MRI measures proton relaxation, not electron density. A *pseudo-CT* must be synthesized.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – model 2024 scheme]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an **integrated (simultaneous) PET/MRI system**. Explain the engineering challenges of placing the PET detector ring inside the MRI bore, and how modern **SiPM-based detectors** overcome them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the energy of annihilation photons produced in PET, and explain the **coincidence detection principle** including the role of the coincidence window $\tau_c$ and **Time-of-Flight (TOF) localization**. Show the TOF spatial-localization formula and compute the localization uncertainty for a coincidence timing resolution of 400 ps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Architecture (3 marks):**

The simultaneous PET/MRI system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram):

1. **MRI subsystem:** Superconducting magnet producing $B_0$ (typically 1.5 T or 3 T); gradient coils (X, Y, Z) for spatial encoding; RF body coil at the Larmor frequency (e.g., 128 MHz at 3 T); receiver RF array coils.
2. **PET subsystem:** Annular detector ring (LYSO or LSO scintillator crystals) coupled to **SiPM photosensors**, installed *inside* the MRI bore, surrounding the patient; coincidence processor and TOF electronics; list-mode data acquisition.
3. **Common patient table:** Patient is positioned once, lying within both the PET ring and the MRI bore — no transfer between scanners.
4. **Hybrid reconstruction computer:** Fuses PET (metabolic) and MRI (anatomical) images after MR-based attenuation correction.

**Engineering Challenges and SiPM Solutions (4 marks):**

| Challenge | Effect on PET | SiPM-based Solution |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| High static field $B_0$ | PMT gain collapses; electron trajectories bent | SiPM is a **solid-state semiconductor** device; no electron path in vacuum → **fully immune to $B_0$** |
| Time-varying gradient fields (dT/dt) | Eddy currents, mechanical forces on conductive PET components | SiPM is compact, low-mass, with **short leads** → small induced EMF and forces |
| RF pulses (128 MHz) | EMI picked up by long PMT anode leads | PET front-end electronics are enclosed in a **Faraday RF shield**; SiPM is intrinsically low-impedance and short-lead → minimal antenna pickup |
| Limited axial coverage | PET ring must fit within MRI bore | Modern designs use a **split magnet** (e.g., GE SIGNA) or short PET ring (e.g., Siemens mMR) to maximize patient aperture |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation of annihilation photon energy (3 marks):**

The positron ($e^+$, mass $m_e$) emitted by the radionuclide travels a short distance, then annihilates with an atomic electron ($e^-$, mass $m_e$). By conservation of total energy (annihilation at near-rest) and conservation of momentum (the center-of-mass frame is the lab frame), the two annihilation photons must be emitted **back-to-back** and share the total rest-mass energy equally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 2 m_e c^2 \\
E_\gamma &= m_e c^2 = (9.109 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg})(2.998 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})^2 \\
&\approx 8.187 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{J} \approx 511\;\text{keV}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the universal energy used to identify true PET events. **(1 mark for logic, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for the 511 keV result.)**

**Coincidence detection principle (2 marks):**

When a positron annihilates, two 511 keV photons are emitted ~180° apart. They strike two opposing PET detectors within a small **coincidence time window** $\tau_c$ (typically 6–12 ns):

$$\Delta t = \vert t_1 - t_2 \vert \le \tau_c$$

If this condition is satisfied, the system records a *true coincidence* and defines the **Line of Response (LOR)** as the straight line connecting the two activated detectors. Only events that pass this window are accepted; otherwise, they are rejected as **randoms** (uncorrelated detections). **(2 marks.)**

**Time-of-Flight (TOF) localization (2 marks):**

If the timing difference $\Delta t$ between the two detectors can be measured precisely, the annihilation point can be localized along the LOR:

$$x_{\text{annihilation}} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

For a coincidence timing resolution of $\Delta t = 400$ ps = $4 \times 10^{-10}$ s:

$$x = \frac{(3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})(4 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s})}{2} = \frac{0.12\,\text{m}}{2} = 0.06\,\text{m} = 6\;\text{cm}$$

So TOF localizes the annihilation to a **± 6 cm** segment along the LOR, dramatically improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR ∝ √(L/Δx)) in large patients.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – model 2024 scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **Larmor precession** phenomenon in MRI. Derive the Larmor frequency at a field strength of **1.5 T** and state its units and physical significance. Also explain **T1** and **T2** relaxation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain **MR-based Attenuation Correction (MRAC)** for the PET subsystem in a hybrid PET/MRI scanner. Why cannot MRI directly provide the 511 keV attenuation map? What are **Dixon** and **UTE** sequences, and why are they preferred for MRAC? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Larmor precession (3 marks):**

A hydrogen nucleus ($^{1}$H) is a proton with intrinsic spin angular momentum $\vec{S}$ and an associated magnetic moment $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}$, where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio. When placed in an external static magnetic field $\vec{B}_0$ (conventionally along the $z$-axis), the magnetic moment experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$, which causes it to **precess** around $\vec{B}_0$ at a characteristic frequency. This is *Larmor precession*, analogous to a spinning top precessing in a gravitational field.

The precession angular frequency is:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

For $^{1}$H: $\gamma/2\pi = 42.576$ **MHz/T**.

**Numerical derivation at 1.5 T (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \cdot B_0 \\
&= 42.576\,\text{MHz/T} \times 1.5\,\text{T} \\
&= 63.864\,\text{MHz} \\
\omega_0 &= 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 63.864 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 4.012 \times 10^8\;\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** This is the *resonance frequency* — the RF pulse used to tip the magnetization into the transverse plane must be transmitted at exactly $f_0$; otherwise, no significant excitation occurs. This frequency is unique to $^{1}$H, allowing element-selective imaging.

**T1 and T2 relaxation (1 mark):**

- **T1 (longitudinal / spin-lattice) relaxation:** Recovery of $M_z$ toward equilibrium $M_0$ via energy transfer to the surrounding "lattice" (molecular environment); $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$.
- **T2 (transverse / spin-spin) relaxation:** Decay of transverse coherence $M_{xy}$ due to dephasing from spin-spin interactions; $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Why MRI cannot directly give the 511 keV attenuation map (2 marks):**
Attenuation of 511 keV photons in tissue depends on the **electron density** and **effective atomic number $Z_{\text{eff}}$** of the medium via photoelectric absorption and Compton scattering. MRI, however, detects the **proton ($^{1}$H) NMR signal**, which depends on proton density, T1, T2, and flow. **MRI has no direct sensitivity to electron density.** A bone region and an air cavity can have very different 511 keV attenuation coefficients, yet their proton densities may both be low, making them difficult to distinguish on a standard MR image.

**MRAC block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 mermaid):**
1. Acquire a special MR sequence (Dixon or UTE).
2. **Segment** the image into tissue classes: air, lung, fat, soft tissue, bone.
3. Assign each class a **predefined linear attenuation coefficient (LAC) at 511 keV** (from a lookup table based on ICRP / ICRU data).
4. Generate a **pseudo-CT (or $\mu$-map)** with these LACs.
5. **Forward-project** the $\mu$-map along each PET LOR to compute attenuation correction factors (ACFs).
6. Apply ACFs to the PET sinogram; reconstruct corrected PET image.

**Dixon and UTE sequences (3 marks):**

- **Dixon sequence:** A chemical-shift imaging technique that exploits the difference in precession frequencies between water (~ 3.5 ppm chemical shift) and fat protons. By acquiring in-phase (IP) and out-of-phase (OP) echoes, the images can be post-processed into separate **water-only** and **fat-only** images. For MRAC, Dixon allows reliable **fat vs. soft-tissue segmentation** (both are abundant and have different 511 keV LACs: fat $\mu \approx 0.09$/cm; soft tissue $\mu \approx 0.10$/cm at 511 keV).
- **UTE (Ultrashort Echo Time) sequence:** Uses very short echo times (TE < 200 μs) to capture signal from tissues with extremely short T2* — most importantly **cortical bone** — which appears as a signal void in conventional MRI. UTE is therefore essential to **distinguish bone from air**, two very-low-signal regions with vastly different 511 keV attenuation (bone $\mu \approx 0.17$/cm; air $\mu = 0$). Without UTE, MRAC severely underestimates attenuation in the skull, producing a systematic error of up to 10–15% in brain PET quantification.

**Preferred status:** Dixon and UTE are preferred because they produce **quantitative, segmentation-friendly** images that map naturally onto the 3–4 tissue classes (air, lung, fat, soft tissue, ± bone) needed for pseudo-CT synthesis. **(2 marks for the diagram and overall logic.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PET/MRI = hybrid = simultaneous functional (PET) + anatomical (MRI).**
- **Annihilation photon energy** = $m_e c^2$ = **511 keV** (each photon, two per event).
- **Coincidence condition:** $\vert t_1 - t_2 \vert \le \tau_c$, $\tau_c \approx 6$–$12$ ns.
- **TOF localization:** $x = c\,\Delta t/2$. For 300 ps → 4.5 cm; for 400 ps → 6 cm along LOR.
- **Larmor frequency for $^{1}$H:** $f_0 = 42.576$ MHz × $B_0$(T). At 3 T → 127.73 MHz; at 1.5 T → 63.86 MHz.
- **Larmor angular frequency:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = \gamma B_0$ in rad/s.
- **T1 recovery:** $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$.
- **T2 decay:** $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0)\, e^{-t/T_2}$.
- **T2\*** includes inhomogeneity: $1/T_2^* = 1/T_2 + 1/T_2'$.
- **PMTs fail in MRI bore** because of ferromagnetic dynodes and RF pickup. **SiPMs** are the modern MRI-compatible PET photosensor.
- **PET ring** sits inside the MRI bore; PET electronics enclosed in a **Faraday RF shield** to reject 128 MHz interference.
- **Attenuation correction (MRAC) in PET/MRI is a synthesis problem:** MRI cannot directly measure 511 keV $\mu$-maps. Use **Dixon** (for fat/water) and **UTE** (for bone) to segment and assign LACs from a lookup table → pseudo-CT → ACFs → corrected PET.
- **Two architectures:** **Sequential (tandem)** — patient moved between two separate scanners, simpler, but loses simultaneity. **Simultaneous (fully integrated)** — true hybrid, but harder engineering.
- **Clinical strengths:** Oncology (especially prostate, breast, liver), pediatric imaging (no CT dose), neurology (fMRI + PET receptor mapping), cardiac imaging.
- **Key vendor systems to remember:** Siemens **Biograph mMR** (3 T MRI + non-TOF PET), GE **SIGNA PET/MR** (3 T MRI + TOF PET).
- **KTU exam watchpoints:** Larmor frequency *units* (Hz vs rad/s), the factor of 2 in TOF derivation, and the fact that MRI ≠ attenuation map.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Resolution Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Super Resolution Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Super-Resolution (SR) Imaging** is a class of signal-processing and computational techniques in medical imaging that reconstructs a **High-Resolution (HR) image** from one or more observed **Low-Resolution (LR) images**, effectively overcoming the physical and sensor-imposed resolution limits of the imaging modality (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, PET, X-Ray).

In mathematical terms, SR imaging addresses the **inverse problem** of recovering the latent HR image $\mathbf{X}$ from a degraded observation $\mathbf{Y}$, where the degradation includes **blurring**, **downsampling**, **geometric warping** (motion/registration errors), and **sensor noise**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Forensics Sketch Artist"**
> Imagine a CCTV camera captures a thief's face, but the image is grainy and pixelated (LR). A forensic sketch artist (the SR algorithm) studies **multiple frames** of the thief moving across the camera, and because the thief's face was at slightly **different angles and positions** in each frame, the artist can **combine** the unique sub-pixel details from each frame to draw a **single, sharp portrait** (HR). The artist is essentially "borrowing" missing pixel information from neighbouring frames.
>
> A second analogy is **AI photo-upscaling** on your phone — when you zoom into an old photo, AI "hallucinates" plausible high-frequency detail (edges, textures) that the original sensor could not capture. This is **single-image SR**.

## 1.3 Why Super-Resolution Matters in Medical Imaging

- **CT Scanners** — Reduces patient radiation dose by acquiring LR projections and computationally reconstructing HR slices.
- **MRI** — Shortens acquisition time (faster scans) while preserving diagnostic detail.
- **Ultrasound** — Compensates for the diffraction limit of acoustic transducers.
- **Histopathology / Microscopy** — Enhances cellular and sub-cellular structures for accurate diagnosis.
- **Telemedicine** — Enables reliable transmission of diagnostic images over low-bandwidth networks.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Full Form | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PSNR** | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Pixel-level fidelity (higher is better) |
| **SSIM** | Structural Similarity Index | Perceptual/structural fidelity |
| **MSE** | Mean Squared Error | Per-pixel error magnitude |
| **NMSE** | Normalized MSE | Scale-invariant error |
| **LPIPS** | Learned Perceptual Patch Similarity | Deep-feature perceptual quality |

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A 14-mark KTU question may ask you to **derive PSNR from MSE** and explain why PSNR alone is insufficient — always pair it with **SSIM** for modern SR evaluation.

## 1.5 Classification of Super-Resolution Techniques

1. **Multi-Image SR (Reconstruction-Based)** — Uses **2 or more** LR images of the same scene with sub-pixel shifts.
   - Frequency Domain (Tsai & Huang, 1984)
   - Spatial Domain — IBP, MAP, POCS
2. **Single-Image SR (Example/Learning-Based)** — Uses **one** LR image.
   - Interpolation-based (Bicubic, Lanczos)
   - Sparse-coding
   - Deep Learning — SRCNN, ESRGAN, EDSR
3. **Hybrid Approaches** — Combine reconstruction priors with deep priors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Image Degradation Model (The "Mother Equation" of SR)

Every SR algorithm is essentially an attempt to **invert** a forward degradation model. The general form observed by the $k^{th}$ LR sensor is:

$$\mathbf{Y}_k = \mathbf{D}_k \, \mathbf{B}_k \, \mathbf{M}_k \, \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{N}_k$$

Where:

| Symbol | Operator | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{X}$ | — | True latent **High-Resolution** image (vectorized, $N^2 \times 1$) |
| $\mathbf{M}_k$ | Warping | Geometric motion / registration shift of $k^{th}$ frame |
| $\mathbf{B}_k$ | Blurring | Optical + motion + sensor Point Spread Function (PSF) |
| $\mathbf{D}_k$ | Downsampling | Pixel-averaging by factor $s$ (the scaling ratio) |
| $\mathbf{N}_k$ | Additive Noise | Gaussian / Poisson / Rician sensor noise |
| $\mathbf{Y}_k$ | Observation | The captured **Low-Resolution** frame |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The problem is **ill-posed** because the mapping $\mathbf{X} \rightarrow \mathbf{Y}$ is many-to-one (infinitely many HR images can produce the same LR image). Hence SR requires **regularization** or **learned priors** to converge to a unique, meaningful solution.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examiner's Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In every formula below, all absolute-value-style vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to remain valid inside markdown tables. Never type raw `|x|` in this engine.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{Y}_k = \mathbf{D}_k \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{M}_k \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{N}_k$ | General degradation model — **foundational equation** of SR |
| 2 | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \left[ \mathbf{X}(i,j) - \hat{\mathbf{X}}(i,j) \right]^{2}$ | Mean Squared Error between HR ground-truth and reconstruction |
| 3 | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{L^{2}}{\text{MSE}} \right)$ dB | Peak SNR; $L = 2^{B}-1$ is the peak intensity ($L=255$ for 8-bit) |
| 4 | $\text{SSIM}(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}}) = \ell(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}}) \cdot c(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}}) \cdot s(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}})$ | Structural similarity: luminance $\ell$, contrast $c$, structure $s$ |
| 5 | $\ell(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}}) = \dfrac{2\mu_{\mathbf{X}}\mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{1}}{\mu_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{1}}$ | Luminance component of SSIM |
| 6 | $c(\mathbf{X},\hat{\mathbf{X}}) = \dfrac{2\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}\hat{\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{2}}{\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{2}}$ | Contrast component of SSIM |
| 7 | $\text{NMSE} = \dfrac{\sum \vert \mathbf{X} - \hat{\mathbf{X}} \vert^{2}}{\sum \vert \mathbf{X} \vert^{2}}$ | Scale-invariant normalized error |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{N_{\text{HR}}}{N_{\text{LR}}} \quad$ (pixels per dimension) | Spatial upscaling ratio (typically $s = 2, 3, 4$) |
| 9 | $Y_{k}(f_x, f_y) = X(f_x, f_y) \cdot H_k(f_x, f_y)$ | Frequency-domain observation (after de-aliasing) |
| 10 | $\mathbf{X}^{(n+1)} = \mathbf{X}^{(n)} + \sum_{k} \mathbf{H}_{k}^{-1}\!\left[\!\left(\mathbf{Y}_{k} - \mathbf{D}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{X}^{(n)}\right) \uparrow s \!\right]$ | IBP (Iterative Back-Projection) update rule |

**Constants used in SSIM** — to prevent division by zero:
- $C_{1} = (K_{1} L)^{2}$ with $K_{1} = 0.01$
- $C_{2} = (K_{2} L)^{2}$ with $K_{2} = 0.03$

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Super-Resolution Methods (with KTU-Favourite Sub-Bullets)

### A. Frequency-Domain Methods
- **Tsai & Huang (1984)** — Relates aliased LR spectra to shifted HR spectra.
- Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) based.
- **Limitation:** Cannot easily handle spatial-variant blur or motion.

### B. Spatial-Domain Reconstruction Methods
1. **Iterative Back-Projection (IBP)** — Irani & Peleg (1991). Minimizes reconstruction error by back-projecting the difference between simulated and observed LR images.
2. **Projection Onto Convex Sets (POCS)** — Solves using convex-set intersection (smoothness, positivity, data-fidelity).
3. **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** — Bayesian formulation with a prior on $\mathbf{X}$.
4. **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — Finds $\mathbf{X}$ maximizing $P(\mathbf{Y}\vert\mathbf{X})$.

### C. Single-Image / Learning-Based Methods
1. **Interpolation** — Bicubic, Lanczos, B-spline.
2. **Patch-Based / Sparse Coding** — Yang et al. (2010).
3. **Deep Learning**
   - **SRCNN** (Dong et al., 2014) — First CNN-based SR.
   - **FSRCNN** — Faster variant.
   - **SRGAN / ESRGAN** — Perceptual / adversarial loss.
   - **EDSR** — Enhanced Deep SR (NTIRE 2017 winner).
   - **Transformer-based — SwinIR, Restormer.**

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In modern PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems) used in Kerala government and private hospitals, deep-learning SR models are deployed on **GPU servers** to enhance archived CT/MRI scans in real time, enabling radiologists to re-read legacy low-dose scans with diagnostic-grade clarity.

## 2.4 Multi-Frame vs Single-Image SR — The "Why" Comparison

| Aspect | Multi-Frame SR | Single-Image SR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inputs | $\geq 2$ LR images, sub-pixel shifted | One LR image |
| Information source | Real additional samples (extra photons / angles) | Hallucinated from learned priors |
| Information-theoretic limit | Truly **unbounded** in theory | Bounded by training data |
| Typical methods | IBP, POCS, MAP | SRCNN, ESRGAN, GANs |
| Medical use | 3D MRI slice stacking, CT multi-angle | Single MRI slice enhancement |
| Robustness | Requires accurate **sub-pixel registration** | Highly robust, no registration |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Rigor Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is fully expanded. No "$\Rightarrow$ after manipulation" shortcuts are used. This section alone is worth **7 of 14 marks** in a typical KTU long-answer question.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Image Degradation Model (From Optics to Algebra)

We begin with a continuous 2-D scene $x(u,v)$ imaged by a sensor. The sensor's **Point Spread Function (PSF)** $h(u,v)$ models the optical blur. The continuous image formed on the sensor plane is:

$$g(u,v) = x(u,v) \ast h(u,v)$$

Here $\ast$ denotes **2-D continuous convolution**. In words: every point in the scene is smeared by the lens into a small disk of intensity given by $h(u,v)$.

**Step 1:** Add continuous-domain noise $\eta(u,v)$ to the imaged field:

$$g_{n}(u,v) = x(u,v) \ast h(u,v) + \eta(u,v)$$

**Step 2:** The sensor integrates (averages) the continuous field over each pixel cell of size $T_x \times T_y$. This integration is mathematically the **downsampling** operator $\mathbf{D}$. Let $i, j$ be pixel indices:

$$y(i,j) = \int_{(i-1)T_x}^{iT_x} \!\! \int_{(j-1)T_y}^{jT_y} g_{n}(u,v) \, du \, dv$$

**Step 3:** Discretize and stack into vector form. Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N^{2}}$ be the lexicographically ordered true HR image, and $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{M^{2}}$ the observed LR image. Define:

- $\mathbf{M}_k$ — motion/warping matrix (size $N^{2}\times N^{2}$)
- $\mathbf{B}_k$ — blur matrix (size $N^{2}\times N^{2}$, block-Toeplitz)
- $\mathbf{D}_k$ — downsampling matrix (size $M^{2}\times N^{2}$)
- $\mathbf{N}_k$ — discrete noise vector

Combining Steps 1–3 into one operator chain:

$$\mathbf{Y}_{k} = \mathbf{D}_{k}\,\mathbf{B}_{k}\,\mathbf{M}_{k}\,\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{N}_{k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** This is **the most-tested equation** in SR. Memorize each operator's role and the **order of multiplication** (right-to-left application to $\mathbf{X}$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PSNR from MSE (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Define the per-image squared error matrix $\mathbf{E}(i,j) = \mathbf{X}(i,j) - \hat{\mathbf{X}}(i,j)$.

**Step 2:** The **Mean Squared Error** is the average of squared differences over an $M \times N$ image:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \left[ \mathbf{X}(i,j) - \hat{\mathbf{X}}(i,j) \right]^{2} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{i,j} \mathbf{E}(i,j)^{2}$$

**Step 3:** The **Peak Signal** is the maximum possible pixel value $L$ (for 8-bit images, $L=255$). Signal power is $L^{2}$ (since for a constant image, the energy is $L^{2}$).

**Step 4:** By definition of signal-to-noise ratio in decibels:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\text{Peak Signal Power}}{\text{Mean Noise Power}} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{L^{2}}{\text{MSE}} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the typical value $L = 255$ to get the working form used in MATLAB/Python:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{255^{2}}{\text{MSE}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{255}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}} \right) \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall Alert:** PSNR is computed on **a single channel** for colour images. For RGB, you must either (a) average per-channel PSNR, or (b) compute it on the Y channel of YCbCr only. Mixing approaches causes **up to 3 dB** evaluation error.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SSIM Luminance/Contrast/Structure Decomposition

Start from the universal image quality index (Wang & Bovik, 2002):

$$Q = \frac{4 \sigma_{\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{X}}}\,\mu_{\mathbf{X}}\,\mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}}{\left(\mu_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2}\right)\left(\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2}\right)}$$

**Step 1 (Luminance):** Isolate the mean-based term using the identity $2ab = a^{2}+b^{2} - (a-b)^{2}$:

$$\ell = \frac{2\mu_{\mathbf{X}}\mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{1}}{\mu_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{1}}$$

**Step 2 (Contrast):** Isolate the standard-deviation term:

$$c = \frac{2\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}\sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{2}}{\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{2}}$$

**Step 3 (Structure):** The cross-correlation normalized by its maximum:

$$s = \frac{\sigma_{\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{3}}{\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}\sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{3}}$$

**Step 4 (Combination):** Multiply:

$$\text{SSIM} = \ell \cdot c \cdot s$$

**Step 5 (Convenient Choice):** With $C_{3} = C_{2}/2$, the multiplicative form simplifies to a closed form used in all KTU-approved textbooks:

$$\text{SSIM} = \frac{\left(2\mu_{\mathbf{X}}\mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{1}\right)\!\left(2\sigma_{\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{X}}} + C_{2}\right)}{\left(\mu_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \mu_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{1}\right)\!\left(\sigma_{\mathbf{X}}^{2} + \sigma_{\hat{\mathbf{X}}}^{2} + C_{2}\right)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Iterative Back-Projection (IBP) Update Rule

**Step 1:** Initialize with an estimate $\mathbf{X}^{(0)}$, typically the bicubic interpolation of any one LR frame.

**Step 2:** **Simulate** what the sensor **would have captured** given the current estimate:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}^{(n)} = \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{M}\,\mathbf{X}^{(n)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **observation error** (difference between simulated and actual LR images):

$$\mathbf{E}_{k}^{(n)} = \mathbf{Y}_{k} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}^{(n)}$$

**Step 4:** **Upsample** the error back to HR grid using the **back-projection kernel** $\mathbf{H}_{k}^{-1}$:

$$\Delta^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbf{H}_{k}^{-1}\!\left[\mathbf{E}_{k}^{(n)} \uparrow s\right]$$

**Step 5:** **Update** the HR estimate by adding a fraction $\lambda$ of the back-projected error:

$$\mathbf{X}^{(n+1)} = \mathbf{X}^{(n)} + \lambda \cdot \Delta^{(n)}$$

**Step 6:** Iterate Step 2–5 until $\Vert \mathbf{X}^{(n+1)} - \mathbf{X}^{(n)} \Vert_{2} < \varepsilon$ (a small threshold, e.g., $10^{-4}$).

> [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** The method **back-projects** residual errors into HR space, analogous to CT's filtered back-projection — except applied **iteratively** to refine the estimate. It converges to a local minimum; uniqueness requires a regularizer (POCS/MAP).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Frequency-Domain SR (Tsai-Huang 1984)

Assume $K$ globally shifted LR images $\mathbf{Y}_k$ with sub-pixel shifts $(\Delta x_k, \Delta y_k)$. The continuous Fourier relationship is:

$$Y_{k}(f_{x}, f_{y}) = X(f_{x}, f_{y}) \cdot H(f_{x}, f_{y}) \cdot e^{-j2\pi(f_{x}\Delta x_{k} + f_{y}\Delta y_{k})}$$

**Step 1:** Re-arrange to isolate the unknown HR spectrum $X(f_x, f_y)$:

$$X(f_{x}, f_{y}) = \frac{Y_{k}(f_{x}, f_{y})}{H(f_{x}, f_{y}) \cdot e^{-j2\pi(f_{x}\Delta x_{k} + f_{y}\Delta y_{k})}}$$

**Step 2:** Since the shifts differ per frame, write a system of $K$ linear equations in the unknown $X$ for each frequency pair $(f_x, f_y)$:

$$Y_{k} = X \cdot H \cdot e^{-j2\pi(f_{x}\Delta x_{k} + f_{y}\Delta y_{k})}, \quad k = 1,\dots,K$$

**Step 3:** In matrix form $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{\Phi} \cdot X$, where $\mathbf{\Phi}$ is a $K \times 1$ phase-shift vector.

**Step 4:** The Least-Squares estimate is:

$$\hat{X}(f_{x}, f_{y}) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} Y_{k}(f_{x}, f_{y}) \cdot H^{*}(f_{x}, f_{y}) \cdot e^{+j2\pi(f_{x}\Delta x_{k} + f_{y}\Delta y_{k})}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert H(f_{x}, f_{y}) \vert^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Take the **inverse 2-D DFT** of $\hat{X}(f_x, f_y)$ to obtain the reconstructed HR image $\hat{\mathbf{X}}(u,v)$.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** This derivation is the **standard KTU 14-mark question** under "Explain frequency-domain approach to SR." Step 4 is the **most-marked** step — examiners award 3 marks for correctly writing the LS solution.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Method-wise)

| Method | Domain | Noise Robustness | Computational Cost | Handles Motion? | Medical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bicubic Interpolation | Spatial | Low | Very Low | No | Quick preview |
| IBP | Spatial | Moderate | Low | Limited | Research prototype |
| POCS | Spatial | High | High | Yes | CT/MRI fusion |
| MAP | Spatial | High | High | Yes | MRI multi-slice |
| Tsai-Huang | Frequency | Low | Low | No (global) | Microscopy |
| SRCNN | Spatial (DL) | Moderate | Medium | Implicit (learned) | Real-time PACS |
| ESRGAN | Spatial (DL) | High | High | Implicit (learned) | Diagnostic enhancement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
