### Feature 2: Contrast (Sum of Squares Variance)

$$
f_2 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} (i - j)^2 \, P(i, j)
$$

### Feature 3: Inverse Difference Moment (Homogeneity)

$$
f_3 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} \frac{P(i, j)}{1 + (i - j)^2}
$$

### Feature 4: Angular Second Moment (Energy / Uniformity)

$$
f_4 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} [P(i, j)]^2
$$

### Feature 5: Entropy

$$
f_5 = -\sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} P(i, j) \log_2 P(i, j)
$$

### Feature 6: Correlation

$$
f_6 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} \frac{(i - \mu_x)(j - \mu_y)\, P(i, j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}
$$

where:

$$
\mu_x = \sum_i i \sum_j P(i,j), \quad \mu_y = \sum_j j \sum_i P(i,j)
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \sum_i (i - \mu_x)^2 \sum_j P(i,j), \quad \sigma_y^2 = \sum_j (j - \mu_y)^2 \sum_i P(i,j)
$$

### Feature 7: Sum Average

$$
f_7 = \sum_{k=0}^{2(L-1)} k \cdot P_{x+y}(k)
$$

where $P_{x+y}(k) = \sum_{i+j=k} P(i,j)$

### Feature 8: Sum Entropy

$$
f_8 = -\sum_{k=0}^{2(L-1)} P_{x+y}(k) \log_2 P_{x+y}(k)
$$

### Feature 9: Sum of Squares (Variance)

$$
f_9 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} (i - \mu)^2 \, P(i, j)
$$

### Feature 10: Difference Variance

$$
f_{10} = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} (k - \mu_{x-y})^2 \, P_{x-y}(k)
$$

where $P_{x-y}(k) = \sum_{\vert i - j \vert = k} P(i,j)$ and $\mu_{x-y} = \sum_k k \cdot P_{x-y}(k)$

### Feature 11: Difference Entropy

$$
f_{11} = -\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} P_{x-y}(k) \log_2 P_{x-y}(k)
$$

### Feature 12 & 13: Measures of Correlation (Information-Theoretic)

$$
f_{12} = \frac{H(XY) - H(XY \vert 1)}{\max\{H(X), H(Y)\}}
$$

$$
f_{13} = (1 - \exp[-2(H(XY) - H(XY \vert 1))])^{1/2}
$$

where $H(XY) = -\sum_i \sum_j P(i,j) \log P(i,j)$ and $H(XY \vert 1) = -\sum_i \sum_j P(i,j) \log P_x(i) P_y(j)$.

### Feature 14: Maximal Correlation Coefficient

$$
f_{14} = (\text{Second largest eigenvalue of } Q)^{1/2}
$$

where $Q(i,j) = \sum_k \frac{P(i,k)\, P(j,k)}{P_x(i)\, P_y(k)}$

## 2.4 Gray Level Run Length Matrix (GLRLM)

A **gray level run** is a maximal set of collinear pixels with the same gray level. The **GLRLM** $R(i, j)$ records the number of runs of gray level $i$ with length $j$.

### Galloway's Five Run-Length Features

Let $N_r$ = total number of runs, $N_p$ = total number of pixels.

**1. Short Run Emphasis (SRE):**

$$
SRE = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_i \sum_j \frac{R(i, j)}{j^2}
$$

**2. Long Run Emphasis (LRE):**

$$
LRE = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_i \sum_j j^2 \, R(i, j)
$$

**3. Gray Level Non-Uniformity (GLNU):**

$$
GLNU = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_i \left( \sum_j R(i, j) \right)^2
$$

**4. Run Length Non-Uniformity (RLNU):**

$$
RLNU = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_j \left( \sum_i R(i, j) \right)^2
$$

**5. Run Percentage (RP):**

$$
RP = \frac{N_r}{N_p}
$$

## 2.5 Gray Level Difference Matrix (GLDM)

For a displacement $\mathbf{d}$, the absolute difference between pixel $(x, y)$ and its neighbor $(x + d_x, y + d_y)$ is:

$$
\Delta(x, y) = \vert I(x, y) - I(x + d_x, y + d_y) \vert
$$

The GLDM $D(k)$ counts how often a difference of $k$ occurs. The **contrast** feature is:

$$
CON = \sum_k k^2 \, D(k)
$$

## 2.6 Laws' Texture Energy Measures

**K. I. Laws (1980)** developed a bank of convolution kernels from 1-D vectors, then combined them to form 2-D texture filters:

- **Level (L5):** $\quad[1, 4, 6, 4, 1]$
- **Edge (E5):** $\quad[-1, -2, 0, 2, 1]$
- **Spot (S5):** $\quad[-1, 0, 2, 0, -1]$
- **Wave (W5):** $\quad[-1, 2, 0, -2, 1]$
- **Ripple (R5):** $\quad[1, -4, 6, -4, 1]$

The 2-D kernels are outer products (e.g., $L5 \times E5$ gives an "edge-level" detector). The output is then **windowed** (typically $15 \times 15$ or $25 \times 25$) and the **variance** of the windowed response is used as the texture energy.

## 2.7 Autocorrelation Function

For a region $R$ of size $M \times N$:

$$
C(d_x, d_y) = \frac{\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x, y) \, I(x + d_x, y + d_y)}{\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [I(x, y)]^2}
$$

A texture with **fine granularity** → autocorrelation drops rapidly with increasing $(d_x, d_y)$.
A texture with **coarse granularity** → autocorrelation remains high over a wide range.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` and `\mid` (NOT `|`) inside markdown table cells to avoid syntax breakage.

| # | Feature | Formula | Physical Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GLCM Contrast | $\sum_i \sum_j (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ | Local intensity variation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | GLCM Homogeneity | $\sum_i \sum_j \frac{P(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2}$ | Closeness to diagonal | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | GLCM Energy | $\sum_i \sum_j [P(i,j)]^2$ | Orderliness of texture | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | GLCM Entropy | $-\sum_i \sum_j P(i,j) \log_2 P(i,j)$ | Randomness/Complexity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | GLCM Correlation | $\sum_i \sum_j \frac{(i-\mu_x)(j-\mu_y)P(i,j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | Linear gray-level dependence | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | GLCM Max Prob | $\max_{i,j} P(i,j)$ | Dominant co-occurrence | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Sum Average | $\sum_k k \cdot P_{x+y}(k)$ | Mean of intensity sums | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Difference Entropy | $-\sum_k P_{x-y}(k) \log_2 P_{x-y}(k)$ | Randomness of differences | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | GLRLM SRE | $\frac{1}{N_r}\sum_i \sum_j R(i,j)/j^2$ | Abundance of short runs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | GLRLM LRE | $\frac{1}{N_r}\sum_i \sum_j j^2 R(i,j)$ | Abundance of long runs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | GLRLM RP | $N_r / N_p$ | Density of runs | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | GLDM Contrast | $\sum_k k^2 D(k)$ | Mean gray-level difference | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 13 | First-Order Entropy | $-\sum_i P(i) \log_2 P(i)$ | Histogram randomness | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 14 | First-Order Energy | $\sum_i [P(i)]^2$ | Histogram uniformity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 15 | Skewness | $\frac{1}{\sigma^3}\sum_i (i-\mu)^3 P(i)$ | Histogram asymmetry | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 16 | Autocorrelation | $C(d_x,d_y) = \frac{\sum I(x,y)I(x+d_x,y+d_y)}{\sum [I(x,y)]^2}$ | Fineness measure | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 17 | Laws' Energy | $\text{Var}(I * K)$ over a window | Filtered response variance | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Mammography** | Differentiating benign vs malignant masses using GLCM contrast & entropy |
| **Brain MRI** | Segmenting white/gray matter via run-length statistics |
| **Liver CT** | Classifying cirrhosis using Haralick features |
| **Bone Radiography** | Trabecular bone texture for osteoporosis screening |
| **Histopathology** | GLCM features feed classifiers (SVM, Random Forest) for cancer grading |
| **Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR)** | Texture feature vectors index medical image databases |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: GLCM Computation from Scratch

We will construct a complete GLCM from a **$4 \times 4$ sub-image** with 4 gray levels (0, 1, 2, 3), using a displacement $\mathbf{d} = (1, 0)$ (one pixel to the right, i.e., $0°$ direction).

**Given Image Region $R$:**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1: Identify All Valid Pixel Pairs

We scan the image left-to-right, top-to-bottom. For each pixel at $(x, y)$, we form a pair with the pixel at $(x+1, y)$.

| Source Pixel $(x, y)$ | Value $i$ | Target Pixel $(x+1, y)$ | Value $j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0,0) | 0 | (1,0) | 0 |
| (1,0) | 0 | (2,0) | 1 |
| (2,0) | 1 | (3,0) | 1 |
| (0,1) | 0 | (1,1) | 0 |
| (1,1) | 0 | (2,1) | 1 |
| (2,1) | 1 | (3,1) | 1 |
| (0,2) | 0 | (1,2) | 1 |
| (1,2) | 1 | (2,2) | 2 |
| (2,2) | 2 | (3,2) | 2 |
| (0,3) | 1 | (1,3) | 1 |
| (1,3) | 1 | (2,3) | 2 |
| (2,3) | 2 | (3,3) | 3 |

**Total number of valid pairs:** $N = 12$.

### Step 2: Build the Raw (Un-normalized) GLCM

We initialize a $4 \times 4$ matrix $M$ (one row per source gray level, one column per target gray level) and increment $M(i, j)$ for each pair.

| Pair $(i, j)$ | Increment |
|---|---|
| (0, 0) | M[0,0]++ |
| (0, 1) | M[0,1]++ |
| (1, 1) | M[1,1]++ |
| (0, 0) | M[0,0]++ |
| (0, 1) | M[0,1]++ |
| (1, 1) | M[1,1]++ |
| (0, 1) | M[0,1]++ |
| (1, 2) | M[1,2]++ |
| (2, 2) | M[2,2]++ |
| (1, 1) | M[1,1]++ |
| (1, 2) | M[1,2]++ |
| (2, 3) | M[2,3]++ |

**Counting the increments:**

- $M(0,0) = 2$ (from pairs (0,0) and (0,0))
- $M(0,1) = 3$ (from pairs (0,1), (0,1), (0,1))
- $M(1,1) = 3$ (from pairs (1,1), (1,1), (1,1))
- $M(1,2) = 2$ (from pairs (1,2), (1,2))
- $M(2,2) = 1$ (from pair (2,2))
- $M(2,3) = 1$ (from pair (2,3))

$$
M_{\text{raw}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Symmetrize the GLCM

We add the transpose to make the GLCM direction-symmetric:

$$
M_{\text{sym}} = M_{\text{raw}} + M_{\text{raw}}^T
$$

$$
M_{\text{sym}} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification:** $\sum_{i,j} M_{\text{sym}}(i,j) = 4+3+0+0+3+6+2+0+0+2+2+1+0+0+1+0 = 24 = 2N$ ✓

### Step 4: Normalize the GLCM

Divide every entry by $2N = 24$ to obtain probabilities:

$$
P(i, j) = \frac{M_{\text{sym}}(i, j)}{24}
$$

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 4/24 & 3/24 & 0 & 0 \\ 3/24 & 6/24 & 2/24 & 0 \\ 0 & 2/24 & 2/24 & 1/24 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/24 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1667 & 0.1250 & 0 & 0 \\ 0.1250 & 0.2500 & 0.0833 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.0833 & 0.0833 & 0.0417 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0417 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Haralick Features on the Above GLCM

### 3.2.1 Compute Row & Column Marginals

The row marginals (i.e., $P_x(i) = \sum_j P(i,j)$) are:

$$
P_x(0) = 0.1667 + 0.1250 = 0.2917
$$

$$
P_x(1) = 0.1250 + 0.2500 + 0.0833 = 0.4583
$$

$$
P_x(2) = 0 + 0.0833 + 0.0833 + 0.0417 = 0.2083
$$

$$
P_x(3) = 0 + 0 + 0.0417 + 0 = 0.0417
$$

Since the GLCM is symmetric, $P_y(j) = P_x(j)$, so $\mu_y = \mu_x$ and $\sigma_y = \sigma_x$.

### 3.2.2 Compute the Mean $\mu_x$

$$
\mu_x = \sum_{i=0}^{3} i \cdot P_x(i) = 0(0.2917) + 1(0.4583) + 2(0.2083) + 3(0.0417)
$$

$$
\mu_x = 0 + 0.4583 + 0.4166 + 0.1251 = 1.0000
$$

### 3.2.3 Compute the Variance $\sigma_x^2$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \sum_{i=0}^{3} (i - 1)^2 \cdot P_x(i)
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = (0-1)^2(0.2917) + (1-1)^2(0.4583) + (2-1)^2(0.2083) + (3-1)^2(0.0417)
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = 0.2917 + 0 + 0.2083 + 0.1668 = 0.6668
$$

$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.6668} \approx 0.8166
$$

### 3.2.4 Compute Contrast $f_2$

Recall the contrast formula:

$$
f_2 = \sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3} (i - j)^2 \, P(i, j)
$$

We expand the sum over all non-zero entries:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= (0-0)^2(0.1667) + (0-1)^2(0.1250) \\
&\quad + (1-0)^2(0.1250) + (1-1)^2(0.2500) + (1-2)^2(0.0833) \\
&\quad + (2-1)^2(0.0833) + (2-2)^2(0.0833) + (2-3)^2(0.0417) \\
&\quad + (3-2)^2(0.0417)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= 0 + 0.1250 + 0.1250 + 0 + 0.0833 + 0.0833 + 0 + 0.0417 + 0.0417 \\
f_2 &= 0.5000
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Compute Homogeneity (IDM) $f_3$

$$
f_3 = \sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3} \frac{P(i, j)}{1 + (i - j)^2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_3 &= \frac{0.1667}{1+0} + \frac{0.1250}{1+1} + \frac{0.1250}{1+1} + \frac{0.2500}{1+0} + \frac{0.0833}{1+1} \\
&\quad + \frac{0.0833}{1+1} + \frac{0.0833}{1+0} + \frac{0.0417}{1+1} + \frac{0.0417}{1+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_3 &= 0.1667 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.2500 + 0.0417 + 0.0417 + 0.0833 + 0.0209 + 0.0209 \\
f_3 &= 0.7501
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.6 Compute Energy (ASM) $f_4$

$$
f_4 = \sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3} [P(i, j)]^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_4 &= (0.1667)^2 + (0.1250)^2 + (0.1250)^2 + (0.2500)^2 + (0.0833)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.0833)^2 + (0.0833)^2 + (0.0417)^2 + (0.0417)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_4 &= 0.0278 + 0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0.0625 + 0.0069 + 0.0069 + 0.0069 + 0.0017 + 0.0017 \\
f_4 &= 0.1456
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.7 Compute Entropy $f_5$

$$
f_5 = -\sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3} P(i, j) \log_2 P(i, j)
$$

We compute $P \log_2 P$ for each non-zero entry:

| $P$ | $-\log_2 P$ | $-P \log_2 P$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1667 | 2.585 | 0.4309 |
| 0.1250 | 3.000 | 0.3750 |
| 0.1250 | 3.000 | 0.3750 |
| 0.2500 | 2.000 | 0.5000 |
| 0.0833 | 3.585 | 0.2987 |
| 0.0833 | 3.585 | 0.2987 |
| 0.0833 | 3.585 | 0.2987 |
| 0.0417 | 4.585 | 0.1912 |
| 0.0417 | 4.585 | 0.1912 |

Summing:

$$
f_5 = 0.4309 + 0.3750 + 0.3750 + 0.5000 + 0.2987 + 0.2987 + 0.2987 + 0.1912 + 0.1912 = 2.9594
$$

### 3.2.8 Compute Correlation $f_6$

$$
f_6 = \sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3} \frac{(i - \mu_x)(j - \mu_y) \, P(i, j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y} = \frac{1}{\sigma_x^2} \sum_i \sum_j (i - 1)(j - 1) P(i, j)
$$

We compute the numerator (assuming $\mu_x = \mu_y = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i,j} (i-1)(j-1) P(i,j) &= (0-1)(0-1)(0.1667) + (0-1)(1-1)(0.1250) \\
&\quad + (1-1)(0-1)(0.1250) + (1-1)(1-1)(0.2500) \\
&\quad + (1-1)(2-1)(0.0833) + (2-1)(1-1)(0.0833) \\
&\quad + (2-1)(2-1)(0.0833) + (2-1)(3-1)(0.0417) \\
&\quad + (3-1)(2-1)(0.0417)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 0.1667 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0.0833 + 0.1668 + 0.1668 = 0.5836
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
f_6 = \frac{0.5836}{0.6668} \approx 0.8752
$$

## 3.3 Summary of Computed Features

| Feature | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Contrast | $0.5000$ | Moderate local variation |
| Homogeneity (IDM) | $0.7501$ | High (texture is somewhat smooth) |
| Energy (ASM) | $0.1456$ | Low (texture is not highly uniform) |
| Entropy | $2.9594$ | Moderate complexity |
| Correlation | $0.8752$ | Strong positive linear dependence |
| Mean ($\mu_x$) | $1.0000$ | — |
| Variance ($\sigma_x^2$) | $0.6668$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** A high correlation ($f_6 \approx 0.88$) and moderate contrast ($f_2 = 0.5$) suggest a **structured but not uniform** region — consistent with, e.g., a tissue that has clear gray-level transitions (perhaps a fibrous region) but is locally correlated.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why GLCM Contrast Detects Texture Strength

**Claim:** For a uniform region, $f_2 \approx 0$. For a high-frequency texture, $f_2$ is large.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. For a perfectly uniform region, $I(x, y) = I(x + d_x, y + d_y)$ for all valid pairs, so $i = j$ for all $P(i, j) > 0$.
2. Then $(i - j)^2 = 0$ for all non-zero entries, so:

$$
f_2 = \sum_{i, j} 0 \cdot P(i, j) = 0
$$

3. For a high-frequency texture (e.g., a checkerboard), neighboring pixels differ maximally. For $L$ gray levels, the expected value of $(i - j)^2$ is high, so $f_2$ is large.

4. Therefore, **contrast is monotonic in texture strength**, which is precisely why it is so widely used in medical imaging.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Texture Characterization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image MRI or CT] --> B[Region of Interest ROI Selection]
    B --> C[Preprocessing]
    C --> C1[Intensity Normalization]
    C --> C2[Noise Filtering Gaussian or Median]
    C --> C3[Quantization to L Gray Levels]
    C3 --> D[Texture Feature Extraction]
    D --> D1[First Order Statistics]
    D --> D2[GLCM Construction at Multiple Offsets]
    D --> D3[GLRLM Construction]
    D --> D4[GLDM Construction]
    D --> D5[Laws Texture Filters]
    D --> D6[Autocorrelation Map]
    D1 --> E[Feature Vector Assembly]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Feature Normalization and Selection]
    F --> G[Output Texture Signature Vector]
    G --> H[Classifier or Diagnostic Engine]
    H --> H1[Benign vs Malignant]
    H --> H2[Tissue Type Classification]
    H --> H3[Disease Severity Grading]
```

## 4.2 GLCM Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Image Region R] --> P2[Choose Displacement Vector d]
    P2 --> P3[Initialize Zero Matrix M of Size L x L]
    P3 --> P4[For Each Pixel x y in R]
    P4 --> P5[Read Source Value i]
    P4 --> P6[Read Target Value j at x plus d]
    P5 --> P7[Increment M i j]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Symmetrize M Transpose Added]
    P8 --> P9[Normalize P i j = M i j divided by 2N]
    P9 --> P10[Apply Haralick Feature Formulas]
    P10 --> P11[Contrast Energy Entropy Correlation etc]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Texture Characterization Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Texture Characterization Methods] --> T1[Statistical]
    T --> T2[Structural]
    T --> T3[Spectral and Model Based]
    T1 --> S1[First Order]
    T1 --> S2[Second Order]
    T1 --> S3[Higher Order]
    S1 --> S1A[Mean Variance Skewness Kurtosis]
    S1 --> S1B[Histogram Entropy and Energy]
    S2 --> S2A[GLCM Haralick Features]
    S2 --> S2B[GLRLM Galloway Features]
    S2 --> S2C[GLDM Contrast]
    S3 --> S3A[Local Binary Patterns]
    S3 --> S3B[Gabor Wavelets]
    T2 --> ST1[Texel Definition]
    T2 --> ST2[Placement Rule Analysis]
    T3 --> SP1[Fourier Power Spectrum]
    T3 --> SP2[Markov Random Fields]
    T3 --> SP3[Fractal Dimension]
```

## 4.4 Directional Offset Strategy for GLCM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Displacement Vector Selection] --> O1[0 Degrees d equals 1 0]
    O --> O2[45 Degrees d equals 1 1]
    O --> O3[90 Degrees d equals 0 1]
    O --> O4[135 Degrees d equals 1 minus 1]
    O1 --> F1[GLCM at 0 Degrees]
    O2 --> F2[GLCM at 45 Degrees]
    O3 --> F3[GLCM at 90 Degrees]
    O4 --> F4[GLCM at 135 Degrees]
    F1 --> AVG[Average All 4 Feature Vectors]
    F2 --> AVG
    F3 --> AVG
    F4 --> AVG
    AVG --> OUT[Direction Invariant Texture Signature]
```

## 4.5 Diagnostic Decision Flow Using Texture Features

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Patient Image Acquired] --> R[Extract ROI]
    R --> S[Compute GLCM and GLRLM]
    S --> FE[Compute 14 Haralick Features]
    S --> FG[Compute Galloway Run Length Features]
    FE --> V[Concatenate Feature Vector]
    FG --> V
    V --> W[Feed to Trained Classifier SVM or Random Forest]
    W --> X1{Decision}
    X1 --> Y1[Class 0 Normal Tissue]
    X1 --> Y2[Class 1 Benign Abnormality]
    X1 --> Y3[Class 2 Malignant Lesion]
    Y1 --> Z[Report Generated]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define texture in the context of digital image processing. List the three major approaches to texture characterization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Texture is a region descriptor that quantifies the spatial distribution and variation of gray-level intensities over a neighborhood of pixels. It is a property of a region rather than a single pixel, and is described by the number, type, and spatial arrangement of its primitive elements (texels).

**Three approaches (1.5 marks):**
1. **Statistical Approach** — Treats texture as a stochastic process; uses methods like GLCM, GLRLM, and histogram features.
2. **Structural Approach** — Treats texture as a repetition of well-defined primitives; uses morphological rules.
3. **Spectral / Model-Based Approach** — Analyzes texture in frequency domain or with stochastic models (Fourier, Gabor, Markov Random Fields).

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between first-order and second-order statistics of texture. Give one example feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | First-Order Statistics | Second-Order Statistics |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Used** | Gray-level histogram only | Joint distribution of pixel pairs |
| **Spatial Info** | Ignored | Captured via displacement vector |
| **Example** | Histogram **Entropy** | **GLCM Contrast** (or Homogeneity) |
| **Sensitivity** | Cannot distinguish textures with same histogram | Distinguishes textures with same histogram |

*(Allocate 1.5 marks for the differentiation table and 1.5 marks for the examples with formulas.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) (7 marks):** With a neat diagram, explain the construction of the **Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)**. Define any **four Haralick features** and give their mathematical formulas.

**Model Answer:**

**GLCM Construction (4 marks):**

The GLCM $P(i, j)$ is constructed by counting the number of times a pixel with gray level $i$ occurs at a displacement $\mathbf{d} = (d_x, d_y)$ from a pixel with gray level $j$. The steps are:

1. **Define the displacement vector** $\mathbf{d}$. Common choices: $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$, $(1,1)$, $(1,-1)$ for $0°, 90°, 45°, 135°$.
2. **Traverse the image** with the chosen offset, forming all valid pixel pairs $(i, j)$.
3. **Increment the GLCM entry** $M(i, j)$ for each pair.
4. **Symmetrize** by adding the transpose: $M_{\text{sym}} = M + M^T$.
5. **Normalize** by dividing by $2N$, where $N$ is the number of valid pairs.

**Four Haralick Features with Formulas (3 marks):**

**1. Contrast:**
$$
f_2 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} (i - j)^2 \, P(i, j)
$$

**2. Homogeneity (Inverse Difference Moment):**
$$
f_3 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{P(i, j)}{1 + (i - j)^2}
$$

**3. Energy (Angular Second Moment):**
$$
f_4 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} [P(i, j)]^2
$$

**4. Entropy:**
$$
f_5 = -\sum_{i}\sum_{j} P(i, j) \log_2 P(i, j)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For the diagram**, students should illustrate the offset $(1, 0)$ showing how each pixel pairs with its right neighbor, and present a sample GLCM matrix highlighting the diagonal concentration for uniform textures. The Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.2 serves as a model.

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):** Explain the **Gray Level Run Length Matrix (GLRLM)** method of texture analysis. Define **SRE, LRE, GLNU, RLNU, and RP** with formulas.

**Model Answer:**

**GLRLM Concept (3 marks):**

A **gray level run** is a maximal set of consecutive, collinear pixels (along a chosen direction) that share the same gray level. The **GLRLM** $R(i, j)$ is a 2-D matrix where:
- Row index $i$ = gray level
- Column index $j$ = run length
- Entry $R(i, j)$ = total number of runs of gray level $i$ having length $j$ in the image.

GLRLM is **direction-dependent**, so it is typically computed along $0°, 45°, 90°, 135°$ and the results averaged.

**Five Galloway Features with Formulas (4 marks):**

**1. Short Run Emphasis (SRE):** Emphasizes fine textures (many short runs).
$$
SRE = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \frac{R(i, j)}{j^2}
$$

**2. Long Run Emphasis (LRE):** Emphasizes coarse textures (few long runs).
$$
LRE = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} j^2 \, R(i, j)
$$

**3. Gray Level Non-Uniformity (GLNU):** Small when runs are evenly distributed across gray levels.
$$
GLNU = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_{i} \left( \sum_{j} R(i, j) \right)^2
$$

**4. Run Length Non-Uniformity (RLNU):** Small when runs are evenly distributed across lengths.
$$
RLNU = \frac{1}{N_r} \sum_{j} \left( \sum_{i} R(i, j) \right)^2
$$

**5. Run Percentage (RP):** Ratio of total runs to total pixels.
$$
RP = \frac{N_r}{N_p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SRE is large for fine textures** (e.g., grainy tumor); **LRE is large for coarse textures** (e.g., uniform fat tissue).

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) (7 marks):** Describe the **Laws' Texture Energy Measures** method. List the five 1-D vectors and explain the procedure to compute texture energy.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** K. I. Laws (1980) developed a set of 1-D convolution kernels that, when combined as outer products, produce 2-D texture-sensitive filters. The output image is windowed (typically $15 \times 15$ or $25 \times 25$) and the **variance of the windowed response** is taken as the texture energy measure.

**The Five 1-D Vectors (3 marks):**

| Vector | Symbol | Coefficients |
|---|---|---|
| Level | L5 | $[1, 4, 6, 4, 1]$ |
| Edge | E5 | $[-1, -2, 0, 2, 1]$ |
| Spot | S5 | $[-1, 0, 2, 0, -1]$ |
| Wave | W5 | $[-1, 2, 0, -2, 1]$ |
| Ripple | R5 | $[1, -4, 6, -4, 1]$ |

**2-D Kernels (1 mark):** 2-D filters are formed by **outer products**, e.g., $L5 \times E5^T$, $E5 \times L5^T$, $S5 \times S5^T$, $R5 \times R5^T$, etc. There are 25 possible 2-D kernels from 5 vectors, but only a subset (often 9 or 16) is used in practice.

**Procedure to Compute Texture Energy (1 mark):**
1. Convolve the image with each 2-D kernel.
2. For each filtered image, compute the local variance over a $W \times W$ window.
3. The set of variances from all kernels forms the **texture energy vector**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application:** Laws' method is widely used in **mammographic mass detection** and **industrial defect inspection**.

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):** Explain the **autocorrelation function** for texture characterization. Show how it is used to measure texture fineness, and compute the autocorrelation for a simple $2 \times 2$ image with $\mathbf{d} = (1, 0)$.

**Model Answer:**

**Autocorrelation Function Definition (2 marks):**

For an image region $R$ of size $M \times N$, the autocorrelation at displacement $(d_x, d_y)$ is:

$$
C(d_x, d_y) = \frac{\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x, y) \, I(x + d_x, y + d_y)}{\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [I(x, y)]^2}
$$

The denominator normalizes $C(0, 0) = 1$ by definition.

**Role in Measuring Texture Fineness (2 marks):**

- A **fine texture** has rapidly varying pixel values → autocorrelation drops **quickly** as $(d_x, d_y)$ increases.
- A **coarse texture** has slowly varying pixel values → autocorrelation remains **high** over a wide range of $(d_x, d_y)$.
- The **rate of decay** of $C(d_x, d_y)$ with distance is therefore a direct measure of texture **fineness**.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given the $2 \times 2$ image:
$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Displacement $\mathbf{d} = (1, 0)$: we pair column 1 with column 2.

**Numerator:**
$$
\text{Num} = I(0,0) \cdot I(1,0) + I(0,1) \cdot I(1,1) = 1 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 4 = 2 + 12 = 14
$$

**Denominator:**
$$
\text{Den} = 1^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 4^2 = 1 + 9 + 4 + 16 = 30
$$

**Autocorrelation:**
$$
C(1, 0) = \frac{14}{30} = 0.4667
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $C(1, 0) = 0.467$ is well below $1$, the texture shows moderate variation between horizontally adjacent pixels.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the GLCM** — features will be off by a factor of $N$. Always divide by $2N$ after symmetrization.
> 2. **Confusing SGLDM and SGLDM** — both refer to the same thing (GLCM); ensure you write the full name and equation.
> 3. **Single offset GLCM** — examiners expect averaging over 4 directions for a robust feature vector.
> 4. **Formula signs** — the contrast formula uses $(i - j)^2$, NOT $(i + j)^2$ or $(i^2 - j^2)$.
> 5. **Entropy base** — always specify $\log_2$ for entropy, as this gives results in **bits**.
> 6. **Units of displacement** — the offset is always in **pixels**, not mm, unless explicitly stated.
> 7. **Skipping intermediate steps** — in numerical problems, ALWAYS show the GLCM, the marginals, the means/variances, and the final feature computation. Skipping steps loses **2-3 marks** per question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Texture** is a **region property** that captures spatial variation of pixel intensities; it is *not* a single-pixel property.
> - **Three approaches:** Statistical, Structural, Spectral/Model-based. KTU focuses on **Statistical**.
> - **First-order statistics** use the histogram; ignore spatial info. Examples: Mean, Variance, Skewness, Entropy, Energy.
> - **GLCM (SGLDM)** is a 2-D matrix counting co-occurrences of gray levels at a displacement $\mathbf{d}$. It must be **symmetrized** and **normalized**.
> - **Haralick's 14 features** are extracted from the normalized GLCM. Most-tested: **Contrast, Homogeneity, Energy, Entropy, Correlation**.
> - **Contrast** is large for high-frequency textures; **Homogeneity** is large for smooth textures; **Energy** is large for ordered textures; **Entropy** is large for complex/random textures; **Correlation** is large when gray levels are linearly predictable.
> - **GLRLM** captures run-length statistics — **SRE** for fine, **LRE** for coarse textures. Direction-dependent — average over 4 directions.
> - **GLDM** captures absolute gray-level differences — used to compute a single **contrast** feature.
> - **Laws' method** uses outer-product kernels (L5, E5, S5, W5, R5) and local variance of the filtered image.
> - **Autocorrelation** measures texture **fineness** — rate of decay with displacement.
> - **Displacement vector** $\mathbf{d}$ should be averaged over **0°, 45°, 90°, 135°** for direction invariance.
> - **Quantization** to 8 or 16 gray levels is standard practice to keep the GLCM tractable.
> - **Applications in PEEBT745:** tumor classification (mammography), tissue segmentation (brain MRI), lesion characterization (CT), and CBMIR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: From Markov Random Fields to Fractals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** From Markov Random Fields to Fractals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Markov Random Fields (MRFs) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Random Field (MRF)**: A two-dimensional stochastic process defined on a discrete lattice $\mathcal{S} = \{1, 2, \ldots, M\} \times \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ where the random variables $X = \{X_s : s \in \mathcal{S}\}$ satisfy the **Markovian property** with respect to a neighborhood system $\mathcal{N} = \{\mathcal{N}_s : s \in \mathcal{S}\}$.

Formally, an MRF must satisfy two conditions:

1. **Positivity**: $P(X = x) > 0$ for all realizations $x \in \mathcal{X}$ (strictly positive joint probability).
2. **Markovian Property**: 
$$P(X_s = x_s \mid X_t = x_t, \forall t \neq s) = P(X_s = x_s \mid X_t = x_t, \forall t \in \mathcal{N}_s)$$

This means that the value at any pixel $s$ depends **only on its local neighborhood** $\mathcal{N}_s$, not on the entire image.

### 1.2 Fractals — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractal**: A geometric object exhibiting **self-similarity** across different scales, characterized by a non-integer **fractal dimension** $D_f$ that is strictly greater than its topological dimension. Formally introduced by Benoît Mandelbrot (1975), a fractal is a set $F$ for which the Hausdorff-Besicovitch dimension strictly exceeds the topological dimension.

The defining features of a fractal are:
- **Self-similarity**: The object looks statistically (or exactly) similar at different magnifications.
- **Scale Invariance**: No characteristic length scale dominates the geometry.
- **Fractal Dimension**: A non-integer dimension $D_f > D_{topo}$ that quantifies space-filling complexity.
- **Infinite Detail**: Infinite intricacy at arbitrarily small scales.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making Sense of It All

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for MRFs — "The Neighborhood Gossip Network"**
> 
> Imagine a tightly-knit community where each resident's mood depends *only* on their immediate neighbors (left, right, top, bottom houses), not on what is happening across town. If you know your 4 immediate neighbors, you can predict yourself. This is exactly the MRF principle: **local interactions govern global patterns**. In medical imaging, this means a tumor pixel's intensity is statistically dependent on its surrounding tissue, not the entire MRI slice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Fractals — "The Romanesco Cauliflower"**
> 
> A Romanesco broccoli is the perfect fractal. Look at the whole head — it has spiral cones. Zoom in on a single cone — it has miniature spiral cones. Zoom into one of those — and again, smaller spiral cones! Each piece is a scaled copy of the whole. In medical imaging, **blood vessel trees, lung bronchial branches, and tumor surfaces** display the same self-similar branching patterns. The **fractal dimension** tells you *how complex* the branching is.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bridge Analogy — From Local Rules to Global Complexity**
> 
> MRFs and fractals are deeply connected: **MRFs describe the local statistical rules**, while **fractal analysis quantifies the global geometric complexity that emerges** from these rules. An MRF on a lattice, iterated with certain clique potentials, can produce textures whose **fractal dimension** characterizes their roughness. Together, they form a powerful pipeline: **MRFs model the local pixel interactions** and **fractals measure the resulting multi-scale complexity** of medical structures.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Medical Imaging

- **MRFs** are heavily used in **image segmentation, denoising, and texture classification** of medical scans (MRI, CT, Ultrasound).
- **Fractal analysis** is used in **diagnosing tumors, characterizing bone trabecular structure, retinal vessel analysis**, and **tissue heterogeneity quantification**.
- The combined approach (MRF-based fractal characterization) is an **emerging high-yield research area** and frequently appears in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.
- **Hurst exponent**, **Hausdorff dimension**, and **box-counting dimension** are standard numerical metrics in **bold** that examiners expect students to know.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of MRFs

#### 2.1.1 Neighborhood System & Cliques

A **neighborhood system** $\mathcal{N}$ on a lattice $\mathcal{S}$ is a collection of subsets $\mathcal{N}_s \subset \mathcal{S}$ such that:
- $s \notin \mathcal{N}_s$ (a site is not in its own neighborhood).
- $t \in \mathcal{N}_s \iff s \in \mathcal{N}_t$ (neighborhood relationship is symmetric).

> **Example (4-connected lattice)**: For pixel $s = (i,j)$, the first-order neighborhood is $\mathcal{N}_s = \{(i-1,j), (i+1,j), (i,j-1), (i,j+1)\}$.

A **clique** $c$ is a subset of $\mathcal{S}$ where every pair of sites are mutual neighbors. The set of all cliques is denoted $\mathcal{C}$. Common clique types:
- **Single-site cliques** $\mathcal{C}_1 = \{\{s\} : s \in \mathcal{S}\}$
- **Pairwise cliques** $\mathcal{C}_2 = \{\{s,t\} : t \in \mathcal{N}_s\}$

#### 2.1.2 The Hammersley-Clifford Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hammersley-Clifford Theorem (1971)**: A random field $X$ defined on $\mathcal{S}$ with respect to $\mathcal{N}$ is an **MRF if and only if** its joint distribution is a **Gibbs distribution** with respect to $\mathcal{N}$.

This theorem is the **cornerstone of MRF theory**. It bridges the **local Markov property** with the **global Gibbs energy formulation**, enabling tractable mathematical analysis.

#### 2.1.3 Gibbs Distribution

The Gibbs distribution takes the form:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\frac{U(x)}{T}\right)$$

where:
- $U(x) = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} V_c(x)$ is the **energy function**, summing clique potentials $V_c$ over all cliques $c \in \mathcal{C}$.
- $Z = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \exp(-U(x)/T)$ is the **partition function** (normalization constant).
- $T$ is the **temperature** parameter (analogous to physical temperature; controls randomness).

> **Engineering Utility**: In image segmentation, low-energy configurations correspond to smooth, homogeneous regions, while high-energy configurations represent edges and boundaries. Minimizing $U(x)$ yields the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate of the segmented image.

#### 2.1.4 Common MRF Models in Medical Imaging

| MRF Model | Energy / Clique Potential | Application in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Ising Model** | $V_c(x_i, x_j) = -J$ if $x_i = x_j$; $+J$ otherwise | Binary tissue classification (e.g., tumor vs. normal) |
| **Potts Model** | $V_c(x_i, x_j) = -\beta \cdot \delta(x_i, x_j)$ | Multi-class segmentation (e.g., white matter, gray matter, CSF in MRI) |
| **Gaussian MRF** | $V_c = (x_s - \mu_s)^2 / (2\sigma^2)$ | Image restoration, denoising |
| **Auto-Logistic** | $V_c(x_i, x_j) = \alpha x_i x_j$ | Texture synthesis, classification |

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Fractals

#### 2.2.1 Types of Self-Similarity

- **Exact Self-Similarity**: The fractal is identical at every scale (e.g., Koch snowflake, Sierpinski triangle).
- **Quasi-Self-Similarity**: Approximately identical at different scales (e.g., Mandelbrot set, Menger sponge).
- **Statistical Self-Similarity**: Random fractals that have identical *statistical* properties at different scales (e.g., fractional Brownian motion, medical image textures).

#### 2.2.2 Fractal Dimension: Three Key Formulations

**1. Self-Similarity Dimension (Exact Fractals)**:
If a fractal is composed of $N$ self-similar copies, each scaled by a factor $r < 1$ relative to the whole, then:

$$D_s = \frac{\log N}{\log(1/r)} = -\frac{\log N}{\log r}$$

> **Example (Sierpinski Triangle)**: $N = 3$ copies, scaling $r = 1/2$. Thus $D_s = \log 3 / \log 2 \approx 1.585$.

**2. Box-Counting Dimension (Empirical, for Medical Images)**:
Cover the fractal with boxes of size $\epsilon$ and count the number $N(\epsilon)$ required:

$$D_B = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)}$$

In practice, plot $\log N(\epsilon)$ vs. $\log(1/\epsilon)$ and fit a line; the slope is $D_B$.

**3. Hurst Exponent (For Fractional Brownian Motion, fBm)**:
For fBm $B_H(t)$ with Hurst parameter $H \in (0,1)$:

$$D_f = E + 1 - H$$

where $E$ is the **Euclidean embedding dimension** (e.g., $E=2$ for a 2D image). For a 2D medical image:

$$D_f = 3 - H$$

#### 2.2.3 Fractional Brownian Motion (fBm) — The Statistical Fractal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractional Brownian Motion (fBm)** is the **canonical statistical fractal model** for medical image textures. Defined by the variance relation:
> 
> $$\mathbb{E}\left[\, \vert B_H(t) - B_H(s) \vert^2 \,\right] = \sigma^2 \vert t - s \vert^{2H}$$
> 
> where $H \in (0,1)$ is the **Hurst exponent** and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. The fractal dimension of an fBm surface in 2D is **$D_f = 3 - H$**.

**Hurst Exponent Interpretation**:
- $H = 0.5$: Standard Brownian motion (no memory, no correlation).
- $H > 0.5$: **Persistent** behavior — smooth, correlated, low-frequency-dominant textures (e.g., healthy tissue).
- $H < 0.5$: **Anti-persistent** behavior — rough, jagged, high-frequency-dominant textures (e.g., malignant tumor surfaces).

### 2.3 The MRF-Fractal Bridge — Connecting Local Rules to Global Complexity

The link between MRFs and fractals is multifold:

1. **MRF-Generated Textures Are Often Fractal**: Iterating an MRF on a lattice produces textures with power-law correlation functions, a hallmark of fractals.
2. **Fractal Parameters Describe MRF Textures**: The Hurst exponent $H$ and fractal dimension $D_f$ quantify the multi-scale complexity of textures generated by MRF models.
3. **Joint MRF-Fractal Analysis**: In medical imaging, MRFs segment the image into homogeneous regions, and fractal analysis then quantifies the complexity *within* each region (e.g., tumor heterogeneity).

> **Real-World Utility**: A radiologist assessing a tumor's aggressiveness can use MRF segmentation to isolate the tumor, then apply fractal analysis to compute $D_f$ within the tumor. **Higher $D_f$ (more complex branching) often correlates with malignancy.**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas and constants below are examinable for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Parameter(s) | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Markov Property** | $P(X_s \mid X_{\mathcal{S}\setminus\{s\}}) = P(X_s \mid X_{\mathcal{N}_s})$ | $\mathcal{N}_s$ = neighborhood | Local pixel dependency |
| 2 | **Gibbs Distribution** | $P(X=x) = \dfrac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{U(x)}{T}\right)$ | $U$, $T$, $Z$ | MRF image modeling |
| 3 | **Energy Function** | $U(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} V_c(x)$ | $V_c$ = clique potential | Total energy of an image |
| 4 | **Partition Function** | $Z = \displaystyle\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{U(x)}{T}\right)$ | Sum over all configurations | Normalization in Gibbs model |
| 5 | **Ising Model** | $V_c = -J \, \delta(x_i, x_j) + J \, (1 - \delta(x_i, x_j))$ | $J$ = coupling constant | Binary segmentation |
| 6 | **Potts Model** | $V_c(x_i, x_j) = -\beta \, \delta(x_i, x_j)$ | $\beta$ > 0 | Multi-class segmentation |
| 7 | **Self-Similarity Dim.** | $D_s = -\dfrac{\log N}{\log r}$ | $N$ = pieces, $r$ = scale | Exact fractals (Koch, Sierpinski) |
| 8 | **Box-Counting Dim.** | $D_B = \displaystyle\lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \dfrac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)}$ | $N(\epsilon)$ = box count | Empirical medical image analysis |
| 9 | **fBm Variance Relation** | $\mathbb{E}\left[ \vert B_H(t) - B_H(s) \vert^2 \right] = \sigma^2 \vert t - s \vert^{2H}$ | $H$ = Hurst, $\sigma$ = std. dev. | Texture modeling |
| 10 | **Hurst-Fractal Relation (2D)** | $D_f = 3 - H$ | $H \in (0,1)$ | 2D medical image fractality |
| 11 | **Hurst-Fractal Relation (1D)** | $D_f = 2 - H$ | $H \in (0,1)$ | 1D boundary/profile analysis |
| 12 | **Power Spectral Density** | $S(f) \propto f^{-\beta}, \quad \beta = 2H + E$ | $E$ = embedding dim. | Frequency-domain fractality |
| 13 | **Kolmogorov Complexity Dim.** | $D_K = \displaystyle\lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \dfrac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)}$ | Same as box-counting | Equivalent to $D_B$ |
| 14 | **Ising Hamiltonian** | $\mathcal{H} = -J \displaystyle\sum_{\langle i,j \rangle} s_i s_j - \mu B \sum_i s_i$ | $J$, $\mu$, $B$ | Statistical physics MRF |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Self-Similarity Dimension of the Sierpinski Triangle

> **Problem**: Derive the fractal dimension of the **Sierpinski triangle**, which is formed by repeatedly removing the central inverted triangle from an equilateral triangle.

**Step 1 — Identify the Self-Similar Pieces**:
At each iteration, the Sierpinski triangle consists of **3 smaller equilateral triangles**, each scaled by a factor of $r = 1/2$ relative to the original. Thus $N = 3$ self-similar pieces.

**Step 2 — Apply the Self-Similarity Formula**:
The self-similarity dimension is defined as the unique real number $D_s$ satisfying the scaling relation:

$$N \cdot r^{D_s} = 1$$

Taking logarithms of both sides:

$$\log N + D_s \cdot \log r = 0$$

Solving for $D_s$:

$$D_s = -\frac{\log N}{\log r}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**:

$$D_s = -\frac{\log 3}{\log(1/2)} = \frac{\log 3}{\log 2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerically**:

$$D_s = \frac{1.0986}{0.6931} \approx 1.5849$$

> **Physical Interpretation**: $D_s \approx 1.585$ lies between a line ($D=1$) and a plane ($D=2$), confirming the Sierpinski triangle is a *true fractal* with non-integer dimension. This value is widely used in KTU exam problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Box-Counting Dimension via Log-Log Regression

> **Problem**: Given the box-count data below for a tumor boundary in a CT image, derive the box-counting fractal dimension.

| Box Size $\epsilon$ | Number of Boxes $N(\epsilon)$ |
|---|---|
| 4 px | 312 |
| 8 px | 92 |
| 16 px | 27 |
| 32 px | 8 |

**Step 1 — Transform the Data via Logarithm**:
The box-counting dimension formula is:

$$D_B = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)} = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{-\log \epsilon}$$

Compute $\log N(\epsilon)$ and $\log(1/\epsilon)$ for each $\epsilon$:

- $\epsilon = 4$: $\log N = \log 312 \approx 2.494$; $\log(1/\epsilon) = \log(1/4) = -\log 4 \approx -0.602$
- $\epsilon = 8$: $\log N = \log 92 \approx 1.964$; $\log(1/\epsilon) = \log(1/8) = -\log 8 \approx -0.903$
- $\epsilon = 16$: $\log N = \log 27 \approx 1.431$; $\log(1/\epsilon) = \log(1/16) = -\log 16 \approx -1.204$
- $\epsilon = 32$: $\log N = \log 8 \approx 0.903$; $\log(1/\epsilon) = \log(1/32) = -\log 32 \approx -1.505$

**Step 2 — Recognize the Linear Relationship**:
The box-counting formula implies $\log N(\epsilon)$ is linearly related to $\log(1/\epsilon)$ with slope $D_B$. We can write:

$$\log N(\epsilon) = D_B \cdot \log(1/\epsilon) + C$$

where $C$ is a constant. We perform **linear regression**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Slope (Fractal Dimension)**:
Let $x_i = \log(1/\epsilon_i)$ and $y_i = \log N(\epsilon_i)$. Using the least-squares slope formula:

$$D_B = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

With $n = 4$:

$$\sum x_i = (-0.602) + (-0.903) + (-1.204) + (-1.505) = -4.214$$

$$\sum y_i = 2.494 + 1.964 + 1.431 + 0.903 = 6.792$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = (0.602 \times 2.494) + (0.903 \times 1.964) + (1.204 \times 1.431) + (1.505 \times 0.903)$$

Compute each term:

\begin{aligned}
x_1 y_1 &= 0.602 \times 2.494 = 1.5014 \\
x_2 y_2 &= 0.903 \times 1.964 = 1.7735 \\
x_3 y_3 &= 1.204 \times 1.431 = 1.7229 \\
x_4 y_4 &= 1.505 \times 0.903 = 1.3590
\end{aligned}

$$\sum x_i y_i = 1.5014 + 1.7735 + 1.7229 + 1.3590 = 6.3568$$

$$\sum x_i^2 = 0.602^2 + 0.903^2 + 1.204^2 + 1.505^2 = 0.3624 + 0.8154 + 1.4496 + 2.2650 = 4.8924$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Slope Formula**:

\begin{aligned}
D_B &= \frac{4 \times 6.3568 - (-4.214)(6.792)}{4 \times 4.8924 - (-4.214)^2} \\
&= \frac{25.4272 - (-28.6215)}{19.5696 - 17.7578} \\
&= \frac{25.4272 + 28.6215}{19.5696 - 17.7578} \\
&= \frac{54.0487}{1.8118} \\
&\approx 1.487
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Interpretation**:
The tumor boundary has a fractal dimension of **$D_B \approx 1.487$**. Since this is between 1 (smooth line) and 2 (space-filling surface), the boundary is **moderately irregular**, consistent with a malignant tumor. Healthy tissue boundaries typically show $D_B \approx 1.1$–$1.2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hurst Exponent from fBm Variance

> **Problem**: For a 1D fBm signal sampled at lag $\tau = |t - s|$, show that the Hurst exponent can be estimated from the log-log plot of the absolute increment variance vs. lag.

**Step 1 — Start with the fBm Variance Relation**:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\, \vert B_H(t) - B_H(s) \vert^2 \,\right] = \sigma^2 \tau^{2H}$$

where $\tau = \vert t - s \vert$.

**Step 2 — Define the Empirical Estimator**:
For a discrete signal $B[n]$ of length $N$, the variance of increments at lag $\tau$ is:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2(\tau) = \frac{1}{N - \tau} \sum_{n=1}^{N-\tau} \left( B[n + \tau] - B[n] \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the Logarithm of Both Sides**:
The fBm variance relation implies:

$$\log \hat{\sigma}^2(\tau) = \log(\sigma^2) + 2H \cdot \log(\tau)$$

This is a **linear equation** of the form $Y = m X + b$ where:
- $Y = \log \hat{\sigma}^2(\tau)$
- $X = \log(\tau)$
- $m = 2H$ (slope)
- $b = \log(\sigma^2)$ (intercept)

**Step 4 — Estimate H from the Slope**:

$$H = \frac{m}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta \log \hat{\sigma}^2(\tau)}{\Delta \log(\tau)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Fractal Dimension (1D signal, $E = 1$)**:

$$D_f = 2 - H$$

**Numerical Example**: If the slope $m = 1.4$ from a log-log plot, then $H = 0.7$ and $D_f = 2 - 0.7 = 1.3$. A higher $D_f$ indicates a more complex, irregular 1D profile (e.g., a convoluted tumor boundary in 1D cross-section).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Hammersley-Clifford Equivalence (Sketch)

> **Problem**: Show the essential steps connecting the MRF local property to the global Gibbs distribution.

**Step 1 — MRF Local Condition**:
For every site $s$, by the Markov property:

$$P(X_s = x_s \mid X_{t}, t \neq s) = P(X_s = x_s \mid X_{t}, t \in \mathcal{N}_s)$$

**Step 2 — Factorization via Conditional Independence**:
Repeated application of the chain rule and conditional independence gives:

$$P(X = x) = \prod_{s \in \mathcal{S}} P(X_s = x_s \mid X_{\mathcal{N}_s})$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Local Conditional as a Gibbs Factor**:
Using the positivity condition and the local Markov property, the local conditional probability takes the form:

$$P(X_s = x_s \mid X_{\mathcal{N}_s}) = \frac{1}{Z_s} \exp\!\left(-\sum_{c \ni s} \frac{V_c(x)}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Combine All Local Conditionals**:

$$P(X = x) = \prod_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \frac{1}{Z_s} \exp\!\left(-\sum_{c \ni s} \frac{V_c(x)}{T}\right) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \frac{V_c(x)}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Gibbs Energy**:
The exponent of the combined exponential defines the total energy:

$$U(x) = \sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} V_c(x)$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{P(X = x) = \frac{1}{Z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{U(x)}{T}\right)}$$

This is the canonical Gibbs distribution. The MRF is now **computationally tractable**: the local Markov property reduces the exponential search over all configurations to a sum of local clique potentials.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — MRF vs. Fractal Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is a high-yield KTU comparison matrix. Examiners frequently ask students to contrast MRFs and fractals.**

| Aspect | Markov Random Fields (MRFs) | Fractals |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Statistical mechanics, probability theory | Geometry, chaos theory |
| **Primary Variable** | Random pixel intensities on a lattice | Geometric set in metric space |
| **Defining Property** | Local Markov property | Self-similarity at all scales |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Probability distribution, energy function | Fractal dimension $D_f$, Hurst exponent $H$ |
| **Key Theorem** | Hammersley-Clifford theorem | Mandelbrot's fractals (no central theorem) |
| **Typical Dimension** | Integer (2D image = 2D MRF) | Non-integer (1.2, 1.585, 2.7, etc.) |
| **Computational Goal** | MAP estimation, segmentation | Complexity quantification, classification |
| **Medical Application** | MRI/CT segmentation, denoising | Tumor boundary analysis, retinal vessels |
| **Computational Cost** | NP-hard in general (relaxation methods) | Low (box-counting) to medium (fBm fitting) |
| **Key Parameters** | $U(x)$, $T$, $\mathcal{N}$, $\mathcal{C}$ | $D_f$, $H$, $r$, $N$, $\epsilon$ |
| **Output** | Segmented/restored image | Single scalar ($D_f$ or $H$) per region |
| **Connection** | MRF textures often exhibit fractal properties | Fractal dimension characterizes MRF-generated textures |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — MRF-Fractal Analytical Pipeline for Medical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Medical Image MRI CT Ultrasound] --> A1[Preprocessing Noise Reduction Intensity Normalization]
    A1 --> A2[Define Lattice and Neighborhood System]
    A2 --> A3[Specify Clique Potentials Vc for Ising Potts or Gaussian MRF]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Energy Function U x as sum of Vc]
    A4 --> A5[Derive Gibbs Distribution P X proportional to exp of minus U x over T]
    A5 --> A6[MAP Estimation Iterated Conditional Modes ICM or Simulated Annealing]
    A6 --> A7[Segmented Image with Labeled Regions]
    A7 --> A8[Region of Interest Extraction e.g. Tumor]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Box Counting Method over ROI]
    A9 --> A10[Compute log N epsilon vs log 1 over epsilon Plot]
    A10 --> A11[Linear Regression to Extract Slope D B]
    A11 --> A12[Optional Compute Hurst Exponent H from fBm Variance]
    A12 --> A13[Fractal Dimension D f equals 3 minus H for 2D]
    A13 --> A14[Diagnostic Output D f value per region]
    A14 --> A15{Decision Threshold}
    A15 -- D f above threshold --> A16[Flag as Malignant or High Complexity]
    A15 -- D f below threshold --> A17[Flag as Benign or Low Complexity]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Box-Counting Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start Binary Edge Map of Region] --> B1[Set initial epsilon to image width divided by 2]
    B1 --> B2[Overlay Grid of Square Boxes of Size epsilon]
    B2 --> B3[Count Boxes N epsilon Containing at Least One Edge Pixel]
    B3 --> B4[Record data point epsilon, N epsilon]
    B4 --> B5{epsilon greater than minimum size}
    B5 -- Yes --> B6[Halve epsilon and Repeat]
    B6 --> B2
    B5 -- No --> B7[Collect All data points epsilon, N epsilon]
    B7 --> B8[Compute log epsilon and log N epsilon]
    B8 --> B9[Perform Linear Regression on log N epsilon vs log 1 over epsilon]
    B9 --> B10[Extract Slope of Regression Line as Box Counting Dimension D B]
    B10 --> B11[Output D B as Fractal Dimension Estimate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — MRF Energy Minimization via Iterated Conditional Modes (ICM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Start Initial Label Configuration X superscript 0] --> C1[Set iteration counter k to 0]
    C1 --> C2[Visit Each Pixel s in Lattice in Raster Scan Order]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Local Energy for Each Possible Label x s]
    C3 --> C4[Select Label x s star that Minimizes Local Energy]
    C4 --> C5[Update Pixel s with New Label x s star]
    C5 --> C6{All Pixels Visited}
    C6 -- No --> C2
    C6 -- Yes --> C7[Increment k by 1]
    C7 --> C8{Convergence Energy Unchanged or k above max}
    C8 -- No --> C2
    C8 -- Yes --> C9[Output Final Label Configuration X star as MAP Estimate]
```

