## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Define the term **Statistical Spatio-Structural Space (SSSS)** as used in advanced medical image analysis. List any four features that constitute its statistical sub-space.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** The Statistical Spatio-Structural Space is a multidimensional feature representation framework in which a medical image region is encoded as a composite vector combining *intensity-distribution-based statistical features* and *spatial-arrangement-based structural features*, enabling robust tissue classification.
- **(1 mark) Statistical sub-space** captures the probabilistic behaviour of pixel/voxel intensities (texture).
- **(1 mark) Four features:** Mean intensity, Variance, Skewness, Entropy (or any four: GLCM Energy, GLCM Contrast, Kurtosis, GLCM Correlation).
- **Scoring key:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for stating the role of statistical sub-space, 1 mark for any four correct features.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
What is a **Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)**? With a suitable mathematical expression, define the **GLCM energy** feature and state its range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) GLCM definition:** The GLCM $P(i, j \mid \delta, \theta)$ is a square matrix of size $G \times G$ that tabulates the joint frequency of occurrence of gray levels $i$ and $j$ at a specified spatial offset $\delta$ and direction $\theta$ in an image.
- **(1 mark) Energy formula:** $f_{ASM} = \sum_{i=0}^{G-1} \sum_{j=0}^{G-1} p_{ij}^2$, where $p_{ij}$ is the normalised GLCM entry.
- **(1 mark) Range and meaning:** $1/G^2 \le f_{ASM} \le 1$. Higher values indicate *more uniform* (homogeneous) textures; lower values indicate *more chaotic* textures.
- **Scoring key:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for range and interpretation.

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse *first-order histogram features* (which ignore spatial position) with *second-order GLCM features* (which encode spatial co-occurrence). The examiner deducts up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> - Always state the **range** of normalised features (energy, homogeneity, etc.) — losing 1 mark if omitted.
> - In Moran's I derivations, students forget the $W$ normalisation factor in the numerator — losing up to **2 marks**.
> - For numerical problems, *show all intermediate steps*. A direct final answer without derivation gets at most **50% marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the **GLCM energy** feature starting from the definition of the co-occurrence matrix. State its range and physical interpretation. Discuss how energy changes for a perfectly uniform image versus a highly textured image. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Statistical Spatio-Structural Space (SSSS)** framework. With neat block diagrams, describe the role of *first-order histogram features*, *GLCM-based Haralick features*, and *morphological structural features* in constructing the SSSS representation of a tumour region in a brain MRI. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

*Definition and derivation (3 marks):*

The GLCM at offset $\boldsymbol{\delta}$ and angle $\theta$ is defined as:
$$C(i, j \mid \boldsymbol{\delta}) = \sum_{(x,y)} \mathbb{1}\big[ I(x,y) = i \;\wedge\; I(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) = j \big]$$

Normalising:
$$p_{ij} = \frac{C(i,j \mid \boldsymbol{\delta})}{\sum_{i',j'} C(i',j' \mid \boldsymbol{\delta})}$$

Energy (Angular Second Moment):
$$f_{ASM} = \sum_{i=0}^{G-1} \sum_{j=0}^{G-1} p_{ij}^2$$

*Range (2 marks):*

By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality on the probability simplex:
$$\frac{1}{G^2} \le f_{ASM} \le 1$$

- The **lower bound** $1/G^2$ is achieved when all $p_{ij}$ are equal (maximum disorder / random texture).
- The **upper bound** $1$ is achieved when $p_{ij} = 1$ for exactly one entry (perfectly uniform texture).

*Physical interpretation (2 marks):*

- For a **uniform image** (e.g., a homogeneous CSF region in MRI), $p_{ij}$ is concentrated on the diagonal $(i=j)$, giving $f_{ASM}$ close to $1$.
- For a **highly textured image** (e.g., a glioblastoma with heterogeneous necrotic, enhancing, and oedematous regions), $p_{ij}$ is spread across many entries, giving $f_{ASM}$ close to $1/G^2$.

**(b) — 7 marks:**

*Framework (2 marks):*

The SSSS is a hybrid feature space $\mathcal{M}_{S^3} = \mathcal{M}_{stat} \otimes \mathcal{M}_{struct}$ that maps a medical image ROI to a vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ combining statistical and structural descriptors. The fusion enables discriminative tissue classification.

*First-order histogram features (2 marks):*

For a tumour ROI with intensities $\{I_i\}_{i=1}^N$:
- Mean $\mu = (1/N)\sum I_i$ — average intensity
- Variance $\sigma^2 = (1/N-1)\sum (I_i - \mu)^2$ — intensity spread
- Skewness $\gamma_1 = (1/N)\sum ((I_i-\mu)/\sigma)^3$ — histogram asymmetry
- Entropy $H = -\sum p(g) \log_2 p(g)$ — information content

*GLCM Haralick features (2 marks):*

The GLCM $p_{ij}$ is computed at four directions $\theta \in \{0^{\circ}, 45^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}, 135^{\circ}\}$ and averaged. The 14 Haralick features (Energy, Contrast, Correlation, Homogeneity, Entropy, etc.) are extracted per ROI. These capture *spatial co-occurrence patterns* of intensities — for example, contrast is high at tumour-oedema boundaries.

*Morphological structural features (1 mark):*

Shape descriptors — Area, Perimeter, Compactness $C = P^2/(4\pi A)$, Elongation, Euler Number — capture the *geometry and topology* of the tumour. Malignant tumours typically have lower compactness and higher surface irregularity than benign lesions.

*Concluding synthesis (bonus, optional):*

The concatenated SSSS vector $\mathbf{x}_{S^3} = [\mathbf{x}_{stat}; \mathbf{x}_{struct}]$ is fed to a classifier (SVM, Random Forest) to predict malignancy, achieving significantly higher accuracy than any single feature family alone.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete architecture of a **Statistical Spatio-Structural Space (SSSS)** based Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for medical images. List the seven families of features used in the SSSS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Moran's I** spatial autocorrelation statistic. Derive its mathematical expression step-by-step from first principles. Explain the physical meaning of $I_{Moran} = +1$, $I_{Moran} = 0$, and $I_{Moran} = -1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks:**

*Block diagram (3 marks):* (See Section 4.1 architecture diagram for full details.)

The pipeline stages are:
1. Image acquisition (CT/MRI)
2. Preprocessing (denoising, normalisation)
3. ROI extraction
4. Statistical sub-space (FOH, GLCM, GLRLM/GLSZM)
5. Structural sub-space (morphology, autocorrelation, Hu moments, graph features)
6. Feature concatenation
7. Dimensionality reduction (PCA/LDA)
8. Classification (SVM/CNN)
9. Diagnostic output

*Seven feature families (4 marks):*

1. First-order histogram features (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, entropy)
2. GLCM-based Haralick features (energy, contrast, correlation, homogeneity, entropy, etc.)
3. GLRLM features (run-length emphasis, gray-level emphasis)
4. GLSZM features (zone size non-uniformity)
5. Morphological features (area, perimeter, compactness, sphericity)
6. Spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I, Geary's C)
7. Shape moment invariants (Hu's seven moments)

**(b) — 7 marks:**

*Definition (1 mark):* Moran's I is a measure of *spatial autocorrelation* — i.e., the degree to which neighbouring voxels/regions have similar (or dissimilar) values of a variable.

*Step-by-step derivation (4 marks):*

**Step 1:** Define the spatial weight matrix $w_{ij}$ for $N$ voxels.

**Step 2:** Compute the global weight sum $W = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij}$.

**Step 3:** Compute the mean $\bar{x} = (1/N)\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ of the variable (e.g., intensity).

**Step 4:** Compute the cross-product covariance in the numerator: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})$.

**Step 5:** Compute the total variance in the denominator: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$.

**Step 6:** Assemble:
$$I_{Moran} = \frac{N}{W} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

*Physical interpretation (2 marks):*

- $I_{Moran} \approx +1$ → **Strong positive spatial autocorrelation** — neighbouring voxels have *similar* intensities. Example: a homogeneous white-matter region in MRI.
- $I_{Moran} \approx 0$ → **No spatial autocorrelation** — voxel values are randomly distributed. Example: pure noise.
- $I_{Moran} \approx -1$ → **Strong negative spatial autocorrelation** — neighbouring voxels have *dissimilar* intensities (checkerboard pattern). Example: interleaved bright-dark structures.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ⭐ **SSSS = Statistical + Structural features** concatenated into one high-D vector. *Always* mention this two-part definition.
- ⭐ **First-order features ignore spatial position**; second-order (GLCM) and higher-order (GLRLM, GLSZM) features capture spatial co-occurrence.
- ⭐ **GLCM size = $G \times G$** where $G$ = number of quantised gray levels (commonly 32 or 64).
- ⭐ **Energy range:** $1/G^2 \le f_{ASM} \le 1$. State this in every answer.
- ⭐ **Contrast = 0** for uniform images. **Energy = 1** for perfectly uniform images.
- ⭐ **Moran's I formula** has the $N/W$ prefactor — do NOT forget it.
- ⭐ **Moran's I interpretation:** $+1$ = clustered, $0$ = random, $-1$ = checkerboard.
- ⭐ **Hu moments** are invariant to translation, rotation, and scale — vital for shape-based diagnosis.
- ⭐ **Compactness formula:** $C = P^2/(4\pi A)$ for 2D; use $C = S^3/(36\pi V^2)$ for 3D (isoperimetric).
- ⭐ **The SSSS is the *fusion* manifold**, not a single equation. The classifier operates on the *concatenated* vector.
- ⭐ **IBSI standards:** mention these for cross-hospital reproducibility — a KTU-favourite add-on credit point.
- ⭐ **Use four GLCM directions** ($0°, 45°, 90°, 135°$) and average for rotational invariance.
- ⭐ **Avoid dimension explosion** — apply PCA or LDA after concatenation (curse of dimensionality).
- ⭐ **Real-world application:** Brain MRI tumour grading, lung nodule CAD in CT, mammography lesion classification, cardiac MRI tissue characterisation.
- ⭐ **Examiner pitfall:** Confusing *histogram entropy* (first-order) with *GLCM entropy* (second-order). They are mathematically different.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monogenic Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monogenic Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Monogenic Signal** is a multidimensional (specifically 2D) generalization of the classical one-dimensional **Analytic Signal**, introduced by Felsberg and Sommer (2001). It extends the concept of local amplitude, local phase, and instantaneous frequency from 1D signals to 2D image signals by replacing the 1D **Hilbert Transform** with the 2D **Riesz Transform**.

Formally, for a 2D real-valued grayscale image $f(x,y)$, the monogenic signal is a **3-dimensional vector-valued signal** defined as:

$$
\mathbf{m}(x,y) = \left[\, f(x,y),\;\; \mathbf{R}\{f\}(x,y) \,\right] = \left[\, f(x,y),\;\; f_x(x,y),\;\; f_y(x,y) \,\right]
$$

where $f_x$ and $f_y$ are the two components of the **Riesz transform** of $f$ along the $x$ and $y$ spatial directions, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The monogenic signal is the foundational 2D operator used in advanced medical image analysis for computing **local amplitude (energy)**, **local phase (structural symmetry)**, and **local orientation (feature direction)** — three physically meaningful local image attributes that are invariant to illumination and contrast variations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor examining a tissue sample** under a polarized-light microscope.

- The **brightness** you see in the image is the *intensity* → this gives you **local amplitude** (how strong the signal is at that pixel).
- The **stripe patterns** formed by tissue fibers have a *peak-and-valley* structure → this is the **local phase** (where on a wave cycle are we?).
- The **angle at which the fibers run** across the slide → this is the **local orientation**.

The monogenic signal is essentially a mathematical "toolbox" that takes any 2D medical image (MRI, CT, Ultrasound, X-ray) and, at *every single pixel*, simultaneously tells you:

1. **How strong** is the local structure? (Amplitude)
2. **What shape** does it locally resemble — peak, valley, or edge? (Phase)
3. **Which way is it pointing**? (Orientation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **hiking compass combined with a topographic map**. The compass needle = orientation; the map elevation lines = phase; the boldness of the lines = amplitude. The monogenic signal computes all three locally for every pixel in one unified framework.

## 1.3 Why a 2D Extension is Needed

The classical 1D analytic signal works beautifully for time series (e.g., ECG, EEG, audio). However, medical images are inherently **2D spatial structures**. A direct "stacking" of two 1D Hilbert transforms fails because:

- It produces a **2D signal** but **lacks rotational isotropy** (behaves differently for horizontal vs. vertical structures).
- It cannot extract a single meaningful 2D **orientation angle**.
- It is not symmetric in all directions.

The Riesz transform fixes this by providing a **rotationally invariant** multidimensional extension.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Property |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega_0$ | Center frequency of bandpass filter | Tunable (e.g., 0.4 cycles/pixel) |
| $\sigma$ | Bandwidth parameter of Log-Gabor | Typically $\sigma/\omega_0 \approx 0.75$ |
| $A$ | Local amplitude | $\geq 0$ (real, non-negative) |
| $\phi$ | Local phase | $\in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| $\theta$ | Local orientation | $\in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| $\mathbf{R}$ | Riesz Transform operator | Linear, shift-invariant |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 1D Analytic Signal — Building Block

For a 1D real signal $f(t)$, the analytic signal is:

$$
f_A(t) = f(t) + j\,\mathcal{H}\{f\}(t)
$$

where $\mathcal{H}$ is the **Hilbert transform** with frequency response:

$$
H(\omega) = -j\,\text{sgn}(\omega)
$$

The Hilbert transform **shifts each frequency component by $-\pi/2$**, effectively creating a 90° phase quadrature component. This is why the analytic signal has the form $A(t)\,e^{j\phi(t)}$ — a complex envelope with instantaneous amplitude and phase.

## 2.2 The 2D Riesz Transform — Core Operator

The Riesz transform $\mathbf{R} = (R_x, R_y)$ is the 2D isotropic counterpart of the Hilbert transform. It is defined by its **transfer function** in the 2D frequency domain $(u,v)$:

$$
\mathbf{H}_R(u,v) = \left[\, H_x(u,v),\;\; H_y(u,v) \,\right] = \left[\, -j\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}},\;\; -j\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}} \,\right]
$$

**Equivalent magnitude and phase of the Riesz kernel:**

$$
\|\mathbf{H}_R(u,v)\| = \sqrt{H_x^2 + H_y^2} = 1 \quad\text{(unit magnitude)}
$$

$$
\angle \mathbf{H}_R(u,v) = -\frac{\pi}{2} \quad\text{(purely imaginary, isotropic 90° shift)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property:** The Riesz transform is the **only** isotropic, shift-invariant, linear operator that generalizes the Hilbert transform from 1D to arbitrary dimensions. This uniqueness is what makes the monogenic signal mathematically elegant and KTU-exam relevant.

## 2.3 Spatial-Domain Riesz Kernels

By inverse Fourier transform, the Riesz kernels in the spatial domain are:

$$
h_x(x,y) = \frac{x}{2\pi\,(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}}
$$

$$
h_y(x,y) = \frac{y}{2\pi\,(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}}
$$

The Riesz-transformed image is the convolution:

$$
f_x(x,y) = (h_x * f)(x,y), \qquad f_y(x,y) = (h_y * f)(x,y)
$$

## 2.4 Bandpass Pre-filtering — The Log-Gabor Stage

A raw monogenic signal of a broadband image $f$ gives a meaningless amplitude/phase (DC dominates). Standard practice is to **pre-filter** $f$ with a **bandpass Log-Gabor filter** $B(\omega)$ to isolate a specific spatial frequency band.

The 2D isotropic Log-Gabor filter is:

$$
B(\omega) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\left[\ln(\omega / \omega_0)\right]^2}{2\,\left[\ln(\sigma / \omega_0)\right]^2}\right)
$$

where $\omega = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ is the radial frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log-Gabor and not Gaussian?** Log-Gabor filters have **no DC component** and an extended high-frequency tail, making them ideal for natural and medical images which have most energy in low frequencies.

## 2.5 Local Amplitude, Phase, and Orientation Extraction

After bandpass filtering, let the filtered image be $f_B$. Its monogenic components are $(f_B, f_{Bx}, f_{By})$. The three local features are:

| Feature | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Amplitude** | $A = \sqrt{f_B^2 + f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2}$ | Local energy / contrast |
| **Local Phase** | $\phi = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2},\; f_B\right)$ | Local symmetry type |
| **Local Orientation** | $\theta = \text{atan2}(f_{By},\; f_{Bx})$ | Dominant gradient direction |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{m}(x,y) = [f,\; f_x,\; f_y]$ | Monogenic signal definition | Vector |
| 2 | $H_x(u,v) = -j\,u/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$ | Riesz x-component TF | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $H_y(u,v) = -j\,v/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$ | Riesz y-component TF | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $h_x(x,y) = x/[2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}]$ | Spatial Riesz x-kernel | 1/length² |
| 5 | $h_y(x,y) = y/[2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}]$ | Spatial Riesz y-kernel | 1/length² |
| 6 | $B(\omega) = \exp\!\big(-[\ln(\omega/\omega_0)]^2 / [2\ln^2(\sigma/\omega_0)]\big)$ | Log-Gabor bandpass | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $A = \sqrt{f_B^2 + f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2}$ | Local amplitude | Intensity |
| 8 | $\phi = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2},\; f_B)$ | Local phase | Radians |
| 9 | $\theta = \text{atan2}(f_{By},\; f_{Bx})$ | Local orientation | Radians |
| 10 | $\|H_x^2 + H_y^2\| = 1$ | Riesz TF magnitude identity | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Phase vs. Orientation Range:** Phase $\phi \in [-\pi, \pi]$ encodes *what kind* of structure (peak, valley, edge) — it is a **scalar**. Orientation $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ encodes *which way* it points — it is also scalar but tied to the local **gradient** direction. Do not confuse them in exams.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Vessel orientation in retinal fundus & MR angiography (orientation $\theta$)
- **Tumor Boundary Detection:** Phase symmetry reveals edges invariant to contrast
- **Ultrasound Elastography:** Phase-based local frequency estimates
- **Fingerprint / Biometric:** Ridge orientation extraction
- **Texture Analysis in Pathology:** Multi-scale amplitude/phase signatures
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Riesz Transfer Function Has Unit Magnitude

We must prove that $\vert H_x(u,v) \vert^2 + \vert H_y(u,v) \vert^2 = 1$.

**Step 1 — Write the components.**

$$
H_x(u,v) = -j\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}, \qquad H_y(u,v) = -j\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared magnitude of each.**

Since $\vert -j \vert^2 = 1$:

$$
\vert H_x \vert^2 = \frac{u^2}{u^2+v^2}, \qquad \vert H_y \vert^2 = \frac{v^2}{u^2+v^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum them.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H_x \vert^2 + \vert H_y \vert^2 &= \frac{u^2}{u^2+v^2} + \frac{v^2}{u^2+v^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{u^2 + v^2}{u^2+v^2} \\[4pt]
&= 1 \quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Meaning:** The Riesz transform redistributes energy isotropically across the two quadrature components — it neither amplifies nor attenuates, which makes it an ideal **all-pass** 90° phase-shifting filter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Local Phase Range and Symmetry Interpretation

We derive the constraint $\phi \in [0, \pi]$ (typically signed to $[-\pi, \pi]$).

**Step 1 — Define the quadrature magnitude.**

$$
q(x,y) = \sqrt{f_x^2(x,y) + f_y^2(x,y)} = \|\nabla f_B(x,y)\| / \text{(effective scaling)}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the atan2 identity.**

Since both arguments of $\text{atan2}$ are non-negative in our formulation (the magnitude $q \geq 0$ and the even component $f_B$ is signed), the resulting angle lies in:

$$
\phi = \text{atan2}\!\left(q,\; f_B\right) \in [-\pi, \pi]
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi = 0 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{local peak (bright spot)} \\
\phi = \pm\pi \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{local valley (dark spot)} \\
\phi = \pm\pi/2 \;&\Rightarrow\; \text{local step / edge}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **phase symmetry** (using $\cos\phi$ or $|\cos\phi|$) is used for **feature detection** in medical images.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Form of Local Orientation in Frequency Domain

The local orientation is the **direction of the gradient** $\nabla f_B = (f_{Bx}, f_{By})$.

**Step 1 — Express the gradient in frequency domain.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{Bx}(u,v) &= H_x(u,v)\cdot F_B(u,v) = -j\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}\,F_B(u,v) \\[4pt]
F_{By}(u,v) &= H_y(u,v)\cdot F_B(u,v) = -j\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}\,F_B(u,v)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute orientation in frequency domain.**

The complex ratio:

$$
\frac{F_{By}(u,v)}{F_{Bx}(u,v)} = \frac{v}{u} = \tan(\theta_{spatial})
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Fourier transform to spatial domain.**

$$
\theta(x,y) = \text{atan2}\!\left(\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{F_{By}\},\;\; \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{F_{Bx}\}\right)
$$

This is computationally efficient — orientation is recovered by **two FFTs and one IFFT per axis**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feature Decoding Table (Exhaustive)

| Local Phase $\phi$ | Even Part $f_B$ | Odd Part Magnitude $q$ | Visual Appearance | Medical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | Positive max | Zero | Bright dot | Microcalcifications |
| $\pi/4$ | Positive | Moderate | Asymmetric peak | Ridge top |
| $\pi/2$ | Zero | Maximum | Sharp edge | Lesion boundary |
| $3\pi/4$ | Negative | Moderate | Dark-valley edge | Vessel wall |
| $\pi$ | Negative max | Zero | Dark dot | Cyst core |
| $-\pi/2$ | Zero | Maximum | Opposite edge | Boundary continuity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The monogenic signal is **amplitude-preserving** ($\|H_R\| = 1$), meaning the original image is fully recoverable from the three monogenic components — no information is lost. This is in contrast to gradient-only methods (Sobel, Prewitt) which discard structural phase information.

## 3.5 Derivation: Multi-Scale Monogenic Signal Energy

For a multi-scale pyramid with $S$ scales, the **total local energy** is:

$$
E_{total}(x,y) = \sum_{s=1}^{S} A_s^2(x,y) = \sum_{s=1}^{S} \left[\,f_{B,s}^2 + f_{Bx,s}^2 + f_{By,s}^2\,\right]
$$

where subscript $s$ denotes the $s$-th scale. This energy map is widely used in **Phase-Based Local Feature Detection** for medical images, as it is robust to intensity scaling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Monogenic Signal Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input 2D Medical Image f of x, y] --> B[FFT 2D Forward Transform F of u, v]
    B --> C[Log Gabor Bandpass Filter B of omega]
    C --> D[Filtered Spectrum F sub B of u, v]
    D --> E1[Multiply by H sub x of u, v]
    D --> E2[Multiply by H sub y of u, v]
    D --> F[Keep as Even Part F sub B of u, v]
    E1 --> G1[IFFT yields f sub Bx of x, y]
    E2 --> G2[IFFT yields f sub By of x, y]
    F --> G3[IFFT yields f sub B of x, y]
    G3 --> H1[Local Amplitude A]
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H2[Local Phase phi]
    G1 --> H2
    G2 --> H2
    G1 --> H3[Local Orientation theta]
    G2 --> H3
    H1 --> I1[Amplitude Map for Energy Detection]
    H2 --> I2[Phase Map for Symmetry and Edge]
    H3 --> I3[Orientation Map for Structure Tracking]
    I1 --> J[Feature Output for Medical Diagnosis]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow: From Image to Three Local Feature Maps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Medical Image] --> B[Bandpass Log Gabor]
    B --> C1[Even Component f sub B]
    B --> C2[Odd Component via Riesz x]
    B --> C3[Odd Component via Riesz y]
    C1 --> D1[Combine to Form Monogenic 3 Vector]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Amplitude A]
    D1 --> E2[Phase phi]
    D1 --> E3[Orientation theta]
    E1 --> F1[Tumor Energy Map]
    E2 --> F2[Edge and Symmetry Map]
    E3 --> F3[Fiber and Vessel Direction Map]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Mapping: 1D Analytic vs 2D Monogenic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[1D Analytic Signal] --> A1[Uses Hilbert Transform]
    A1 --> A2[Single Transfer Function H of omega equals negative j sign of omega]
    A2 --> A3[Produces 2 Vector Complex Signal]
    B[2D Monogenic Signal] --> B1[Uses Riesz Transform]
    B1 --> B2[Two Transfer Functions H sub x and H sub y]
    B2 --> B3[Produces 3 Vector Real Signal]
    A3 --> C[Amplitude and Phase]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Feature Maps for Medical Image Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a mathematical stage. Solid arrows are forward data flow. The three output feature maps (Amplitude, Phase, Orientation) are the **engineered deliverables** used downstream by segmentation, registration, and computer-aided-diagnosis (CAD) systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Monogenic Signal. Why is the Riesz transform preferred over two independent Hilbert transforms for 2D image analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **monogenic signal** of a 2D grayscale image $f(x,y)$ is the vector-valued extension $\mathbf{m}(x,y) = [f(x,y),\; f_x(x,y),\; f_y(x,y)]$ where $(f_x, f_y) = \mathbf{R}\{f\}$ is the **Riesz transform** of $f$. It is the multidimensional generalization of the 1D analytic signal.

**Why Riesz over two Hilbert transforms (3 key points):**

1. **Rotational Isotropy:** The Riesz transform is the *only* isotropic 2D operator generalizing the 1D Hilbert transform — independent x- and y-Hilbert transforms are not rotationally symmetric.
2. **Single Orientation Angle:** It yields a unique, well-defined local orientation $\theta = \text{atan2}(f_y, f_x)$, while two independent Hilbert transforms cannot.
3. **Energy Preservation:** $\|H_x\|^2 + \|H_y\|^2 = 1$, so the transform is all-pass and lossless.

*(Examiner: All three points needed for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three local features extracted from a 2D monogenic signal. State the formula and the physical meaning of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given the monogenic components $(f_B, f_{Bx}, f_{By})$ after bandpass filtering:

| Feature | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Amplitude** | $A = \sqrt{f_B^2 + f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2}$ | Local energy / contrast strength |
| **Local Phase** | $\phi = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2},\; f_B\right)$ | Structural symmetry (peak/valley/edge) |
| **Local Orientation** | $\theta = \text{atan2}(f_{By}, f_{Bx})$ | Direction of dominant local gradient |

*(1 mark for listing all three + 1 mark for correct formulas + 1 mark for physical meanings.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of the 2D Riesz transform. Prove that its magnitude is unity for all non-zero spatial frequencies. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a bandpass-filtered 2D image $f_B(x,y)$ with monogenic components $(f_B,\; f_{Bx},\; f_{By})$, derive expressions for the **local amplitude, local phase, and local orientation**, and explain how phase values $\phi = 0,\; \pi/2,\; \pi$ correspond to specific structural features. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Motivation:** The 1D Hilbert transform is defined by $H(\omega) = -j\,\text{sgn}(\omega)$, a 90° phase shifter. To generalize it to 2D with isotropy, we require a vector-valued operator whose output is rotated by 90° in *all* directions.

**Step 2 — Construction:** The Riesz transform is a 2D vector $\mathbf{R} = (R_x, R_y)$ with components in the frequency domain:

$$
H_x(u,v) = -j\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}, \qquad H_y(u,v) = -j\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\mathbf{H}_R\|^2 &= \vert H_x \vert^2 + \vert H_y \vert^2 \\
&= \frac{u^2}{u^2+v^2} + \frac{v^2}{u^2+v^2} \\
&= \frac{u^2+v^2}{u^2+v^2} = 1 \quad\forall\,(u,v) \neq (0,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Phase Property:** Both components are purely imaginary ($H_x = -j \cdot \text{real}$), so they shift phase by $-\pi/2$ uniformly — confirming isotropic 90° rotation.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The Riesz transform is a **unit-magnitude, isotropic 90° phase shifter**, generalizing the 1D Hilbert transform to 2D.

*(Marking: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 2, Step 3 = 2, Step 4 = 1, Step 5 = 1.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Local Amplitude:** The amplitude is the Euclidean norm of the monogenic 3-vector:

$$
A(x,y) = \sqrt{f_B^2 + f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Local Phase:** Defined via the two-argument arctangent:

$$
\phi(x,y) = \text{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2},\; f_B\right) \in [-\pi, \pi]
$$

**Step 3 — Local Orientation:** Defined as the angle of the odd (gradient) component in the 2D plane:

$$
\theta(x,y) = \text{atan2}(f_{By}, f_{Bx}) \in \left[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Phase-Structure Correspondence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi = 0 &\;\Rightarrow\; f_B > 0,\; q = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{local peak (bright spot)} \\
\phi = \pi/2 &\;\Rightarrow\; f_B = 0,\; q > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{local step / edge} \\
\phi = \pi &\;\Rightarrow\; f_B < 0,\; q = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{local valley (dark spot)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Application:** In **mammography**, $\phi \approx \pi/2$ highlights microcalcification edges; in **MR angiography**, $\theta$ maps vessel directions; in **dermatology**, $A$ reveals lesion boundaries independent of skin tone contrast.

*(Marking: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1, Step 3 = 1, Step 4 = 2, Step 5 = 2.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain why a **Log-Gabor bandpass filter** is applied before computing the Riesz transform in a monogenic signal framework. Provide its mathematical form and discuss the role of $\omega_0$ and $\sigma$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **1D Analytic Signal** and the **2D Monogenic Signal** in terms of: (i) underlying transform, (ii) dimensionality of the output signal, (iii) extracted local features, and (iv) range of orientation/phase. Include a block diagram in your explanation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Need for Bandpass:** A monogenic signal of a raw image $f$ (which contains DC and broadband noise) yields a dominant low-frequency amplitude with no useful spatial structure. A **bandpass pre-filter** is essential to isolate meaningful structural frequencies.

**Step 2 — Why Log-Gabor:** Log-Gabor filters have:
- **Zero DC component** (good for natural and medical images where DC carries little diagnostic info)
- **Extended high-frequency tail** (matches the 1/f power spectrum of medical images)
- **Compact log-scale bandwidth** controlled by a single parameter

**Step 3 — Mathematical Form:**

$$
B(\omega) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\left[\ln(\omega/\omega_0)\right]^2}{2\,\left[\ln(\sigma/\omega_0)\right]^2}\right)
$$

where $\omega = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ is the radial frequency.

**Step 4 — Role of Parameters:**
- $\omega_0$ (center frequency): selects the **scale** of analysis (small $\omega_0$ → coarse structures, large $\omega_0$ → fine details)
- $\sigma$ (bandwidth parameter): controls the **frequency spread**; the ratio $\sigma/\omega_0 \approx 0.75$ yields an octave bandwidth

**Step 5 — Multi-scale Extension:** A monogenic pyramid uses $S$ Log-Gabor filters with different $\omega_0$ values, producing a multi-scale feature set $(A_s, \phi_s, \theta_s)$ for $s = 1, \dots, S$.

*(Marking: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1, Step 3 = 1, Step 4 = 2, Step 5 = 2.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Aspect | 1D Analytic Signal | 2D Monogenic Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | Underlying transform | Hilbert transform $\mathcal{H}$ | Riesz transform $\mathbf{R} = (R_x, R_y)$ |
| ii | Output dimensionality | 2-vector (complex) | 3-vector (real) |
| iii | Local features | Amplitude, phase | Amplitude, phase, orientation |
| iv | Phase range | $\phi \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | $\phi \in [-\pi, \pi]$; $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| v | Isotropy | N/A (1D) | Yes — only isotropic 2D extension |

**Block Diagram Comparison (textual):**

1D pipeline: $f(t) \rightarrow \mathcal{H} \rightarrow f_A(t) = f + j\mathcal{H}\{f\} \rightarrow A(t),\;\phi(t)$

2D pipeline: $f(x,y) \rightarrow$ Log-Gabor $\rightarrow f_B \rightarrow$ Riesz $(R_x, R_y) \rightarrow (f_B, f_{Bx}, f_{By}) \rightarrow A,\;\phi,\;\theta$

**Key Advantage of 2D:** The 1D signal gives only amplitude and phase, while the 2D monogenic signal adds a **third feature — orientation** — which is crucial for medical structures with directional anatomy (vessels, muscle fibers, nerve tracts, trabecular bone).

*(Marking: Table = 3 marks, Pipeline = 2 marks, Discussion = 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Phase and Orientation** — Phase is a *symmetry indicator* (peak/edge/valley), Orientation is a *directional indicator* (angle of gradient). They are NOT the same and have DIFFERENT ranges.
> 2. **Forgetting the Bandpass Step** — Many students apply the Riesz transform directly to a raw image and report meaningless DC-dominated amplitude maps. Always **explicitly mention the Log-Gabor pre-filtering** in your answers.
> 3. **Writing $\|H_x\| + \|H_y\| = 1$** — The correct identity is the **sum of squares** $\vert H_x \vert^2 + \vert H_y \vert^2 = 1$ (Parseval-like energy preservation).
> 4. **Skipping the 2D spatial kernel form** — KTU frequently asks for both frequency- and spatial-domain expressions of the Riesz kernel. Memorize both $H_x(u,v)$ and $h_x(x,y)$.
> 5. **Not stating the range of phase/orientation** — Examiners explicitly test whether you know $\phi \in [-\pi,\pi]$ vs. $\theta \in [-\pi/2,\pi/2]$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Monogenic Signal** = 2D generalization of the analytic signal using the **Riesz transform**.
- ✅ Output is a **3-vector**: $\mathbf{m} = (f,\; f_x,\; f_y)$.
- ✅ **Riesz Transfer Function:** $H_x = -j\,u/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$, $H_y = -j\,v/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$.
- ✅ **Unit-magnitude property:** $\vert H_x \vert^2 + \vert H_y \vert^2 = 1$ — proves energy preservation.
- ✅ **Spatial Riesz kernels:** $h_x = x/[2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}]$, $h_y = y/[2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}]$.
- ✅ **Log-Gabor bandpass** is mandatory before feature extraction to remove DC and noise.
- ✅ **Local Amplitude:** $A = \sqrt{f_B^2 + f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2}$ — energy/contrast.
- ✅ **Local Phase:** $\phi = \text{atan2}(\sqrt{f_{Bx}^2 + f_{By}^2},\; f_B)$ — symmetry (peak/edge/valley).
- ✅ **Local Orientation:** $\theta = \text{atan2}(f_{By},\; f_{Bx})$ — directional angle of structure.
- ✅ **Phase values:** $0$ = peak, $\pi/2$ = edge, $\pi$ = valley.
- ✅ **Orientation range:** $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$; **Phase range:** $[-\pi, \pi]$.
- ✅ **Riesz is the ONLY** isotropic 2D extension of the Hilbert transform.
- ✅ **Applications:** Vessel/ridge orientation, tumor edge symmetry, elastography, multi-scale texture analysis in CT, MRI, ultrasound, and mammography.
- ✅ **Multi-scale energy:** $E_{total} = \sum_s A_s^2$ — robust to intensity scaling.
- ✅ **Computational efficiency:** Only 1 forward FFT + 1 IFFT per output channel in frequency domain.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: From Monogenic Signal Back to Gabor Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** From Monogenic Signal Back to Gabor Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **monogenic signal** is the 2D generalization of the 1D analytic signal, constructed by combining a real, band-limited image $f(x,y)$ with its two **Riesz transform** components, $h_x * f$ and $h_y * f$. Formally, the monogenic signal is a 3-vector in the spatial domain:

$$
f_M(x,y) = \left( f(x,y),\; (h_x * f)(x,y),\; (h_y * f)(x,y) \right)
$$

where $h_x$ and $h_y$ are the 2D Riesz kernels defined in the frequency domain as:

$$
H_x(u,v) = -i\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2 + v^2}}, \qquad H_y(u,v) = -i\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2 + v^2}}
$$

A **Gabor function** is a Gaussian-windowed complex sinusoid that achieves the joint localization lower bound in both spatial and frequency domains. Its 2D form is:

$$
g(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\,\exp\!\big(i(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y + \phi)\big)
$$

The bridge between the two concepts lies in the observation that **a Gabor bandpass filter, when split into its even (real) and odd (imaginary) parts and then rotated to align with local orientation, can be re-expressed as the local components of a monogenic signal**. This is the essence of the topic *"From Monogenic Signal Back to Gabor Functions"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Module 2):** The monogenic signal is the natural 2D / 3D generalization of the analytic signal and provides a **rotation-invariant** decomposition of local image structure into amplitude, phase, and orientation. When the bandpass pre-filter is chosen to be a Gabor function, the two frameworks become mathematically equivalent.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are standing on a beach looking at incoming waves. Each wave can be described by three things:
1. **How tall is it?** (amplitude)
2. **Where is its crest right now?** (phase)
3. **Which direction is it rolling?** (orientation)

In 1D (a single microphone recording), the **analytic signal** is the tool engineers use to extract these three things. But an image is not 1D — it has waves running in many directions. The **monogenic signal** is the 2D upgrade of the analytic signal: it listens to the image in two perpendicular "ears" simultaneously, allowing the computer to measure **how strong, where, and in which direction** local patterns exist.

A **Gabor filter** is like a tunable lens that the computer uses to focus on a particular spatial frequency and orientation. Now, here's the beautiful part: when you look at what the monogenic signal does, the filter it implicitly uses to extract local structure is *exactly* a Gabor-shaped window in the frequency domain. So the monogenic signal can be seen as the "engine," and the Gabor function is the "lens" — they are two sides of the same coin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful in medical imaging?** Ultrasound speckle, MRI texture, and X-ray bone patterns all have characteristic local orientation. The monogenic signal can encode all of them in one pass, which is critical for Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\pi$ | Geometric constant | **3.14159...** |
| $\sigma$ | Gaussian standard deviation (Gabor) | **pixels** |
| $f_0 = \sqrt{u_0^2 + v_0^2}$ | Centre frequency of Gabor | **cycles/pixel** |
| $\theta_0 = \mathrm{atan2}(v_0, u_0)$ | Preferred orientation of Gabor | **radians** |
| $r = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ | Radial frequency | **cycles/pixel** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — From Monogenic Signal to Gabor Reconstruction

The monogenic signal performs two roles: a **bandpass pre-filter** (typically a Gabor or log-Gabor) and a **Riesz rotation** of the local spectrum. The reverse mapping is reconstructed through these steps:

1. **Apply a Gabor bandpass filter** $G(u,v)$ centred at radial frequency $r_0$ and orientation $\theta_0$.
2. **Take the real (even) and imaginary (odd) parts** of the inverse Fourier transform — these are quadrature pairs.
3. **Apply the Riesz transform** to obtain the two orthogonal components that are sensitive to local orientation.
4. **Combine via local energy / amplitude**, **local phase**, and **local orientation** formulas to form a feature vector identical to a bank of rotated Gabor responses.

This duality allows a system designer to:
- **Design in the Gabor domain** (filter bank design is intuitive), and
- **Analyse in the monogenic domain** (rotation-invariant features are easy to extract).

### 2.2 Why This Matters — The "How" and "Why"

- **Why bandpass first?** The Riesz transform has a singularity at the origin (DC). To avoid amplifying noise, the signal must be bandpass-filtered before applying the Riesz transform. Gabor is the natural choice because it has optimal joint spatial-frequency localization.
- **Why two Riesz components, not one?** A 2D image has waves in all directions. Two orthogonal components (one for $x$, one for $y$) are needed to capture orientation as a *vector* rather than a *scalar* sign.
- **How does the Gabor re-emerge?** The Gabor function's Fourier transform is also a Gabor function. When this 2D Gaussian envelope is decomposed into a radial envelope (bandpass) and an angular envelope (orientation), the Riesz transform becomes the *natural angular derivative* of that envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This duality is exploited in:
> - Ultrasound elastography (orientation of tissue fibres)
> - Retinal vessel segmentation (vessel orientation)
> - Fingerprint ridge analysis
> - MRI texture classification for tumour grading

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 2D Gabor function | $g(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\exp\!\big(i(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y + \phi)\big)$ | Even part = real, Odd part = imag |
| 2 | Gabor Fourier transform | $G(u,v) = C\cdot\exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2\big((u-u_0)^2+(v-v_0)^2\big)\right)$ | Also a Gaussian |
| 3 | Riesz kernel (spatial) | $h_x(x,y) = -\dfrac{x}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}},\; h_y(x,y) = -\dfrac{y}{2\pi(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}}$ | Singularity at origin |
| 4 | Riesz kernel (frequency) | $H_x(u,v) = -i\,\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}},\; H_y(u,v) = -i\,\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}}$ | $H_x^2 + H_y^2 = -1$ (approx.) |
| 5 | Monogenic signal vector | $f_M = (f,\; h_x * f,\; h_y * f)$ | 3-component 2D signal |
| 6 | Local amplitude | $A = \sqrt{f^2 + (h_x * f)^2 + (h_y * f)^2}$ | Rotation-invariant |
| 7 | Local phase | $\phi = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{(h_x * f)^2 + (h_y * f)^2},\; f\right)$ | Encodes feature type |
| 8 | Local orientation | $\theta = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(h_y * f,\; h_x * f\right)$ | Encodes edge direction |
| 9 | Gabor bandpass condition | $G_{\text{even}} \approx f \cdot \cos(\cdot),\; G_{\text{odd}} \approx f \cdot \sin(\cdot)$ | Quadrature pair |
| 10 | Energy identity (Parseval-like) | $A^2 = f^2 + \vert h_x * f \vert^2 + \vert h_y * f \vert^2$ | Energy conservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** When using the formula $A = \sqrt{f^2 + (h_x * f)^2 + (h_y * f)^2}$, do **not** use vertical pipes `|x|` inside the markdown table — use the $\vert$ LaTeX command. KTU valuation scripts may render the pipe incorrectly and lose a mark.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions and Engineering Constraints

- **Bandpass requirement:** $\int\int f(x,y)\,dx\,dy = 0$ (DC removed) — required before Riesz transform.
- **Isotropy:** The Riesz transform is **isotropic** (rotation-invariant) by construction.
- **Causality:** Lost in 2D (no unique causal extension) — only analytic signal in 1D has causality.
- **Numerical stability:** The factor $\sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ in the denominator vanishes at $(u,v) = (0,0)$ — must be regularized by the Gabor bandpass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Analytic Signal (1D) to Monogenic Signal (2D)

We begin in 1D. The analytic signal of a real signal $f(t)$ is:

$$
f_A(t) = f(t) + i\,(H * f)(t)
$$

where $H(t) = \dfrac{1}{\pi t}$ is the Hilbert transform kernel with frequency response $H(\omega) = -i\,\mathrm{sgn}(\omega)$.

**Step 1 — Recognize the limitation of 1D.** The 1D analytic signal works for one spatial axis only. In 2D, an image has an *orientation* (an angle $\theta$), not a single sign. We need a transform that maps the real signal into a *vector* aligned with the local orientation.

**Step 2 — Generalize the signum function to 2D.** In 1D, $\mathrm{sgn}(\omega) = \omega / \vert \omega \vert$. In 2D, the natural generalization is the **unit vector** pointing in the radial direction in the frequency plane:

$$
\widehat{\mathbf{R}}(u,v) = \frac{(u, v)}{\sqrt{u^2 + v^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Riesz transfer function.** The Riesz transform is the 2D vector-valued operator whose frequency response is:

$$
\mathbf{H}(u,v) = -i\,\widehat{\mathbf{R}}(u,v) = \left( -i\,\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}},\; -i\,\frac{v}{\sqrt{u^2+v^2}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a 3-vector signal.** Applying the bandpass filter $B$ first, then the Riesz operator:

$$
f_M(x,y) = \begin{pmatrix} (B * f)(x,y) \\[4pt] (h_x * B * f)(x,y) \\[4pt] (h_y * B * f)(x,y) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the energy identity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^2(x,y) &= f^2 + (h_x * f)^2 + (h_y * f)^2 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\int\int \vert F(u,v)\vert^2 \left[ 1 + \frac{u^2}{u^2+v^2} + \frac{v^2}{u^2+v^2} \right] du\,dv \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\int\int \vert F(u,v)\vert^2 \cdot 2 \cdot du\,dv
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The "1" in the bracket is the energy of the original signal, and the two fractional terms (summing to 1) are the energy of the Riesz components. Together they yield **twice the original energy** — a well-known constant of the monogenic signal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recovering a Gabor Function from the Monogenic Components

We now work **backwards**: given a 2D Gabor filter $g(x,y)$, show that its even/odd parts and rotated Riesz components reproduce the monogenic decomposition.

**Step 1 — Write the Gabor function with explicit quadrature.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x,y) &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\left[\cos(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y + \phi) + i\sin(\cdot)\right] \\[4pt]
&= g_e(x,y) + i\,g_o(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $g_e$ is the **even (real)** part and $g_o$ is the **odd (imaginary)** part.

**Step 2 — Rotate the Gabor to its principal axis.** Define rotated coordinates $(x', y')$ such that the Gabor's principal orientation aligns with $x'$:

$$
x' = x\cos\theta_0 + y\sin\theta_0, \qquad y' = -x\sin\theta_0 + y\cos\theta_0
$$

where $\theta_0 = \mathrm{atan2}(v_0, u_0)$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Riesz transform to the rotated Gabor.** In the rotated frame, the Riesz transform acts only on the $x'$ direction:

$$
h_{x'} * g_e = g_o
$$

This is the **key identity** — it shows that the odd part of a 1D Gabor is the Riesz transform of its even part.

**Step 4 — Reconstruct the 2D monogenic components.** The two orthogonal Riesz components in the original $(x, y)$ frame are obtained by inverse rotation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_x * f &= \cos\theta_0 \cdot (h_{x'} * f) - \sin\theta_0 \cdot (h_{y'} * f) \\[4pt]
h_y * f &= \sin\theta_0 \cdot (h_{x'} * f) + \cos\theta_0 \cdot (h_{y'} * f)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Local feature extraction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(x,y) &= \sqrt{g_e^2 + (h_x * f)^2 + (h_y * f)^2} \\[4pt]
\theta(x,y) &= \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(h_y * f,\; h_x * f\right) \\[4pt]
\phi(x,y) &= \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{(h_x*f)^2+(h_y*f)^2},\; g_e\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Monogenic Component to a Gabor Quantity

| Monogenic Quantity | Gabor Counterpart | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f$ (real part) | $g_e$ (even Gabor) | Symmetric blob response |
| $h_x * f$ | $g_o \cos\theta_0$ | Edge detector along $x$ |
| $h_y * f$ | $g_o \sin\theta_0$ | Edge detector along $y$ |
| $A$ | $\vert g \vert$ envelope | Local energy (spot/edge/bar) |
| $\phi$ | $\arg(g)$ | Feature class (step, line, roof) |
| $\theta$ | $\arg(g)$ gradient | Edge / ridge orientation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Hint (for KTU practice):** For a Gabor with $\sigma = 2$, $u_0 = 0.1$, $v_0 = 0$, $\phi = 0$ at point $(0,0)$:
> - $g_e(0,0) = 1$
> - $g_o(0,0) = 0$
> - $\theta_0 = 0$
> - $h_x * g_e \big\vert_{0,0} = 0$ (by symmetry)
> - $h_y * g_e \big\vert_{0,0} = 0$ (by symmetry)
> - $A(0,0) = 1$, $\phi(0,0) = 0$, $\theta(0,0) = $ undefined (isotropic point)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Monogenic-to-Gabor Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    imgA[Input Image f of x,y] --> bpF[Bandpass Pre-filter: Gabor G of u,v]
    bpF --> evP[Even Part: g subscript e]
    bpF --> odP[Odd Part: g subscript o]
    evP --> rieX[Apply Riesz Kernel h subscript x]
    evP --> rieY[Apply Riesz Kernel h subscript y]
    rieX --> cmp1[Component f subscript Rx]
    rieY --> cmp2[Component f subscript Ry]
    evP --> cmp0[Component f subscript 0 equals g subscript e]
    cmp0 --> featA[Local Amplitude A]
    cmp1 --> featA
    cmp2 --> featA
    cmp0 --> featP[Local Phase phi]
    cmp1 --> featP
    cmp2 --> featP
    cmp1 --> featT[Local Orientation theta]
    cmp2 --> featT
    featA --> outV[Feature Vector for CAD System]
    featP --> outV
    featT --> outV
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fdA[2D Frequency Domain: u,v plane] --> rgA[Radial Direction r]
    fdA --> anA[Angular Direction theta]
    rgA --> bpG[Bandpass Envelope: Gabor in r]
    anA --> rieG[Angular Derivative: Riesz Operator]
    bpG --> mfvA[Monogenic Feature Vector]
    rieG --> mfvA
    mfvA --> recon[Reconstruction: Equivalent Rotated Gabor Bank]
    recon --> appA[Application: Vessel Tracking, Elastography, Texture CAD]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lvl1[1D Analytic Signal] -->|Generalize to 2D using unit vector in frequency| lvl2[Monogenic Signal]
    lvl2 -->|Choose Gabor as bandpass filter| lvl3[Gabor-Enhanced Monogenic Signal]
    lvl3 -->|Inverse rotation yields rotated Gabors| lvl4[Bank of Oriented Gabor Filters]
    lvl4 -->|Local amplitude phase orientation| lvl5[Rotation-Invariant Image Features]
    lvl5 -->|Classification, segmentation, tracking| lvl6[Medical Imaging Applications]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU students:** The first diagram shows the *forward* (analysis) path. The second shows the *inverse* (synthesis / reconstruction) path. Together they justify why studying the monogenic signal is mathematically equivalent to studying a Gabor filter bank — the answer to the topic title "From Monogenic Signal Back to Gabor Functions."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Q:** Define the **monogenic signal** of a 2D image $f(x,y)$ and state its three components.

**Model Answer:**
The monogenic signal of a 2D image $f(x,y)$ is the 2D generalization of the analytic signal. It is a 3-component vector obtained by combining the original image with the two Riesz transform components:

$$
f_M(x,y) = (f(x,y),\; h_x * f(x,y),\; h_y * f(x,y))
$$

The first component is the original (bandpass-filtered) image, the second and third are the Riesz transform outputs along the $x$ and $y$ axes respectively. These three components jointly encode local amplitude, phase, and orientation in a rotation-invariant way. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Q:** Why must a 2D image be **bandpass-filtered** before applying the Riesz transform?

**Model Answer:**
The Riesz transform has a frequency response $H_x(u,v) = -i\,u/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$ and $H_y(u,v) = -i\,v/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$, which is singular (undefined) at the origin $(0,0)$. Without bandpass filtering, the DC component of the image would be amplified infinitely, causing numerical instability and severe low-frequency noise amplification. A Gabor or log-Gabor bandpass filter ensures the signal has no DC content, allowing the Riesz transform to be applied safely and to produce meaningful local phase and orientation estimates. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Monogenic Signal Theory*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected, Module 2]**

**Q(a)** Derive the local amplitude, local phase, and local orientation formulas from the monogenic signal components $f$, $f_{R1} = h_x * f$, and $f_{R2} = h_y * f$. Explain the physical meaning of each quantity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Local amplitude.** Amplitude is the magnitude of the 3D vector $(f, f_{R1}, f_{R2})$:

$$
A = \sqrt{f^2 + f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2}
$$

This is rotation-invariant — it does not change if the image is rotated, because the Riesz transform is isotropic. Physically, $A$ measures the local signal energy / contrast (e.g., strength of an edge or ridge). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Local phase.** The phase is the angle between the real component $f$ and the "imaginary" plane spanned by $(f_{R1}, f_{R2})$:

$$
\phi = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2},\; f\right) \;\in\; (-\pi, \pi]
$$

Physically, $\phi$ encodes the local feature type:
- $\phi \approx 0$ → bright blob / line centre
- $\phi \approx \pm \pi/2$ → step edge
- $\phi \approx \pi$ → dark blob **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Local orientation.** Orientation is the angle of the projection onto the Riesz plane:

$$
\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(f_{R2},\; f_{R1}) \;\in\; (-\pi/2, \pi/2]
$$

Physically, $\theta$ gives the local edge / ridge direction, which is needed in vessel tracking, fingerprint analysis, and cardiac ultrasound strain mapping. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Summary statement.** The triplet $(A, \phi, \theta)$ is a complete, rotation-invariant, illumination-invariant descriptor of local image structure, which is the main reason the monogenic signal is preferred in medical image CAD pipelines. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q(b)** A 2D image patch is processed by a Gabor bandpass filter centred at $u_0 = 0.2$, $v_0 = 0$, with $\sigma = 1.5$ pixels. At a candidate point $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$ on a bright line oriented horizontally, compute the local orientation $\theta$ and the local phase $\phi$. Assume the bandpass output is $f(0,0) = 0.9$, $f_{R1}(0,0) = 0.1$, $f_{R2}(0,0) \approx 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the principal orientation of the Gabor.**

$$
\theta_0 = \mathrm{atan2}(v_0, u_0) = \mathrm{atan2}(0, 0.2) = 0
$$

So the Gabor is aligned with the $x$-axis. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute local orientation.**

$$
\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(f_{R2},\; f_{R1}) = \mathrm{atan2}(0,\; 0.1) = 0
$$

This correctly identifies the line as horizontal, matching the Gabor's orientation. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute local phase.**

$$
\phi = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2},\; f\right) = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{0.01 + 0},\; 0.9\right)
$$

$$
\phi = \mathrm{atan2}(0.1,\; 0.9) \approx 0.1107 \text{ rad} \approx 6.34^\circ
$$

This is close to 0, which correctly classifies the point as a **bright line centre** (rather than a step edge or dark blob). **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.** A phase value near 0 with non-zero amplitude and orientation $\theta = 0$ indicates a horizontal bright ridge passing through the origin. This is consistent with the underlying anatomy (e.g., a horizontal vessel cross-section) and validates the monogenic decomposition. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Gabor Reconstruction Pathway*

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, Dec 2024 Expected, Module 2]**

**Q(a)** Starting from the 2D Gabor function $g(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\exp\!\big(i(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y)\big)$, show that the even part of the Gabor and the Riesz-transformed even part can be combined to recover the original complex Gabor response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose the Gabor into even and odd parts.**

$$
g(x,y) = g_e(x,y) + i\,g_o(x,y)
$$

where $g_e = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\cos(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y)$ and $g_o = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\sin(2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Rotate to the Gabor's principal axis.** Let $\theta_0 = \mathrm{atan2}(v_0, u_0)$ and define $(x', y')$ as the rotated frame.

In the rotated frame, the Gabor becomes:

$$
g(x',y') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2+y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\exp\!\big(i\,2\pi r_0 x'\big)
$$

where $r_0 = \sqrt{u_0^2 + v_0^2}$. This is a 1D-modulated Gabor along $x'$ only. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Apply the 1D Hilbert (Riesz-in-1D) along $x'$.** The Hilbert transform shifts the phase of a 1D signal by $-\pi/2$:

$$
\mathcal{H}_{x'}\{g_e\} = g_o
$$

In 2D, the Riesz transform restricted to the $x'$ direction gives the same result. So $h_{x'} * g_e = g_o$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Reconstruct via inverse rotation.** The two orthogonal Riesz components in the original frame are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_x * g_e &= \cos\theta_0 \cdot g_o \\[4pt]
h_y * g_e &= \sin\theta_0 \cdot g_o
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining, the complex Gabor is recovered as:

$$
g = g_e + i\,(h_x * g_e)\cos\theta_0 + i\,(h_y * g_e)\sin\theta_0
$$

This shows the Gabor function is fully reconstructed from the monogenic components. **(2 marks)**

---

**Q(b)** A medical imaging system captures an ultrasound B-mode image. You are asked to design a **rotation-invariant texture descriptor** based on the monogenic signal. Specify:
- (i) the bandpass pre-filter choice and its parameters;
- (ii) the three local features to extract;
- (iii) one engineering limitation of this approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bandpass pre-filter.** Choose a **log-Gabor filter** (preferred over Gabor for octave-band decomposition) with 4 scales (centre wavelengths $\lambda \in \{3, 6, 12, 24\}$ pixels) and 6 orientations ($\theta = 0^\circ, 30^\circ, \dots, 150^\circ$). Parameters: Gaussian envelope in $\log(r)$ with bandwidth $\approx 1$ octave. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Three local features.** From each filter response, compute:
- Local amplitude: $A = \sqrt{f^2 + f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2}$
- Local phase: $\phi = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2},\; f\right)$
- Local orientation: $\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(f_{R2}, f_{R1})$

These form a 72-dimensional descriptor per pixel (4 scales × 6 orientations × 3 features), which is then histogram-pooled for texture classification. **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Engineering limitation.** The Riesz transform assumes **single-scale, single-orientation local structure**. In real ultrasound images, **speckle noise** is multi-scale and not strictly band-limited, so the monogenic decomposition may produce unstable phase estimates at low SNR. Mitigation: pre-filtering with a Lee or Wiener filter before applying the monogenic decomposition. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bandpass step.** Many students apply the Riesz transform directly to the raw image, which gives undefined (or huge) values at low frequencies. Always state the bandpass requirement explicitly. *(–2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing the analytic signal (1D) with the monogenic signal (2D).** The 1D analytic signal has *one* Hilbert transform; the 2D monogenic signal has *two* Riesz components. Mixing these up is an automatic 0 for the question. *(–3 marks)*
> 3. **Using $\tan^{-1}(y/x)$ instead of $\mathrm{atan2}(y, x)$.** The arctan2 function is the *only* correct way to extract orientation in $[-\pi, \pi]$ without quadrant ambiguity. Examiners will deduct 1 mark per occurrence. *(–1 mark each)*
> 4. **Sign error in Riesz kernel.** The kernel is $-i\,u/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$, *not* $+i\,u/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$. Wrong sign flips orientation by $\pi/2$. *(–1 mark)*
> 5. **Confusing phase with orientation.** Phase $\phi$ encodes *what* the feature is (line, edge, blob); orientation $\theta$ encodes *which way* it points. Do not interchange them. *(–2 marks)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **monogenic signal** is the 2D generalization of the 1D analytic signal, built using the **Riesz transform**.
- The monogenic signal has **three components**: $f$, $h_x * f$, $h_y * f$.
- The **Riesz transform** is isotropic, vector-valued, and has frequency response $-i\,(u,v)/\sqrt{u^2+v^2}$.
- A **Gabor function** is a Gaussian-windowed complex sinusoid, the optimal joint spatial-frequency filter.
- A **bandpass pre-filter** (typically Gabor or log-Gabor) is **mandatory** before applying the Riesz transform.
- The monogenic signal yields three local features: **amplitude $A$** (energy), **phase $\phi$** (feature type), and **orientation $\theta$** (feature direction).
- The **duality**: a 2D Gabor filter, when rotated and split into even/odd parts, can be reconstructed from the monogenic signal components. This is the "back to Gabor functions" mapping.
- **Local amplitude formula:** $A = \sqrt{f^2 + f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2}$ (rotation-invariant).
- **Local phase formula:** $\phi = \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{f_{R1}^2 + f_{R2}^2},\; f\right)$ (feature class).
- **Local orientation formula:** $\theta = \mathrm{atan2}(f_{R2},\; f_{R1})$ (edge direction).
- The Gabor **Fourier transform is also a Gabor**, making it the natural choice for bandpass pre-filtering.
- **Applications in medical imaging:** vessel tracking, elastography, tumour texture classification, retinal analysis.
- **Exam tip:** always write $\mathrm{atan2}$ — never plain $\tan^{-1}$ — for orientation and phase.
- **Exam tip:** explicitly mention the bandpass requirement in *every* answer involving the monogenic signal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Learning Techniques on Texture Analysis - Lung Nodule Detection in CT Scans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deep Learning Techniques on Texture Analysis - Lung Nodule Detection in CT Scans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deep Learning for Texture Analysis in Lung Nodule Detection** is a specialized branch of computer-aided detection (CADe) / computer-aided diagnosis (CADx) systems that employs multi-layer artificial neural networks—predominantly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)—to automatically learn hierarchical, discriminative texture representations from volumetric Computed Tomography (CT) data, enabling the localization (detection), characterization (benign/malignant classification), and morphological profiling of pulmonary nodules.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic integrates three converging domains:
- **Texture descriptors** (statistical: GLCM; structural: LBP; spectral: Gabor wavelets)
- **Deep feature learning** (hierarchical convolution, pooling, non-linear activation)
- **Volumetric CT processing** (2D slice-based, 2.5D multi-view, and true 3D volumetric CNNs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The phrase *"Deep Learning Techniques on Texture Analysis"* does **NOT** imply that deep learning replaces texture analysis. Rather, modern CNNs perform **implicit, data-driven texture learning**—replacing handcrafted GLCM/LBP features with learned convolutional filters—while classical texture features still serve as **complementary inputs** or **interpretability anchors** in hybrid architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Trainee Radiologist

Imagine teaching a first-year radiology resident to spot lung nodules in a CT stack:

1. **Layer 1 (Low-level)** — The resident first notices *edges, blobs, and small bright dots* in a slice (analogous to detecting Gabor-like edges or gradient magnitudes).
2. **Layer 2 (Mid-level)** — These dots are grouped into *rounded structures* with specific intensities (analogous to texture patterns: homogeneity, contrast).
3. **Layer 3 (High-level)** — The resident learns that a *spiculated, heterogeneous, juxtavascular* rounded mass with a certain diameter is highly suspicious (analogous to a CNN fully-connected layer mapping to "malignant nodule").

A CNN performs **exactly this hierarchical abstraction**, but in a mathematically optimized, data-driven manner across millions of CT slices.

### 1.3 The "Big Picture" Inputs

A typical deep lung-nodule pipeline ingests:

- **Voxel intensities** in **Hounsfield Units (HU)** (range typically $[-1000, +3000]$, with lung parenchyma near $-700$ HU and soft tissue near $+40$ HU).
- **Spatial neighborhood** information (3D context).
- **Optional handcrafted texture maps** (GLCM energy, entropy, LBP histograms).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Diagnostic Definition — Pulmonary Nodule (Fleischner Society)**:
> A roughly spherical, approximately well-marginated pulmonary opacity measuring **$\leq 3$ cm** in diameter, completely surrounded by aerated lung parenchyma, without associated atelectasis, lymphadenopathy, or pleural effusion. Lesions $>3$ cm are termed *masses* and carry a higher malignancy probability.

### 1.4 Why Texture Matters in Lung CT

Lung nodules are distinguished from vessels, bronchi, and noise **primarily by texture**:
- Solid nodules: homogeneous, high HU.
- Part-solid (GGO): heterogeneous, mixed density.
- Non-solid (pure GGO): low-contrast, fuzzy margins.
- Spiculated: radial texture discontinuities.
- Calcified: hyperdense, often with concentric texture rings.

These distinctions are **texture-based**, not shape-based alone — hence the critical role of texture-aware deep networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Paradigms of Texture Analysis

#### Paradigm A — Handcrafted Texture + Shallow Classifier (Pre-2012)

Classical pipeline:
$$\text{CT Volume} \xrightarrow{\text{ROI Extraction}} \text{Handcrafted Features (GLCM, LBP, Gabor)} \xrightarrow{\text{Vector}} \text{SVM / RF} \xrightarrow{} \text{Class}$$

#### Paradigm B — Deep Hierarchical Texture Learning (2012–Present)

End-to-end pipeline:
$$\text{CT Volume} \xrightarrow{\text{Convolution + Pooling (multi-layer)}} \text{Learned Texture Hierarchy} \xrightarrow{\text{FC Layer}} \text{Class}$$

### 2.2 Classical Texture Descriptors (Still Examinable in KTU 2024)

#### 2.2.1 Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)

For an image $I$ with $N_g$ gray levels, the GLCM $P(i,j)$ counts how often a pixel with gray-level $i$ is adjacent to a pixel with gray-level $j$ at displacement $(d, \theta)$.

After normalization $\displaystyle p(i,j) = \frac{P(i,j)}{\sum_{i,j} P(i,j)}$, the **Haralick texture features** are:

| Feature | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---------|---------|------------------|
| Energy (Angular Second Moment) | $\displaystyle f_1 = \sum_i \sum_j p(i,j)^2$ | Uniformity; high for smooth nodules |
| Contrast | $\displaystyle f_2 = \sum_i \sum_j (i-j)^2 \, p(i,j)$ | Local intensity variation; high for spiculated/heterogeneous lesions |
| Correlation | $\displaystyle f_3 = \frac{\sum_i \sum_j (i-\mu_i)(j-\mu_j)\,p(i,j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | Linear dependency of gray levels |
| Homogeneity (IDM) | $\displaystyle f_4 = \sum_i \sum_j \frac{p(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2}$ | Closeness of distribution to diagonal; high for solid nodules |
| Entropy | $\displaystyle f_5 = -\sum_i \sum_j p(i,j) \log p(i,j)$ | Randomness; high for GGO and noise |
| Dissimilarity | $\displaystyle f_6 = \sum_i \sum_j \vert i - j \vert \, p(i,j)$ | Variation; related to contrast |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute-value bars $\vert i-j \vert$ are written using the **vertical line character with LaTeX spacing** to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe-delimiter syntax.

#### 2.2.2 Local Binary Pattern (LBP)

For each pixel, compare it with its $P$ circular neighbors of radius $R$:
$$\text{LBP}_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(g_p - g_c) \cdot 2^p, \quad s(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

A histogram of these codes forms the **LBP texture descriptor**, invariant to monotonic intensity changes—useful for compensating CT scanner calibration drift.

#### 2.2.3 Gabor Wavelet Filters

A 2D Gabor filter is a Gaussian-modulated complex sinusoid:
$$g(x,y;\lambda,\theta,\psi,\sigma,\gamma) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{x'^2 + \gamma^2 y'^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cos\!\left(2\pi \frac{x'}{\lambda} + \psi\right)$$
where $x' = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ and $y' = -x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$.

The **mean magnitude response** across orientations yields a multi-scale texture map, capturing nodule boundary orientation patterns.

### 2.3 CNN Fundamentals for Volumetric CT

A CNN learns a hierarchy of texture filters. For an input CT volume (or slice) $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$, the $l$-th convolutional layer computes:
$$Y^l = f\!\left( W^l * X^l + b^l \right)$$

where $*$ is convolution, $W^l$ is the learned filter bank, $b^l$ is the bias, and $f(\cdot)$ is a non-linearity (typically ReLU: $f(z) = \max(0, z)$).

**3D Extension** for volumetric CT:
$$Y^l_{i,j,k} = f\!\left( \sum_{c} \sum_{u,v,w} W^l_{u,v,w,c} \cdot X^l_{i+u,\, j+v,\, k+w,\, c} + b^l \right)$$

### 2.4 The Class Imbalance Problem in Lung Nodule Detection

Lung CT scans contain **far more non-nodule voxels than nodule voxels** (often ratio $1:10^4$ to $1:10^6$). A naïve classifier can achieve $>99.9\%$ accuracy by predicting "no nodule" everywhere.

**Solution: Focal Loss (Lin et al., 2017)** — now a standard KTU 2024 high-yield topic:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{FL}}(p_t) = -\alpha_t (1-p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)$$
where $p_t$ is the model's estimated probability for the true class, $\alpha_t \in [0,1]$ is a class-balancing weight, and $\gamma \geq 0$ is the **focusing parameter** (typically $\gamma = 2$). The modulating factor $(1-p_t)^\gamma$ down-weights easy (well-classified) examples.

For comparison, standard **binary cross-entropy** is:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(p, y) = -[y \log p + (1-y) \log(1-p)]$$

### 2.5 Free-Response ROC (FROC) — The Standard Evaluation Metric

Unlike standard ROC (which evaluates per-image classification), **FROC** evaluates **per-nodule detection**:
- **X-axis**: Average number of false positives per scan (FP/scan), e.g., $0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8$.
- **Y-axis**: Sensitivity (true positive rate) at each FP/scan operating point.

The **Competition Performance Metric (CPM)** used in LUNA16 / LIDC-IDRI is the average sensitivity at these 7 operating points:
$$\text{CPM} = \frac{1}{7} \sum_{i=1}^{7} \text{Sensitivity}(\text{FP/scan} = f_i)$$

### 2.6 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Description | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $f_1 = \sum_{i,j} p(i,j)^2$ | GLCM Energy (ASM) | $[0,1]$ |
| $f_2 = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 p(i,j)$ | GLCM Contrast | $[0, (N_g-1)^2]$ |
| $f_4 = \sum_{i,j} p(i,j)/(1+(i-j)^2)$ | GLCM Homogeneity (IDM) | $[0,1]$ |
| $f_5 = -\sum_{i,j} p(i,j)\log p(i,j)$ | GLCM Entropy | $[0, \log N_g^2]$ |
| $\text{LBP}_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(g_p - g_c) 2^p$ | Local Binary Pattern code | Integer |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{FL}} = -\alpha_t(1-p_t)^\gamma \log p_t$ | Focal Loss (nodule detection) | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $\text{CPM} = \frac{1}{7}\sum_{i=1}^{7}\text{Sens}(f_i)$ | LUNA16 Competition metric | $[0,1]$ |
| $Y^l = f(W^l * X^l + b^l)$ | CNN convolution layer output | Tensor |
| $\text{IOU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Jaccard Index for segmentation | $[0,1]$ |
| $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Dice Similarity Coefficient | $[0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These texture + deep-learning pipelines are deployed clinically in commercial CAD systems (e.g., Siemens syngo.via, Philips IntelliSpace Portal, Riverain ClearRead). They assist radiologists as **second readers**, reducing missed nodules by **$5$–$15\%$** and shortening reading time by approximately $20\%$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: GLCM Energy, Contrast, and Homogeneity from a Toy CT Sub-Image

We compute the three most-examined Haralick features on a $4 \times 4$ intensity matrix representing a hypothetical nodule cross-section.

Let the sub-image be:
$$I = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 50 & 60 & 70 \\ 50 & 60 & 70 & 80 \\ 60 & 70 & 80 & 90 \\ 70 & 80 & 90 & 90 \end{bmatrix}$$

Gray levels are $N_g = 5$ distinct values: $\{50, 60, 70, 80, 90\}$.

**Step 1** — Choose displacement $(d=1, \theta = 0°)$ (horizontal neighbors).
**Step 2** — Count co-occurrences of each $(i,j)$ pair.

Row-by-row horizontal neighbor pairs:
- Row 1: (50,50), (50,60), (60,70), (70,70) → counts: $P(50,50)=1$, $P(50,60)=1$, $P(60,70)=1$, $P(70,70)=1$
- Row 2: (50,60), (60,70), (70,80), (80,80) → $P(50,60)+1$, $P(60,70)+1$, $P(70,80)=1$, $P(80,80)=1$
- Row 3: (60,70), (70,80), (80,90), (90,90) → $P(60,70)+1$, $P(70,80)+1$, $P(80,90)=1$, $P(90,90)=1$
- Row 4: (70,80), (80,90), (90,90), (90,90) → $P(70,80)+1$, $P(80,90)+1$, $P(90,90)+1$

**Step 3** — Aggregate count matrix $P$ (symmetric, 5×5):
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Total co-occurrences: $N = \sum_{i,j} P(i,j) = 1+1+1+1+1+2+1+2+1+2+1+2+1+3 = 20$.

**Step 4** — Normalize to get probabilities $p(i,j) = P(i,j)/N$:
$$p = \frac{1}{20}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5** — Compute Energy (ASM):
$$f_1 = \sum_{i,j} p(i,j)^2 = \frac{1}{400}\left(1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 2^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 3^2\right)$$

Expanding:
$$= \frac{1}{400}(1+1+0+0+0+1+0+4+0+0+0+4+1+4+0+0+4+1+1+0+0+0+1+9)$$

Summing term-by-term:
$$= \frac{1}{400}(1+1+1+4+4+1+4+1+1+1+9) = \frac{28}{400} = 0.070$$

**Step 6** — Compute Contrast:
$$f_2 = \sum_{i,j}(i-j)^2 p(i,j)$$

Using gray-level indices $0,1,2,3,4$ for $\{50,60,70,80,90\}$:
$$f_2 = \frac{1}{20}\!\left[(0-0)^2 \cdot 1 + (0-1)^2 \cdot 1 + (1-0)^2 \cdot 1 + (1-2)^2 \cdot 2 + (2-1)^2 \cdot 2 + (2-2)^2 \cdot 1 + (2-3)^2 \cdot 2 + (3-2)^2 \cdot 2 + (3-3)^2 \cdot 1 + (3-4)^2 \cdot 1 + (4-3)^2 \cdot 1 + (4-4)^2 \cdot 3\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{20}(0 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 2 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0) = \frac{12}{20} = 0.60$$

**Step 7** — Compute Homogeneity (IDM):
$$f_4 = \sum_{i,j}\frac{p(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{20}\!\left[\frac{1}{1+0} + \frac{1}{1+1} + \frac{1}{1+1} + \frac{2}{1+1} + \frac{2}{1+1} + \frac{1}{1+0} + \frac{2}{1+1} + \frac{2}{1+1} + \frac{1}{1+0} + \frac{1}{1+1} + \frac{1}{1+1} + \frac{3}{1+0}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{20}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} + 3\right) = \frac{12}{20} = 0.60$$

**Interpretation**: Low Energy ($0.07$), moderate Contrast ($0.60$), moderate Homogeneity ($0.60$) — consistent with a **heterogeneous, non-uniform nodule texture** (e.g., part-solid or spiculated lesion), in contrast to a smooth solid nodule which would yield Energy $> 0.30$ and Contrast $< 0.20$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Focal Loss Modulating Factor Behavior

For a misclassified example, $p_t \approx 0$ (e.g., $p_t = 0.1$), so $(1-p_t)^\gamma = (0.9)^\gamma$.

For a well-classified example, $p_t \approx 1$ (e.g., $p_t = 0.99$), so $(1-p_t)^\gamma = (0.01)^\gamma$.

Comparing **modulating factors** at $\gamma = 2$:

$$\frac{(1-p_t^\text{easy})^2}{(1-p_t^\text{hard})^2} = \frac{(0.01)^2}{(0.9)^2} = \frac{0.0001}{0.81} \approx 1.23 \times 10^{-4}$$

The easy example contributes **$\approx 8000\times$ less** to the loss than the hard example. This is precisely the desired behavior — focal loss forces the network to focus learning capacity on **hard, informative samples** (i.e., subtle nodules vs. vessel cross-sections).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Lung Nodule Detection Paradigms

| Criterion | Handcrafted (GLCM/LBP) + SVM | 2D Slice CNN | 2.5D Multi-view CNN | 3D Volumetric CNN |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Spatial Context | ROI-only | Single slice | Multi-axis slices | Full volume |
| Texture Representation | Explicit (interpretable) | Implicit (learned) | Implicit (fused) | Implicit (hierarchical) |
| False Positive Rate | High (8–15 FP/scan) | Moderate | Low | Very Low (1–4 FP/scan) |
| Computational Cost | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate | High (GPU memory-bound) |
| LUNA16 CPM Score (typical) | 0.55 – 0.70 | 0.75 – 0.85 | 0.85 – 0.92 | 0.90 – 0.97 |
| Interpretability | High | Low | Low | Lowest |
