#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Dimension | ASM | AAM | Snakes / Active Contours |
|---|---|---|---|
| Representation | PDM of landmarks + local profiles | PDM + holistic texture PCA | Parametric curve $v(s) = (x(s), y(s))$ |
| Prior knowledge | Statistical (mean + eigenvectors) | Statistical (combined shape+texture) | Smoothness regulariser only |
| Cost function | Mahalanobis distance on profile match | Residual image vs. modelled appearance | $E = \int (E_{\text{int}} + E_{\text{ext}})\,ds$ |
| Optimisation | Iterative per-landmark scan + Procrustes | One-step Newton / learned Jacobian | Variational Euler–Lagrange or greedy |
| Robustness to init. | Good (multi-resolution) | Moderate (sensitive to start) | Poor (small capture range) |
| Typical medical app. | LV in echo, prostate in TRUS, hippocampus in MRI | Brain MRI, full-organ appearance | Cell boundaries, simple lesions |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per row, plus 1 mark for the overall concluding sentence: "ASMs and AAMs embed *learnt* priors, whereas snakes embed only *generic* smoothness.")*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ASM = statistical deformable template** = PDM + grey-level profile model + iterative search constrained to the $\pm 3\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ hyper-ellipsoid.
- **Five stages**: landmark labelling → Procrustes alignment → shape PCA → profile PCA → multi-resolution search.
- **Two key equations** to memorise:
$$
\mathbf{x} = \overline{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{b} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{P}^{T}(\mathbf{x} - \overline{\mathbf{x}})
$$
- **Mahalanobis bound**: $\vert b_i \vert \le 3\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ — not $3\lambda_i$, and not $\pm 1$.
- **Procrustes alignment** removes nuisance translation, scale, rotation; uses SVD with $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$ check.
- **PCA mode selection**: keep the smallest $t$ such that $V_t \ge 0.98$.
- **Search uses a multi-resolution Gaussian pyramid** to widen capture range and reduce sensitivity to initialisation.
- **ASM is *not* a snake** — it does not minimise an image energy; it maximises statistical match to a learnt prior.
- **AAM** = ASM + full-region texture PCA; more powerful but more sensitive to initialisation.
- **Medical-imaging use cases** to quote: left ventricle (echocardiography), prostate (TRUS), hippocampus (MRI), kidney (CT), vertebrae (X-ray).
- **Examiner hot-spots**: the shape equation pair, the $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ in the bound, the role of the profile model, and the need for Procrustes alignment before PCA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Active Appearance Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Active Appearance Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Active Appearance Model (AAM)** is a generative, parametric, and statistical model of an object's shape and its associated texture (intensity/appearance) that is built by combining a statistical **Point Distribution Model (PDM)** of shape with a statistical model of the **shape-normalized intensity texture**. It was formally introduced by Cootes, Edwards, and Taylor (1998/2001) and has since become a foundational tool in medical image analysis, face recognition, and object segmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> "The Active Appearance Model is a statistical model of the shape and gray-level appearance of a deformable object, learned from a training set of annotated images, which can be used to search for the object in new images by adjusting its model parameters to minimize the residual error between the synthesized model image and the target image."

Mathematically, the AAM describes a target object using two coupled linear models:

$$x = \bar{x} + P_s b_s \quad \text{(Shape Model)}$$

$$g = \bar{g} + P_g b_g \quad \text{(Appearance / Texture Model)}$$

where $x$ is the concatenated vector of 2D landmark coordinates, $\bar{x}$ is the **mean shape**, $P_s$ contains the eigenvectors of the shape covariance matrix, and $b_s$ is a vector of **shape parameters**. Similarly, $g$ is the vectorized shape-normalized texture, $\bar{g}$ is the mean texture, $P_g$ contains the texture eigenvectors, and $b_g$ is the **texture parameter** vector.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The "Clay-Mold Stamp"**
> Imagine you are a forensic artist trying to recreate a person's face from a blurry CCTV frame. You don't draw freehand; instead, you have a **statistical stamp** made by studying thousands of faces.
>
> - The **stamp's outline** = the **shape model** (where the eyes, nose, mouth are positioned).
> - The **ink pattern inside the stamp** = the **texture model** (skin tone, wrinkles, smoothness).
> - The **slider knobs** on the stamp = the **model parameters** ($b_s$ and $b_g$).
> - **Matching the stamp to the CCTV face** = the **search/fitting algorithm** that adjusts the knobs until the stamp "stamps" perfectly onto the target face.
>
> In medical imaging, instead of faces, you might stamp a **left ventricle of the heart**, a **prostate gland**, or a **kidney cross-section** onto an MRI or ultrasound slice.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Statistical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Notation | Standard Value/Role |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| Number of training samples | $N$ | Typically 100–500 in medical AAMs |
| Number of landmarks | $n$ | 50–200 points outlining the object |
| Number of shape modes | $t_s$ | Retain ~95% of variance (usually 10–30) |
| Number of texture modes | $t_g$ | Retain ~95% of variance (usually 50–200) |
| Number of combined modes | $t_c$ | Concatenated modes after weighting |
| Image resolution | — | $\approx 50 \times 50$ to $200 \times 200$ patch |
| Procrustes scale | $s$ | Normalized to unity for shape alignment |
| Texture weighting | $w_s$ | Balances shape vs texture units |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Term Distinction**
> - **Shape** ($x$): the spatial layout (geometry) of landmarks.
> - **Texture / Appearance** ($g$): the gray-level intensity pattern inside the shape.
> - **Combined AAM** ($b$ or $c$): a single linear model that captures *correlated* shape–texture variation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pipeline Overview — The AAM Lifecycle

The AAM operates in **two distinct phases**: (i) **Offline Training** and (ii) **Online Search/Fitting**.

### Phase A — Offline Training

1. **Landmark Annotation** — Place $n$ landmark points on each of $N$ training images, forming shape vectors $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
2. **Procrustes Alignment** — Remove translation, rotation, and scale by Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA) so that shape differences are purely intrinsic.
3. **Statistical Shape Model (PDM)** — Apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to aligned shapes to obtain $\bar{x}$ and $P_s$.
4. **Warp-to-Mean** — For each training image, warp (typically using a piecewise affine or thin-plate spline) the texture into the reference frame of $\bar{x}$. This gives normalized texture vectors $g_i$.
5. **Statistical Texture Model** — Apply PCA to normalized textures to obtain $\bar{g}$ and $P_g$.
6. **Combined Model** — Concatenate weighted parameters $b = \begin{pmatrix} W_s b_s \\ b_g \end{pmatrix}$ and re-apply PCA to get a unified appearance model.
7. **Learn the Update Matrix** — Use multivariate linear regression on perturbations to learn $R$, the update matrix for fitting.

### Phase B — Online Search

1. Initialize shape parameters near the target.
2. Render the current model instance into the image plane.
3. Compute the **residual** $r(p) = g_{\text{image}} - g_{\text{model}}$.
4. Predict parameter update $\Delta p = -R \cdot r$.
5. Repeat until $\|r\| < \epsilon$.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Theoretical Justification

> [!NOTE] **WHY Procrustes Alignment?**
> Raw landmark positions are confounded by global position, orientation, and size. PCA on raw coordinates would waste modes on rigid transformations. Removing them via GPA isolates **intrinsic shape variation**, which is what we want to model.

> [!NOTE] **WHY a Combined Model?**
> Shape and texture are *correlated* (e.g., a fatter face has different skin shading). Two independent models miss this. The combined model captures **coupled variation** and is more compact.

> [!NOTE] **WHY the Linear Update Rule?**
> Cootes et al. empirically showed that under modest displacements, the relationship $\delta p = R \cdot \delta g$ is approximately **linear** in image residuals. This avoids costly gradient computation per iteration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Constraint |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $x_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, \dots, x_{i,n}, y_{i,n})^T$ | Shape vector (2n × 1) | Concatenated landmark coords |
| 2 | $X = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Mean shape | After Procrustes alignment |
| 3 | $S = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_i - X)(x_i - X)^T$ | Shape covariance matrix | Symmetric, PSD |
| 4 | $S = P_s \, \Lambda_s \, P_s^T$ | Eigendecomposition | $\Lambda_s = \text{diag}(\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots)$ |
| 5 | $x = \bar{x} + P_s \, b_s$ | Shape instance | $b_s \in \mathbb{R}^{t_s}$ |
| 6 | $V_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{2n}\lambda_i}$ | Variance retained | Choose $t$ such that $V_t \ge 0.95$ |
| 7 | $g = \bar{g} + P_g \, b_g$ | Texture instance | Sampled in mean-shape frame |
| 8 | $b = \begin{pmatrix} W_s b_s \\ b_g \end{pmatrix}$ | Combined parameter vector | $W_s = r \cdot I$, weights shape units |
| 9 | $b = P_c \, c$ | Combined AAM | $c \in \mathbb{R}^{t_c}$ final params |
| 10 | $\Delta p = -R \cdot r(p)$ | Search update | $R$ is pre-learned update matrix |
| 11 | $E = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} [A(\mathbf{x}; p) - I(W(\mathbf{x}; p))]^2$ | Fit objective | Minimize image-difference |
| 12 | $W_s = r \cdot I$, $r = \sqrt{\frac{\text{var}(g)}{\text{var}(x)}}$ | Unit-balancing weight | Makes $b_s, b_g$ scale-equivalent |

> [!WARNING] **Markdown Note**: All absolute value and conditioning bars in the table use generic descriptive text (`norm of r`, `var(...)`) rather than the pipe character to keep the table well-formed.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology**: Automated left-ventricle boundary tracking in echocardiograms and MRI.
- **Radiation Therapy**: Contouring of organs-at-risk (OARs) for treatment planning.
- **Neurology**: Hippocampus and ventricle segmentation in brain MRI for Alzheimer's studies.
- **Prostate Imaging**: Multi-parametric MRI prostate segmentation for biopsy guidance.
- **Dentistry**: 3D cephalometric landmarking from CBCT scans.
- **Ophthalmology**: Optic disc and cup segmentation in fundus images for glaucoma screening.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Takeaway**
> The AAM is the **first widely-adopted model that treated image segmentation as a constrained optimization problem on a learned statistical manifold** — a paradigm that evolved into the **Constrained Local Models (CLM)**, **Active Shape Models (ASM)**, and ultimately modern **deep learning segmentation networks (U-Net, nnU-Net)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1 — Statistical Shape Model (PDM)

### Step 1: Vectorize the Landmarks

Each annotated training image $i$ contributes a shape vector:

$$x_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, x_{i,2}, y_{i,2}, \dots, x_{i,n}, y_{i,n})^T \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$$

where $(x_{i,j}, y_{i,j})$ is the $j$-th landmark.

### Step 2: Generalized Procrustes Alignment

We seek to remove translation $T$, rotation $R_\theta$, and uniform scale $s$ from each shape. The cost is:

$$\min_{T,R_\theta,s} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert s \, R_\theta \, x_i + T - \bar{x} \Vert^2$$

This is solved **iteratively**:

1. Translate each $x_i$ so its centroid is at origin.
2. Scale each $x_i$ to unit norm: $x_i \leftarrow x_i / \Vert x_i \Vert$.
3. Choose one shape as initial mean; align all others to it via Procrustes.
4. Recompute mean; re-align; repeat until convergence (typically 5–10 iterations).

### Step 3: Compute Mean and Covariance

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \qquad S = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$$

### Step 4: Eigen-Decomposition

$$S \, v_k = \lambda_k \, v_k, \qquad \lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_{2n} \ge 0$$

The orthogonal eigenvector matrix $P_s = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_{2n}]$ and the diagonal eigenvalue matrix $\Lambda_s = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_{2n})$ give:

$$S = P_s \, \Lambda_s \, P_s^T$$

### Step 5: Truncate to Top $t_s$ Modes

Choose $t_s$ so that the retained variance exceeds a threshold (typically 95%):

$$\sum_{k=1}^{t_s} \lambda_k \;\Big/\; \sum_{k=1}^{2n} \lambda_k \;\ge\; 0.95$$

The shape of any training (or new) example is then approximated as:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &\approx \bar{x} + P_s \, b_s \\
b_s &= P_s^T (x - \bar{x})
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**: $b_s \in \mathbb{R}^{t_s}$ is a low-dimensional "shape code" — typically $t_s \approx 10$–$30$ versus the original $2n = 100$–$400$ dimensions.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2 — Statistical Texture (Appearance) Model

### Step 1: Triangulation and Warping

Connect the landmarks of each training image to form a triangulation (Delaunay). For each pixel $\mathbf{u}$ inside the mean shape $\bar{x}$, the corresponding pixel $\mathbf{x}_i(\mathbf{u})$ in image $i$ is obtained via **piecewise affine warp**:

$$\mathbf{x}_i(\mathbf{u}) = A_i(\mathbf{u}) \, \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{d}_i(\mathbf{u})$$

where $A_i(\mathbf{u})$ and $\mathbf{d}_i(\mathbf{u})$ are local affine coefficients determined by the enclosing triangle.

### Step 2: Sample Normalized Texture

The texture sample is:

$$g_i = T(I_i(x_i(\mathbf{u}_1))), T(I_i(x_i(\mathbf{u}_2))), \dots)^T$$

Optionally, normalize to zero mean and unit variance (to remove global lighting/contrast):

$$g_i \leftarrow \frac{g_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$$

### Step 3: PCA on Textures

$$\bar{g} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i, \qquad S_g = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum (g_i - \bar{g})(g_i - \bar{g})^T$$

Eigen-decompose: $S_g = P_g \Lambda_g P_g^T$, retain top $t_g$ modes.

$$g = \bar{g} + P_g b_g, \qquad b_g = P_g^T (g - \bar{g})$$

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3 — Combined Appearance Model

### Step 1: Concatenate Parameters

Because $b_s$ and $b_g$ have different physical units (coordinate units vs. gray-level units), we rescale $b_s$ to match $b_g$:

$$b = \begin{pmatrix} W_s \, b_s \\ b_g \end{pmatrix}, \qquad W_s = r \, I_{t_s \times t_s}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{\text{Var}(g)}{\text{Var}(x)}}$$

### Step 2: Second PCA

Apply PCA to the $N$ vectors $b_i$:

$$S_b = P_c \, \Lambda_c \, P_c^T$$

The combined model becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
b &= P_c \, c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad c = P_c^T b \\
b_s &= W_s^{-1} \, P_{cs} \, c, \qquad b_g = P_{cg} \, c
\end{aligned}$$

where $P_{cs}$ and $P_{cg}$ are the rows of $P_c$ corresponding to shape and texture parts.

### Step 3: Final Synthesized Image

Given appearance parameters $c$, the model can render a full image patch:

1. Compute shape: $x = \bar{x} + P_s W_s^{-1} P_{cs} c$
2. Compute texture: $g = \bar{g} + P_g P_{cg} c$
3. Warp the texture $g$ into the shape $x$ to get a synthetic image patch.

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation 4 — The Search / Fitting Update

Let the combined parameters be $p = c \in \mathbb{R}^{t_c}$. We wish to minimize:

$$E(p) = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} \big[ A(\mathbf{x}; p) - I(W(\mathbf{x}; p)) \big]^2$$

where $A$ is the model-synthesized image and $I \circ W$ is the warped input image.

### Linear Update Assumption

Cootes et al. observed that for small perturbations $\delta p$ around the current estimate $p_0$:

$$\delta p \;\approx\; R \, \delta g$$

where $\delta g$ is the residual image (vectorized) and $R \in \mathbb{R}^{t_c \times m}$ is a fixed **regression matrix**. Substituting $\delta p = p - p_0$ and $\delta g = g_{\text{image}} - g_{\text{model}}$:

$$p \;=\; p_0 \;-\; R \, r(p_0)$$

### Learning R by Displacement Regression

1. Displace the model by $\delta p_k$ along each mode $k$.
2. Measure the residual $\delta g_k$ in the image.
3. Stack rows $\delta p_k^T$ and $\delta g_k^T$ into matrices $D_p, D_g$.
4. Solve by multivariate least squares:

$$R = -D_p \, D_g^T \, (D_g D_g^T + \epsilon I)^{-1}$$

The negative sign in the iteration reflects that we move *opposite* to the residual direction.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — From Theory to Practice

| AAM Stage | Mathematical Operation | Dimensionality | Output |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------|--------|
| Shape alignment | Generalized Procrustes | $2n$ | Aligned shapes |
| Shape PCA | $S = P_s \Lambda_s P_s^T$ | $2n \times 2n$ | $\bar{x}, P_s$ |
| Texture warping | Piecewise affine | $m$ pixels | $g_i$ |
| Texture PCA | $S_g = P_g \Lambda_g P_g^T$ | $m \times m$ | $\bar{g}, P_g$ |
| Combined PCA | $S_b = P_c \Lambda_c P_c^T$ | $(t_s + t_g) \times (t_s+t_g)$ | $P_c$ |
| Search | $\Delta p = -R r$ | $t_c \times m \cdot m$ | New $p$ |
| Termination | $\|r\| < \epsilon$ | scalar | Converged $p^*$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AAM Training Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Image Set] --> B[Manual Landmark Annotation]
    B --> C[Shape Vectors x_i]
    C --> D[Generalized Procrustes Analysis]
    D --> E[Aligned Shapes]
    E --> F[Compute Mean Shape x_bar]
    E --> G[Shape Covariance S_x]
    G --> H[Eigendecomposition]
    H --> I[Shape PCA: P_s, b_s]
    F --> J[Piecewise Affine Warp to Mean Frame]
    J --> K[Normalized Texture Samples g_i]
    K --> L[Texture Covariance S_g]
    L --> M[Eigendecomposition]
    M --> N[Texture PCA: P_g, b_g]
    I --> O[Concatenate Weighted Parameters]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Combined PCA]
    P --> Q[Final AAM: P_c, c]
    Q --> R[Learn Update Matrix R via Regression]
    R --> S[Trained AAM Ready for Search]
```

## 4.2 AAM Search / Fitting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Input Image] --> B[Initialize Model Parameters p_0]
    B --> C[Render Model Instance: x, g]
    C --> D[Warp Model Texture into Image]
    D --> E[Compute Residual r = g_image - g_model]
    E --> F{Converged: r_norm less than epsilon?}
    F -- No --> G[Predict Update: delta_p = -R r]
    G --> H[Update Parameters: p = p + delta_p]
    H --> C
    F -- Yes --> I[Output Fitted Shape and Appearance]
```

## 4.3 Combined AAM Parameter Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Combined Params c] --> CS[Project to Shape Subspace]
    C --> CG[Project to Texture Subspace]
    CS --> S[Shape b_s = W_s^-1 P_cs c]
    S --> XS[Shape x = x_bar + P_s b_s]
    CG --> G[Texture b_g = P_cg c]
    G --> GS[Texture g = g_bar + P_g b_g]
    XS --> SYNTH[Synthesize Image Patch]
    GS --> SYNTH
```

## 4.4 AAM in the Medical Imaging Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MRI or CT or Ultrasound Volume] --> B[Slice Selection]
    B --> C[2D Image Frame]
    C --> D[AAM Initialization near Target Organ]
    D --> E[AAM Search Iterations]
    E --> F[Fitted Boundary Landmarks]
    F --> G[Organ Contour Extraction]
    G --> H[Clinical Metric Computation: Volume, Thickness, Asymmetry]
    H --> I[Diagnosis or Treatment Planning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]

**Q: Differentiate between the Active Shape Model (ASM) and the Active Appearance Model (AAM).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Shape Model (ASM) | Active Appearance Model (AAM) |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Models | Shape only | Shape + Texture (appearance) |
| Search | Local profile matching at each landmark | Holistic image-difference minimization |
| Update Rule | Position adjustment per landmark | Global parameter update $\Delta p = -R r$ |
| Generative | No (uses boundary only) | Yes (synthesizes full image patch) |
| Robustness to appearance variation | Lower | Higher |

> [!NOTE] **Memory Tip**: ASM = "Shape only"; AAM = "Appearance + Shape".

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: What is the role of Generalized Procrustes Analysis (GPA) in AAM construction?**

**Model Answer:**
GPA removes global **translation, rotation, and uniform scale** variations across the training shapes so that the remaining variation captured by PCA is purely **intrinsic shape** variation. Without GPA, the dominant PCA modes would trivially represent position and orientation, wasting model capacity. After GPA, the mean shape $\bar{x}$ and shape eigenvectors $P_s$ represent only meaningful anatomical deformations. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]

**Q: Explain the construction of the Active Appearance Model (AAM) in detail, covering the shape model, texture model, and combined appearance model. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Shape Model Construction (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Landmark Annotation**: Each of $N$ training images is annotated with $n$ corresponding landmarks, yielding shape vectors $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.

2. **Generalized Procrustes Alignment**: Iteratively align all $x_i$ to a common reference frame by removing translation $T$, rotation $\theta$, and scale $s$:

$$\min_{T,\theta,s} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert s R_\theta x_i + T - \bar{x} \Vert^2$$

3. **Mean and Covariance**:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \qquad S = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$$

4. **PCA**:
$$S = P_s \Lambda_s P_s^T$$

5. **Truncation**: Keep top $t_s$ modes such that $\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{t_s} \lambda_k}{\sum_k \lambda_k} \ge 0.95$.

6. **Shape Synthesis**:
$$x = \bar{x} + P_s b_s$$

#### (b) Texture & Combined Model (7 marks)

1. **Warp-to-Mean**: For each training image, warp the texture inside the landmark boundary into the mean shape's reference frame using piecewise affine interpolation. Vectorize to obtain $g_i$.

2. **Optional Normalization**: Subtract mean and divide by std. dev. to remove lighting variation:
$$g_i \leftarrow \frac{g_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$$

3. **Texture PCA**:
$$\bar{g} = \frac{1}{N}\sum g_i, \qquad S_g = P_g \Lambda_g P_g^T, \qquad g = \bar{g} + P_g b_g$$

4. **Combined Parameters**: To make shape and texture units comparable:
$$b = \begin{pmatrix} W_s b_s \\ b_g \end{pmatrix}, \qquad W_s = rI, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{\text{Var}(g)}{\text{Var}(x)}}$$

5. **Second PCA on $b$**:
$$S_b = P_c \Lambda_c P_c^T, \qquad b = P_c c$$

6. **Final Model**:
$$x = \bar{x} + P_s W_s^{-1} P_{cs} c, \qquad g = \bar{g} + P_g P_{cg} c$$

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall #1**
> Students often forget the **weighting matrix $W_s$** when describing the combined model. Without it, the concatenated PCA is dominated by the larger-magnitude parameter set. (Deduct 1–2 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]

**Q: With suitable equations, describe the AAM search / fitting algorithm. Explain how the update matrix R is learned. (14 marks)**

#### (a) The Search Algorithm (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The goal is to find appearance parameters $p$ that minimize the squared residual between the warped input image and the model-synthesized image:

$$E(p) = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} \big[ A(\mathbf{x}; p) - I(W(\mathbf{x}; p)) \big]^2$$

**Iterative Procedure**:

1. **Initialize** parameters $p^{(0)}$ near the expected object location.

2. **Render** the current model instance:
   - Compute shape: $x = \bar{x} + P_s W_s^{-1} P_{cs} p$
   - Compute texture: $g = \bar{g} + P_g P_{cg} p$

3. **Warp** the input image $I$ into the mean-shape frame using $W(\mathbf{x}; p)$ to obtain $g_{\text{image}}$.

4. **Residual**:
$$r = g_{\text{image}} - g_{\text{model}}$$

5. **Update**:
$$\Delta p = -R \, r, \qquad p \leftarrow p + \Delta p$$

6. **Convergence Check**: Stop when $\|r\| < \epsilon$ or $\|\Delta p\| < \delta$ or maximum iterations reached.

7. **Multi-Resolution Strategy** (standard practice): Run the search from coarse to fine image pyramids to improve robustness and speed.

#### (b) Learning the Update Matrix R (7 marks)

The update matrix $R$ is learned **offline** by linear regression of parameter displacements against image residuals.

1. **Displacement Generation**: For each training image and each mode $k$, displace $p$ by $\delta p_k$ (e.g., $\pm 0.5 \sigma_k$ along mode $k$). Collect $K$ such pairs.

2. **Residual Measurement**: For each displacement, render the perturbed model and compute the image residual $\delta g_k$.

3. **Form Matrices**: Stack displacements as rows of $D_p \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times t_c}$ and residuals as rows of $D_g \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times m}$.

4. **Ridge Regression** (to avoid singularity):
$$R = -D_p D_g^T (D_g D_g^T + \epsilon I)^{-1}$$

5. **Justification**: The linear model assumes $\delta p \approx R \, \delta g$ locally around the current $p$. Empirical studies show this is a good first-order approximation, making the search computationally cheap (no Jacobian computation per iteration).

6. **Validation**: $R$ is usually validated on a held-out set of training images with known ground truth.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall #2**
> Students frequently confuse the **sign** of $R$. Remember: we move **opposite** to the residual, hence the minus sign in $\Delta p = -R r$. (Deduct 1 mark)
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall #3**
> Forgetting the **ridge regularization term** $\epsilon I$ in the pseudo-inverse. Without it, $D_g D_g^T$ may be ill-conditioned. (Deduct 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] AAM = **Shape Model + Texture Model + Combined Model + Search Algorithm**.
- [ ] **Shape** uses 2D landmark coordinates vectorized into a $2n$-dim vector.
- [ ] **Procrustes** removes translation, rotation, scale — keeps intrinsic shape.
- [ ] **PCA** is performed twice: once on shapes, once on textures, then on concatenated weighted parameters.
- [ ] **Variance retention** is the rule for choosing the number of modes (95% is the KTU standard).
- [ ] **Texture normalization** may include mean/std removal for illumination invariance.
- [ ] **Weighting matrix $W_s = rI$** balances shape and texture units — commonly forgotten in exams.
- [ ] **Search update**: $\Delta p = -R r$, with $R$ pre-learned by regression on displacements.
- [ ] **Multi-resolution** (image pyramid) is standard for robustness.
- [ ] **Medical applications**: LV segmentation, prostate, hippocampus, optic disc, cephalometrics.
- [ ] AAM is **generative** (can synthesize the object), unlike ASM which only models boundaries.
- [ ] Linear update assumption is the key trick that makes AAM fast; modern variants (e.g., **Inverse Compositional AAM**, **Project-Out AAM**) refine it for efficiency.
- [ ] AAM paved the way for **CLMs** and influences modern deep segmentation as a statistical prior over shape.

> [!WARNING] **Final KTU Valuation Tips**
> 1. Always **state the dimensionality** of vectors and matrices in derivations.
> 2. Always **mention variance-retention** when discussing PCA truncation.
> 3. Always **include the $W_s$ weighting** in combined-model derivations.
> 4. Always show the **minus sign** in the update rule.
> 5. Mention **medical imaging applications** explicitly to gain examiner goodwill.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deficiencies of Statistical Shape Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deficiencies of Statistical Shape Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Statistical Shape Model (SSM)** is a parametric, probabilistic representation of a class of shapes learned from a set of annotated training examples, typically constructed using **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** over aligned shape vectors. The most widely used instantiation is the **Point Distribution Model (PDM)** introduced by Cootes, Taylor, and Cooper (1992), in which each training shape is represented as a vector of landmark coordinates and the variation across the population is captured by the eigenvectors of the shape covariance matrix.

The reconstructed shape is given generically by:

$$x \approx \bar{x} + \Phi b$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the mean shape, $\Phi$ is the matrix of retained eigenvectors (modes of variation), and $b$ is the vector of shape parameters constrained within a hyper-ellipsoid derived from the eigenvalues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Despite their wide adoption in medical image segmentation, **Statistical Shape Models exhibit several well-documented deficiencies** that limit their robustness, generalization, and clinical applicability. These deficiencies form a critical portion of Module 3 and are frequently tested in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **art teacher** trying to teach a student to draw human faces. You give the student 100 photographs of faces and ask them to extract the "average face" plus the "ways in which faces vary." The student computes an average face and a few dominant modes of variation (e.g., "how round the face is," "how long the nose is"). Now the student can synthesize new faces by tweaking these modes.

**The Problem (the "deficiencies"):**

- The student only learned **linear combinations** — so they cannot draw faces with very unusual proportions (e.g., very asymmetric, or pathological deformations) that lie outside the linear space of the 100 photos.
- If you ask the student to draw a face in a new photograph but the photo is rotated, partially occluded, or has poor lighting, the student gets confused — they need a **good starting guess** (initialization) close to the answer.
- The student memorized **where the dots (landmarks) should go**, but doesn't truly understand what a face "is" — so they might place a landmark on a tumor or an artifact, mistaking it for normal anatomy.
- The student cannot draw a face that has **two noses, a missing eye, or a hole** in the cheek — SSMs cannot handle topological variations.

This "art student" is exactly what an SSM is — and these are precisely the deficiencies we will study.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module 3, the deficiencies of SSMs are presented as a **motivation for advanced non-linear and learning-based shape models** (e.g., Kernel PCA, Gaussian Process Latent Variable Models, Deep Boltzmann Machines, Convolutional Neural Networks). Understanding these deficiencies is essential for answering analytical "compare-and-contrast" questions in the ESE.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in SSM Evaluation (KTU Board Terminology)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Generalization Ability | $G$ | Ability to represent unseen shapes; lower = better |
| Specificity | $S$ | Fraction of synthetic shapes that resemble real training shapes |
| Compactness | $C$ | Number of modes required to explain 95%–98% of variance |
| Mahalanobis Distance Threshold | $b_k \in [-3\sqrt{\lambda_k}, +3\sqrt{\lambda_k}]$ | Hard limit on each shape parameter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of SSMs (Recap for Context)

A shape $x_i$ is represented as a $2n$-dimensional vector (for $n$ landmarks in 2D):

$$x_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, x_{i,2}, y_{i,2}, \dots, x_{i,n}, y_{i,n})^T$$

After **Procrustes alignment** to remove translation, rotation, and scale, the mean shape and covariance matrix are computed:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

$$S = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$$

Eigendecomposition yields:

$$S \phi_k = \lambda_k \phi_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, 2n$$

The shape model reconstruction is:

$$x = \bar{x} + \sum_{k=1}^{t} b_k \phi_k, \quad t \ll 2n$$

with the parameter constraint enforcing statistical plausibility:

$$-3\sqrt{\lambda_k} \leq b_k \leq +3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$$

### 2.2 Structured Analysis of Each Deficiency

#### Deficiency 1: Sensitivity to Initialization

- **Why it happens:** Active Shape Models (ASMs) and Active Appearance Models (AAMs) use iterative search that minimizes a cost function in the multi-dimensional shape parameter space $b$.
- **How it fails:** The cost function is **non-convex** and contains local minima. If the initial mean shape is far from the target (e.g., in a different organ or in a flipped/rotated orientation), the gradient descent converges to a spurious local minimum.
- **Real-world consequence:** In cardiac segmentation from MRI, misplaced initialization causes the model to lock onto adjacent tissues (e.g., liver or lungs).

#### Deficiency 2: Limited Expressivity of Linear (Gaussian) Variation

- **Why it happens:** PCA can only model **linear, second-order (Gaussian) statistics** of the training distribution.
- **How it fails:** Anatomical variation is often **non-linear** and **multi-modal** (e.g., diseased vs. healthy hearts look like two distinct clusters, not one Gaussian).
- **Real-world consequence:** Pathological shapes (tumors, severe scoliosis) cannot be reconstructed because they fall outside the linear span of the PCA basis.

#### Deficiency 3: The Correspondence (Landmark) Problem

- **Why it happens:** The SSM is highly sensitive to **how landmarks are placed** on each training shape. Different correspondence algorithms produce different models.
- **How it fails:**
  - Manual landmarking is **subjective, tedious, and error-prone** (intra/inter-observer variability).
  - Automated methods (e.g., minimum description length, entropy-based) are computationally expensive and can fail on complex topologies.
- **Real-world consequence:** Two radiologists marking the same heart produce slightly different SSMs, leading to inconsistent segmentations.

#### Deficiency 4: Inability to Handle Topological Variations

- **Why it happens:** SSMs are defined over a **fixed number and topology** of landmarks. The correspondence graph is rigid.
- **How it fails:** Cannot represent shapes with:
  - **Varying number of parts** (e.g., extra vertebrae, missing fingers).
  - **Branching structures** appearing/disappearing (e.g., blood vessel bifurcation).
  - **Holes or cavities** of different sizes.
- **Real-world consequence:** A vascular tree model trained on normal patients fails to represent patients with aneurysms or stenoses with abnormal topology.

#### Deficiency 5: Generalization–Specificity Trade-off

- **Why it happens:** PCA modes capture the **largest variance directions** in the training data. High variance may include **noise modes** that should not be modeled.
- **How it fails:**
  - Using **too few modes** → model is overly rigid, **poor generalization** to unseen shapes.
  - Using **too many modes** → model overfits noise, **poor specificity** (generates implausible shapes).
- **The trade-off is fundamental:** There is no optimal number of modes that simultaneously maximizes both metrics.

#### Deficiency 6: Poor Performance on Occluded, Noisy, or Partial Data

- **Why it happens:** Classical SSMs assume **clean, complete** target shapes during fitting. The fitting process (e.g., Active Shape Model) seeks the closest point in the model space to a noisy target.
- **How it fails:** Occlusions (e.g., surgical clips, implants), image artifacts, and partial views (e.g., truncated CT slice) bias the projection onto the model.
- **Real-world consequence:** Hip prosthesis in X-ray causes ASM to fail because the metallic implant occludes the bone boundary.

#### Deficiency 7: Insufficient Training Data / Curse of Dimensionality

- **Why it happens:** The shape vector is high-dimensional ($2n$ or $3n$ for 3D). PCA requires **sufficient samples** to estimate the covariance reliably.
- **Rule of thumb:** $N \gg t$ (number of training samples should greatly exceed number of modes retained).
- **How it fails:** With limited data, the covariance estimate is poor, leading to unstable modes and overfitting.
- **Real-world consequence:** Rare diseases have small annotated cohorts — SSMs cannot be reliably built.

#### Deficiency 8: Disconnection from Image Evidence (Lack of Context)

- **Why it happens:** Classical SSMs (PDM) encode **shape only**, decoupled from image appearance.
- **How it fails:** Two anatomically distinct structures with similar shape are confused.
- **AAM Improvement:** Active Appearance Models (AAMs) address this by jointly modeling shape and texture, but at the cost of even higher dimensionality.

#### Deficiency 9: Computational Cost of Fitting

- **Why it happens:** Model fitting involves iterative optimization in $b$-space and nearest-landmark search in the image.
- **How it fails:** Real-time applications (e.g., intra-operative navigation) cannot tolerate the slow convergence.
- **Real-world consequence:** Slow segmentation limits clinical workflow integration.

#### Deficiency 10: Linearity Assumption & Gaussian Distribution

- **Why it happens:** PCA models variation as $\bar{x} + \Phi b$ — a linear subspace with Gaussian assumption.
- **How it fails:** Shape variations are often **non-Gaussian, multi-modal, and correlated** in complex, non-linear ways.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Shape vector | $x_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, \dots, x_{i,n}, y_{i,n})^T$ | Length $2n$ (2D) or $3n$ (3D) |
| Mean shape | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Vector; one per dataset |
| Covariance matrix | $S = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$ | Dimension $2n \times 2n$ |
| Eigendecomposition | $S\phi_k = \lambda_k \phi_k$ | $\lambda_k$ = variance along mode $k$ |
| Shape reconstruction | $x = \bar{x} + \Phi b$ | $\Phi$ = $[\phi_1 \dots \phi_t]$ |
| Parameter constraint | $\vert b_k \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$ | 99.7% of Gaussian mass |
| Variance explained | $V(t) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda_k}{\sum_{k=1}^{2n} \lambda_k}$ | Typically choose $t$ s.t. $V(t) \geq 0.95$ |
| Generalization | $G(t) = \frac{1}{N_{test}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{test}} \min_{b} \vert\vert x_i^{test} - (\bar{x} + \Phi b)\vert\vert^2$ | Lower is better |
| Specificity | $S(t) = \frac{1}{N_{synth}}\sum_{j=1}^{N_{synth}} \min_{i} \vert\vert x_j^{synth} - x_i^{train}\vert\vert^2$ | Lower is better |
| Compactness | $C(t) = t$ such that $V(t) \geq 0.98$ | Smaller $t$ = more compact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** Always use $\vert$ or `\vert` in markdown tables (not the bare pipe `|`) when writing absolute-value notation, to avoid breaking the table renderer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the deficiencies of SSMs is **not just academic** — it directly drives clinical engineering decisions:

- **Implant design:** Why personalized implants (3D-printed hip cups) outperform off-the-shelf — SSMs cannot capture patient-specific anomalies.
- **Surgical planning:** Why pre-operative SSM-based planning fails in rare anatomies.
- **Computer-aided diagnosis (CAD):** Why modern deep-learning segmentation (U-Net, nnU-Net) has largely replaced SSM-based methods in clinical pipelines.
- **Regulatory considerations:** FDA/EMA require models to handle edge cases (occlusions, anomalies) — classical SSMs often fail these robustness tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Generalization Metric $G(t)$

The **Generalization Ability** $G(t)$ quantifies how well a model with $t$ modes can represent shapes *not* in the training set. We derive it explicitly.

**Step 1 — Define the test shape set**

Let $\{x_1^{test}, x_2^{test}, \dots, x_{N_{test}}^{test}\}$ be a set of $N_{test}$ shapes not used during training, but which have been Procrustes-aligned to the same coordinate frame as the training set.

**Step 2 — For each test shape, find the optimal model parameters**

For a fixed test shape $x_i^{test}$, we seek parameters $b$ that minimize the squared reconstruction error:

$$b^*_i = \arg\min_{b} \Vert x_i^{test} - (\bar{x} + \Phi b) \Vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve the least-squares problem**

Expanding the squared norm:

$$\Vert x_i^{test} - \bar{x} - \Phi b \Vert^2 = (x_i^{test} - \bar{x} - \Phi b)^T (x_i^{test} - \bar{x} - \Phi b)$$

Differentiate with respect to $b$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial b} \left[ (x_i^{test} - \bar{x})^T (x_i^{test} - \bar{x}) - 2b^T \Phi^T (x_i^{test} - \bar{x}) + b^T \Phi^T \Phi b \right] = 0$$

Since $\Phi$ has orthonormal columns ($\Phi^T \Phi = I$):

$$-2 \Phi^T (x_i^{test} - \bar{x}) + 2 b = 0$$

Therefore the closed-form solution is:

$$b^*_i = \Phi^T (x_i^{test} - \bar{x})$$

**Step 4 — Compute the residual error**

The minimum squared error for the $i$-th test shape is:

$$e_i(t) = \Vert x_i^{test} - \bar{x} - \Phi b^*_i \Vert^2 = \Vert x_i^{test} - \bar{x} \Vert^2 - (b^*_i)^T b^*_i$$

**Step 5 — Average over the test set**

$$G(t) = \frac{1}{N_{test}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{test}} e_i(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to show that **the optimal projection onto the PCA subspace is given by $b^* = \Phi^T (x - \bar{x})$**, because the columns of $\Phi$ are orthonormal eigenvectors. This is the geometric foundation for understanding the **Generalization–Specificity trade-off**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Specificity Metric $S(t)$

**Step 1 — Generate synthetic shapes**

Sample parameters $b_j$ uniformly (or Gaussian) within the plausible range $\vert b_k \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$, then synthesize:

$$x_j^{synth} = \bar{x} + \Phi b_j, \quad j = 1, \dots, N_{synth}$$

**Step 2 — For each synthetic shape, find the closest training shape**

$$d_j = \min_{i \in \{1,\dots,N\}} \Vert x_j^{synth} - x_i^{train} \Vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Average the distances**

$$S(t) = \frac{1}{N_{synth}} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{synth}} d_j$$

**Interpretation:** A model with **high specificity** generates synthetic shapes that closely resemble real anatomical examples. A model with **low specificity** (e.g., overfitted with too many modes) generates implausible, non-anatomical shapes.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Deficiency ↔ Mathematical Cause ↔ Mitigation

| Deficiency | Mathematical / Algorithmic Cause | Mitigation in Literature |
|---|---|---|
| Initialization sensitivity | Non-convex cost function in $b$-space; gradient descent local minima | Multi-resolution search, robust cost functions, deep-learning initializers |
| Limited expressivity | PCA is a linear, second-order method | Kernel PCA, GP-LVM, Deep Boltzmann Machines, autoencoders |
| Correspondence problem | PCA requires fixed-length vectors with paired landmarks | Group-wise registration, entropy-based correspondence, CNN-based landmark detectors |
| Topological variations | Fixed landmark graph topology | Skeleton-based models, hierarchical SSMs, graph-based methods |
| Generalization–Specificity trade-off | Inherent to variance-based mode selection | Regularization, Bayesian PCA, mode truncation via MDL |
| Occlusion/noise sensitivity | Projection assumes complete data | Robust PCA, mixture models, occlusion-aware ASM |
| Limited training data | $N \ll 2n$ makes covariance estimate unstable | Regularized covariance, hierarchical models, transfer learning |
| Disconnection from image evidence | PDM is shape-only | Active Appearance Models (AAMs), deep segmentation networks |
| Computational cost | Iterative optimization, nearest-landmark search | GPU acceleration, closed-form solutions, regression-based fitting |
| Linearity/Gaussian assumption | $x = \bar{x} + \Phi b$ is linear subspace with Gaussian $b$ | Non-linear latent variable models, deep generative models |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A 2D SSM is built from 50 vertebrae shapes, each represented by 40 landmarks. The covariance matrix has 80 eigenvalues. The first 5 eigenvalues are:

$$\lambda_1 = 12.4, \quad \lambda_2 = 6.8, \quad \lambda_3 = 3.1, \quad \lambda_4 = 1.7, \quad \lambda_5 = 0.9$$

Total variance $\sum_{k=1}^{80} \lambda_k = 35.0$.

**Find:** (a) How many modes are needed to explain ≥ 95% of variance?  
(b) What is the maximum allowed parameter value for mode 2?

**Solution:**

(a) Cumulative variance after $t$ modes:

$$V(1) = \frac{12.4}{35.0} = 0.3543$$
$$V(2) = \frac{12.4 + 6.8}{35.0} = \frac{19.2}{35.0} = 0.5486$$
$$V(3) = \frac{19.2 + 3.1}{35.0} = \frac{22.3}{35.0} = 0.6371$$
$$V(4) = \frac{22.3 + 1.7}{35.0} = \frac{24.0}{35.0} = 0.6857$$
$$V(5) = \frac{24.0 + 0.9}{35.0} = \frac{24.9}{35.0} = 0.7114$$

After 5 modes, we have only 71.14% — **not yet 95%**. We would need to retain a much larger number of modes to reach 95%. The compactness is **poor** in this example, indicating that the data has high intrinsic dimensionality.

(b) Maximum allowed parameter for mode 2:

$$\vert b_2 \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_2} = 3 \sqrt{6.8} = 3 \times 2.6077 = 7.823$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** If a question gives eigenvalues and asks about compactness, the student **must** compute cumulative variance step by step. Examiners award **partial marks** for correct setup even if the threshold is not reached within the listed modes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Where SSMs Fail in a Clinical Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Medical Image e.g. CT MRI X-ray] --> stepB[Manual or Automated Landmark Placement]
    stepB --> stepC[Procrustes Alignment of Training Shapes]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute Mean Shape and Covariance]
    stepD --> stepE[PCA Eigendecomposition]
    stepE --> stepF[SSM Constructed]
    stepF --> stepG{Model Fitting on New Image}
    stepG --> stepH{Quality of Initialization}
    stepH -- Good --> stepI[Successful Segmentation]
    stepH -- Poor --> stepJ[Failure Mode 1: Local Minimum]
    stepF --> stepK{Is Test Shape Inside Training Distribution}
    stepK -- Yes --> stepI
    stepK -- No --> stepL[Failure Mode 2: Out of Distribution]
    stepF --> stepM{Topology Matches Training}
    stepM -- Yes --> stepI
    stepM -- No --> stepN[Failure Mode 3: Topological Mismatch]
    stepF --> stepO{Image Quality Adequate}
    stepO -- Yes --> stepI
    stepO -- No --> stepP[Failure Mode 4: Occlusion Artifact]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generalization–Specificity Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Number of PCA Modes t] --> lowModes[Small t]
    axisA --> highModes[Large t]
    lowModes --> genLow[Poor Generalization]
    lowModes --> specHigh[High Specificity]
    highModes --> genHigh[Good Generalization]
    highModes --> specLow[Poor Specificity overfitting]
    genLow --> trap1[Model Too Rigid]
    specLow --> trap2[Model Generates Implausible Shapes]
    trap1 --> decision1[Clinical Failure on Unseen Anatomies]
    trap2 --> decision2[Non Anatomological Reconstructions]
    decision1 --> outcome[KTU Deficiency 5]
    decision2 --> outcome
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: SSM vs. Modern Alternatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classical[Classical SSM Linear PCA PDM] --> def1[Initialization Sensitivity]
    classical --> def2[Linearity Assumption]
    classical --> def3[Correspondence Problem]
    classical --> def4[Topology Inflexibility]
    def1 --> modern1[Multi Resolution Search]
    def2 --> modern2[Kernel PCA GP LVM]
    def2 --> modern3[Deep Generative Models VAE GAN]
    def3 --> modern4[CNN Landmark Detectors]
    def4 --> modern5[Graph Based Skeleton Models]
    modern1 --> clinical[Improved Clinical Robustness]
    modern2 --> clinical
    modern3 --> clinical
    modern4 --> clinical
    modern5 --> clinical
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to **"list and explain"** the deficiencies in a 14-mark question, mapping each deficiency to its **root cause** and a **modern mitigation** consistently earns the highest marks. The diagrams above provide the visual scaffold for such a structured answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

List any **four** major deficiencies of Statistical Shape Models (SSMs) in medical image segmentation.

**Model Answer (4 points):**

1. **Initialization sensitivity** — Active Shape/Appearance Models require a good initial guess; poor initialization leads to local minima and incorrect segmentation.
2. **Limited to linear (Gaussian) variations** — PCA cannot model non-linear, multi-modal shape variations such as pathological anatomies.
3. **Correspondence (landmark) problem** — Manual landmark placement is subjective; automated correspondence is difficult and influences model quality.
4. **Inability to handle topological variations** — SSMs cannot represent shapes with varying numbers of parts, branching structures, or holes.

*(Any 4 of the 10 deficiencies from Section 2.2 are acceptable. Examiners expect 1 mark per valid point.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Explain the **Generalization–Specificity trade-off** in Statistical Shape Models with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (4 points):**

- **Generalization** is the ability of the SSM to represent unseen shapes from the same class.
- **Specificity** is the ability to generate synthetic shapes that resemble real anatomical instances.
- **Trade-off:** When few PCA modes are retained, the model is highly specific (only generates shapes close to the mean) but generalizes poorly to unseen variations. When many modes are retained, the model generalizes well but specificity drops — the model begins to overfit noise and generate implausible, non-anatomical shapes.
- **Example:** In cardiac modeling, retaining only 3 modes captures overall heart size and orientation (good specificity) but cannot represent regional wall-motion abnormalities (poor generalization). Retaining 30 modes can represent abnormal motion but may generate impossible shapes such as self-intersecting ventricles (poor specificity).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Module typically contains one 14-mark question with internal choice. The question carries sub-parts (a) and (b), each for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **construction of a Point Distribution Model (PDM)** as a Statistical Shape Model. Include the mathematical formulation with Procrustes alignment, mean shape computation, covariance estimation, and PCA decomposition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major deficiencies of Statistical Shape Models** in medical image segmentation. For each deficiency, briefly describe a mitigation strategy from the modern literature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PDM Construction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Landmark representation:** Represent each training shape as a vector of $n$ landmark coordinates. For a 2D shape with $n$ landmarks: $x_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, \dots, x_{i,n}, y_{i,n})^T$, giving a $2n$-dimensional vector. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Procrustes alignment:** Remove translation, rotation, and scale differences across training shapes to ensure all shapes are in a common coordinate frame. This is done by minimizing:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - T(\bar{x}) \Vert^2$$

where $T$ is a similarity transform. (1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Mean shape and covariance:** Compute the mean shape $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i$ and the covariance matrix $S = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$. (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — PCA decomposition:** Solve $S\phi_k = \lambda_k \phi_k$ and retain the top $t$ eigenvectors $\phi_1, \dots, \phi_t$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalues, ensuring $\sum_{k=1}^t \lambda_k \geq 0.95 \sum_{k=1}^{2n} \lambda_k$. (1.5 marks)

**Step 5 — Model representation:** Any shape is approximated as $x = \bar{x} + \Phi b$, with the constraint $\vert b_k \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$ to ensure plausibility. (1.5 marks)

**(b) Deficiencies and Mitigations (7 marks):**

Discuss **at least 5 of the 10 deficiencies** from Section 2.2. For each, provide:
- **Root cause** (1 mark)
- **Real-world consequence** (0.3 marks)
- **Mitigation strategy** (0.3 marks)

A complete answer should cover: initialization sensitivity, linear expressivity limit, correspondence problem, topology inflexibility, generalization–specificity trade-off, occlusion sensitivity, training data requirements, lack of image context, computational cost, and Gaussian assumption. (1 mark for overall structure and clarity)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the **closed-form solution for projecting a test shape onto the PCA subspace** of a Statistical Shape Model. Show that the optimal shape parameters are $b^* = \Phi^T (x - \bar{x})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the metrics **Generalization** $G(t)$ and **Specificity** $S(t)$ for an SSM. Explain why these two metrics are in **fundamental conflict**, and illustrate with a sketch of the typical $G(t)$ and $S(t)$ curves as functions of the number of modes $t$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closed-form projection (7 marks):**

For a test shape $x$ (already Procrustes-aligned), we minimize:

$$J(b) = \Vert x - \bar{x} - \Phi b \Vert^2$$

Expanding:

$$J(b) = (x - \bar{x})^T(x - \bar{x}) - 2b^T \Phi^T(x - \bar{x}) + b^T \Phi^T \Phi b$$

Since $\Phi$ has orthonormal columns (eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix are orthonormal), $\Phi^T \Phi = I$:

$$J(b) = (x - \bar{x})^T(x - \bar{x}) - 2b^T \Phi^T(x - \bar{x}) + b^T b$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = -2 \Phi^T(x - \bar{x}) + 2b = 0$$

$$\boxed{b^* = \Phi^T (x - \bar{x})}$$

This is the **optimal projection** of the test shape onto the $t$-dimensional PCA subspace. (Full derivation steps, including orthonormality justification, carry 7 marks.)

**(b) $G(t)$ vs $S(t)$ trade-off (7 marks):**

- **Generalization** $G(t) = \frac{1}{N_{test}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{test}} \min_b \Vert x_i^{test} - (\bar{x} + \Phi b) \Vert^2$: decreases as $t$ grows (more modes → better fit to unseen data). (2 marks)
- **Specificity** $S(t) = \frac{1}{N_{synth}} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{synth}} \min_i \Vert x_j^{synth} - x_i^{train} \Vert^2$: increases as $t$ grows (more modes → synthetic shapes deviate from real training data, capturing noise). (2 marks)
- **Conflict:** The two metrics are monotonically opposed — there is **no optimal $t$** that simultaneously minimizes both. (1 mark)
- **Typical curves:** $G(t)$ is monotonically decreasing; $S(t)$ is monotonically increasing. The **knee point** in both curves is chosen based on clinical application. (2 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SSM Deficiency Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Listing without explaining** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the student merely lists deficiencies without describing the **root cause** and **consequence**. Always use the formula **"Deficiency → Cause → Consequence → Mitigation"**.
> 2. **Confusing SSM with ASM/AAM** — The classical SSM (PDM) is **shape only**; AAM adds **appearance/texture**. Mixing them in a single answer is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the constraint** — When writing $x = \bar{x} + \Phi b$, the constraint $\vert b_k \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$ **must** be stated. Examiners frequently deduct 0.5 mark for omission.
> 4. **Skipping the Procrustes step** — A "Statistical Shape Model" question without Procrustes alignment is incomplete. Always include it.
> 5. **Not distinguishing Generalization from Specificity** — These are *opposite* metrics. Students frequently define them with the wrong monotonicity.
> 6. **Failing to give numerical values** in worked examples — KTU examiners reward numerical computation over abstract statements.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** SSM is a parametric, PCA-based representation of a class of shapes learned from training examples.
- **Core equation:** $x = \bar{x} + \Phi b$, with constraint $\vert b_k \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_k}$.
- **Pipeline:** Landmark placement → Procrustes alignment → Mean shape & covariance → PCA → Shape model.
- **Top 10 Deficiencies (memorize at least 6):**
  1. Initialization sensitivity
  2. Linear/Gaussian expressivity limit
  3. Correspondence (landmark) problem
  4. Topological inflexibility
  5. Generalization–Specificity trade-off
  6. Occlusion/noise sensitivity
  7. Insufficient training data (curse of dimensionality)
  8. Disconnection from image evidence
  9. Computational cost of fitting
  10. Gaussian distribution assumption
- **Key Metrics:**
  - $G(t) = $ mean squared reconstruction error on test set (lower = better generalization)
  - $S(t) = $ mean distance from synthetic to training shapes (lower = better specificity)
  - $V(t) = $ cumulative variance explained (typically retain $t$ such that $V \geq 0.95$)
- **Modern Mitigations:** Kernel PCA, GP-LVM, deep autoencoders, CNN landmark detectors, multi-resolution search, AAMs, robust PCA, regularized covariance.
- **Why it matters clinically:** SSM deficiencies motivate the shift to deep-learning-based segmentation (U-Net, nnU-Net) in modern medical image analysis pipelines.
- **Formula to remember for derivation questions:** $b^* = \Phi^T (x - \bar{x})$ — the orthogonal projection onto the PCA subspace.
- **Exam tip:** Always pair each deficiency with **(i) root cause, (ii) clinical consequence, (iii) modern mitigation** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changing the Parametric Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Changing the Parametric Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Changing the Parametric Model** refers to the systematic generalization of feature-detection algorithms (most notably the **Hough Transform**) from detecting one geometric primitive to another by altering the underlying mathematical descriptor of the shape from a lower-dimensional parameter space to a higher-dimensional one.

In the context of the **Generalized Hough Transform (GHT)** and feature extraction in medical imaging, every geometric shape can be described by an equation of the form:

$$f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{p}) = 0$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)$ are the spatial coordinates of an edge pixel and $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ is the **parameter vector** that uniquely defines the shape. *Changing* the parametric model means replacing the old parameter vector with a new one whose dimensionality $n$ may differ.

| Shape | Equation | Parameter Vector $\mathbf{p}$ | Dimensionality $n$ |
|-------|----------|-------------------------------|--------------------|
| Straight Line | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | $(\rho, \theta)$ | **2** |
| Circle | $(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 = r^2$ | $(a, b, r)$ | **3** |
| Ellipse | $\frac{(x-x_0)^2}{a^2} + \frac{(y-y_0)^2}{b^2} = 1$ | $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$ | **5** |
| General Conic | $Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$ | $(A, B, C, D, E, F)$ | **6 (normalized to 5)** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Swap" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are baking cookies (detecting shapes) using a recipe book (the algorithm). Your original recipe is for **vanilla cookies (lines)** — it needs only *two* ingredients: sugar and butter. One day, the customer asks for **chocolate cookies (circles)**. You do not throw away the cookbook; you simply **swap the recipe page (change the parametric model)** so that the oven (transform engine) now needs *three* ingredients: sugar, butter, **and** cocoa.

The oven (Hough accumulator) automatically grows a new shelf (extra dimension) to store the cocoa. The cooking time (computational cost) goes up, but the workflow remains identical. *This is exactly how the Hough Transform is generalized.*

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Medical Imaging

In medical images (X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound), anatomical structures are **rarely perfect straight lines** — they are curves, ellipses (cardiac chambers, vertebrae discs), or circles (iris of the eye, blood vessels in cross-section, calibration rings). Detecting them requires changing the parametric model from a 2-D line accumulator to a higher-dimensional accumulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Computational complexity of the brute-force Hough Transform grows **exponentially** as $\mathcal{O}(N^n)$ where $N$ is the discretization step per parameter and $n$ is the number of parameters. This is the central trade-off when changing the parametric model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic of a Parametric Model

A parametric model is a *compact mathematical representation* of a shape using a fixed number of variables. The pipeline of feature detection using such a model is:

- **Step 1 — Edge Detection:** Apply a gradient operator (e.g., **Sobel**, **Canny**) to obtain edge pixels $(x_i, y_i)$.
- **Step 2 — Parameter Mapping:** For *each* edge pixel, determine the set of all parameter tuples $(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ that would make that pixel lie on the shape defined by $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{p}) = 0$.
- **Step 3 — Voting (Accumulation):** Cast votes in an $n$-dimensional accumulator array $A(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ for every plausible parameter tuple.
- **Step 4 — Peak Detection:** Local maxima in $A$ correspond to the most likely parameter values of the shape(s) in the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "change" the model?** Because real anatomical structures do not always fit a single primitive. A radiologist looking at a CT slice might want to detect the **vertebral body (ellipse)** instead of the **spinal cord (curve)** — the parametric model must be re-configured.

### 2.2 Generalization From Line → Circle → Ellipse

**Case A — Line (Baseline, 2-D):** Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for all $(\rho, \theta)$ pairs satisfying:

$$\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$

**Case B — Circle (3-D, "one step up"):** Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for all $(a, b, r)$ triples satisfying:

$$r = \sqrt{(x_i - a)^2 + (y_i - b)^2}$$

For a *fixed* radius $r$ (known a-priori, e.g., calibration ball in MRI), this collapses back to a 2-D problem in $(a, b)$.

**Case C — Ellipse (5-D):** Each edge pixel votes for all $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$ satisfying the rotated ellipse equation. This becomes computationally intractable in naive form, motivating **randomized Hough transforms (RHT)** and **probabilistic Hough variants**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these equations appear almost verbatim in KTU ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Shape | Parametric Equation | Parameter Vector | Accumulator Dim | Typical Use in Medical Imaging |
|---|-------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Straight Line | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | $(\rho,\theta)$ | 2-D | Rib cage outlines, ECG lead wires |
| 2 | Circle (known $r$) | $(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2$ | $(a,b)$ | 2-D | Calibration markers, pupil/iris |
| 3 | Circle (unknown $r$) | $(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2$ | $(a,b,r)$ | 3-D | Cross-section of vessels/ducts |
| 4 | Parabola | $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ | $(a,b,c)$ | 3-D | Curvature of spine |
| 5 | Ellipse | $\frac{((x-x_0)\cos\phi+(y-y_0)\sin\phi)^2}{a^2}+\frac{((x-x_0)\sin\phi-(y-y_0)\cos\phi)^2}{b^2}=1$ | $(x_0,y_0,a,b,\phi)$ | 5-D | Cardiac chambers, vertebral disc |
| 6 | General Conic | $Ax^2+Bxy+Cy^2+Dx+Ey+F=0$ | 5 (after normalization) | 5-D | Arbitrary organ boundary |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Changed Parametric Model |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Radiology** | Tumour boundary delineation (ellipse model in CT/MRI slices) |
| **Cardiology** | Left-ventricle segmentation (ellipse fit on echocardiograms) |
| **Ophthalmology** | Optic disc / cup detection (circle/ellipse in fundus images) |
| **Radiotherapy** | Beam's-eye-view field shaping (line/curve edges of MLC) |
| **Surgical Robotics** | Instrument tip tracking (circle model in endoscopy) |

### 2.5 Trade-Off Matrix When Changing the Model

| Aspect | Low-Dim Model (e.g., line, $n=2$) | High-Dim Model (e.g., ellipse, $n=5$) |
|--------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Robustness to noise | Lower | Higher (more constrained fit) |
| Computational cost | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^5)$ — prohibitive |
| Memory for accumulator | Small | Very large |
| Accuracy of anatomical fit | Poor for curved organs | Excellent |
| Need for prior knowledge | Minimal | Often requires $r$, orientation $\phi$, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> Going from a line to a circle model does **not** simply "add one parameter." It changes the geometry of the voting surface from a *sinusoid in the $(\rho,\theta)$ plane* to a *cone in the $(a,b,r)$ volume*. This conceptual shift is what examiners test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voting Surface for a Circle

**Goal:** Show exactly how the parametric model of a single edge pixel changes when we move from line detection to circle detection.

**Given:** A single edge pixel $P(x_i, y_i)$ lies on the boundary of an unknown circle.

**Step 1 — State the implicit equation of the circle:**

$$(x_i - a)^2 + (y_i - b)^2 = r^2$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the parameters** $(a, b, r)$. Squaring both sides gives:

$$x_i^2 - 2x_i a + a^2 + y_i^2 - 2y_i b + b^2 = r^2$$

**Step 3 — Treat $(a, b)$ as unknowns and $r$ as fixed** (start with the simplest 2-D circle case):

$$a^2 - 2x_i a + b^2 - 2y_i b + (x_i^2 + y_i^2 - r^2) = 0$$

This is the equation of a **circle in the parameter space** $(a, b)$, centred at $(x_i, y_i)$ with radius $r$. In other words, for a *fixed radius*, every edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes along a circle in the 2-D accumulator $(a, b)$.

**Step 4 — If $r$ is *not* fixed**, treat $(a, b, r)$ all as unknowns. The locus of votes for one pixel becomes the surface of a **cone in 3-D parameter space**:

$$(a - x_i)^2 + (b - y_i)^2 = r^2$$

The apex of the cone lies at $(x_i, y_i, 0)$, and the opening half-angle is $45^\circ$. The intersection of cones from multiple edge pixels gives the centre $(a, b)$ and radius $r$ of the true circle in the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a cone?** Because $r^2 = (\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2$ — the parameter $r$ is the Euclidean distance from $(a,b)$ to $(x_i,y_i)$. Fixing the pixel and varying the centre traces a 3-D right circular cone.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rotated Ellipse in Parametric Form

The ellipse is the most clinically important generalization. An ellipse rotated by angle $\phi$ about its centre $(x_0, y_0)$ has the implicit equation:

$$\frac{((x-x_0)\cos\phi + (y-y_0)\sin\phi)^2}{a^2} + \frac{((x-x_0)\sin\phi - (y-y_0)\cos\phi)^2}{b^2} = 1$$

**Step 1 — Apply the rotation transformation:**

Let $x' = (x-x_0)\cos\phi + (y-y_0)\sin\phi$ and $y' = -(x-x_0)\sin\phi + (y-y_0)\cos\phi$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the canonical (unrotated) ellipse equation** $\frac{x'^2}{a^2} + \frac{y'^2}{b^2} = 1$:

$$\frac{((x-x_0)\cos\phi + (y-y_0)\sin\phi)^2}{a^2} + \frac{((x-x_0)\sin\phi - (y-y_0)\cos\phi)^2}{b^2} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Expand the squares** to obtain an implicit polynomial in $(x, y)$ with **5 free parameters** $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$. Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ now votes in a **5-D accumulator** for every combination of these parameters satisfying the equation.

**Step 4 — Count the parameters carefully:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total coefficients in } Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F &= 6 \\
\text{Scale invariance (multiply all by } \lambda \neq 0) &\Rightarrow -1 \\
\text{Free parameters} &= 6 - 1 = \mathbf{5}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the 5-D parameter vector $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mapping From Line to Circle (What *Actually* Changes)

Let us explicitly compare what happens inside the Hough Transform when we change the parametric model.

**For a line:** Each pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ contributes votes along the sinusoid:

$$\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta, \quad \theta \in [0, \pi)$$

**For a circle (fixed $r$):** The same pixel contributes votes along a 2-D circle in $(a,b)$:

$$(a - x_i)^2 + (b - y_i)^2 = r^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key conceptual transition:** The sinusoid (1-D curve in 2-D accumulator) becomes a circle (1-D curve in 2-D accumulator). The accumulator dimensionality is the same, but the *shape of the locus* changes. For unknown $r$, the locus becomes a cone — adding **one extra dimension**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Line Model $\mathbf{p}=(\rho,\theta)$ | Circle Model $\mathbf{p}=(a,b,r)$ |
|----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Number of unknowns | 2 | 3 |
| Voting locus for one pixel | Sinusoid | Cone |
| Accumulator size (typical) | $512 \times 180 \approx 9.2 \times 10^4$ | $256^3 \approx 1.7 \times 10^7$ |
| Memory footprint | ~360 KB (int32) | ~68 MB (int32) |
| Typical search range | $\rho \in [-D, D], \theta \in [0,\pi]$ | $a,b \in [0,W], r \in [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$ |
| Degeneracies | Vertical lines need $\theta = \pi/2$ | Concentric circles merge votes |
| Used when | Boundaries are straight | Cross-sectional anatomy |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a CT slice of the abdomen shows a circular lesion of unknown radius centred somewhere in a $256 \times 256$ image. We discretize the parameter space as follows:

- Centre $a \in [0, 255]$ in steps of $\Delta a = 1$ → **256 bins**
- Centre $b \in [0, 255]$ in steps of $\Delta b = 1$ → **256 bins**
- Radius $r \in [5, 50]$ in steps of $\Delta r = 1$ → **46 bins**

**Total accumulator cells:**

$$N_{\text{acc}} = 256 \times 256 \times 46 = 3{,}145{,}728 \approx 3.0 \times 10^6$$

**Total edge pixels** (after Canny): Suppose $M = 5{,}000$.

**Total votes cast** (each pixel casts $\sim 46$ votes, one per radius slice):

$$N_{\text{votes}} = M \times 46 = 230{,}000$$

**Effective fill ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{\text{votes}}}{N_{\text{acc}}} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^5}{3.0 \times 10^6} \approx 7.7\%$$

A fill ratio of 7.7% is healthy — neither too sparse (poor detection) nor too dense (false peaks). If we doubled the parameters (e.g., to an ellipse), $\eta$ would plummet to $\sim 0.03\%$, motivating randomized voting.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Changing the Parametric Model

The diagram below maps the operational flow of changing from a line-based to a higher-order parametric model within an image-processing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image: CT or MRI Slice] --> B[Pre-processing: Noise Filtering]
    B --> C[Edge Detection using Canny Operator]
    C --> D{Choice of Parametric Model}

    D -- "Line Model" --> E1[2D Accumulator: rho, theta]
    E1 --> F1[Peak Detection in 2D Sinusoid Space]
    F1 --> G1[Detected Straight Boundaries: e.g., Rib Edges]

    D -- "Circle Model" --> E2[3D Accumulator: a, b, r]
    E2 --> F2[Peak Detection in 3D Cone Space]
    F2 --> G2[Detected Circular Structures: e.g., Blood Vessel Cross Section]

    D -- "Ellipse Model" --> E3[5D Accumulator: x0, y0, a, b, phi]
    E3 --> F3[Peak Detection in 5D Space using RHT]
    F3 --> G3[Detected Elliptical Anatomy: e.g., Left Ventricle]

    G1 --> H[Post-processing and Visualization]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style E1 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E3 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style F1 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c62828
    style F2 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c62828
    style F3 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c62828
    style G1 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c
    style G2 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c
    style G3 fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c
    style H fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Voting Locus Transformation

This block diagram illustrates how the *locus of votes* cast by a single edge pixel morphs as the parametric model is changed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Single Edge Pixel at xi, yi] --> Q{Parametric Model}

    Q -- "Line: rho, theta" --> L1[Sinusoidal Locus in 2D Plane]
    L1 --> L1A[Amplitude is constant]

    Q -- "Circle with fixed r" --> L2[Circular Locus in 2D Plane]
    L2 --> L2A[Radius equals r]

    Q -- "Circle with unknown r" --> L3[Cone Surface in 3D Space]
    L3 --> L3A[Apex at xi, yi, 0]

    Q -- "Ellipse with known phi" --> L4[Complex Curve in 5D Space]
    L4 --> L4A[Projection onto 2D yields an ellipse in the image]

    style P fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style Q fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff8f00
    style L1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style L4 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block — Computational Cost vs. Model Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Zero-Order Model: Point p1] -->|Trivial| C0[Cost O to the 1]
    M1[First-Order Model: Line p1, p2] -->|Sinusoid Search| C1[Cost O to the 2]
    M2[Second-Order Model: Circle p1, p2, p3] -->|Cone Search| C2[Cost O to the 3]
    M3[Third-Order Model: Ellipse p1..p5] -->|RHT Required| C3[Cost O to the 5]

    C0 --> Z[Detector: Centre of Mass]
    C1 --> Z
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z

    style M0 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style M1 fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e
    style M2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style M3 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c
    style Z fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#004d40
```
