## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "parametric model" in the context of the Hough Transform. Why is it necessary to change the parametric model when detecting different anatomical structures?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A parametric model is a mathematical representation of a shape using a fixed number of parameters $n$, expressed as $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{p}) = 0$. The Hough Transform uses such a model to detect shapes by mapping edge pixels into an $n$-dimensional parameter accumulator.
(1½ marks)
Different anatomical structures have different geometries — ribs are approximately straight lines (2 parameters), the left ventricle is elliptical (5 parameters), and a blood vessel cross-section is circular (3 parameters). Hence the parametric model must be changed to suit the target structure for accurate detection.
(1½ marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*State the parametric equation of a circle and explain why its detection requires a 3-D accumulator in the Hough Transform.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The parametric equation of a circle is:

$$(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$$

with parameter vector $(a, b, r)$ of dimension 3.
(1 mark)
Since the equation contains three unknowns — the centre $(a, b)$ and the radius $r$ — every edge pixel must vote in a 3-D accumulator $A(a, b, r)$. The locus of votes from one pixel forms a cone, and the apex of intersecting cones gives the true centre and radius.
(2 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the change in voting-locus geometry** (sinusoid → cone → 5-D surface). Always mention this explicitly. **(−2 marks)**
> - Students forget to specify the **dimensions and units** of the accumulator. Always quote the dimensionality and a numerical memory estimate. **(−1 mark)**
> - Do **not** confuse the *radius of the image-space circle* with the *radius of the parameter-space voting locus*. The latter is determined by $r$ (assumed/known). **(−1 mark)**
> - When the question asks "with a neat sketch", a labelled diagram of the cone-voting geometry is mandatory. **(−2 marks if missing)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the procedure for detecting a straight line in an image using the Hough Transform. Discuss the limitation of this approach when applied to anatomical structures in medical images. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the parametric model is changed to detect circles in medical images. Derive the locus of votes cast by a single edge pixel and estimate the accumulator size for a $256 \times 256$ image with $r \in [5, 50]$ pixels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Line Detection via Hough Transform (7 marks):**

1. Apply Canny edge detector to obtain binary edge map. **(1 mark)**
2. Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for all $(\rho, \theta)$ pairs satisfying:
$$\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$
**(1 mark)**
3. Discretize $\rho \in [-D, D]$ (with $D = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}$) and $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ into accumulator bins $A(\rho, \theta)$. **(1 mark)**
4. Increment $A(\rho, \theta)$ for every $(\rho, \theta)$ that the sinusoid passes through. **(1 mark)**
5. Locate peaks in $A(\rho, \theta)$; each peak corresponds to one detected line. **(1 mark)**

*Limitation for anatomy:* Real anatomical structures (cardiac chambers, vertebral discs, pupil, blood vessels) are **curved**, not straight. The 2-D line model will either miss them or fit them poorly, leading to inaccurate boundary detection in CT/MRI/X-ray images. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Circle Detection by Changing the Model (7 marks):**

1. **State the new parametric equation:** $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ with parameters $(a, b, r)$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Derive the voting locus for a single pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:** Solving for the centre coordinates gives the cone equation:
$$(a - x_i)^2 + (b - y_i)^2 = r^2$$
This is a cone in $(a, b, r)$ space with apex at $(x_i, y_i, 0)$ and $45^\circ$ half-angle. **(2 marks)**
3. **Explain the voting:** For each pixel and each discretized radius $r_k$, increment $A(a, b, r_k)$ for all $(a, b)$ lying on the corresponding circle in the parameter plane. **(1 mark)**
4. **Estimate the accumulator size** for a $256 \times 256$ image:
- $a$ bins: 256, $b$ bins: 256, $r$ bins: $50 - 5 + 1 = 46$.
- Total cells: $256 \times 256 \times 46 = 3{,}145{,}728$.
- Memory (int32): $\approx 12$ MB. **(2 marks)**
5. **Trade-off note:** Computational cost rises from $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ (lines) to $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ (circles), justifying techniques like the Randomized Hough Transform for real-time medical imaging. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a parametric model in feature detection. Discuss in detail the change from a line to a circle parametric model, highlighting the change in (i) parameter vector, (ii) accumulator dimensionality, and (iii) voting locus geometry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, derive the parametric equation of a rotated ellipse and explain why detecting it requires a 5-D accumulator. Mention any one practical medical imaging application where this model change is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Parametric Model & Change Line → Circle (7 marks):**

1. **Definition:** A parametric model describes a shape as $f(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{p}) = 0$ where $\mathbf{x}$ is the image coordinate and $\mathbf{p}$ is a vector of $n$ parameters uniquely defining the shape. **(1 mark)**

2. **Change of parameter vector:**
- Line: $\mathbf{p}_{\text{line}} = (\rho, \theta)$, $n = 2$.
- Circle: $\mathbf{p}_{\text{circle}} = (a, b, r)$, $n = 3$.
We add the centre coordinates $(a, b)$ and the radius $r$. **(1 mark)**

3. **Change of accumulator dimensionality:** From $A(\rho, \theta)$ (2-D) to $A(a, b, r)$ (3-D). The number of bins grows as $N^n$. **(1 mark)**

4. **Change of voting locus geometry:**
- For a line, each pixel casts votes along a *sinusoid*: $\rho(\theta) = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$.
- For a circle, each pixel casts votes along a *cone*: $(a - x_i)^2 + (b - y_i)^2 = r^2$.
The sinusoid is a 1-D curve in 2-D space; the cone is a 2-D surface in 3-D space. **(2 marks)**

5. **Practical implication:** A line detector finds rib cage outlines (straight edges in a chest X-ray), while a circle detector finds the cross-section of blood vessels or iris of the eye. The same algorithm framework (Hough Transform) is reused; only the parametric descriptor changes. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Rotated Ellipse Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **State the canonical (unrotated) ellipse equation** centred at origin:
$$\frac{x'^2}{a^2} + \frac{y'^2}{b^2} = 1$$
**(1 mark)**

2. **Apply rotation by angle $\phi$** about the centre $(x_0, y_0)$:
$$x' = (x-x_0)\cos\phi + (y-y_0)\sin\phi$$
$$y' = -(x-x_0)\sin\phi + (y-y_0)\cos\phi$$
**(1 mark)**

3. **Substitute** to obtain the parametric/rotated ellipse:
$$\frac{((x-x_0)\cos\phi + (y-y_0)\sin\phi)^2}{a^2} + \frac{((x-x_0)\sin\phi - (y-y_0)\cos\phi)^2}{b^2} = 1$$
**(1 mark)**

4. **Identify the 5 parameters:** $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$. Therefore the accumulator $A(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$ is **5-dimensional**. **(1 mark)**

5. **Neat diagram:** Show the rotated ellipse in image space with centre $(x_0, y_0)$, semi-major axis $a$ along the rotated $x'$-axis, semi-minor axis $b$ along $y'$-axis, and orientation angle $\phi$ measured from the horizontal. **(2 marks)**

6. **Medical application:** Segmentation of the **left ventricle** in echocardiography or cardiac MRI. The LV chamber in a short-axis view closely resembles an ellipse, and detecting it requires fitting the rotated-ellipse parametric model. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** A parametric model $f(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{p})=0$ represents a shape; "changing the model" means altering $\mathbf{p}$ to suit a different shape.
- **Line model:** $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$, $n=2$, locus is a **sinusoid**.
- **Circle model:** $(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2$, $n=3$, locus is a **cone** (apex at pixel, half-angle $45^\circ$).
- **Ellipse model:** $n=5$ parameters $(x_0, y_0, a, b, \phi)$; cone generalizes to a complex 5-D surface.
- **General conic:** $n=5$ after scale normalization (6 raw coefficients).
- **Voting locus geometry changes drastically** — this is the *core* concept examiners test.
- **Cost growth:** $\mathcal{O}(N^n)$ — going from line to ellipse is $N^3$ times more expensive in the worst case.
- **Memory growth:** From ~360 KB (line, $512 \times 180$) to ~68 MB (circle, $256^3$) — a real engineering constraint.
- **Worked accumulator size for circle in $256\times256$, $r \in [5,50]$:** $256 \times 256 \times 46 = 3{,}145{,}728$ cells (~12 MB int32).
- **Fill ratio $\eta = N_{\text{votes}}/N_{\text{acc}}$** — healthy range 5–20% for robust detection.
- **Randomized Hough Transform (RHT)** is the standard remedy when $n \geq 4$ — sample $k$ random pixels, solve analytically, vote once.
- **Clinical examples to memorize:** Line → ribs; Circle → vessel cross-section, iris; Ellipse → left ventricle, vertebral disc.
- **Examiner pet-traps:** Confusing image-space radius with parameter-space voting-radius; omitting sketch; missing the dimensionality argument; forgetting the cone's $45^\circ$ half-angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using a Part-Based Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Using a Part-Based Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Part-Based Model (PBM)** is a computational framework in computer vision and medical image analysis that represents a target object (anatomical structure, organ, lesion, or landmark configuration) as a collection of **independently detectable local parts** connected through **learned geometric/spatial relationships**. The model jointly encodes: 1. **Local Appearance Models** — describing what each part looks like in the image (intensity, texture, gradient, or learned features). 2. **Geometric/Structural Priors** — describing how the parts are spatially arranged relative to one another (relative position, scale, and orientation). 3. **Global Deformation Constraints** — restricting how the configuration can deform from a canonical reference pose. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the Part-Based Model operationalizes a **compositional recognition principle**: an object is recognized not as a single rigid template, but as a constellation of smaller, robustly detectable components arranged in a flexible, statistically learned geometric layout. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, PEEBT745 Module 3):** Part-Based Models sit at the intersection of *Statistical Shape Modelling*, *Deformable Registration*, and *Machine-Learned Feature Detection*. They are foundational to landmark localization, organ segmentation, and pathology detection pipelines used in clinical CAD (Computer-Aided Diagnosis) systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Face in a Crowd" Imagine you are trying to recognize your friend **Dr. Sharma** in a crowded hospital lobby. You do not scan the entire crowd as one giant pixel pattern. Instead, you unconsciously perform a **part-based search**: - You first locate a **distinctive local cue** — perhaps his spectacles, or his white coat ID badge. - Once anchored, your brain then *expects* the **nose** to be a few centimetres below and the **mouth** slightly further down and to the left. - Each expected part is searched in a **small neighbourhood** around its predicted location, not across the whole image. - If multiple parts are found in the **right relative configuration**, you confidently declare: "That is Dr. Sharma." This is exactly how a Part-Based Model works. Each "spectacle" or "ID badge" is a **part** with its own appearance template, and the "few centimetres below" rule is the **geometric prior** (often modelled as a spring or Gaussian cost). The classical formalization of this idea is the **Pictorial Structure Model** of Fischler & Elschlager (1973), later extended by Felzenszwalb et al. into the famous **Deformable Part Model (DPM)**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Box > [!NOTE] > **Critical Vocabulary (commit to memory for KTU exams):** > > | Term | Meaning | > |---|---| > | **Part (Local Patch)** | A small sub-region of the object with a learnable appearance model. | > | **Root Filter** | The global, coarse template covering the entire object at low resolution. | > | **Part Filter** | A higher-resolution template applied at each part location. | > | **Anchor Position** | The canonical (reference) coordinates of a part in the model frame. | > | **Deformation Cost** | A penalty for displacing a part from its anchor (typically quadratic). | > | **Score** | A scalar confidence value produced by matching filters to image evidence. | > | **Latent Variable** | A hidden variable (e.g., part location) inferred during detection. | > | **Latent SVM (LSVM)** | The discriminative training algorithm used to learn DPM weights. | > | **Pictorial Structure** | A graph $G=(V,E)$ with appearance and pairwise spring costs. | > | **Star Model** | A pictorial structure in which all parts connect to a single root (no part–part edges). | ## 1.4 Why Part-Based Models Matter in Medical Imaging Medical images (CT, MRI, X-ray, Ultrasound, PET) are **noisy, low-contrast, and highly variable across patients**. A rigid whole-object template fails because organs deform with breathing, pathology, and individual anatomy. Part-Based Models provide: - **Robustness to occlusion** (e.g., a tumour partially covering an organ boundary). - **Robustness to intra-class variation** (e.g., one patient's liver looks quite different from another's). - **Interpretability** — each detected part corresponds to a meaningful anatomical sub-structure. - **Compositional generalisation** — new variants can be assembled from learned parts. > [!TIP] > **Standard Default Hyperparameters** you may quote in KTU answers: > - Number of parts in a star-structured DPM: typically $n \in [6, 12]$ for an organ. > - Part resolution: usually **2× the root filter resolution**. > - Deformation cost standard deviation: $\sigma \in [0.05, 0.15]$ in normalized image coordinates. > - HOG cell size: $8 \times 8$ pixels (Dalal–Triggs standard). > - SVM regularization constant: $C \in [0.001, 0.01]$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of a Part-Based Model A Part-Based Model is specified by a **6-tuple**: $$ \mathcal{M} = (F_0,\; \{F_i\}_{i=1}^{n},\; \{p_i\}_{i=0}^{n},\; \{c_i\}_{i=1}^{n},\; b,\; \pi) $$ where: | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $F_0$ | Root filter (covers whole object at coarse scale). | | $F_i,\; i=1..n$ | Part filters (cover local regions at finer scale). | | $p_i = (x_i, y_i, \ell_i)$ | Anchor position of part $i$ — offset from root and octave/level. | | $c_i$ | Quadratic deformation coefficients (2×2 matrix + vector). | | $b$ | Global scalar bias (model score offset). | | $\pi$ | Mixture component index (for multi-pose/multi-view models). | ## 2.2 The Pictorial Structure Energy Function For a hypothesized configuration $L = (l_0, l_1, \ldots, l_n)$, where $l_i = (x_i, y_i)$ is the 2-D image location of part $i$, the **score** of placing the model on an image $I$ is: $$ S(I, L) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=0}^{n} m_i(I, l_i)}_{\text{appearance evidence}} \;-\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i(l_i - l_0)}_{\text{deformation cost}} \;+\; b $$ This is the **single mixture-component score**. Let us expand each term: ### 2.2.1 Appearance Term The appearance of part $i$ at location $l_i$ is the **dot product of the filter with HOG features** extracted at that location: $$ m_i(I, l_i) \;=\; \langle F_i,\; \Phi(I, l_i) \rangle $$ where $\Phi(I, l_i)$ is the **HOG feature pyramid** at the part's anchor position. A positive $m_i$ means the image evidence supports part $i$ being at that location; a negative value means it does not. > [!NOTE] > **HOG = Histogram of Oriented Gradients.** Each cell of size $8 \times 8$ pixels yields a 9-bin orientation histogram (or 18-bin for DPM sign-sensitive features). Cells are grouped into $2 \times 2$ blocks with L2-normalization. ### 2.2.2 Deformation Term The deformation cost penalizes the displacement of part $i$ from its anchor relative to the root: $$ d_i(\Delta x, \Delta y) \;=\; (d_x,\; d_y)\cdot\mathbf{c}_i \cdot (d_x,\; d_y)^T \;=\; c_{i,xx}\, d_x^2 \;+\; 2\,c_{i,xy}\, d_x d_y \;+\; c_{i,yy}\, d_y^2 $$ where $(d_x, d_y) = (x_i, y_i) - 2(x_0, y_0) - p_i$ accounts for the **2× resolution doubling** of part filters relative to the root. The cost is **quadratic**, equivalent to a Hookean spring: large displacements are punished quadratically. ### 2.2.3 Bias Term The scalar $b$ shifts the overall score so that negative scores can be thresholded to zero (standard LSVM convention). ## 2.3 Mixture Models for Multi-View / Multi-Pose Real organs and pathologies exhibit **multi-modal appearance distributions** (e.g., a kidney seen from anterior vs. lateral view). The model is therefore extended to a **mixture of $K$ components**: $$ S(I, L) \;=\; \max_{k \in \{1..K\}} \left[ S_k(I, L) \;-\; \alpha_k \right] $$ where $S_k$ is the score of mixture component $k$ and $\alpha_k$ is its learned threshold. The argmax over $k$ is itself a latent variable inferred at test time. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are the heart of every Part-Based Model answer on the KTU exam.** | # | Equation | LaTeX | Purpose / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pictorial structure score | $S = \sum m_i - \sum d_i + b$ | Total model confidence on a hypothesis. | | 2 | Appearance evidence | $m_i = \langle F_i, \Phi(I, l_i)\rangle$ | Part–image match score. | | 3 | Quadratic deformation | $d_i = \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{c}_i \mathbf{d}$ | Penalize part displacement. | | 4 | Anchor offset | $\mathbf{d} = (x_i,y_i) - 2(x_0,y_0) - p_i$ | Account for 2× scale. | | 5 | HOG cell descriptor | $\Phi_{\text{cell}} \in \mathbb{R}^{9 \text{ or } 18}$ | Gradient orientation histogram. | | 6 | Latent SVM objective | $\min_{w} \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 + C \sum_{i} \max(0, 1 - y_i\, f_w(x_i, z_i))$ | Discriminative training. | | 7 | Energy-minimization (DP) | $D_p(I) = m_p(I) + \min_{q \in \text{children}(p)} \big[ D_q(I) + d_{pq} \big]$ | Recursive inference. | | 8 | Distance transform | $D(x,y) = \min_{(x',y')} \big[ (d_{xx}(x'-x)^2 + d_{yy}(y'-y)^2) + M(x',y') \big]$ | Efficient quadratic minimization. | | 9 | Root score at octave $\ell$ | $R_{\ell}(x_0, y_0) = m_0(I_\ell, x_0, y_0) + \sum_{i} D_{i,\ell+1}(2x_0 + p_{ix},\, 2y_0 + p_{iy})$ | Combined root + best-part score. | | 10 | Detection threshold | $S \geq 0$ | Standard zero-bias decision rule. | | 11 | Bounding box | $bb = (x_0, y_0, w_0, h_0) + \text{transform}(\text{part offsets})$ | Final output region. | | 12 | Mixture model max | $S = \max_k (S_k - \alpha_k)$ | Select best mixture/pose. | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Part-Based Models are deployed in: - **Left-Ventricle Landmark Detection in Cardiac MRI** — landmarking 6–12 control points around the myocardium. - **Prostate Segmentation in TRUS (Trans-Rectal Ultrasound)** — DPMs for boundary delineation in noisy data. - **Lung-Nodule Detection in Chest CT** — star-structured models with rib-suppressed HOG features. - **Vertebra Localization in Spine MRI/CT** — articulated chain models for vertebral column tracking. - **Cephalometric Landmarking in Dental X-rays** — 19-landmark ASM/AAM pipeline (active shape/appearance models are special part-based variants). - **Tumour Co-Segmentation across longitudinal scans** — part-based priors with growth deformation costs. > [!TIP] > In your KTU answer, always **draw the model architecture before writing the equation**. Examiners reward structural intuition alongside the math. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Pictorial Structure Score from First Principles We start from the **Bayesian posterior** of an object configuration $L$ given an image $I$: $$ P(L \mid I) \;\propto\; P(I \mid L)\, P(L) $$ **Step 1 — Conditional independence of parts.** Assuming the appearance of each part depends only on the local image evidence at that part (Markovian assumption): $$ P(I \mid L) \;=\; \prod_{i=0}^{n} P(I \mid l_i) $$ In words: knowing where all parts are, the image is rendered independently at each part location. **Step 2 — Factorization of the prior.** Assuming the geometric prior factorizes over edges of a tree (or star) graph: $$ P(L) \;=\; P(l_0)\, \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(l_i \mid l_0) $$ For a **star model** all parts are children of the root. **Step 3 — Take logarithms.** Monotonic transformation preserves the argmax: $$ \log P(L \mid I) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{n} \log P(I \mid l_i) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(l_i \mid l_0) \;+\; \text{const.} $$ **Step 4 — Match to energy form.** Define appearance score as log-likelihood $m_i \equiv \log P(I \mid l_i)$ and deformation cost as the negative log-prior $d_i \equiv -\log P(l_i \mid l_0)$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \log P(L \mid I) &\propto \sum_{i=0}^{n} m_i(I, l_i) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i(l_i, l_0) \\ &= S(I, L) - b \end{aligned} $$ Hence the **score function $S(I,L)$ is exactly the negative energy (log-posterior)** of the configuration. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quadratic Deformation Cost as a Gaussian Prior Assume the part displacement $\mathbf{d} = (d_x, d_y)$ follows a **bivariate Gaussian** centered at zero with covariance $\Sigma_i$: $$ P(l_i \mid l_0) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\vert \Sigma_i \vert}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mathbf{d}\right) $$ Taking the negative log and dropping constants: $$ \begin{aligned} d_i(\mathbf{d}) &\equiv -\log P(l_i \mid l_0) \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mathbf{d} + \text{const.} \end{aligned} $$ Writing $\Sigma_i^{-1} = 2 \mathbf{c}_i$ (a convention from Felzenszwalb et al.): $$ d_i(d_x, d_y) \;=\; c_{i,xx}\, d_x^2 \;+\; 2\,c_{i,xy}\, d_x d_y \;+\; c_{i,yy}\, d_y^2 $$ The coefficients $c_{i,xx}, c_{i,xy}, c_{i,yy}$ are precisely the entries of $\tfrac{1}{2}\Sigma_i^{-1}$. The **standard deviation** $\sigma_i$ of the part's location scatter is recovered as: $$ \sigma_i \;=\; \sqrt{c_{i,xx} \cdot c_{i,yy} - c_{i,xy}^2}^{\,-1/2} $$ This is why small $c_{i,xx}, c_{i,yy}$ imply a "loose" part (high variance) and large values imply a "rigid" part. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficient Inference via Generalized Distance Transform Naively, for a root at $(x_0, y_0)$ we would minimize the part deformation term by **enumerating every part location** — a costly $O(N^2)$ operation. The **Generalized Distance Transform (GDT)** of Felzenszwalb & Huttenlocher (2004) reduces this to $O(N)$ using dynamic programming over a separable cost. For a 1-D example, consider: $$ D(x) \;=\; \min_{x'} \big[\, a\,(x - x')^2 + b\,(x' - x_0)^2 + M(x') \,\big] $$ Expanding the quadratic: $$ \begin{aligned} D(x) &= \min_{x'} \big[ a x^2 - 2 a x x' + a x'^2 + b x'^2 - 2 b x' x_0 + b x_0^2 + M(x') \big] \\ &= a x^2 + b x_0^2 + \min_{x'} \big[ (a+b) x'^2 - 2(a x + b x_0) x' + M(x') \big] \end{aligned} $$ The inner minimization is a classic **1-D quadratic distance transform** computed in two passes (forward and backward) in $O(N)$ time. The 2-D case is obtained by separability: $$ D(x, y) \;=\; \min_{x',y'} \big[ a_x (x-x')^2 + a_y (y-y')^2 + b_x (x'-x_0)^2 + b_y (y'-y_0)^2 + M(x',y') \big] $$ $$ D(x, y) \;=\; \min_{x'} \big[ a_x (x-x')^2 + b_x (x'-x_0)^2 + \big( \min_{y'} [a_y (y-y')^2 + b_y (y'-y_0)^2 + M(x',y')] \big) \big] $$ which is computed by **first** running the 1-D transform on each column of $M$, **then** on each resulting row. Total complexity: $O(N^2)$ in image area — same as one filter convolution, but yielding a **global optimum** of the part placement problem. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Latent SVM Training Objective The Deformable Part Model is trained with a **Latent SVM (LSVM)** because part locations $z_i$ are not annotated in training data. Given positive examples $\{x_i\}$ and negative examples $\{y_j\}$ with latent configurations $z$, the LSVM objective is: $$ \min_{w} \; \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 \;+\; C \sum_{i} \max\!\big(0,\; 1 - w^\top \phi(x_i, z_i^*)\big) $$ with $$ z_i^* \;=\; \underset{z}{\arg\max}\; w^\top \phi(x_i, z) $$ The **inner maximization** is the inference step (GDT); the **outer minimization** is a standard convex SVM problem once $z_i^*$ is fixed. This is the classic **CCCP (Concave–Convex Procedure)** or **SVM-struct** style alternating optimization. Convergence is guaranteed to a **local optimum** of the non-convex LSVM objective. > [!NOTE] > **Practical note:** Stochastic gradient descent with positive mining and hard-negative mining (Felzenszwalb et al., 2010) is the standard training recipe. Cache feature pyramids to disk; one DPM on PASCAL VOC takes ~1 day on 1 CPU. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Computing the Score Suppose a star model with **$n=2$ parts** has filters $F_0, F_1, F_2$ and at a candidate configuration $L$ the following are measured: - Root score: $m_0 = 1.8$ - Part 1 score: $m_1 = 0.9$, displacement $\mathbf{d}_1 = (3, -1)$ pixels, $\mathbf{c}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}0.02 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.04\end{pmatrix}$ - Part 2 score: $m_2 = 1.2$, displacement $\mathbf{d}_2 = (-2, 2)$ pixels, $\mathbf{c}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}0.03 & 0.005 \\ 0.005 & 0.03\end{pmatrix}$ - Bias: $b = 0.4$ **Compute deformation costs:** $$ \begin{aligned} d_1 &= 0.02 \cdot 3^2 + 0 \cdot 3 \cdot (-1) + 0.04 \cdot (-1)^2 = 0.18 + 0 + 0.04 = 0.22 \\ d_2 &= 0.03 \cdot (-2)^2 + 2(0.005)(-2)(2) + 0.03 \cdot 2^2 = 0.12 - 0.04 + 0.12 = 0.20 \end{aligned} $$ **Total score:** $$ \begin{aligned} S(I, L) &= (1.8 + 0.9 + 1.2) - (0.22 + 0.20) + 0.4 \\ &= 3.9 - 0.42 + 0.4 \\ &= 3.88 \end{aligned} $$ Since $S \geq 0$, this configuration is **accepted as a detection**. The model has high confidence in this hypothesis. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Pipeline | Stage | Input | Process | Output | Key Equation | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Feature extraction | Raw image $I$ | Compute HOG feature pyramid $\Phi(I, \ell)$ | Multi-scale HOG pyramid | $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 31 \times L}$ | | 2. Root filter matching | $\Phi$, $F_0$ | Cross-correlate root filter at every position & octave | Root response map $R_0$ | $R_0(x_0,y_0,\ell) = \langle F_0, \Phi_\ell(x_0,y_0) \rangle$ | | 3. Part filter matching | $\Phi$, $F_i$ | Cross-correlate each part filter at $\ell+1$ | Part response maps $R_i$ | $R_i(x_i,y_i,\ell+1) = \langle F_i, \Phi_{\ell+1}(x_i,y_i) \rangle$ | | 4. Deformation transform | $R_i$, $\mathbf{c}_i$ | Apply GDT to incorporate spring costs | Best-part-scored maps $D_i$ | $D_i = \min_{\Delta} \big[ R_i(x_i + \Delta) + d_i(\Delta) \big]$ | | 5. Root + best-parts combination | $R_0$, $D_i$, $\mathbf{p}_i$ | Sum root response with best-part responses shifted to root frame | Final score map $S$ | $S(x_0,y_0) = R_0(x_0,y_0) + \sum_i D_i(2x_0 + p_{ix}, 2y_0 + p_{iy}) + b$ | | 6. Non-maximum suppression | $S$ | Suppress overlapping high-scoring boxes | Detection list $\mathcal{D}$ | IoU threshold $\tau = 0.5$ | | 7. (Optional) Mixture selection | $\{\mathcal{D}_k\}_{k=1}^K$ | Pick highest-scoring mixture per detection | Final detections | $k^* = \arg\max_k (S_k - \alpha_k)$ | # 4.
