KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. Define a Part-Based Model and list its three core components. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar)]** **Model Answer:** A Part-Based Model is a computational representation of an object as a collection of local, independently detectable parts connected by learned geometric relationships. Its three core components are: 1. **Root filter** — a coarse, global template covering the whole object. 2. **Part filters** — fine-resolution templates for local sub-regions. 3. **Deformation model** — quadratic cost functions penalising part displacement from anchor positions. These are combined in the score function $S = \sum m_i - \sum d_i + b$, where $m_i$ is appearance evidence and $d_i$ is deformation cost. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for components, 1 for score expression.)* ### Question 2. What is the role of the Generalised Distance Transform in a Part-Based Model? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]** **Model Answer:** The Generalised Distance Transform (GDT) is used at the **inference stage** to efficiently compute, for every possible root location, the **best part configuration** that minimises the combined appearance + deformation cost. It reduces the naive $O(N^2)$ search over part positions to $O(N)$ using a separable 1-D + 1-D two-pass algorithm (forward and backward sweeps). It allows global optimum inference of part locations without exhaustive search, making real-time detection feasible. *(3 marks: 1 for inference role, 1 for complexity, 1 for the two-pass idea.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete pipeline of using a Part-Based Model for object detection in medical images**, listing the role of each stage. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar)]** **Model Answer:** The pipeline has six stages, shown in the block diagram below: ```mermaid graph LR IMG["Input Image"] --> HOG["Feature
Pyramid"] HOG --> ROOT["Root Filter
Convolution"] HOG --> PART["Part Filter
Convolution"] PART --> GDT["Generalized
Distance
Transform"] ROOT --> COMB["Combine
Root and
Best Parts"] GDT --> COMB COMB --> NMS["Non-Maximum
Suppression"] NMS --> OUT["Final
Detections"] ``` | Stage | Function | Output | |---|---|---| | 1. Feature pyramid | Build HOG at multiple octaves | $\Phi(I, \ell)$ | | 2. Root matching | Convolve $F_0$ at every $(x_0, y_0, \ell)$ | Root response map | | 3. Part matching | Convolve each $F_i$ at $\ell+1$ | Part response maps | | 4. GDT | Add deformation cost to part responses via DP | Best-part response maps | | 5. Combination | Sum root + shifted best-part responses | Final score map $S$ | | 6. NMS | Suppress overlapping detections with IoU $> 0.5$ | List of bounding boxes | The final score is $S(x_0, y_0) = m_0 + \sum_i D_i(2x_0 + p_{ix}, 2y_0 + p_{iy}) + b$, thresholded at zero. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for stage-wise explanation, 1 for final score, 1 for NMS threshold.)* **(b)** Derive the **quadratic deformation cost** as the negative log of a Gaussian prior on part displacement. State clearly the relationship between the deformation coefficients $\mathbf{c}_i$ and the covariance $\Sigma_i$. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Gaussian prior assumption.** Let $\mathbf{d} = (d_x, d_y)$ be the displacement of part $i$ from its anchor position. Assume: $$ P(\mathbf{d}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\vert \Sigma_i \vert}}\, \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mathbf{d}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Take negative logarithm.** $$ \begin{aligned} d_i(\mathbf{d}) &\equiv -\log P(\mathbf{d}) \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mathbf{d} + \tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi)^2 \vert \Sigma_i \vert \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Drop constants** (they do not affect argmax). $$ d_i(\mathbf{d}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mathbf{d} + \text{const.} $$ **Step 4 — Write the precision matrix as $2\mathbf{c}_i$** (a convention from Felzenszwalb et al.). Hence: $$ \Sigma_i^{-1} \;=\; 2 \mathbf{c}_i \;=\; 2 \begin{pmatrix} c_{i,xx} & c_{i,xy} \\ c_{i,xy} & c_{i,yy} \end{pmatrix} $$ and $$ d_i(d_x, d_y) \;=\; c_{i,xx}\, d_x^2 \;+\; 2\,c_{i,xy}\, d_x d_y \;+\; c_{i,yy}\, d_y^2 $$ **Step 5 — Relationship to covariance.** Inverting: $$ \Sigma_i \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{c}_i^{-1} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2(c_{i,xx}c_{i,yy} - c_{i,xy}^2)} \begin{pmatrix} c_{i,yy} & -c_{i,xy} \\ -c_{i,xy} & c_{i,xx} \end{pmatrix} $$ The **standard deviation** of part scatter along the principal axis is $\sigma = \sqrt{\Sigma_{ii}}$. Small $c_{i,xx}, c_{i,yy}$ → loose part; large values → rigid part. *(7 marks: 1 for prior, 2 for derivation, 2 for matrix form, 1 for constants, 1 for physical interpretation.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain the **role of the Latent SVM** in training a Deformable Part Model. Why is the standard SVM formulation insufficient, and how does LSVM overcome this? **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]** **Model Answer:** In Part-Based Models, the **locations of parts are not annotated** in training data — only a bounding box around the whole object is provided. The true part positions $z_i$ are therefore **latent (hidden) variables** that must be inferred jointly with the model weights. **Why standard SVM fails:** The standard SVM objective $\min_w \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 + C \sum \max(0, 1 - y_i\, w^\top \phi(x_i))$ requires the feature vector $\phi(x_i)$ to be **fully observable**. With latent parts, the feature depends on unknown $z$, and the objective becomes non-convex: $$ \min_w \; \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 + C \sum_i \max_z \max\big(0,\; 1 - y_i\, w^\top \phi(x_i, z)\big) $$ **LSVM solution (CCCP / SVM-struct style):** Alternate between two steps: 1. **Inner maximization:** Fix $w$, find the best $z^* = \arg\max_z w^\top \phi(x_i, z)$ using the GDT-based inference. 2. **Outer minimization:** Treat $z^*$ as fixed and solve the standard convex SVM problem. This is repeated until $w$ converges to a (local) optimum. **Hard-negative mining** is performed in parallel: false detections in the current model are re-fed as negatives. **Result:** LSVM learns both filter weights $F_i$ and deformation coefficients $\mathbf{c}_i$ from weakly-supervised data, making the DPM trainable directly from bounding-box annotations. *(7 marks: 2 for latent variable issue, 2 for non-convexity, 2 for CCCP alternation, 1 for hard-negative mining.)* **(b)** A Part-Based Model with 3 parts has the following parameters. Compute the total score at the configuration $L = (l_0, l_1, l_2, l_3)$ and decide whether the configuration is accepted. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar)]** Given data: - Root score $m_0 = 2.1$ - Part 1: $m_1 = 1.0$, displacement $\mathbf{d}_1 = (2, 0)$, $\mathbf{c}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05\end{pmatrix}$ - Part 2: $m_2 = 0.8$, displacement $\mathbf{d}_2 = (0, 3)$, $\mathbf{c}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.04\end{pmatrix}$ - Part 3: $m_3 = 1.5$, displacement $\mathbf{d}_3 = (1, 1)$, $\mathbf{c}_3 = \begin{pmatrix}0.02 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.03\end{pmatrix}$ - Bias $b = 0.3$ **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Deformation cost for part 1:** $$ d_1 = 0.05 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2 \cdot 0 + 0.05 \cdot 0^2 = 0.20 + 0 + 0 = 0.20 $$ **Step 2 — Deformation cost for part 2:** $$ d_2 = 0.04 \cdot 0^2 + 2(0.01)(0)(3) + 0.04 \cdot 3^2 = 0 + 0 + 0.36 = 0.36 $$ **Step 3 — Deformation cost for part 3:** $$ d_3 = 0.02 \cdot 1^2 + 0 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 + 0.03 \cdot 1^2 = 0.02 + 0 + 0.03 = 0.05 $$ **Step 4 — Total appearance sum:** $$ \sum m_i = 2.1 + 1.0 + 0.8 + 1.5 = 5.4 $$ **Step 5 — Total deformation sum:** $$ \sum d_i = 0.20 + 0.36 + 0.05 = 0.61 $$ **Step 6 — Total score:** $$ \begin{aligned} S(I, L) &= \sum m_i - \sum d_i + b \\ &= 5.4 - 0.61 + 0.3 \\ &= 5.09 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Decision rule:** Since $S = 5.09 \geq 0$, **the configuration is accepted as a valid detection** with high confidence. *(7 marks: 1 mark per deformation cost, 1 for appearance sum, 1 for total, 1 for decision.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $2\times$ resolution factor** when computing part displacements. Part filters operate at $\ell+1$ (twice the resolution of root at $\ell$); the offset formula is $(x_i, y_i) - 2(x_0, y_0) - p_i$, not $(x_i, y_i) - (x_0, y_0) - p_i$. This is the **#1 cause of part-placement errors** in KTU scripts. > > 2. **Sign confusion in deformation cost.** $d_i$ is *subtracted* in the score, so positive $d_i$ *reduces* the score. Some students incorrectly add it. > > 3. **Conflating HOG and SIFT.** HOG uses dense overlapping cells with block normalization; SIFT uses sparse keypoints. PBMs use HOG. Mixing them costs marks. > > 4. **Skipping the bias term $b$.** Forgetting $b$ changes the decision threshold and can flip accept/reject outcomes. > > 5. **Treating Latent SVM as standard SVM.** Examiners specifically test whether you understand *why* it is non-convex and *how* CCCP alternation resolves it. > > 6. **Ignoring mixture components.** For multi-pose organs (e.g., heart in long-axis vs. short-axis view), a single-mixture model collapses. Always state whether the model uses $K=1$ or $K>1$ mixtures. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Part-Based Models (KTU PEEBT745 Module 3)** > > - **Definition (1-liner):** A Part-Based Model represents an object as a constellation of local part filters connected by quadratic-cost spatial springs anchored to a global root filter. > - **Score function:** $S = \sum m_i - \sum d_i + b$, where $m_i = \langle F_i, \Phi(I, l_i) \rangle$ and $d_i = \mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{c}_i \mathbf{d}$. > - **Displacement formula:** $\mathbf{d} = (x_i, y_i) - 2(x_0, y_0) - p_i$ (the $2\times$ accounts for part's higher resolution). > - **Gaussian interpretation:** $d_i$ is the negative log of a Gaussian prior with precision $2\mathbf{c}_i = \Sigma_i^{-1}$. > - **HOG features:** $8 \times 8$ cells, 9-bin orientation histograms, L2 block normalization. > - **Generalized Distance Transform:** Used for $O(N)$ inference of best part locations given root; based on separable 1-D DP sweeps. > - **Latent SVM training:** CCCP alternation between (a) inferring latent part positions via GDT and (b) solving a convex SVM with fixed latents; combined with hard-negative mining. > - **Mixture models:** Multiple components $\mathcal{M}_k$ with view-specific filters, selected by $\arg\max_k (S_k - \alpha_k)$. > - **Number of parts:** Typically $n = 6$–$12$ for a medical organ DPM. > - **Bias $b$:** Learnt scalar; threshold of 0 means $S \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ detection. > - **NMS threshold:** IoU $\geq 0.5$ is the KTU-accepted standard. > - **Medical applications:** Cardiac LV landmarking, prostate TRUS segmentation, lung nodule CT detection, vertebra localization, cephalometric landmarking, tumour co-segmentation. > - **Advantages:** Robust to occlusion, intra-class variation; interpretable; compositional. > - **Limitations:** Star/topology restricted; needs sufficient training data; HOG may underperform deep features on very large datasets. > - **Key reference paper:** Felzenszwalb, McAllester & Ramanan, *"A Discriminatively Trained, Multiscale, Deformable Part Model,"* CVPR 2008 — the canonical DPM paper cited in nearly every KTU answer. > - **Pictorial structure origin:** Fischler & Elschlager, 1973 — the original spring-template model. > - **Distance transform origin:** Felzenszwalb & Huttenlocher, *"Distance Transforms of Sampled Functions,"* 2004 — the algorithmic enabler. > - **Equation to memorise cold (most likely 14-mark derivation):** $S = \sum_i \langle F_i, \Phi(I, l_i) \rangle - \sum_i d_i(\mathbf{d}_i) + b$ with $d_i = \mathbf{d}_i^T \mathbf{c}_i \mathbf{d}_i$.

topics.title: Using a Non-parametric Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using a Non-parametric Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Medical Imaging and Image Processing Techniques**, a **Non-parametric Model** is a statistical estimation framework in which the underlying probability distribution of pixel intensities, regional features, or image texture descriptors is **not assumed to follow any predefined parametric family** (e.g., Gaussian, Rayleigh, or Rician distributions). Instead, the model is constructed **entirely from the observed data samples** using density estimation techniques such as the **Parzen Window (Kernel Density Estimation)** method, the **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) estimator**, or **histogram-based approximations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A non-parametric model in medical image analysis is a data-driven statistical representation that estimates the probability density function (PDF) of image features *directly* from the sample set, without enforcing a fixed functional form on the distribution. It is particularly useful when the true underlying distribution of medical image intensities is unknown, multimodal, or non-Gaussian (e.g., in ultrasound, MRI with bias fields, or PET images).

Mathematically, a non-parametric model seeks to estimate $p(\mathbf{x})$ for a feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ such that no fixed number of parameters fully characterises the model — the effective parameter count **grows with the number of samples $N$**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Flexible Clay vs. The Pre-cast Mould"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to model the height distribution of patients arriving at a hospital.
>
> - A **parametric model** (like a Gaussian) is like buying a *pre-cast mould* — you assume all patients' heights follow a bell curve, and you only estimate the mean and standard deviation. If the real distribution is skewed or bimodal (e.g., men and women mixed), the mould fails to fit.
> - A **non-parametric model** is like working with a *flexible lump of clay* — you place a small mound of clay (a kernel) at the location of each actual patient in your dataset, and the total shape of the clay naturally conforms to whatever pattern the data exhibits. The more patients you add, the more refined and accurate the model becomes.
>
> In medical imaging, this flexibility is critical because tissue intensity distributions are notoriously irregular and cannot be forced into rigid parametric forms.

## 1.3 Why Non-parametric Models Matter in Medical Imaging

Medical images exhibit characteristics that make parametric assumptions unreliable:

| Imaging Characteristic | Parametric Assumption Failure | Non-parametric Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **MRI bias field inhomogeneity** | Gaussian assumption is violated | Adaptive kernel width per region |
| **Ultrasound speckle noise** | Rayleigh assumption is only approximate | Captures heavy tails naturally |
| **Multi-modal tissue classes** (e.g., GM, WM, CSF) | Single Gaussian cannot model multiple peaks | Naturally captures multi-modality |
| **Lesion heterogeneity** | Unknown distribution shape | Data-driven estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations**
> - **Parametric Model**: Fixed functional form $p(\mathbf{x}\vert\theta)$, finite parameter vector $\theta$ (e.g., $\mu, \sigma^2$ for Gaussian).
> - **Non-parametric Model**: No fixed functional form; effective parameter count grows with sample size $N$ (e.g., bandwidth $h$ in Parzen, neighbour count $k$ in k-NN).
> - **Semi-parametric Model**: Mixture of the two (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models with unknown $K$).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

- **Kernel Function** $\kappa(\cdot)$: A symmetric, non-negative weighting function placed at each sample (commonly Gaussian, Epanechnikov, or uniform).
- **Bandwidth / Window Width** $h$: The smoothing parameter controlling the spread of the kernel. Critical hyperparameter.
- **Volume of Region** $V$: The hypervolume in feature space enclosing the sample neighbourhood.
- **Voxel**: The 3D analogue of a pixel, representing a volume element in a medical image.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework of Non-parametric Density Estimation

The objective of a non-parametric model is to construct an estimate $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$ of the true underlying probability density function $p(\mathbf{x})$ from a finite set of $N$ i.i.d. samples $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ drawn from the unknown distribution. The two dominant techniques are:

### 2.1.1 Parzen Window Estimation (Fixed Kernel, Variable Sample Count)

The Parzen window estimator places a kernel function $\kappa(\cdot)$ of width $h$ (bandwidth) at **every** sample point and sums their contributions. The core operational logic proceeds as follows:

- **Step 1 (Region Definition)**: Define a small hypercube $\mathcal{R}$ of side length $h$ in $d$-dimensional feature space, centred at the query point $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Step 2 (Sample Counting)**: Count the number $k_N$ of training samples $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}$ that fall inside $\mathcal{R}$.
- **Step 3 (Density Estimation)**: Estimate the density as the ratio of probability mass to region volume:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{V} \kappa\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

- **Step 4 (Kernel Properties)**: The kernel must satisfy $\kappa(\mathbf{u}) \geq 0$ and $\int \kappa(\mathbf{u})\,d\mathbf{u} = 1$, ensuring that $\hat{p}$ is itself a valid probability density.

### 2.1.2 k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) Estimation (Variable Kernel, Fixed Sample Count)

Instead of fixing the volume and counting samples, the k-NN estimator **fixes the sample count $k$** and lets the volume $V(\mathbf{x})$ adapt to the local density:

- **Step 1 (Distance Computation)**: For a query point $\mathbf{x}$, compute the distance to every training sample.
- **Step 2 (Neighbour Selection)**: Identify the $k$-th nearest neighbour and the enclosing hypersphere radius $r_k(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Step 3 (Volume Computation)**: Compute the volume $V_k(\mathbf{x}) = c_d \cdot r_k(\mathbf{x})^d$, where $c_d$ is the volume of a unit hypersphere in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Step 4 (Density Estimation)**: Compute $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{k}{N \cdot V_k(\mathbf{x})}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why k-NN adapts to local density**: In dense regions, $r_k$ is small (high local density → high $\hat{p}$); in sparse regions, $r_k$ is large (low local density → low $\hat{p}$). This makes k-NN excellent for **lesion boundary detection** in heterogeneous tumours.

## 2.2 Bandwidth Selection: The Critical Trade-off

The choice of bandwidth $h$ controls the **bias-variance trade-off**:

- **Small $h$ (undersmoothing)**: Low bias, high variance — the estimate is spiky and overfits to individual samples.
- **Large $h$ (oversmoothing)**: High bias, low variance — the estimate is overly smooth and hides structure.
- **Optimal $h$**: Minimises the **Mean Integrated Squared Error (MISE)**: $\text{MISE}(h) = \mathbb{E}\left[\int \left(\hat{p}_h(\mathbf{x}) - p(\mathbf{x})\right)^2 d\mathbf{x}\right]$.

A common reference rule is **Silverman's Rule of Thumb**:

$$h_{\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{4 \hat{\sigma}^5}{3N}\right)^{1/5} \approx 1.06 \, \hat{\sigma} \, N^{-1/5}$$

where $\hat{\sigma}$ is the standard deviation of the samples.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-parametric models underpin several critical medical imaging applications:

- **Image Segmentation**: Level-set methods and snakes use non-parametric intensity models to evolve contours.
- **Texture Classification**: Haralick features are statistically summarised via non-parametric histograms in tumour grading.
- **Anomaly Detection**: Statistical deviations from the non-parametric model of normal tissue flag potential lesions.
- **Image Registration**: Mutual information between joint histograms (non-parametric) drives multimodal MRI-PET registration.
- **CAD Systems**: Computer-Aided Detection pipelines rely on non-parametric feature distributions for classification.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** | **Application in Medical Imaging** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parzen Window Estimator | $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{Nh^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$ | $h$ in intensity units; $N$ dimensionless | Tissue intensity distribution modelling |
| k-NN Density Estimator | $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{k}{N \cdot c_d \cdot r_k(\mathbf{x})^d}$ | $r_k$ in feature units | Lesion boundary detection |
| Gaussian Kernel | $\kappa(u) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right)$ | Dimensionless, integrates to **1** | Smooth density estimation |
| Epanechnikov Kernel (optimal MISE) | $\kappa(u) = \frac{3}{4}(1 - u^2)$ for $\vert u \vert \leq 1$ | Optimal asymptotic MISE | Computationally efficient |
| Uniform (Rectangular) Kernel | $\kappa(u) = \frac{1}{2}$ for $\vert u \vert \leq 1$ | Simplest kernel; discontinuous | Histogram approximation |
| Silverman's Bandwidth | $h \approx 1.06 \hat{\sigma} N^{-1/5}$ | Univariate Gaussian reference | Rule-of-thumb starting point |
| Mean Integrated Squared Error | $\text{MISE}(h) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\int \left(\hat{p}_h - p\right)^2 d\mathbf{x}\right]$ | Squared probability density | Bandwidth optimisation criterion |
| Bias-Variance Asymptotics | $\text{Bias} \sim h^2$, $\text{Variance} \sim \frac{1}{Nh^d}$ | As $N \to \infty$, $h \to 0$ | Convergence guarantee |
| Histogram Estimator | $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{k_i}{N \cdot \Delta_i}$ for bin $i$ | $k_i$ = count in bin $i$, $\Delta_i$ = bin width | Simplest non-parametric method |
| Convergence Rate (Parzen) | $\text{MISE} = O\!\left(N^{-4/(d+4)}\right)$ | Slows exponentially with $d$ | **Curse of dimensionality** warning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Symbols**
> When using $\vert u \vert$ or $\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i \vert$ in derivations, use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., `\vert u \vert`) to prevent markdown table parsing errors. This is a common pitfall in KTU answer script formatting when tables are used in the main body.

## 2.5 The Curse of Dimensionality — A Critical Limation

For a $d$-dimensional feature space, the required sample size to maintain a given estimation accuracy grows **exponentially** with $d$. This is known as the **curse of dimensionality**:

$$N_{\text{required}} \propto h^{-d}$$

In medical imaging, this motivates:
- **Dimensionality reduction** (PCA, t-SNE) before non-parametric modelling.
- **Feature selection** to keep $d$ small (typically $d \leq 5$ for reliable Parzen estimation).
- **Use of semi-parametric models** (e.g., GMMs) when $d$ is large.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Parzen Window Density Estimator

We begin with the foundational derivation that every KTU student must be able to reproduce. Given $N$ i.i.d. samples $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ from an unknown PDF $p(\mathbf{x})$, we wish to derive the Parzen window estimator.

**Step 1: Probability Mass Inside a Region**

The probability that a sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ falls inside a small region $\mathcal{R}$ of volume $V$ centred at $\mathbf{x}$ is given by:

$$P = \int_{\mathcal{R}} p(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u}$$

**Step 2: Empirical Estimate via Sample Counting**

Since we do not know $p(\mathbf{u})$, we estimate $P$ empirically. If $k_N$ out of $N$ samples fall inside $\mathcal{R}$, the empirical estimate is:

$$P \approx \frac{k_N}{N}$$

**Step 3: Local Density Approximation**

Assuming $\mathcal{R}$ is small and $p(\mathbf{u})$ is approximately constant within $\mathcal{R}$, we have:

$$P = \int_{\mathcal{R}} p(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u} \approx p(\mathbf{x}) \cdot V$$

**Step 4: Solve for $p(\mathbf{x})$**

Rearranging the approximation from Step 3:

$$p(\mathbf{x}) \approx \frac{P}{V} \approx \frac{k_N}{N \cdot V}$$

This yields the **histogram-like estimator**:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{k_N}{N \cdot V}$$

**Step 5: Continuous Kernel Formulation**

To obtain a smooth estimator, replace the discontinuous region indicator with a continuous kernel $\kappa(\cdot)$. The indicator function $I(\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R})$ is replaced by $\frac{1}{V}\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$, where $h$ is the bandwidth controlling the effective volume $V = h^d$. The smoothed estimator becomes:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{h^d}\,\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

**Step 6: Verification of Validity (Integrates to Unity)**

We verify that $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$ is a valid PDF by integrating over all space:

$$\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \int \kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right) d\mathbf{x}$$

Applying the substitution $\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}$ so that $d\mathbf{x} = h^d\, d\mathbf{u}$:

$$= \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} h^d \int \kappa(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (1) = 1$$

This confirms $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$ is a properly normalised PDF. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Bias and Variance of the Parzen Estimator

To understand the asymptotic behaviour, we compute the bias and variance of $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$.

**Step 1: Compute the Mean (Expected Value)**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{E}\!\left[\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)\right]$$

Since the samples are i.i.d. from $p(\cdot)$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \int \kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u}$$

$$= \int \frac{1}{h^d} \kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u} = (p * \kappa_h)(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\kappa_h(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{h^d}\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{v}}{h}\right)$ and $*$ denotes convolution.

**Step 2: Taylor Expansion of $p(\mathbf{x})$**

Assume $p(\mathbf{x})$ is twice continuously differentiable. Expanding around $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{u}$:

$$p(\mathbf{u}) = p(\mathbf{x}) + (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x})^T \nabla p(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{H}_p(\mathbf{x}) (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x}) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the Mean**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \int \kappa_h(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}) \left[ p(\mathbf{x}) + (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x})^T \nabla p(\mathbf{x}) + \cdots \right] d\mathbf{u}$$

Using the substitution $\mathbf{v} = \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}}{h}$:

$$= p(\mathbf{x}) \int \kappa(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v} + h \nabla p(\mathbf{x})^T \int (-\mathbf{v}) \kappa(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v} + \frac{h^2}{2} \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2) + \cdots$$

where $\mathbf{M}_2 = \int \mathbf{v} \mathbf{v}^T \kappa(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v}$ is the second-moment matrix of the kernel. Using $\int \kappa(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v} = 1$ and assuming a symmetric kernel with zero mean, $\int \mathbf{v}\kappa(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v} = 0$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = p(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{h^2}{2} \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

**Step 4: Bias**

The bias of the estimator is therefore:

$$\text{Bias}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] - p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{h^2}{2} \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$

**Step 5: Variance**

Using $\text{Var}(\hat{p}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{p}^2] - (\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}])^2$ and the independence of samples:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \frac{1}{N h^{2d}} \int \kappa^2\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{u}) d\mathbf{u} - \frac{1}{N}(\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}])^2$$

For small $h$, the leading term dominates:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{N h^d} \int \kappa^2(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v} = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}) \, R(\kappa)}{N h^d}$$

where $R(\kappa) = \int \kappa^2(\mathbf{v}) d\mathbf{v}$ is the *roughness* of the kernel.

**Step 6: Mean Integrated Squared Error (MISE)**

Combining the squared bias and variance integrated over feature space:

$$\text{MISE}(h) = \int \text{Bias}^2[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] d\mathbf{x} + \int \text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] d\mathbf{x}$$

$$\text{MISE}(h) \approx \frac{h^4}{4} \int [\text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2)]^2 d\mathbf{x} + \frac{R(\kappa)}{N h^d}$$

**Step 7: Optimal Bandwidth**

Differentiating MISE with respect to $h$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{MISE})}{dh} = h^3 \int [\text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2)]^2 d\mathbf{x} - \frac{d R(\kappa)}{N h^{d+1}} = 0$$

Solving for the optimal $h$:

$$h_{\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{d R(\kappa)}{N \int [\text{tr}(\mathbf{H}_p \mathbf{M}_2)]^2 d\mathbf{x}}\right)^{1/(d+4)}$$

**Step 8: Optimal Convergence Rate**

Substituting $h_{\text{opt}}$ back into MISE gives the optimal rate:

$$\text{MISE}_{\text{opt}} = O\!\left(N^{-4/(d+4)}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Interpretation for KTU Examinations**
> The exponent $\frac{4}{d+4}$ decreases as dimensionality $d$ increases. For $d=1$, the rate is $N^{-4/5}$; for $d=10$, it drops to $N^{-4/14} = N^{-2/7}$. This formally demonstrates the curse of dimensionality.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: 1D Parzen Estimation

Consider $N = 5$ intensity samples: $\{10, 12, 14, 16, 18\}$ from a hypothetical tissue ROI. Estimate $\hat{p}(13)$ using a Gaussian kernel with $h = 2$.

**Step 1: Compute the kernel argument for each sample**

$$u_i = \frac{13 - x_i}{h}$$

- For $x_1 = 10$: $u_1 = \frac{13-10}{2} = 1.5$
- For $x_2 = 12$: $u_2 = \frac{13-12}{2} = 0.5$
- For $x_3 = 14$: $u_3 = \frac{13-14}{2} = -0.5$
- For $x_4 = 16$: $u_4 = \frac{13-16}{2} = -1.5$
- For $x_5 = 18$: $u_5 = \frac{13-18}{2} = -2.5$

**Step 2: Evaluate Gaussian kernel $\kappa(u) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp(-u^2/2)$**

- $\kappa(1.5) = 0.3989 \cdot e^{-1.125} = 0.3989 \cdot 0.3247 = 0.1295$
- $\kappa(0.5) = 0.3989 \cdot e^{-0.125} = 0.3989 \cdot 0.8825 = 0.3521$
- $\kappa(-0.5) = \kappa(0.5) = 0.3521$
- $\kappa(-1.5) = \kappa(1.5) = 0.1295$
- $\kappa(-2.5) = 0.3989 \cdot e^{-3.125} = 0.3989 \cdot 0.0439 = 0.0175$

**Step 3: Sum the kernel values**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} \kappa(u_i) = 0.1295 + 0.3521 + 0.3521 + 0.1295 + 0.0175 = 0.9807$$

**Step 4: Apply the Parzen formula**

$$\hat{p}(13) = \frac{1}{N \cdot h} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \kappa(u_i) = \frac{1}{5 \cdot 2} \cdot 0.9807 = \frac{0.9807}{10} = 0.0981$$

**Final Answer**: $\hat{p}(13) \approx 0.0981$ intensity$^{-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Non-parametric Model Pipeline in Medical Imaging

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of a non-parametric modelling pipeline as deployed in a typical medical image analysis system (e.g., tumour segmentation from MRI).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Image Input] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Noise Filtering]
    B --> B2[Intensity Normalisation]
    B --> B3[Bias Field Correction]
    B1 --> C[Region of Interest Extraction]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Feature Space Construction]
    D --> D1[Pixel Intensity]
    D --> D2[Local Texture Descriptors]
    D --> D3[Gradient Magnitude]
    D1 --> E[Sample Set Assembly]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Bandwidth Selection}
    F --> F1[Silverman Rule h equals 1.06 sigma N power negative 1 over 5]
    F --> F2[Cross Validation h tuned empirically]
    F --> F3[Plugin Bandwidth h data driven]
    F1 --> G[Kernel Function Choice]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Gaussian Kernel]
    G --> G2[Epanechnikov Kernel]
    G --> G3[Uniform Kernel]
    G1 --> H[Non Parametric Density Estimation]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Parzen Window Estimator]
    H --> J[k Nearest Neighbour Estimator]
    I --> K[Density Map Generation]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Downstream Task Selection]
    L --> L1[Image Segmentation]
    L --> L2[Anomaly Detection]
    L --> L3[Tissue Classification]
    L --> L4[CADx Decision Support]
    L1 --> M[Clinical Output]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    M --> N[Validation and Quality Assurance]
    N --> O[Final Diagnostic Report]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Decision Flow: Choosing Between Parzen and k-NN

The following Mermaid diagram helps in selecting the appropriate non-parametric technique for a given medical imaging task.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Non Parametric Model Selection] --> Q1{Is sample size N small less than 50}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is feature dimensionality d less than 3}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is local density variation high}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Rec1[Use Parzen with Gaussian Kernel]
    Q2 -->|No| Rec2[Apply PCA or feature selection first]
    Rec2 --> Rec1
    Q3 -->|Yes| Rec3[Use k Nearest Neighbour Estimator]
    Q3 -->|No| Rec4{Is computational budget tight}
    Rec4 -->|Yes| Rec5[Use Parzen with Uniform Kernel]
    Rec4 -->|No| Rec6[Use Parzen with Epanechnikov Kernel]
    Rec1 --> End[End: Selected Non Parametric Model]
    Rec3 --> End
    Rec5 --> End
    Rec6 --> End
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Density Estimation Visualisation

The following Mermaid diagram presents a conceptual plot representation showing how the Parzen window estimator builds up a smooth density estimate from discrete samples.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Sample Point x1] --> K1[Kernel centered at x1]
    P2[Sample Point x2] --> K2[Kernel centered at x2]
    P3[Sample Point x3] --> K3[Kernel centered at x3]
    P4[Sample Point x4] --> K4[Kernel centered at x4]
    P5[Sample Point x5] --> K5[Kernel centered at x5]
    K1 --> Sum[Weighted Sum of Kernels]
    K2 --> Sum
    K3 --> Sum
    K4 --> Sum
    K5 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Smooth Density Estimate p hat of x]
    H[Bandwidth h] --> K1
    H --> K2
    H --> K3
    H --> K4
    H --> K5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**
> Each sample point $x_i$ contributes a "bump" of shape determined by the kernel $\kappa$ and width controlled by $h$. The sum of all bumps yields the smooth density estimate $\hat{p}(x)$. Larger $h$ → wider, smoother bumps; smaller $h$ → narrow, spiky bumps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Non-parametric Model in the Context of Medical Image Analysis

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer**:
A non-parametric model in medical image analysis is a statistical estimation technique that derives the probability density function of image features (such as pixel intensities, texture descriptors, or ROI statistics) **directly from observed data**, without assuming any predefined functional form for the underlying distribution. The number of effective parameters grows with the sample size $N$, allowing the model to adapt flexibly to complex, multimodal, or non-Gaussian intensity distributions commonly encountered in MRI, CT, ultrasound, and PET imaging. Common examples include the Parzen window estimator and the k-nearest neighbour (k-NN) density estimator.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Parametric and Non-parametric Density Estimation

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:

| **Aspect** | **Parametric Estimation** | **Non-parametric Estimation** |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution form | Assumed (e.g., Gaussian) | Not assumed |
| Number of parameters | Fixed, finite | Grows with sample size $N$ |
| Computational cost | Low | Higher |
| Data efficiency | Efficient with small $N$ | Requires larger $N$ |
| Flexibility | Limited by assumed form | Highly flexible |
| Example in medical imaging | Gaussian model of brain tissue | Parzen window of tumour intensity |
| Risk | Model misspecification | Curse of dimensionality |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 3]`

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Parzen window method** of non-parametric density estimation. Discuss the role of the kernel function and the bandwidth parameter. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **bias** of the Parzen window estimator and show that the **optimal bandwidth** scales as $h_{\text{opt}} = O(N^{-1/(d+4)})$, leading to an MISE convergence rate of $O(N^{-4/(d+4)})$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition**: The Parzen window estimator (also called kernel density estimation, KDE) estimates the PDF $p(\mathbf{x})$ at a query point $\mathbf{x}$ by placing a kernel function $\kappa(\cdot)$ of bandwidth $h$ at each of the $N$ training samples and summing their contributions:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

**Step-by-step procedure**:
1. Choose a query point $\mathbf{x}$ in the feature space.
2. For each sample $\mathbf{x}_i$, compute the normalised distance $\mathbf{u}_i = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)/h$.
3. Evaluate the kernel $\kappa(\mathbf{u}_i)$ at this distance.
4. Sum the kernel values over all $N$ samples and divide by $N h^d$.

**Role of the kernel function**:
- It defines the shape of contribution from each sample.
- It must be non-negative, symmetric, and integrate to unity.
- Common choices: Gaussian $\kappa(u) = (1/\sqrt{2\pi})e^{-u^2/2}$, Epanechnikov, uniform.

**Role of the bandwidth $h$**:
- Controls the smoothness of the density estimate.
- Small $h$ → under-smoothed (spiky) estimate; large $h$ → over-smoothed estimate.
- Bandwidth selection is the central challenge; trade-off between bias and variance.

**Application in medical imaging**: Parzen windows are used in statistical parametric mapping (SPM) for brain activation maps, in lesion intensity modelling, and in non-parametric segmentation of heterogeneous tumours.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1**: The mean of the Parzen estimator is the convolution of $p(\mathbf{x})$ with the rescaled kernel:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \int \frac{1}{h^d}\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{u}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{u})\, d\mathbf{u}$$

**Step 2**: Taylor-expand $p(\mathbf{u})$ around $\mathbf{x}$:

$$p(\mathbf{u}) = p(\mathbf{x}) + (\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x})^T \nabla p(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{H}_p(\mathbf{x})(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{x}) + \cdots$$

**Step 3**: Substitute and use kernel properties $\int \kappa = 1$ and $\int \mathbf{v}\kappa(\mathbf{v})d\mathbf{v} = 0$ (zero mean):

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = p(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{h^2}{2}\,\mu_2(\kappa)\, \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) + O(h^4)$$

**Step 4**: Bias is the leading error term:

$$\text{Bias}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \frac{h^2}{2}\,\mu_2(\kappa)\, \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) + O(h^4)$$

**Step 5**: Squared bias integrated over $\mathbb{R}^d$:

$$\int \text{Bias}^2 d\mathbf{x} \approx \frac{h^4}{4}\mu_2^2(\kappa) \int [\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})]^2 d\mathbf{x} \propto h^4$$

**Step 6**: Variance asymptotically:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{p(\mathbf{x})\,R(\kappa)}{N h^d} \implies \int \text{Var}\, d\mathbf{x} \propto \frac{1}{N h^d}$$

**Step 7**: MISE combines both:

$$\text{MISE}(h) \approx C_1 h^4 + \frac{C_2}{N h^d}$$

**Step 8**: Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{MISE})}{dh} = 4 C_1 h^3 - \frac{d C_2}{N h^{d+1}} = 0$$

$$\implies h_{\text{opt}}^4 = \frac{d\,C_2}{4 N C_1} \implies h_{\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{d\,C_2}{4 N C_1}\right)^{1/(d+4)} = O(N^{-1/(d+4)})$$

**Step 9**: Substituting $h_{\text{opt}}$ back:

$$\text{MISE}_{\text{opt}} \propto \left(\frac{1}{N}\right)^{4/(d+4)} = O(N^{-4/(d+4)})$$

**Interpretation**: As feature dimension $d$ increases, the convergence rate slows dramatically. For $d=1$, rate is $N^{-0.8}$; for $d=10$, rate is $N^{-0.286}$. This quantifies the curse of dimensionality. **Q.E.D.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) density estimation** method. Compare it with the Parzen window method in terms of adaptive vs. fixed bandwidth, computational cost, and suitability for medical image segmentation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A radiologist extracts intensity values from a tumour ROI in an MRI scan, yielding the following 1D samples: $X = \{80, 85, 88, 90, 92, 130, 135\}$. Estimate the density at $x = 100$ using: **(i)** Parzen window with Gaussian kernel and $h = 5$; **(ii)** k-NN with $k = 2$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**k-NN density estimation procedure**:
1. Choose a query point $\mathbf{x}$ and the neighbour count $k$.
2. Compute the Euclidean distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to every training sample.
3. Identify the $k$-th nearest neighbour and its distance $r_k(\mathbf{x})$.
4. Compute the enclosing hypersphere volume $V_k = c_d r_k^d$ where $c_d = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)}$.
5. Estimate density: $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{k}{N \cdot V_k(\mathbf{x})}$.

**Comparison with Parzen window**:

| **Aspect** | **Parzen Window** | **k-NN Estimator** |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | Fixed globally ($h$ constant) | Adaptive (volume varies with local density) |
| Sample count in region | Variable | Fixed ($k$ constant) |
| Computational cost | $O(Nd)$ per query | $O(Nd + N\log N)$ per query (with KD-tree) |
| Sparsity handling | Poor (fixed $h$ misses structure) | Excellent (adapts to local density) |
| Boundary effects | Suffers from edge bias | Better preservation of local structure |
| Parameter sensitivity | Critical $h$ choice | Critical $k$ choice |
| Medical image segmentation | Good for homogeneous tissues | Superior for heterogeneous lesions |

**Suitability for medical image segmentation**: k-NN is preferred for **heterogeneous tumour segmentation** where local density varies significantly (e.g., necrotic core vs. enhancing rim). Parzen is preferred for **statistical parametric mapping** in brain imaging where global smoothness is desired.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Parzen window with Gaussian kernel, $h = 5$, query at $x = 100$**:

For each sample $x_i$, compute $u_i = (100 - x_i)/5$ and $\kappa(u_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-u_i^2/2}$:

| $x_i$ | $u_i = (100 - x_i)/5$ | $\kappa(u_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 80 | 4.0 | 0.0001338 |
| 85 | 3.0 | 0.004432 |
| 88 | 2.4 | 0.022371 |
| 90 | 2.0 | 0.053991 |
| 92 | 1.6 | 0.110921 |
| 130 | -6.0 | $6.076 \times 10^{-9}$ |
| 135 | -7.0 | $1.487 \times 10^{-11}$ |

Sum of kernel values: $\sum \kappa(u_i) \approx 0.0001 + 0.0044 + 0.0224 + 0.0540 + 0.1109 + 0 + 0 = 0.1918$

$$\hat{p}(100) = \frac{1}{N h}\sum \kappa(u_i) = \frac{0.1918}{7 \times 5} = \frac{0.1918}{35} = 0.00548 \text{ intensity}^{-1}$$

**(ii) k-NN with $k = 2$**:

Step 1: Distances from $x = 100$ to all samples:
- $|100 - 80| = 20$, $|100 - 85| = 15$, $|100 - 88| = 12$, $|100 - 90| = 10$, $|100 - 92| = 8$, $|100 - 130| = 30$, $|100 - 135| = 35$.

Step 2: Sort: $8, 10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 35$. The 2nd nearest distance is $r_2 = 10$.

Step 3: Volume in 1D ($c_1 = 2$): $V_2 = 2 \times 10 = 20$.

Step 4: Apply the formula:

$$\hat{p}(100) = \frac{k}{N V_2} = \frac{2}{7 \times 20} = \frac{2}{140} = 0.01429 \text{ intensity}^{-1}$$

**Comparative interpretation**: The k-NN estimate (0.01429) is higher than the Parzen estimate (0.00548) because $x=100$ is in a relatively sparse region between the cluster $\{80, 85, 88, 90, 92\}$ and the cluster $\{130, 135\}$. The k-NN method adapts to this local sparsity by using a larger effective volume, while the Gaussian Parzen kernel's tails decay rapidly, yielding a smaller density estimate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Missing normalisation factor $1/(Nh)$**: Students frequently write $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum \kappa(\cdot)$ **without dividing by $h$** or $h^d$. This loses one mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Kernel symmetry confusion**: When computing $(x - x_i)$, the sign matters! Always centre the kernel at the sample $x_i$, not at the query $x$.
> 3. **Volume formula for k-NN**: Forgetting that $V_k = c_d r_k^d$ where $c_d$ depends on dimension ($c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = \pi$, $c_3 = 4\pi/3$).
> 4. **Bandwidth convergence statement**: The optimal bandwidth scales as $h \propto N^{-1/(d+4)}$, **not** $N^{-1/5}$ in general — the $N^{-1/5}$ formula is only valid for $d = 1$.
> 5. **Confusing bias and variance trade-off**: Many students reverse the relationship — small $h$ → high variance (NOT high bias); large $h$ → high bias (NOT high variance).
> 6. **Units of density**: The estimated density has units of (intensity)$^{-1}$ in 1D, (intensity)$^{-2}$ in 2D, etc. Forgetting units loses the dimensional-analysis mark.
> 7. **Confusing non-parametric with semi-parametric**: A Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with unknown number of components $K$ is **semi-parametric**, not non-parametric.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist: Using a Non-parametric Model**

- **Definition**: A non-parametric model estimates $p(\mathbf{x})$ without assuming a fixed functional form; parameter count grows with $N$.
- **Parzen window formula**: $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N h^d}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\kappa\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$.
- **k-NN density formula**: $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{k}{N c_d r_k(\mathbf{x})^d}$ where $c_d = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)}$.
- **Kernel properties**: $\kappa(\mathbf{u}) \geq 0$, $\int \kappa(\mathbf{u})d\mathbf{u} = 1$, symmetric about origin.
- **Common kernels**: Gaussian, Epanechnikov (optimal MISE), uniform (simplest).
- **Bandwidth trade-off**: $h \uparrow$ → bias $\uparrow$, variance $\downarrow$; $h \downarrow$ → bias $\downarrow$, variance $\uparrow$.
- **Silverman's rule**: $h \approx 1.06 \hat{\sigma} N^{-1/5}$ (univariate, Gaussian reference).
- **Optimal bandwidth**: $h_{\text{opt}} = O(N^{-1/(d+4)})$.
- **Optimal MISE rate**: $O(N^{-4/(d+4)})$ — formal proof of curse of dimensionality.
- **Parzen uses fixed $h$, variable sample count**; k-NN uses **fixed $k$, variable volume**.
- **Curse of dimensionality**: Required $N$ grows exponentially with $d$ for constant accuracy.
- **Application domains**: Statistical parametric mapping, lesion segmentation, texture classification, anomaly detection, multimodal registration.
- **Normalisation check**: $\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = 1$ must always hold.
- **Computational cost**: Parzen = $O(Nd)$ per query; k-NN = $O(N\log N)$ with KD-tree.
- **Edge effects**: Parzen suffers from boundary bias; k-NN handles edges better.
- **Always state the kernel name and bandwidth** when presenting an estimate.
- **For 1D**: $c_1 = 2$; **2D**: $c_2 = \pi$; **3D**: $c_3 = 4\pi/3$.
- **Convergence guarantee**: $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \to p(\mathbf{x})$ as $N \to \infty$ and $h \to 0$ with $Nh^d \to \infty$.
- **MISE decomposition**: MISE = $\int \text{Bias}^2 d\mathbf{x} + \int \text{Var} d\mathbf{x}$.
- **Clinical relevance**: Non-parametric models are essential when tissue intensity distributions are non-Gaussian (ultrasound, MRI bias, multi-modal lesions).
- **Last-line exam tip**: Always mention **bias-variance trade-off**, **curse of dimensionality**, and **kernel validity properties** in long-answer questions to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physically-Based Shape Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physically-Based Shape Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physically-Based Shape Models** are computational geometry models that represent an object boundary (a 1-D curve or 2-D surface) as a **deformable elastic body** governed by the laws of **classical mechanics** and **continuum mechanics**. The model evolves under the influence of an *energy functional* (or equivalently a *force balance equation*) until it converges to the boundary of a region of interest in an image.

The most canonical instance is the **Active Contour Model (Snake)** introduced by **Kass, Witkin & Terzopoulos (1988)**, in which a parameterized contour $\mathbf{v}(s) = (x(s), y(s))$ minimizes a total energy:

$$
E_{\text{snake}} \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} \left[\, E_{\text{int}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr) \;+\; E_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr) \,\right] ds
$$

The internal energy encodes **elasticity** and **rigidity**, while the external energy is derived from **image features** (typically edges). The two foundational paradigms are:

| Paradigm | Governing Principle | Typical Use |
|----------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Energy Minimization (Variational)** | Euler–Lagrange PDE on a functional | Active contours (snakes), level sets |
| **Force-Balance (Dynamic / Newtonian)** | $m\ddot{\mathbf{v}} + \gamma \dot{\mathbf{v}} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{int}} + \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ | Mass-spring meshes, FEM, balloon snakes |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Magnetic Rubber Band:**
> Imagine stretching a small **rubber-band loop** over a coin lying on a table. The band has two natural "wishes":
> 1. **Stay smooth and round** (this is the *internal energy* — elasticity + bending stiffness).
> 2. **Snap onto the coin's edge** (this is the *external energy* — attraction to image edges, which act like a magnetic force).
> The **final shape** of the band, where it perfectly hugs the coin, is the equilibrium between these two competing tendencies. *Now give the band tiny "mass" and "friction" so it doesn't oscillate forever — and you have a physics-based shape model!*

In a medical image, the "coin" becomes the boundary of an organ, tumour, vessel wall, or cartilage. The "rubber band" is the deformable contour. This makes the technique exceptionally well-suited for **organ segmentation, tumour delineation, and tracking of moving structures** in CT, MRI, and ultrasound.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Parameters

- **$\alpha(s)$** — First-order *elasticity* weight (resists stretching). Typical range: **0.01 – 0.5**.
- **$\beta(s)$** — Second-order *rigidity* weight (resists bending). Typical range: **0.01 – 0.5**.
- **$\gamma$** — *Damping coefficient* (dynamic models only), removes kinetic energy. Typical range: **0.1 – 1.0**.
- **$\mu$** — *Mass density* along the contour.
- **$\sigma$** — Standard deviation of the Gaussian pre-smoothing kernel applied to the image before computing gradients. Typical: **0.5 – 3.0 pixels**.
- **$k$** — Hookean spring constant in mass-spring models.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Snake Energy Functional

The total energy is split into **Internal Energy** (a *regularizer* that enforces smoothness, derived from the **mechanics of an elastic rod**) and **External Energy** (a *data-attachment* term derived from the image).

### 2.1.1 Internal Energy — Elasticity + Rigidity

$$
E_{\text{int}} \;=\; \alpha(s)\,\bigl\|\mathbf{v}'(s)\bigr\|^{2} \;+\; \beta(s)\,\bigl\|\mathbf{v}''(s)\bigr\|^{2}
$$

- The **first term** $\alpha\lVert\mathbf{v}'\rVert^{2}$ is the **membrane / stretching energy** — analogous to Hooke's law $F = -k\Delta x$ for a string. It penalizes stretching between adjacent control points.
- The **second term** $\beta\lVert\mathbf{v}''\rVert^{2}$ is the **thin-plate / bending energy** — analogous to the bending moment of a beam ($M = EI\,\kappa$). It penalizes sharp curvature.
- Together they make the contour behave like a flexible **spline on an elastic foundation**.

### 2.1.2 External Energy — Image-Data Attraction

For a grayscale image $I(x,y)$ the canonical external energy is the *negative* of a smoothed gradient magnitude:

$$
E_{\text{ext}}(x,y) \;=\; -\,\bigl\|\nabla\bigl[G_{\sigma}(x,y)\,*\,I(x,y)\bigr]\bigr\|^{2}
$$

The negative sign means the contour is *attracted* to high-gradient pixels (edges). A region-based variant uses:

$$
E_{\text{ext}} \;=\; -\,\bigl(I(x,y) - \mu_{\text{obj}}\bigr)^{2}
$$

which pulls the contour toward pixels matching a target mean intensity $\mu_{\text{obj}}$ (used in the **Chan–Vese** model).

## 2.2 From Energy to Force — Euler–Lagrange Equation

Minimization of $E_{\text{snake}}$ with respect to $\mathbf{v}(s)$ yields the **Euler–Lagrange PDE** (assuming constant $\alpha,\beta$):

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''(s) \;-\; \beta\,\mathbf{v}''''(s) \;-\; \nabla E_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr) \;=\; \mathbf{0}\;}
$$

Physically, this is a **static force balance**:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{elastic}} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{bending}} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{image}} \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

## 2.3 Dynamic / Lagrangian Extension (Physics-Based)

To make the contour behave like a physical object with inertia and friction, the gradient-descent flow is upgraded to a **Lagrangian dynamic equation**:

$$
\boxed{\;\mu\,\frac{\partial^{2}\mathbf{v}}{\partial t^{2}} \;+\; \gamma\,\frac{\partial\mathbf{v}}{\partial t} \;-\; \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2}\mathbf{v}}{\partial s^{2}} \;+\; \beta\,\frac{\partial^{4}\mathbf{v}}{\partial s^{4}} \;=\; \mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s,t)\bigr)\;}
$$

- $\mu\,\ddot{\mathbf{v}}$ — inertia term (mass × acceleration, Newton's 2nd law).
- $\gamma\,\dot{\mathbf{v}}$ — viscous damping (energy dissipation).
- $-\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''$ — internal elastic force.
- $+\beta\,\mathbf{v}''''$ — internal bending force.
- $\mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}} = -\nabla E_{\text{ext}}$ — image-derived potential force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This second-order PDE is the *exact* continuum-mechanics equation for a **viscoelastic Timoshenko beam** lying in an image-derived potential well. By tuning $\mu$ and $\gamma$ the engineer controls **convergence speed vs. stability** — identical to designing a critically-damped mechanical oscillator.

## 2.4 Mass-Spring Deformable Models

A 2-D surface is discretized into a **mesh of mass points** connected by **massless springs** of rest length $L_0$ and stiffness $k$.

For a node $i$ connected to neighbour $j$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{spring}}^{(i)} \;=\; \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} k_{ij}\,\bigl(\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert - L_{0,ij}\bigr)\,\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{ij}
$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{ij}$ is the unit vector from $j$ to $i$. Adding damping and image forces, the full Newton's law for node $i$ is:

$$
m_{i}\,\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \;=\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{spring}}^{(i)} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{damp}}^{(i)} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}^{(i)}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Snake total energy | $E = \int (E_{\text{int}} + E_{\text{ext}})\,ds$ | Variational objective |
| 2 | Internal energy | $\alpha\lVert\mathbf{v}'\rVert^{2} + \beta\lVert\mathbf{v}''\rVert^{2}$ | Elasticity + bending |
| 3 | Edge-based external energy | $E_{\text{ext}} = -\lVert\nabla(G_{\sigma}*I)\rVert^{2}$ | Attracts to edges |
| 4 | Region-based external energy | $E_{\text{ext}} = -(I - \mu_{\text{obj}})^{2}$ | Attracts to target intensity |
| 5 | Euler–Lagrange (static) | $\alpha\mathbf{v}'' - \beta\mathbf{v}'''' - \nabla E_{\text{ext}} = 0$ | Force balance |
| 6 | Dynamic snake PDE | $\mu\mathbf{v}_{tt} + \gamma\mathbf{v}_{t} - \alpha\mathbf{v}_{ss} + \beta\mathbf{v}_{ssss} = \mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}}$ | Viscoelastic beam |
| 7 | Gradient descent update | $\mathbf{v}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{v}^{(t)} - \eta\bigl(\alpha\mathbf{v}'' - \beta\mathbf{v}'''' - \nabla E_{\text{ext}}\bigr)$ | Iterative solver |
| 8 | Discrete elasticity (1-D) | $v_{i+1} - 2v_{i} + v_{i-1}$ | Finite-difference $\mathbf{v}''$ |
| 9 | Discrete bending (1-D) | $v_{i+2} - 4v_{i+1} + 6v_{i} - 4v_{i-1} + v_{i-2}$ | Finite-difference $\mathbf{v}''''$ |
| 10 | Spring force (Hooke) | $\mathbf{F}_{ij} = k_{ij}(\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert - L_{0,ij})\,\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{ij}$ | Linear elastic mesh edge |
| 11 | Damping force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{damp}} = -c_{i}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ | Viscous dissipation |
| 12 | Balloon force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{balloon}} = k_{b}\,\mathbf{n}(s)$ | Outward normal pressure |
| 13 | GVF field | $\mathbf{g} = \nabla\mathbf{u}$, where $\mathbf{u}$ minimizes $\mu\lVert\nabla\mathbf{u}\rVert^{2} + \lVert\mathbf{u} - \nabla f\rVert^{2}$ | Edge-attraction vector field |
| 14 | Level-set evolution | $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + F\,\lVert\nabla\phi\rVert = 0$ | Implicit curve evolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equations (5), (6), (7), and (10) are the *highest-yield* derivations. Memorize them with their physical interpretations; long-answer questions in ESE almost always test one of these.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler–Lagrange Equation for the Snake

We minimize the functional:

$$
J[\mathbf{v}] \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} L\bigl(s, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}', \mathbf{v}''\bigr)\,ds
$$

where the Lagrangian is

$$
L \;=\; \alpha(s)\,\lVert\mathbf{v}'\rVert^{2} \;+\; \beta(s)\,\lVert\mathbf{v}''\rVert^{2} \;+\; E_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr)
$$

### Step 1 — Write the First Variation

A perturbation $\mathbf{v}(s) \to \mathbf{v}(s) + \epsilon\,\boldsymbol{\eta}(s)$ with $\boldsymbol{\eta}(0)=\boldsymbol{\eta}(1)=0$ gives:

$$
\left.\frac{dJ}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} \left[\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta} \;+\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}' \;+\; \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}''\,\right]ds
$$

**Explanation:** We differentiate under the integral sign with respect to the small perturbation $\epsilon$. The chain rule produces three terms because $L$ depends on $\mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{v}'$, and $\mathbf{v}''$.

### Step 2 — Integration by Parts (Twice)

The middle term:

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}'\,ds \;=\; \left[\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\right]_{0}^{1} \;-\; \int_{0}^{1} \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\right)\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\,ds
$$

The boundary term vanishes because $\boldsymbol{\eta}(0)=\boldsymbol{\eta}(1)=0$.

The third term requires **two integrations by parts**:

$$
\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}''\,ds \;=\; \left[\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}' - \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right)\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\right]_{0}^{1} \;+\; \int_{0}^{1} \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right)\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\,ds
$$

All boundary terms vanish (assuming $\boldsymbol{\eta}$ and $\boldsymbol{\eta}'$ vanish at endpoints).

### Step 3 — Collect Terms

Combining the three results:

$$
\left.\frac{dJ}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} \left[\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}} \;-\; \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\right) \;+\; \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right)\,\right]\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\,ds
$$

**Explanation:** The integration-by-parts procedure moves all derivatives off $\boldsymbol{\eta}$ and onto the Lagrangian terms — a standard variational maneuver.

### Step 4 — Stationarity Condition

Since the perturbation $\boldsymbol{\eta}$ is arbitrary, the **Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations** requires the bracketed integrand to vanish identically:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}} \;-\; \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\right) \;+\; \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right) \;=\; \mathbf{0}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Partial Derivatives

Compute each piece:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}} \;=\; \nabla E_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'} \;=\; 2\alpha(s)\,\mathbf{v}'(s) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\right) \;=\; 2\alpha'\,\mathbf{v}' + 2\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''} \;=\; 2\beta(s)\,\mathbf{v}''(s) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right) \;=\; 2\beta''\,\mathbf{v}'' + 4\beta'\,\mathbf{v}''' + 2\beta\,\mathbf{v}''''
$$

**Explanation:** The derivatives of $E_{\text{ext}}$ with respect to $\mathbf{v}'$ and $\mathbf{v}''$ are zero because $E_{\text{ext}}$ depends only on the position $\mathbf{v}$, not on its derivatives.

### Step 6 — Final Assembled Form (General)

$$
\nabla E_{\text{ext}} \;-\; 2\alpha'\,\mathbf{v}' - 2\alpha\,\mathbf{v}'' \;+\; 2\beta''\,\mathbf{v}'' + 4\beta'\,\mathbf{v}''' + 2\beta\,\mathbf{v}'''' \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

### Step 7 — Simplification for Constant $\alpha,\beta$

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants (the standard KTU assumption), then $\alpha' = \alpha'' = \beta' = \beta'' = 0$:

$$
\nabla E_{\text{ext}} \;-\; 2\alpha\,\mathbf{v}'' \;+\; 2\beta\,\mathbf{v}'''' \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

Dividing by 2:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''(s) \;-\; \beta\,\mathbf{v}''''(s) \;-\; \nabla E_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}(s)\bigr) \;=\; \mathbf{0}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of each term (high-yield for viva/ESE):**
> - $\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''$ → **Elastic restoring force** (resists stretching of the contour, like a spring).
> - $-\beta\,\mathbf{v}''''$ → **Bending rigidity** (resists sharp curvature, like a stiff beam).
> - $-\nabla E_{\text{ext}}$ → **Image force** pulling the contour toward edges (potential gradient).
> - The equation states: **Internal elastic + bending forces = External image force** (static equilibrium).

## 3.2 Discretization for Numerical Implementation

Replace the contour derivatives with central finite differences evaluated at control point $i$:

$$
\mathbf{v}''(s_{i}) \;\approx\; \mathbf{v}_{i+1} - 2\mathbf{v}_{i} + \mathbf{v}_{i-1}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}''''(s_{i}) \;\approx\; \mathbf{v}_{i+2} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i+1} + 6\mathbf{v}_{i} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \mathbf{v}_{i-2}
$$

Substituting into the static Euler–Lagrange equation:

$$
\alpha\bigl(\mathbf{v}_{i+1} - 2\mathbf{v}_{i} + \mathbf{v}_{i-1}\bigr) \;-\; \beta\bigl(\mathbf{v}_{i+2} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i+1} + 6\mathbf{v}_{i} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \mathbf{v}_{i-2}\bigr) \;-\; \mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v}_{i}) \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

where $\mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v}_{i}) = -\nabla E_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v}_{i})$.

**Explanation:** Each control point's position is now a linear combination of its neighbours plus a non-linear image force — a sparse 5-point stencil.

## 3.3 Iterative Gradient-Descent Solution (Dynamic Snake)

The dynamic PDE is solved by explicit time-stepping. Define the *internal force operator*:

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i} \;=\; \alpha\bigl(\mathbf{v}_{i+1} - 2\mathbf{v}_{i} + \mathbf{v}_{i-1}\bigr) \;-\; \beta\bigl(\mathbf{v}_{i+2} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i+1} + 6\mathbf{v}_{i} - 4\mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \mathbf{v}_{i-2}\bigr)
$$

Then for each iteration $t$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{i}^{(t+1)} \;=\; \mathbf{v}_{i}^{(t)} \;-\; \Delta t\,\bigl[\,\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v}_{i}^{(t)} \;+\; \mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}}\bigl(\mathbf{v}_{i}^{(t)}\bigr)\,\bigr]
$$

**Explanation:** We move each control point a small step in the direction opposite to the net force (steepest descent on energy). $\Delta t$ is the time-step; stability requires $\Delta t \leq 0.25$ for the explicit scheme.

## 3.4 Derivation of Single-Node Force Balance (Mass-Spring Model)

For node $i$ with mass $m_i$ at position $\mathbf{x}_i$, Newton's second law gives:

$$
m_{i}\,\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \;=\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} \mathbf{F}_{\text{spring}}^{(ij)} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{damp}}^{(i)} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}^{(i)}
$$

The Hookean spring force from neighbour $j$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{spring}}^{(ij)} \;=\; k_{ij}\,\bigl(\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert - L_{0,ij}\bigr)\,\frac{\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j}}{\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert}
$$

**Explanation:** The factor $(\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert - L_{0,ij})$ is the *stretch* — deviation from rest length. The unit-vector term $\frac{\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j}}{\lVert\mathbf{x}_{i}-\mathbf{x}_{j}\rVert}$ directs the force along the spring axis.

Add the damping force $\mathbf{F}_{\text{damp}}^{(i)} = -c_{i}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{i}$ and external image force $\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}^{(i)} = -\nabla E_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{x}_{i})$:

$$
\boxed{\;m_{i}\,\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \;=\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} k_{ij}\!\left(\lVert \mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j} \rVert - L_{0,ij}\right)\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{ij} \;-\; c_{i}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{i} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}^{(i)}\;}
$$

**Explanation:** The left side is mass-times-acceleration (inertia). The right side has three physical contributions: (1) *internal* spring forces that try to restore the mesh to its rest configuration, (2) *viscous damping* that dissipates kinetic energy, and (3) *external* image-derived force that drives the mesh toward the target boundary.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Active Contour vs. Mass-Spring)

| Aspect | Active Contour (Snake) | Mass-Spring Mesh |
|--------|------------------------|------------------|
| Representation | Parametric curve $\mathbf{v}(s)$ | Discrete node grid $\{\mathbf{x}_{i}\}$ |
| Governing equation | Variational (Euler–Lagrange) | Newtonian ($m\ddot{\mathbf{x}} = \sum \mathbf{F}$) |
| Topological change | Difficult (parameterization fixed) | Possible via remeshing or level-set hybrid |
| Computational cost | $O(N)$ per iteration | $O(N \cdot k)$ per iteration (k = connectivity) |
| Best for | Tubular / convex organs | Soft tissue, cardiac, brain surfaces |
| Inertia/damping | Optional (dynamic snake) | Native to formulation |
| Medical example | Vessel wall in angiography | Left-ventricle surface in 3-D echo |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Physics-Based Shape Model Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image I x y] --> B[Gaussian Smoothing G sigma convolve I]
    B --> C[Compute Image Gradient nabla I]
    C --> D[Build Edge Map and External Energy E ext]
    D --> E[Initialize Contour v0 s or Mesh x i 0]
    E --> F[Compute Internal Force F int alpha v doubleprime minus beta v quadrupleprime]
    G[Image Potential Force F ext equals minus nabla E ext] --> F
    F --> H{Force Balance F int plus F ext approx zero?}
    H -->|No| I[Update Control Points v i t plus 1 equals v i t minus delta t times net force]
    I --> J[Apply Damping gamma v dot]
    J --> F
    H -->|Yes| K[Converged Boundary Output]
    K --> L[Post processing: smoothing, hole filling, label map]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop from `F` → `H` → `I` → `J` → `F` is the **iterative solver** — equivalent to time-stepping the Lagrangian PDE. Termination occurs when the net force per node drops below a small threshold $\varepsilon$.

## 4.2 Force-Interaction Schematic on a 1-D Contour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Snake1[Snake Segment]
        P1((v i minus 1)) --- P2((v i)) --- P3((v i plus 1))
    end
    P2 -->|Elastic force alpha v doubleprime| P2
    P2 -->|Bending force minus beta v quadrupleprime| P2
    P2 -->|Image force minus nabla E ext| P2
    Edge[(Image Edge Boundary)] -.->|Attraction| P2
    P2 -->|Damping minus gamma v dot| P2
```

**Explanation:** At any instant, three "physical" forces act on a single control point `v_i`: the elastic pull from its neighbours, the bending moment from the second derivative, and the magnetic-like pull from the underlying image edge. Damping removes energy so the system settles into equilibrium.

## 4.3 Energy-vs-Iteration Convergence Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Iteration 0: E equals E max] --> B[Iteration 50: E decreasing rapidly]
    B --> C[Iteration 200: E decreasing slowly]
    C --> D[Iteration 500: E approx E min: converged]
    D --> E[Output final contour]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The energy curve is **monotonically non-increasing** (gradient descent) and exhibits the classic exponential-decay shape $E(t) \approx E_{\min} + (E_0 - E_{\min})e^{-t/\tau}$, where the time-constant $\tau$ is controlled by damping $\gamma$.

## 4.4 Mass-Spring Mesh — Node-Force Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1((Node x1)) ---|k12, L012| N2((Node x2))
    N2 ---|k23, L023| N3((Node x3))
    N3 ---|k34, L034| N4((Node x4))
    N2 ---|k24, L024| N4
    N1 -->|F spring 12| N2
    N2 -->|F spring 23| N3
    N3 -->|F spring 34| N4
    N2 -->|F spring 24| N4
    N2 -->|F ext equals minus nabla E ext| N2
    N2 -->|F damp equals minus c v dot| N2
    N2 -->|Newton: m x doubledot equals sum F| N2
```

**Explanation:** Every node obeys Newton's 2nd law. The mesh behaves like a **tunable trampoline** stretched over the image — springs keep it smooth, edges pull it down, and damping kills oscillations.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the active contour model (snake) and list its three energy components. State the role of each component in 2 lines.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **active contour** is a deformable parameterized curve $\mathbf{v}(s) = (x(s), y(s))$, $s \in [0,1]$, that minimizes a total energy functional to fit image features.
>
> The three energy components are:
> 1. **Continuity / Elasticity energy** $\alpha\lVert\mathbf{v}'\rVert^{2}$ — penalizes stretching between control points; keeps the contour taut.
> 2. **Curvature / Bending energy** $\beta\lVert\mathbf{v}''\rVert^{2}$ — penalizes sharp bends; keeps the contour smooth.
> 3. **External energy** $E_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v}(s))$ — derived from the image (e.g., negative gradient magnitude); pulls the contour toward edges.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for list, 1 for roles.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Distinguish between parametric active contours and geometric (level-set) active contours. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Parametric (Snake) | Geometric (Level-Set) |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Explicit $\mathbf{v}(s)$ | Implicit $\phi(x,y,t) = 0$ |
| Topology | Fixed (no splitting) | Free (auto-handles splits/merges) |
| Numerical | Finite differences on control points | PDE on a fixed grid |
| **Advantage** | Fast, easy to constrain with prior shape | Naturally handles topological changes |

*(3 marks — 1 per correct row plus 1 for the advantage pair.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B is a *full 14-mark* question. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. The model answers below are written in the style of a *high-scoring* KTU answer script.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q A (a).** Derive the Euler–Lagrange equation for the active contour model starting from the energy functional

$$
E_{\text{snake}} = \int_{0}^{1}\!\bigl[\,\alpha\lVert\mathbf{v}'\rVert^{2} + \beta\lVert\mathbf{v}''\rVert^{2} + E_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v})\,\bigr]\,ds
$$

Explain the physical meaning of every term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the variational problem (1 mark).**
We seek $\mathbf{v}(s)$ that minimizes $E_{\text{snake}}$. Introduce a perturbation $\mathbf{v} \to \mathbf{v} + \epsilon\boldsymbol{\eta}$ with $\boldsymbol{\eta}(0)=\boldsymbol{\eta}(1)=0$. The first variation must vanish:

$$
\left.\frac{dE_{\text{snake}}}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the integrand (1 mark).**

$$
\frac{d}{d\epsilon}L\bigl(\mathbf{v}+\epsilon\boldsymbol{\eta}, \mathbf{v}'+\epsilon\boldsymbol{\eta}', \mathbf{v}''+\epsilon\boldsymbol{\eta}''\bigr)\bigg|_{\epsilon=0} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta} + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}' + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}''
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate by parts (1 mark).**
The $\boldsymbol{\eta}'$ and $\boldsymbol{\eta}''$ terms are moved onto the $L$-derivatives (boundary terms vanish):

$$
\int_{0}^{1}\!\left[\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}} - \frac{d}{ds}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'}\right) + \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''}\right)\right]\cdot\boldsymbol{\eta}\,ds = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute partial derivatives and substitute (2 marks).**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = \nabla E_{\text{ext}}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}'} = 2\alpha\mathbf{v}', \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{v}''} = 2\beta\mathbf{v}''
$$

For constant $\alpha,\beta$:

$$
\frac{d}{ds}(2\alpha\mathbf{v}') = 2\alpha\mathbf{v}'', \quad \frac{d^{2}}{ds^{2}}(2\beta\mathbf{v}'') = 2\beta\mathbf{v}''''
$$

**Step 5 — Final equation and physical meaning (2 marks).**

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''(s) - \beta\,\mathbf{v}''''(s) - \nabla E_{\text{ext}}(\mathbf{v}(s)) = \mathbf{0}\;}
$$

| Term | Physical meaning |
|---|---|
| $\alpha\,\mathbf{v}''$ | **Elastic restoring force** — Hookean stretching resistance between control points. |
| $-\beta\,\mathbf{v}''''$ | **Bending rigidity** — penalizes high curvature; keeps contour smooth. |
| $-\nabla E_{\text{ext}}$ | **Image force** — potential gradient pulling the contour toward image edges. |

**Engineering interpretation:** The contour is in static equilibrium when the *internal* mechanical forces ($\alpha$ elastic + $\beta$ bending) exactly balance the *external* image-derived potential force.

---

**Q A (b).** With a neat block diagram, describe the iterative algorithm for snake evolution. Discuss why **mass ($\mu$)** and **damping ($\gamma$)** are introduced in the physics-based dynamic extension. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Algorithm description in pseudocode (3 marks).**

```
Initialize contour v[i], i=1..N
Initialize parameters alpha, beta, gamma, mu, dt
REPEAT
    For each control point i:
        F_int[i] = alpha*(v[i+1] - 2v[i] + v[i-1])
                 - beta *(v[i+2] - 4v[i+1] + 6v[i] - 4v[i-1] + v[i-2])
        F_ext[i] = -grad(E_ext) at v[i]
        F_damp[i] = -gamma * (v[i] - v_prev[i]) / dt       // numerical damping
        a[i] = (F_int[i] + F_ext[i] + F_damp[i]) / mu       // Newton's 2nd law
        v_new[i] = v[i] + dt * (v[i] - v_prev[i]) / dt + dt*dt*a[i] // Verlet/Euler
    END
    Convergence test: max |v_new - v| < epsilon
UNTIL converged
```

**Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks).** *(See Section 4.1 — same block diagram.)*

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Image Pre-process | Gaussian smoothing + gradient |
| Initialize | Place initial contour near ROI |
| Internal Force | Compute $\alpha\mathbf{v}'' - \beta\mathbf{v}''''$ |
| External Force | Compute $-\nabla E_{\text{ext}}$ |
| Damping | Apply $-\gamma\dot{\mathbf{v}}$ |
| Update | Integrate Newton's equation |
| Convergence Check | $\lVert\mathbf{v}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{v}^{(t)}\rVert < \varepsilon$ |

**Step 3 — Why mass and damping? (2 marks).**

- **Mass ($\mu$):** Introduces **inertia** — the contour "carries momentum" across iterations. It smooths the trajectory and helps the contour **escape shallow local minima** (especially in noisy medical images). However, large $\mu$ can cause overshoot, so it must be tuned.
- **Damping ($\gamma$):** Provides a **viscous drag** $-\gamma\dot{\mathbf{v}}$ that **dissipates kinetic energy** and prevents infinite oscillation. Critical for **stability** of the explicit time-stepping scheme; mathematically, the system is **overdamped** when $\gamma$ is large, **underdamped** when small. The optimal choice gives **critical damping** — fastest convergence without oscillation.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q B (a).** Discuss the **three major limitations** of the classical snake model and explain how (i) the **balloon-force** model and (ii) the **Gradient Vector Flow (GVF)** model overcome them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Limitations of the classical snake (3 marks).**

1. **Initialisation sensitivity:** The contour must be placed *close* to the true boundary; otherwise it collapses onto spurious local minima.
2. **Poor convergence to concavities:** The external force field $\mathbf{f}_{\text{ext}} = -\nabla E_{\text{ext}}$ has a **small capture range** and points *away* from the boundary inside concave regions — the contour cannot "turn the corner" into indentations.
3. **Numerical instability / parameter tuning:** Explicit time-stepping demands small $\Delta t$; $\alpha,\beta$ require careful balancing; the 4th-order $\mathbf{v}''''$ term is ill-conditioned.

**Step 2 — Balloon-force model (Cohen, 1991) — 2 marks.**

Add a constant **outward (or inward) pressure** force:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{int}} \;+\; \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} \;+\; k_{b}\,\mathbf{n}(s)
$$

where $\mathbf{n}(s)$ is the unit normal to the contour and $k_b$ a scalar weight.

- **Solves limitation (1):** The constant pressure **inflates/deflates** the contour, expanding its capture range so it can find the boundary from a more distant initialisation.
- **Caveat:** The contour may *cross over* the boundary if $k_b$ is too large — a classic KTU viva question.

**Step 3 — Gradient Vector Flow (GVF) — Xu & Prince, 1998 — 2 marks.**

Replace the external force with a **smooth vector field** $\mathbf{g}(x,y) = (u(x,y), v(x,y))$ that minimizes:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{GVF}} \;=\; \iint \mu\,\lVert\nabla u\rVert^{2} + \lVert\mathbf{g} - \nabla f\rVert^{2}\,\lvert\nabla f\rvert^{2}\,dx\,dy
$$

where $f$ is the edge map.

- **Solves limitations (1) and (2):** The vector field has a **large capture range** (slow decay far from edges) and **rotates into concavities**, allowing the snake to crawl into indented boundaries such as the **inter-ventricular grooves of the heart** in cardiac MRI.

---

**Q B (b).** Explain the **mass-spring deformable model** for 2-D shape representation. Derive the **force balance equation** for a single node and discuss how image forces are incorporated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark).**
A 2-D region is discretized as a triangular/quadrilateral mesh of **point masses** connected by **massless linear springs** of rest length $L_0$ and stiffness $k$. The mesh deforms under image forces while internal springs resist deformation, mimicking a **stretchy fabric**.

**Step 2 — Spring force derivation (2 marks).**
