KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2] **Define Graph Cuts in the context of pre-segmented medical images. List any two pre-segmentation methods used as the front-end of region-based graph cuts.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Graph Cuts is a combinatorial optimization technique for binary image segmentation that finds a globally optimal partition of an image into object and background by minimizing an energy functional over a weighted graph. In the context of pre-segmented images, the image is first over-segmented into homogeneous atomic regions (super-pixels), and a graph is constructed where nodes represent these regions, and edges represent both inter-region adjacency and connections to two terminal nodes (Source $s$ and Sink $t$). The minimum s-t cut on this graph gives the refined segmentation. Two pre-segmentation methods used: 1. **Watershed Transform** — Treats the image gradient as a topographic surface and floods it from local minima, producing closed, connected regions. (1.5 marks) 2. **SLIC (Simple Linear Iterative Clustering)** — A k-means variant that clusters pixels in a 5-D space (Lab color + xy position) to produce compact, uniform super-pixels. (1.5 marks) *(Alternative valid methods: Mean-Shift, Felzenszwalb-Huttenlocher graph-based segmentation, normalized cuts.)* --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Model] **State the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem. Why is this theorem essential for graph-cut based segmentation?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956):** For any flow network with a source $s$ and a sink $t$, the maximum value of a flow from $s$ to $t$ equals the minimum capacity of any s-t cut. Mathematically: $$ \max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{\mathcal{C}(S,T)} \text{cap}(\mathcal{C}) $$ (2 marks) **Why essential for segmentation:** The theorem establishes the fundamental duality that allows us to convert the combinatorial problem of *minimizing an energy function* $E(\mathbf{x})$ over exponentially many labelings $2^R$ into the *polynomial-time* problem of finding a maximum flow. Since the cut capacity $\text{cap}(\mathcal{C})$ is mathematically equivalent to the energy $E(\mathbf{x})$ (up to an additive constant), minimizing the cut automatically minimizes the energy, giving us the **global optimum** in polynomial time. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified] ### (a) Derive the energy functional for graph-cut based segmentation on pre-segmented images. Clearly state the role of the data term and the smoothness (Potts) term, and explain why the Potts model is submodular. **(7 Marks)** ### (b) A $5 \times 5$ ultrasound image is over-segmented into 3 regions with mean intensities $\mu_1 = 30$, $\mu_2 = 90$, $\mu_3 = 110$. Object model: $\mu_O = 100, \sigma_O = 8$. Background model: $\mu_B = 35, \sigma_B = 12$. Inter-region boundary weights: $w_{12} = 6$, $w_{23} = 4$, $w_{13} = 9$. Construct the s-t graph and determine the optimal labeling using the min-cut principle. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### **Part (a) — Derivation of the Energy Functional (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Define the labeling problem (1 mark).** Given $R$ pre-segmented regions $\{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_R\}$ obtained from an initial over-segmentation, we seek a binary labeling $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_R)$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. Conventionally, $x_i = 1$ means region $r_i$ belongs to the object and $x_i = 0$ means it belongs to the background. **Step 2 — State the general energy functional (2 marks).** The total energy to be minimized is: $$ E(\mathbf{x}) = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{R} D_i(x_i)}_{\text{Data Term } E_D} + \underbrace{\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{N}} V_{ij}(x_i, x_j)}_{\text{Smoothness Term } E_S} $$ **Step 3 — Data term $D_i(x_i)$ (2 marks).** The data term $D_i(x_i)$ is the cost of assigning label $x_i$ to region $r_i$, derived from the negative log-likelihood of the region's appearance under each class: $$ D_i(x_i) = -\ln P(\text{appearance of } r_i \mid \text{class } x_i) $$ For a Gaussian appearance model with class mean $\mu_{x_i}$ and variance $\sigma_{x_i}^2$: $$ D_i(x_i) = \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_{x_i})^2}{2\sigma_{x_i}^2} + \text{const} $$ This term measures how well the region's intensity matches the expected intensity of the object or background. **Step 4 — Smoothness term and Potts model (1 mark).** The smoothness term penalizes label discontinuities between neighboring regions. The Potts model is the simplest binary case: $$ V_{ij}(x_i, x_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x_i = x_j \\ w_{ij} & \text{if } x_i \neq x_j \end{cases} $$ The weight $w_{ij}$ is large when regions $r_i$ and $r_j$ have similar boundaries (should be merged) and small when there is a strong edge between them (should be separated). **Step 5 — Submodularity of the Potts model (1 mark).** A binary energy $V_{ij}$ is **submodular** if and only if: $$ V_{ij}(0, 0) + V_{ij}(1, 1) \leq V_{ij}(0, 1) + V_{ij}(1, 0) $$ For the Potts model, this becomes $0 + 0 \leq w_{ij} + w_{ij} = 2w_{ij}$, which is always true for $w_{ij} \geq 0$. Submodularity is the **mathematical requirement** that ensures the energy can be minimized exactly by a graph cut. Without it, only approximations like $\alpha$-expansion are possible. --- ### **Part (b) — Numerical Graph Construction and Min-Cut (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Compute the data term for each region (2 marks).** Using $D_i(x_i) = \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_{x_i})^2}{2\sigma_{x_i}^2}$: For $r_1$ ($\mu_1 = 30$): $$ D_1(0) = \frac{(30 - 35)^2}{2(12)^2} = \frac{25}{288} \approx 0.087 $$ $$ D_1(1) = \frac{(30 - 100)^2}{2(8)^2} = \frac{4900}{128} = 38.28 $$ For $r_2$ ($\mu_2 = 90$): $$ D_2(0) = \frac{(90 - 35)^2}{2(12)^2} = \frac{3025}{288} \approx 10.50 $$ $$ D_2(1) = \frac{(90 - 100)^2}{2(8)^2} = \frac{100}{128} \approx 0.78 $$ For $r_3$ ($\mu_3 = 110$): $$ D_3(0) = \frac{(110 - 35)^2}{2(12)^2} = \frac{5625}{288} \approx 19.53 $$ $$ D_3(1) = \frac{(110 - 100)^2}{2(8)^2} = \frac{100}{128} \approx 0.78 $$ **Step 2 — Construct the graph $\mathcal{G}$ (1.5 marks).** Nodes: $\{s, t, r_1, r_2, r_3\}$. T-edges (terminal edges): | Edge | Capacity | |------|----------| | $(s, r_1)$ | $D_1(0) = 0.087$ | | $(r_1, t)$ | $D_1(1) = 38.28$ | | $(s, r_2)$ | $D_2(0) = 10.50$ | | $(r_2, t)$ | $D_2(1) = 0.78$ | | $(s, r_3)$ | $D_3(0) = 19.53$ | | $(r_3, t)$ | $D_3(1) = 0.78$ | N-edges (neighbor edges): $(r_1, r_2)$ with $w_{12} = 6$, $(r_2, r_3)$ with $w_{23} = 4$, $(r_1, r_3)$ with $w_{13} = 9$. **Step 3 — Evaluate candidate labelings (2.5 marks).** There are $2^3 = 8$ possible labelings. Energy: $$ E(\mathbf{x}) = D_1(x_1) + D_2(x_2) + D_3(x_3) + w_{12}[x_1 \neq x_2] + w_{23}[x_2 \neq x_3] + w_{13}[x_1 \neq x_3] $$ Let us evaluate the two most plausible ones based on intensity: **Candidate 1:** $\mathbf{x}_A = (0, 1, 1)$ — only $r_1$ is background: $$ E_D = 0.087 + 0.78 + 0.78 = 1.647 $$ Cuts: $w_{12} = 6$ (since $r_1 \neq r_2$), $w_{13} = 9$ (since $r_1 \neq r_3$). $w_{23}$ is between $r_2$ and $r_3$ both 1, no cut. $$ E_S = 6 + 9 = 15 $$ $$ E(\mathbf{x}_A) = 1.647 + 15 = 16.647 $$ **Candidate 2:** $\mathbf{x}_B = (0, 0, 1)$ — only $r_3$ is object: $$ E_D = 0.087 + 10.50 + 0.78 = 11.367 $$ Cuts: $w_{13} = 9$, $w_{23} = 4$. $w_{12}$ is between $r_1$ and $r_2$ both 0, no cut. $$ E_S = 9 + 4 = 13 $$ $$ E(\mathbf{x}_B) = 11.367 + 13 = 24.367 $$ **Candidate 3:** $\mathbf{x}_C = (0, 1, 0)$ — only $r_2$ is object (unrealistic but check): $$ E_D = 0.087 + 0.78 + 19.53 = 20.397 $$ $$ E_S = 6 + 4 + 9 = 19 $$ $$ E(\mathbf{x}_C) = 39.397 $$ **Step 4 — Select the optimal labeling (1 mark).** The minimum energy is $E(\mathbf{x}_A) = 16.647$ for $\mathbf{x}_A = (0, 1, 1)$. Thus the optimal segmentation labels **regions $r_2$ and $r_3$ as object** and **region $r_1$ as background**. This matches the min-cut partition where $r_1$ is on the source side and $r_2, r_3$ are on the sink side. $\blacksquare$ --- ## **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified] ### (a) Explain the construction of a Region Adjacency Graph (RAG) for a pre-segmented medical image. How are the t-edges and n-edges weighted in this graph? Discuss the use of intensity gradients in setting the boundary weights $w_{ij}$. **(7 Marks)** ### (b) With a neat sketch, describe the Boykov-Kolmogorov (BK) max-flow algorithm. List its two search tree structures and explain how an augmenting path is detected and the bottleneck flow is pushed. Why is BK preferred over Ford-Fulkerson for medical image segmentation? **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### **Part (a) — Region Adjacency Graph Construction and Weighting (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Pre-segmentation output (1 mark).** Given an input medical image $I(x, y)$ of size $N \times N$, an over-segmentation algorithm (Watershed, SLIC, FH) produces a label map partitioning the image into $R$ atomic regions $\{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_R\}$. Each region $r_i$ is a connected set of pixels with homogeneous appearance and has computed features: mean intensity $\mu_i$, variance $\sigma_i^2$, area $A_i$, and centroid. **Step 2 — Definition of RAG (1.5 marks).** The Region Adjacency Graph is defined as $G_{\text{RAG}} = (\mathcal{V}_{\text{RAG}}, \mathcal{E}_{\text{RAG}})$ where: - $\mathcal{V}_{\text{RAG}} = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_R\}$ - $\mathcal{E}_{\text{RAG}} = \{(r_i, r_j) : r_i \text{ and } r_j \text{ share a non-zero length boundary}\}$ Two regions are neighbors if $\vert \partial r_i \cap \partial r_j \vert > 0$, where $\partial r_i$ denotes the boundary of region $r_i$. Typically a 4-neighborhood is used for the underlying pixel grid, which translates to face-adjacency for regions. **Step 3 — Augmentation with terminals (1 mark).** To convert the RAG into an s-t graph, two terminal nodes are added: source $s$ (foreground) and sink $t$ (background). The vertex set becomes $\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{V}_{\text{RAG}} \cup \{s, t\}$. **Step 4 — T-edge weighting (1.5 marks).** Each region $r_i$ is connected to $s$ and $t$ with capacities derived from the data term: $$ c(s, r_i) = D_i(0) = -\ln P(I_{r_i} \mid \text{background}) $$ $$ c(r_i, t) = D_i(1) = -\ln P(I_{r_i} \mid \text{object}) $$ For a Gaussian model with class statistics $(\mu_B, \sigma_B)$ and $(\mu_O, \sigma_O)$: $$ c(s, r_i) = \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_B)^2}{2\sigma_B^2}, \quad c(r_i, t) = \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_O)^2}{2\sigma_O^2} $$ These weights represent the "preference" of each region for being labeled object or background. **Step 5 — N-edge weighting using intensity gradients (2 marks).** The capacity of an n-edge between adjacent regions $r_i$ and $r_j$ is set based on the **mean intensity gradient across their shared boundary**: $$ w_{ij} = \frac{1}{\vert \partial(r_i, r_j) \vert} \sum_{p \in \partial(r_i, r_j)} \sum_{q \in \mathcal{N}(p) \cap r_j} \frac{1}{\text{dist}(p, q)} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert I(p) - I(q) \vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$ Where: - $\partial(r_i, r_j)$ is the set of boundary pixels of $r_i$ adjacent to $r_j$. - $I(p) - I(q)$ is the intensity jump across the boundary. - $\sigma$ is a noise parameter (often estimated from the image). - The exponential is the **Bouton-Planes weighting** function. **Interpretation:** - **High $w_{ij}$** means weak gradient — regions should be MERGED (same label). - **Low $w_{ij}$** means strong gradient (clear edge) — regions should be SEPARATED (different labels). This is critical in medical imaging because tumor boundaries are often weak and noisy, and proper weighting allows the graph cut to "see through" intensity inhomogeneities. --- ### **Part (b) — Boykov-Kolmogorov Algorithm (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — The two search trees (2 marks).** The BK algorithm maintains two dynamic, non-overlapping trees rooted at the terminals: - **Tree $\mathcal{S}$** rooted at source $s$: Contains "active" and "passive" nodes reachable from $s$ via non-saturated edges in the **forward direction**. - **Tree $\mathcal{T}$** rooted at sink $t$: Contains nodes reachable from $t$ via non-saturated edges in the **reverse direction**. Each node in these trees has a **parent pointer** to the edge through which it was discovered. The trees grow by exploring neighbors of their frontier nodes. **Step 2 — Three-stage iteration (2.5 marks).** The algorithm iterates through three stages: 1. **Growth Stage:** Both $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ expand by acquiring new child nodes via non-saturated edges. The active nodes (frontier) are explored in breadth-first order. The process continues until a node in $\mathcal{S}$ is adjacent to a node in $\mathcal{T}$ — an **augmenting path** is found. 2. **Augmentation Stage:** The bottleneck capacity $\Delta$ along the augmenting path is computed (the minimum residual capacity among the path's edges). This $\Delta$ amount of flow is pushed from $s$ to $t$ along the path, saturating at least one edge. The saturated edges become "orphan" nodes, removed from the trees. 3. **Adoption Stage:** Orphan nodes try to find a new parent in their respective tree via non-saturated edges. If they cannot, they become free nodes and may be adopted by the other tree or declared inactive. This stage restores the invariant that the trees are properly rooted. **Step 3 — Augmenting path detection and bottleneck push (1.5 marks).** When a node $u \in \mathcal{S}$ discovers a free node $v \in \mathcal{T}$ via a non-saturated edge $(u, v)$, an augmenting path $s \leadsto u \to v \leadsto t$ is formed. The bottleneck capacity is: $$ \Delta = \min\{c(e) : e \in \text{path}\} $$ This $\Delta$ is added to the flow of each edge on the path, and subtracted from the residual capacities. Edges with zero residual capacity become saturated and may be removed from the trees. **Step 4 — Why BK is preferred for medical imaging (1 mark).** BK is preferred over vanilla Ford-Fulkerson because: - **Polynomial time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V^2 E \sqrt{E})$ worst-case, but empirically near-linear for vision problems. - **Reduces the number of augmenting paths:** The tree structures allow reuse of search effort across iterations. - **Handles dense medical graphs well:** 3D CT/MRI volumes have $E \gg V$ — BK exploits this structure. - **Practical speed:** It is 2-5× faster than BK-predecessors on typical medical benchmarks (BraTS, SLIVER07). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the additive constant** when writing $\text{cap}(\mathcal{C}) = E(\mathbf{x}) + \text{const}$ — the constant does not affect the min-cut, but students who omit it lose 1 mark. **Always mention the constant explicitly.** > 2. **Confusing n-edges with t-edges:** T-edges connect regions to terminals (Source/Sink); N-edges connect regions to regions. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 3. **Missing the "global optimum" justification:** Graph cuts give global min ONLY for binary + submodular. For multi-label or non-submodular, you must mention $\alpha$-expansion as the workaround. > 4. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\text{vert } x \text{ vert}$ in tables:** This BREAKS the markdown table. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ in tables. > 5. **Skipping the submodularity proof:** A 1-mark submodularity check is almost always asked. The four-line proof $0 + 0 \leq w + w$ is mandatory. > 6. **Mixing up Source and Sink semantics:** The Source $s$ represents the OBJECT (label 1) by convention in this formulation; Sink $t$ represents BACKGROUND (label 0). Get this right or the entire cut is reversed. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - [x] **Graph Cuts = Min-Cut = Min-Energy** for binary submodular problems. - [x] **Pre-segmentation** reduces the problem size from $N$ pixels to $R \ll N$ regions. - [x] **Standard pre-segmenters:** Watershed, SLIC, Mean-Shift, FH graph-based. - [x] **Energy:** $E = \text{Data Term} + \text{Smoothness Term}$. - [x] **Data Term:** Negative log-likelihood of region under object/background model (often Gaussian). - [x] **Smoothness Term (Potts):** $V_{ij} = 0$ if same label, $w_{ij}$ if different. - [x] **Submodularity:** $V_{ij}(0,0) + V_{ij}(1,1) \leq V_{ij}(0,1) + V_{ij}(1,0)$ — REQUISITE for global min. - [x] **T-edges** = terminal edges (region-to-source/sink). - [x] **N-edges** = neighbor edges (region-to-region). - [x] **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** (Ford-Fulkerson 1956): $\max \vert f \vert = \min \text{cap}(S, T)$. - [x] **BK Algorithm:** Two-tree augmentation, $\mathcal{O}(V^2 E \sqrt{E})$ worst-case. - [x] **Boundary weight** $w_{ij} \propto \exp(-\nabla I^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ — high weight means weak edge. - [x] **Multi-label / non-submodular:** Use $\alpha$-expansion or $\alpha\beta$-swap (local optima). - [x] **Region-based graph cuts** are standard for **3D medical volumes** (CT, MRI, PET). - [x] **Numerical problems:** Always compute $D_i(0)$ and $D_i(1)$ FIRST, then enumerate, then pick the minimum. **Key Constants to Memorize:** - $w_{ij}$: typically a positive real number; common form $w_{ij} = K \cdot \exp(-\nabla I^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ where $K$ is a normalization constant. - $\sigma$ (Gaussian data term): estimated from the image as the standard deviation of homogeneous background. - $R$ (number of regions): typically 100-10000 depending on pre-segmentation resolution. **Standard KTU Viva Questions:** 1. "Why is submodularity needed for graph cuts?" → Without it, the min-cut does not equal min-energy. 2. "What happens if your smoothness term is the L2 norm instead of Potts?" → It is no longer submodular, and you need $\alpha$-expansion. 3. "Why not just use pixel-based graph cuts on medical images?" → 3D medical images are too large; region-based is faster and enforces topology. 4. "How do you choose $w_{ij}$ in a noisy ultrasound image?" → Use a higher $\sigma$ in the Bouton-Planes weighting to be more permissive of weak edges. 5. "What is the difference between BK and Ford-Fulkerson?" → BK uses two non-overlapping search trees to reuse exploration effort. --- **End of Module 3 Topic Notes: Graph Cuts on Pre-Segmented Images** **Total KTU Coverage:** Definition + Theory + Derivations + Diagrams + 2 Part-A + 2 Part-B Questions (14 marks each) + Examiner Warnings + Recap Checklist

topics.title: Image Management: Archiving - Image Storage and Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Management: Archiving - Image Storage and Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Management in Medical Imaging** refers to the systematic acquisition, archival, retrieval, distribution, and long-term preservation of diagnostic medical images and their associated metadata across healthcare enterprises. It is governed internationally by the **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) standard (NEMA PS3 / ISO 12052)** and operationally realized through **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the sub-component **"Image Storage and Transfer"** encompasses the hardware hierarchies, network protocols, file formats, and redundancy mechanisms that ensure diagnostic-grade images are preserved losslessly for the legally mandated minimum of **5–7 years (in India, per AERB and hospital accreditation norms, often 10 years for pediatric records)** and delivered on-demand to clinicians with sub-second latency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> *"Image Management – Archiving: PACS architecture, DICOM standard, image storage hierarchy (online/near-line/offline), compression strategies, teleradiology, IHE integration framework, and network topologies for image transfer."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital's Digital Postal Service"

Imagine the Radiology Department as a **massive international post office**:

1. **The Imaging Modalities (CT, MRI, CR)** are the *senders* — they generate the "letters" (DICOM image files).
2. **DICOM** is the *envelope format* — every letter has a standardized address block, patient ID, study date, and modality stamp so any post office in the world can read it.
3. **PACS Server + Archive** is the *central sorting office and warehouse* — it sorts, stores, and routes.
4. **The Hospital Network (LAN/WAN)** is the *truck fleet* — physical cables or radio waves.
5. **The Radiologist's Workstation** is the *recipient* — opens the envelope and reads the contents.

Without this infrastructure, every radiologist would need to physically walk to the CT scanner with a **USB hard drive** — exactly what happened before PACS in the 1980s!

### 1.3 Why This Topic is a High-Weightage Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality Check:** A modern tertiary-care hospital generates **15–25 GB of imaging data per day**. A 1500-bed hospital running 6 CT, 4 MRI, and digital X-ray produces **>8 TB/year**. Without proper archiving and transfer engineering, this data becomes unrecoverable within weeks — leading to misdiagnosis, legal liability, and accreditation failure (NABH/JCI).

The cardinal parameters of any image management system are:

- **Integrity (Lossless preservation)** — bit-for-bit fidelity for diagnostic use.
- **Availability (24×7 access)** — measured by **uptime percentage**, target **≥ 99.95%**.
- **Confidentiality (HIPAA / DISHA 2018 compliance in India)** — encryption at rest and in transit.
- **Interoperability (DICOM + HL7 + IHE)** — vendor-neutral communication.
- **Scalability (Petabyte-tier growth)** — modular expansion without downtime.

**Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (must memorize):**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| DICOM default transfer syntax | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 (Explicit VR Little Endian) |
| Typical CR resolution | **2048 × 2500 × 12 bits** |
| Typical CT slice matrix | **512 × 512 × 16 bits** |
| Typical MRI slice matrix | **256 × 256 × 16 bits** |
| Standard DICOM port | **104 (TCP)** |
| DICOMweb RESTful port | **80 (HTTP) / 443 (HTTPS)** |
| Legal retention in India | **5 years (general), 10 years (pediatric/oncology)** |
| MTU of standard Ethernet | **1500 bytes** |
| Optical fiber speed (single-mode) | **10 Gbps – 100 Gbps** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Medical Image Storage and Transfer

#### Pillar 1 — DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)

DICOM is **not just a file format**; it is a complete **network protocol suite + file specification + workflow grammar**. A single DICOM file (`.dcm`) contains two distinct parts:

1. **Header (Meta-information):** Patient name, ID, study UID, modality, slice thickness, kVp, mAs, etc.
2. **Pixel Data:** The actual image matrix, which may be uncompressed, JPEG-lossless, JPEG2000, or RLE-encoded.

The structure is conceptually a **"Tag-Length-Value" (TLV)** nested dataset. Every tag is identified by a **(Group, Element)** pair — for example, `(0010,0010)` is Patient Name, `(0008,1030)` is Study Description.

#### Pillar 2 — PACS Architecture (4-Tier Logical Model)

- **Tier 1 — Acquisition Layer:** Modalities (CT/MRI/CR/US) acting as DICOM Storage SCUs (Service Class Users).
- **Tier 2 — Archive/Storage Layer:** DICOM C-STORE SCP (Service Class Provider), backed by RAID-6/NAS/SAN arrays and a tiered storage hierarchy.
- **Tier 3 — Database/Routing Layer:** RIS (Radiology Information System) + HL7 broker + DICOM router + IHE workflow engine.
- **Tier 4 — Display Layer:** Diagnostic workstations, web clients, mobile viewers.

#### Pillar 3 — Storage Hierarchy (The "Hot-Warm-Cold" Model)

| Tier | Access Time | Media | Cost/GB | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------|---------|-------------|
| **Online (Hot)** | < 2 s | SSD / SAN HDD | ₹35–60 | Current patient (≤ 18 months) |
| **Near-line (Warm)** | 5–60 s | RAID NAS / Blu-ray Jukebox | ₹5–15 | Recent archive (18 mo – 5 yr) |
| **Offline (Cold)** | Minutes–hours | Tape LTO-9 / Cloud Glacier | ₹0.5–2 | Long-term legal archive |

#### Pillar 4 — Image Compression Strategies

- **Lossless (diagnostic-safe):** JPEG-LS, JPEG2000 reversible, Run-Length Encoding. Compression ratio **2:1 to 3:1**.
- **Lossy (NOT for primary diagnosis, OK for teleconsultation preview):** JPEG baseline, JPEG2000 irreversible. Compression ratio **10:1 to 30:1**.
- **Mammography mandate:** FDA/MQSA / AERB requires **lossless only** for primary diagnosis mammograms.

#### Pillar 5 — Network Topologies and Transfer Protocols

- **Intra-hospital (LAN):** 10 Gbps Ethernet over Cat6a / fiber, TCP/IP, DICOM upper layer protocol.
- **Inter-hospital / Teleradiology (WAN):** VPN over MPLS, dedicated leased line, or secured cloud (TLS 1.3).
- **Modern API layer:** **DICOMweb (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS)** — RESTful JSON/XML over HTTP/HTTPS.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Raw image size (bits) | $S_{raw} = R \times C \times B \times N_f$ | R = rows, C = cols, B = bit-depth, $N_f$ = frames |
| 2 | Raw image size (MB) | $S_{MB} = \dfrac{R \times C \times B \times N_f}{8 \times 2^{20}}$ | Divide by $8 \times 1{,}048{,}576$ |
| 3 | Compression ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Higher = more saving |
| 4 | Space saved (%) | $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$ | Useful for backup planning |
| 5 | Network transfer time | $T_t = \dfrac{S_{bits}}{BW_{effective}}$ | BW in bits/s, includes overhead |
| 6 | Effective bandwidth | $BW_{eff} = BW \times (1 - \eta_{proto})$ | $\eta_{proto}$ = protocol overhead (~8% TCP) |
| 7 | Annual storage growth | $G_{yr} = \dfrac{N_{studies}}{day} \times S_{avg} \times 365 \times (1 + r)^n$ | $r$ = annual growth rate (~20–30%) |
| 8 | RAID-5 usable capacity | $C_{usable} = (N-1) \times D_{disk}$ | $N$ = disks, tolerates 1 failure |
| 9 | RAID-6 usable capacity | $C_{usable} = (N-2) \times D_{disk}$ | Tolerates 2 simultaneous failures |
| 10 | MTBF of mirrored system | $MTBF_{mirror} = \dfrac{MTBF_{disk}^2}{2 \times MTTR}$ | Critical for redundancy design |
| 11 | Storage with replication | $S_{total} = S_{logical} \times (1 + k)$ | $k$ = number of replicas |
| 12 | Hamming distance (DICOM) | Must be ≥ 4 for safety-critical transfer | Detects all 1-bit and 2-bit errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`\vert`) allowed inside markdown tables per KTU formatter rules — use `\mid` for "divides" notation if ever needed.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital IT procurement:** Formula 7 justifies the **5-year CAPEX** of an enterprise PACS.
- **Bandwidth contracting:** Formula 5 determines whether **100 Mbps** or **1 Gbps** leased line is needed for teleradiology.
- **Disaster recovery:** Formula 11 (replication factor) directly maps to **RPO/RTO** (Recovery Point/Time Objective) requirements.
- **Cloud archiving:** Drives the choice between **AWS S3 Standard vs S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** based on access time thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1: Uncompressed Storage Calculation for a CT Study

**Problem Statement:** A CT scanner generates 512 × 512 pixel slices at 16-bit depth. A standard chest CT has 350 slices. Calculate:
(i) Raw study size in MB
(ii) Size after lossless 2.5:1 compression
(iii) Size after lossy 15:1 compression
(iv) Annual storage for 25 such studies/day across 1 hospital.

**Given:** R = 512, C = 512, B = 16, $N_f$ = 350, CR_lossless = 2.5, CR_lossy = 15.

**Step 1 — Raw size in bits**

We begin by applying the fundamental formula $S_{raw} = R \times C \times B \times N_f$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{raw} &= 512 \times 512 \times 16 \times 350 \\
&= 262{,}144 \times 16 \times 350 \\
&= 4{,}194{,}304 \times 350 \\
&= 1{,}468{,}006{,}400 \;\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the total number of binary digits that must be stored on disk for the entire study before any compression is applied.

**Step 2 — Convert bits to Megabytes (using $2^{20}$ for true binary MB)**

Using the conversion 1 MB = $8 \times 2^{20}$ bits = 8,388,608 bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MB} &= \frac{1{,}468{,}006{,}400}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \\
&= \frac{1{,}468{,}006{,}400}{8{,}388{,}608} \\
&\approx 175.0 \;\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, a single chest CT study consumes approximately **175 MB** of raw uncompressed storage.

**Step 3 — Apply 2.5:1 lossless compression**

Lossless compression preserves every bit of diagnostic information, hence legally permissible for primary diagnosis.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{lossless} &= \frac{175.0}{2.5} = 70.0 \;\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 15:1 lossy compression**

Lossy compression discards high-frequency information. It is **NOT permitted** for primary diagnosis but acceptable for thumbnail/preview/teleconsultation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{lossy} &= \frac{175.0}{15} \approx 11.67 \;\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual storage for 25 CT studies per day**

Assuming a working year of 365 days and an annual growth rate of 25% (typical for Indian hospitals adopting digital modalities):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{yr} &= 25 \times 175.0 \times 365 \times (1 + 0.25)^1 \\
&= 25 \times 175.0 \times 365 \times 1.25 \\
&= 25 \times 175.0 \times 456.25 \\
&\approx 1{,}996{,}094 \;\text{MB} \\
&\approx 1.90 \;\text{TB/year (raw)}
\end{aligned}
$$

With lossless compression, the same workload consumes **0.76 TB/year** — illustrating why every hospital PACS enables lossless compression by default.

---

#### Example 2: Network Transfer Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 70 MB losslessly compressed CT study is to be transmitted over a 100 Mbps WAN link for teleconsultation. The TCP/IP protocol overhead is 8% (headers + retransmission). Calculate the actual transfer time.

**Given:** $S$ = 70 MB, $BW$ = 100 Mbps, $\eta_{proto}$ = 0.08.

**Step 1 — Convert payload to bits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{bits} &= 70 \times 8 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \\
&= 70 \times 8{,}388{,}608 \\
&= 587{,}202{,}560 \;\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective bandwidth**

TCP/IP stack overhead includes Ethernet header (14 B), IP (20 B), TCP (20 B), and TCP ACKs (~8% bidirectional chatter).

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{eff} &= 100 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.08) \\
&= 100 \times 10^6 \times 0.92 \\
&= 92 \times 10^6 \;\text{bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transfer time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= \frac{S_{bits}}{BW_{eff}} \\
&= \frac{587{,}202{,}560}{92 \times 10^6} \\
&\approx 6.38 \;\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus a single CT study traverses a 100 Mbps WAN in approximately **6.4 seconds** — perfectly within the clinical SLA of **< 30 seconds** for emergency teleradiology (e.g., stroke "drip-and-ship" workflow).

---

#### Example 3: RAID-6 Usable Capacity for Hospital Archive

**Problem Statement:** A hospital PACS archive uses a RAID-6 array of 12 disks, each 8 TB. Calculate the usable storage. If the archive must hold 500 TB of unique logical data, how many such arrays are required?

**Given:** N = 12, $D_{disk}$ = 8 TB, $S_{logical}$ = 500 TB.

**Step 1 — Apply RAID-6 capacity formula**

RAID-6 uses two parity disks, hence tolerates simultaneous failure of any 2 disks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (N - 2) \times D_{disk} \\
&= (12 - 2) \times 8 \\
&= 10 \times 8 = 80 \;\text{TB per array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of arrays needed**

Accounting for a replication factor $k$ = 1 (one full mirror for disaster recovery):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total\;required} &= S_{logical} \times (1 + k) = 500 \times 2 = 1000 \;\text{TB} \\
N_{arrays} &= \frac{1000}{80} = 12.5 \Rightarrow 13 \;\text{arrays}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **13 RAID-6 shelves** (156 disks total) are required to provide 500 TB of logical archive with full mirroring. This is the standard engineering justification cited in NABH accreditation audits.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Transfer Protocols

| Protocol | Layer | Latency | Diagnostic Use | DICOM Compliant | KTU Exam Probability |
|---------|-------|---------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **DICOM C-STORE (TCP port 104)** | Application | Low | Primary diagnosis | Yes (native) | Very High |
| **DICOMweb WADO-RS (HTTPS)** | Application (REST) | Medium | Web/mobile viewing | Yes (Supplement 161) | High |
| **HL7 v2.x (port 2575)** | Messaging | Low | Metadata only (orders/reports) | N/A — orthogonal | High |
| **HL7 FHIR (REST + JSON)** | Modern | Low | Unified clinical exchange | Pairs with DICOMweb | Medium |
| **FTP / SFTP** | Generic | Variable | Backup only (not diagnostic) | No | Low |
| **Cloud S3 multipart** | Object storage | Variable | Archive/Disaster Recovery | Wrapper required | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PACS Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a hospital PACS, mapping every interaction from modality acquisition to clinician viewing, including the storage hierarchy and IHE workflow engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Modality Layer CT MRI CR US PET] -->|DICOM C-STORE| B[Acquisition Gateway DICOM Router]
    B -->|C-STORE SCP| C[Short Term Online Archive SSD SAN]
    B -->|HL7 ORM ORU| D[RIS Radiology Information System]
    D -->|IHE SWF Profile| E[IHE Workflow Engine XDS Registry]
    B -->|DICOM C-MOVE WADO| F[Diagnostic Workstation 4MP Monitors]
    B -->|DICOMweb HTTPS| G[Web Mobile Client Zero Footprint]
    C -->|Auto Migration Policy 18 months| H[Nearline Archive RAID NAS Blu Ray]
    H -->|Legal Retention 5 10 years| I[Offline Cold Archive LTO 9 Tape or Cloud Glacier]
    C -->|Encrypted Replication| J[Disaster Recovery Site Geo Redundant]
    E -->|Audit Trail ATNA| K[NABH Audit Log Server]
    F --> L[Radiologist Reporting]
    G --> L
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**

- **Modalities → Acquisition Gateway** uses DICOM's C-STORE protocol.
- **Gateway → Archive** deduplicates, validates (per DICOM PS3.4 Annex B), and writes to SSD tier.
- **Archive → Nearline → Offline** is governed by an auto-migration policy triggered at 18 months (typical).
- **Workstation and Web client** retrieve via **C-MOVE** (legacy) or **WADO-RS** (modern).
- **Disaster Recovery site** receives async replication (typical RPO = 15 minutes).
- **ATNA (Audit Trail and Node Authentication)** is the IHE profile that logs every access for **HIPAA/DISHA compliance**.

### 4.2 DICOM File Structure — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DCM File 128 byte Preamble] --> B[DICM Magic Word 4 bytes]
    B --> C[File Meta Information Group 0002]
    C --> D[Dataset Patient Tags 0010]
    C --> E[Dataset Study Tags 0008 0020]
    C --> F[Dataset Series Tags 0028 0028]
    C --> G[Dataset Image Tags 7FE0 0010 Pixel Data]
    D --> H[TLV Encoded Nested Sequences]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

**Engineering Insight:** The 128-byte preamble is **historically reserved for proprietary use** (older vendors hid private data here). The `DICM` magic word at offset 128 confirms DICOM validity — without it, parsers reject the file. The pixel data tag `(7FE0,0010)` is the only mandatory image-carrying tag in a DICOM Information Object Definition (IOD).

### 4.3 Storage Tier Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Image Received] --> B{Age less than 18 months?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Online SSD Tier Retrieval under 2 s]
    B -- No --> D{Age 18 months to 5 years?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Nearline NAS Tier Retrieval 5 to 60 s]
    D -- No --> F{Required for active care?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Nearline Exception Override]
    F -- No --> H[Offline Tape or Cloud Glacier Retrieval minutes]
    H --> I{Retention 5 to 10 years passed?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Secure Wipe per DISHA 2018]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Offline Storage]
```

This state machine is the **canonical lifecycle policy** documented in every PACS commissioning report and is a direct KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper – Set B]**
**Define DICOM. List any four mandatory DICOM information objects for a CT image.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is the international standard (NEMA PS3 / ISO 12052) that defines the formats, communication protocols, and workflow grammar for medical imaging information exchange across vendors and institutions.

**Mandatory Information Objects (IOD modules) for a CT image:**

1. **Patient Module** (Group 0010) — Patient Name, Patient ID, Birth Date, Sex.
2. **Study Module** (Group 0008) — Study Instance UID, Study Date, Study Time, Referring Physician.
3. **Series Module** (Group 0028) — Modality (CT), Series Instance UID, Series Number.
4. **Image Module** (Group 0028) — Rows, Columns, Pixel Spacing, Slice Thickness, Bits Allocated.
5. **CT Image Module** (Group 0018) — kVp, mAs, Reconstruction Diameter, Exposure.
6. **SOP Common & Pixel Data** (Group 7FE0) — the actual image matrix.

*(Any four well-explained modules earn full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary – Model Paper Set A]**
**Differentiate between lossless and lossy image compression in medical imaging. State the legal constraint for mammographic images.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Information retained | **100% (bit-exact)** | Partial (high-freq discarded) |
| Typical CR | **2:1 to 3:1** | **10:1 to 30:1** |
| Diagnostic safety | Primary diagnosis OK | **NOT for primary diagnosis** |
| Examples | JPEG-LS, JPEG2000 reversible, RLE | JPEG baseline, JPEG2000 lossy |
| Use case | Long-term archive, primary reading | Thumbnail, web preview, teleconsultation cache |

**Legal Constraint:** Per **MQSA (USA) and AERB (India)** guidelines, mammographic images used for primary diagnosis **must be stored using lossless compression only**. Lossy compression is permitted only for derived display copies that are not used for definitive interpretation.

*(Full 3 marks for tabular comparison + legal constraint statement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the **four-tier architecture of a Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS)** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital performs 30 CT scans per day. Each CT study has 400 slices of 512 × 512 resolution at 16-bit depth. Calculate:
   (i) The raw uncompressed size of one CT study in MB.
   (ii) The size after applying a 2.8:1 lossless compression.
   (iii) The annual storage requirement (in TB) assuming 360 working days and 30% annual data growth.
   (iv) The number of **8 TB** RAID-6 arrays (12 disks each, 2 parity) required to store 5 years of logical data **without replication**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Tier PACS Architecture:**

A modern PACS is logically organized into four interacting tiers, each performing a distinct role:

1. **Tier 1 — Image Acquisition Layer:** Comprises the imaging modalities (CT, MRI, CR, US, PET) and the modality worklist server (MWL). They act as **DICOM Storage SCUs** (clients that push images). They also receive patient demographics via the **Modality Worklist (MWL) SOP Class** from the RIS/HIS.

2. **Tier 2 — Storage and Archive Layer:** The heart of PACS. It receives images via the **DICOM C-STORE SCP** (server) service. Storage is organized as the **Online–Nearline–Offline (Tiered) hierarchy** with RAID-6 or RAID-10 disk arrays for online tier, NAS/Blu-ray jukebox for nearline, and LTO tape or cloud for offline. The **DICOMDIR** file indexes all studies.

3. **Tier 3 — Database, Routing and Integration Layer:** This includes the **RIS**, the **HL7 broker**, the **IHE workflow engine** (XDS Registry, ATNA, SWF profiles), and the **DICOM router** that dispatches studies to the correct reading workstation based on radiologist subspecialty, location, and workload.

4. **Tier 4 — Display and Review Layer:** Comprises **diagnostic workstations** (2–8 MP medical-grade monitors, calibrated to DICOM PS3.14 GSDF), **clinical review stations**, **mobile/zero-footprint web viewers**, and **3D post-processing workstations**. Retrieval uses **DICOM C-MOVE / C-GET** or modern **DICOMweb WADO-RS**.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the canonical Mermaid schematic.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Raw uncompressed size of one CT study:**

Given R = 512, C = 512, B = 16 bits, $N_f$ = 400.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{raw} &= 512 \times 512 \times 16 \times 400 \\
&= 4{,}194{,}304 \times 400 \\
&= 1{,}677{,}721{,}600 \;\text{bits} \\
S_{MB} &= \frac{1{,}677{,}721{,}600}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \\
&= \frac{1{,}677{,}721{,}600}{8{,}388{,}608} \\
&= 200 \;\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Size after 2.8:1 lossless compression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{compressed} &= \frac{200}{2.8} \\
&\approx 71.43 \;\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Annual storage requirement with 30% growth:**

Per-day raw size = 30 × 200 MB = 6000 MB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= 6000 \times 360 = 2{,}160{,}000 \;\text{MB} = 2.06 \;\text{TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compounded over years at 30% growth — total for 5 years (geometric series):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{5yr} &= Y_1 \times \frac{(1.30^5 - 1)}{(1.30 - 1)} \\
&= 2.06 \times \frac{(3.71293 - 1)}{0.30} \\
&= 2.06 \times 9.0431 \\
&\approx 18.63 \;\text{TB (raw)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Number of RAID-6 arrays required:**

Each 12-disk array with 2 parity yields $(12 - 2) \times 8 = 80$ TB usable. Without replication, the 18.63 TB fits in **one array** (with 80 − 18.63 = 61.37 TB spare for other modalities like MRI, CR, US). Therefore, **1 RAID-6 array** is sufficient for the 5-year CT data alone. A second array would be added for redundancy as per best practice.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **DICOM communication protocol stack**. Explain the role of the **DICOM Upper Layer (DUL) protocol over TCP/IP**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **three-tier storage hierarchy** (Online, Nearline, Offline). For a hospital generating 5 TB/year, design a storage plan assuming **70% of retrievals must occur in under 5 seconds**. Justify your choice of media for each tier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DICOM Protocol Stack:**

DICOM communication is structured as a layered protocol stack that mirrors the OSI model:

1. **Physical & Data Link Layer:** Standard Ethernet (10/100/1000 Mbps), Wi-Fi (802.11ac/ax), or fiber (10 Gbps). Frames carry the upper-layer payload.
2. **Network Layer (IP):** IPv4 or IPv6 addressing of the Application Entity Titles (AETs).
3. **Transport Layer (TCP):** Provides **reliable, ordered, connection-oriented** byte stream. DICOM uses **TCP port 104** as the well-known port.
4. **DICOM Upper Layer (DUL):** The session/presentation layer of DICOM. It manages:
   - **Association establishment** (A-ASSOCIATE-RQ / AC / RJ PDUs)
   - **Negotiation of presentation contexts** (transfer syntaxes, SOP classes)
   - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit) framing** with a 4-byte length prefix
   - **Graceful release** (A-RELEASE-RQ / RP)
5. **DICOM Message Exchange:** P-DATA-TF PDUs carry DIMSE messages (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-ECHO).

**Role of DUL:** The DUL abstracts the underlying TCP/IP transport and provides **vendor-neutral session management**. It ensures that before any image is exchanged, both ends have **agreed on a common SOP class and transfer syntax** (e.g., both must support CT Image Storage with JPEG Lossless). Without DUL negotiation, heterogeneous vendor interoperability is impossible.

**(b) Three-Tier Storage Design:**

| Tier | Media Chosen | Capacity Allocated | Retrieval Time | Justification |
|------|--------------|-------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Online (Hot)** | SSD-backed SAN (RAID-6, 16 × 4 TB SSDs) | ~10 TB (2 years of "hot" data) | < 2 s | SSD gives deterministic sub-2 s latency required for active clinical reads. RAID-6 ensures no single point of failure. |
| **Nearline (Warm)** | Enterprise NAS with 7,200 RPM NL-SAS HDDs (RAID-6, 24 × 10 TB) | ~200 TB | 5–60 s | Cost-effective bulk storage for recent archive (2–5 yr). Acceptable latency for non-emergency retrievals. |
| **Offline (Cold)** | LTO-9 Tape Library (e.g., 40-slot autoloader) + Optional Cloud Glacier mirror | Petabytes | Minutes–hours | Lowest ₹/TB. Satisfies 5–10 year legal retention. Cloud mirror provides geo-redundant DR. |

**Retrieval Distribution Analysis:** If 70% of retrievals must complete within 5 seconds, then **70% × 5 TB/year = 3.5 TB/year** must reside in the **Online tier**. To leave headroom (studies may be accessed randomly within 18–24 months of acquisition), we provision **~10 TB online** (≈2 years of fresh data). The remaining **30% (1.5 TB/year)** can be served from nearline/offline with acceptable delay.

**Justification Summary:** This tiered design optimises the **cost-vs-performance** Pareto curve: SSDs are expensive but mandatory for active reads; HDDs balance cost and latency for warm data; tapes are virtually free per TB for cold archives. This is the standard architecture certified by **NABH and ISO 27001** hospital audits.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DICOM and PACS:** DICOM is a **standard/protocol**; PACS is a **system that implements DICOM**. Writing "DICOM is a server" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** When computing storage, always show conversion to **MB or GB explicitly**. A bare number without units = 0 marks.
> 3. **Using $10^6$ instead of $2^{20}$:** Strict examiners deduct for using decimal MB (10⁶) vs binary MiB (2²⁰) — clarify which you are using.
> 4. **Skipping the growth factor:** In annual storage problems, ignoring the **$(1+r)^n$** compounding term loses the "growth" sub-mark.
> 5. **Calling lossless compression "without any loss" without naming the algorithm:** Name **JPEG-LS or JPEG2000 reversible** for full credit.
> 6. **Drawing a PACS diagram without labelling tiers or arrows:** A diagram without labels earns only 2/7 marks.
> 7. **Mixing up DICOM port 104 with HTTP port 80:** Examiners specifically test whether you remember **port 104** is the DICOM standard port.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **PACS = system; DICOM = standard that PACS implements.**
- **DICOM default port = 104 (TCP); DICOMweb = 80/443 (HTTP/HTTPS).**
- **DICOM file has 128-byte preamble + "DICM" magic word + TLV-encoded dataset.**
- **Key pixel tag = (7FE0, 0010).**
- **PACS has 4 logical tiers:** Acquisition → Storage → Integration/Routing → Display.
- **Storage is 3 physical tiers:** Online (SSD, < 2 s) → Nearline (HDD NAS, 5–60 s) → Offline (LTO tape / Cloud Glacier, minutes).
- **Lossless compression:** 2:1 to 3:1, used for primary diagnosis; **mandatory for mammography.**
- **Lossy compression:** 10:1 to 30:1, only for preview/web/teleconsultation.
- **Key formulas (memorize):**
  - $S_{raw} = R \times C \times B \times N_f$ bits
  - $S_{MB} = S_{bits} / (8 \times 2^{20})$
  - $T_t = S_{bits} / BW_{eff}$
  - $C_{RAID6} = (N-2) \times D_{disk}$
- **IHE profiles to remember:** **SWF** (Scheduled Workflow), **ATNA** (Audit Trail), **XDS** (Cross-Enterprise Document Sharing).
- **Teleradiology bandwidth:** 100 Mbps WAN suffices for emergency CT push in < 10 s.
- **Legal retention (India):** **5 years general, 10 years pediatric/oncology.**
- **Cloud options:** AWS S3 Standard = hot; S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval = warm; S3 Glacier Deep Archive = cold.
- **Encryption:** TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, mandatory for DISHA 2018 compliance.
- **DICOMweb trio:** **WADO-RS** (retrieve), **STOW-RS** (store), **QIDO-RS** (query).

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"If you can draw PACS tiers from memory, name the DICOM port, compute a CT storage size, and justify a RAID-6 capacity — you will score full marks on any question in this module."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Systems in a Hospital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Systems in a Hospital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hospital Information System (HIS) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Hospital Information System (HIS)** is a comprehensive, integrated, computer-based information system designed to manage the **administrative, financial, and clinical operations** of a healthcare institution. It collects, stores, processes, retrieves, and disseminates the data required for the smooth functioning of the hospital — encompassing patient registration, electronic medical records, laboratory information, radiology imaging, pharmacy, billing, and decision support — under a unified digital framework.

The HIS, as defined under the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Biomedical Engineering syllabus (PEEBT745, Module 4)**, is not merely a single software application but an **ecosystem of interoperating subsystems** (often called "modules" or "sub-HISs") that together enable evidence-based clinical practice, paperless workflows, and regulatory compliance.

### Sub-Components of HIS (KTU Syllabus Taxonomy)

| Sub-System | Full Form | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **EMR** | Electronic Medical Record | Digital version of patient's clinical chart |
| **EHR** | Electronic Health Record | Longitudinal, interoperable patient record across providers |
| **ADT** | Admission, Discharge, Transfer | Demographic & bed-management tracking |
| **LIS** | Laboratory Information System | Manages pathology & biochemistry test results |
| **RIS** | Radiology Information System | Manages radiology scheduling, reports, workflow |
| **PACS** | Picture Archiving & Communication System | Stores, retrieves, displays DICOM medical images |
| **CPOE** | Computerized Physician Order Entry | Electronic ordering of drugs/tests by clinicians |
| **CDSS** | Clinical Decision Support System | Alerts, reminders, drug-interaction checks |
| **PAS** | Patient Administration System | Front-desk, billing, insurance claims |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU board exams, distinguishing between **HIS** (hospital-wide), **EMR** (single facility, clinical focus) and **EHR** (multi-facility, interoperable) is a **guaranteed 2-mark question**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital as an "Airport"

Imagine a **modern international airport** with thousands of passengers (patients), dozens of airlines (departments), luggage (samples & images), runways (operation theatres), and control towers (administrators).

- The **HIS is the airport's central command-and-control system**: it tells staff when a passenger has arrived (ADT), which gate (consultation room) they must go to, which security check (lab test) is pending, where their luggage (blood sample, X-ray) is located, and what time their flight (surgery) is scheduled.
- Without it, every counter would use paper chits — slow, lossy, and error-prone.
- The HIS ensures **right information → right person → right time**, just like an airport's SITA/AMADEUS system does for flights.

In simpler words:

> **A Hospital Information System is to a hospital what an Operating System is to a computer — it manages all resources (data, personnel, equipment) so that every "application" (doctor, lab, billing desk) runs smoothly without colliding with another.**

---

## 1.3 Why HIS Matters — The KTU-Mandated Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Learning Outcome (CO4 — Module 4):**
> *"Describe the architecture, components, and workflow integration of Hospital Information Systems with emphasis on imaging subsystems (RIS/PACS) and standards (HL7/DICOM)."*

The HIS directly addresses the **3 Big Problems** of modern healthcare:
1. **Information Fragmentation** — patient data scattered across departments.
2. **Medical Errors** — handwritten prescriptions kill ~1.3 M people/year (WHO).
3. **Inefficiency** — duplicate tests, lost films, delayed reports.

**Standard Performance Metrics used in HIS evaluation (KTU expects awareness):**
- **Mean Order-to-Report Turn-Around-Time (TAT)** — ideally **< 30 minutes** for routine radiology.
- **System Uptime**: **99.9 %** (only **8.76 hours/year** allowed downtime).
- **DICOM Compliance Rate**: target **100 %**.
- **HL7 Message Success Rate**: target **> 99.5 %**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of HIS

A modern HIS follows the classical **3-tier client–server architecture**, identical to enterprise IT systems. KTU examiners expect students to draw and explain this in 14-mark questions.

### Layer 1 — Presentation Layer (Front-End / Client)
- Web browsers, mobile apps, ward terminals, kiosk PCs.
- Technologies: HTML5, React, Angular, REST APIs.
- Used by doctors, nurses, patients, billing staff.

### Layer 2 — Application / Logic Layer (Middle-Tier)
- Business rules engine, workflow orchestrator, CDSS engine.
- Implements HL7 v2.x / FHIR message routing.
- Hosts application servers (Java Spring Boot, .NET, Node.js).

### Layer 3 — Data Layer (Back-End)
- **Relational DB** (patient demographics, billing) — Oracle, PostgreSQL, MS SQL.
- **DICOM Archive** (images) — often separate PACS storage with WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many) RAID.
- **Data Warehouse** — for analytics and research (OLAP cubes).

---

## 2.2 HIS ↔ RIS ↔ PACS Integration (Imaging Focus — PEEBT745 Specialisation)

Since this course is *Advanced Medical Imaging*, the integration between HIS–RIS–PACS is the **most heavily tested topic**. The data flow is:

1. Patient visits OPD → **HIS** registers & generates **MRN (Medical Record Number)**.
2. Doctor orders an X-ray → order travels via **HL7 ORM message** from HIS → RIS.
3. RIS schedules the scan, generates **Accession Number**.
4. Modality (CT/MRI) performs scan → images stored in **PACS** using **DICOM**, keyed by Accession Number.
5. Radiologist reads from PACS workstation → report dictated → report (text) sent back via **HL7 ORU message** to HIS.
6. Final report appears in the patient's **EMR** inside HIS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Explain the role of HL7 and DICOM in HIS integration."*
> **Answer Framework:** HL7 = **textual/clinical data** (orders, reports, demographics). DICOM = **binary imaging data** (pixels + metadata). They are **complementary, not competing**.

---

## 2.3 Communication Standards — The "Languages" of HIS

### 2.3.1 HL7 (Health Level Seven)
- An **International Standard (ISO/HL7 27931)** for exchanging clinical & administrative data.
- Operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application Layer)**.
- Versions: **v2.x** (pipe-delimited, most widely deployed), **v3** (XML-based), **FHIR** (REST + JSON, modern).
- Common message types:
  - `ADT^A01` — Patient admission
  - `ORM^O01` — Order message
  - `ORU^R01` — Observation result (used for lab/radiology reports)
  - `SIU^S12` — Scheduling

### 2.3.2 DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)
- **ISO 12052** standard, governs medical imaging.
- Each DICOM file contains: **preamble + header (metadata) + pixel data**.
- DICOM uses **TCP/IP port 104**; supports modalities CT, MR, CR, US, PT, MG, XA, etc.
- **DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL)** service lets modalities pull scheduled patient lists from RIS — eliminates manual typing → **zero demographic errors**.

### 2.3.3 IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)
- **Profiles** that define HOW HL7 + DICOM work together in specific workflows (e.g., *Scheduled Workflow* SWF, *Patient Information Reconciliation* PIR).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **complete quantitative toolkit** KTU expects for HIS-related numericals. Note: Healthcare informatics is qualitative-dominant, so numerical problems are rare. When they appear, they fall into the categories below.

| Metric / Formula | Symbol | Equation | Typical / Target Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| System Availability | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | **99.9 %** | % |
| Mean Downtime per Year | $DT_{yr}$ | $DT_{yr} = (1-A) \times 8760$ | $\leq$ **8.76 h** | hours/year |
| Storage per CR/DR X-ray | $S_{XR}$ | $S_{XR} = W \times H \times D / 8$ | **~ 15 – 25 MB** | MB/image |
| Storage per CT slice | $S_{CT}$ | $S_{CT} = 512 \times 512 \times 2 / 1024^2$ | **0.5 MB/slice** | MB |
| CT exam size | $S_{exam}$ | $S_{exam} = S_{CT} \times N_{slices}$ | **100 – 250 MB** | MB |
| MRI exam size | $S_{MR}$ | $S_{MR} \approx 256 \times 256 \times 2 \times N$ | **30 – 80 MB** | MB |
| Annual PACS storage | $S_{yr}$ | $S_{yr} = N_{exams} \times \bar{S}_{exam} \times 1.4$ (40 % overhead) | **TB-scale** | TB |
| Network Throughput | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{S}{t}$ | **≥ 1 Gbps** for CT streaming | Mbps |
| TAT (Turn-Around Time) | $TAT$ | $TAT = T_{report} - T_{order}$ | **< 30 min (routine)** | min |
| Compression Ratio | $CR$ | $CR = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | **2 : 1 (lossless), 10 : 1 (visually lossless JPEG 2000)** | ratio |
| HL7 Pipe-Count | $n_f$ | Number of `$\vert$` delimiters in a segment | varies | count |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Safety Rule Applied:** All delimiter symbols inside the above table have been rendered using LaTeX `\vert` or `/` to **avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax**. Do NOT insert a raw vertical bar `|` inside any table cell — it will split the row.

---

## 2.5 Security, Privacy & Compliance

A HIS must conform to privacy frameworks. KTU expects students to know at least **two**:

- **HIPAA** (USA, 1996) — Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification Rule.
- **DISHA / DPDP Act 2023** (India) — Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act.
- **GDPR** (EU) — Patient right to erasure.

**Three pillars of HIS data security** (the **CIA Triad**):
1. **Confidentiality** — only authorized eyes see PHI (Protected Health Information).
2. **Integrity** — audit trails prevent tampering; checksums on DICOM files.
3. **Availability** — RAID, redundant servers, off-site DR (Disaster Recovery) site.

**Engineering controls:** AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), MFA for clinicians.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Teleradiology** relies entirely on HIS-RIS-PACS integration across cities.
- **AI-based triage** (e.g., stroke detection on CT) plugs into PACS via DICOM C-STORE/C-FIND.
- **IoMT (Internet of Medical Things)** — wearables stream vitals → HIS cloud → doctor's tablet.
- **Smart Hospitals** (AIIMS, Apollo, Cleveland Clinic) use HIS as the digital backbone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> HIS is qualitative-heavy. The few quantitative derivations are **(a) storage sizing** and **(b) availability / downtime**. KTU board exams occasionally ask these as 7-mark sub-questions inside Module 4. Below are the **fully-worked step-by-step derivations**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual PACS Storage Requirement

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-Mark KTU Numerical):**
A hospital performs on average **120 radiological exams/day**, with the following mix. Calculate total annual PACS storage assuming **40 % overhead** for DICOM headers, audit logs, and pre-fetching. Use a **2 : 1 lossless compression**.

| Modality | Exams/day | Avg. uncompressed size (MB) |
|---|---|---|
| CR (X-ray) | 50 | 20 |
| CT | 30 | 200 |
| MRI | 20 | 60 |
| Ultrasound | 15 | 8 |
| Mammography | 5 | 40 |

### Step 1 — Compute average un-compressed exam size

Let the total number of exams per day be:

$$
N_{day} = 50 + 30 + 20 + 15 + 5 = 120 \text{ exams/day}
$$

Let $S_i$ = uncompressed size per exam of modality $i$, and $n_i$ = exams/day of modality $i$. Average un-compressed size per exam is the weighted mean:

$$
\bar{S}_{exam} = \frac{\sum_{i} n_i \, S_i}{\sum_{i} n_i}
$$

$$
\bar{S}_{exam} = \frac{(50 \cdot 20) + (30 \cdot 200) + (20 \cdot 60) + (15 \cdot 8) + (5 \cdot 40)}{120}
$$

$$
\bar{S}_{exam} = \frac{1000 + 6000 + 1200 + 120 + 200}{120} = \frac{8520}{120}
$$

$$
\bar{S}_{exam} = 71 \text{ MB/exam}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply 2:1 lossless compression

Compression ratio $CR = 2$ reduces size by half:

$$
S_{compressed} = \frac{\bar{S}_{exam}}{CR} = \frac{71}{2} = 35.5 \text{ MB/exam}
$$

### Step 3 — Add 40 % system overhead

Overhead factor $k = 1.40$ accounts for DICOM headers, audit trails, redundant copies:

$$
S_{on-disk} = S_{compressed} \times k = 35.5 \times 1.40 = 49.7 \text{ MB/exam}
$$

### Step 4 — Annualize

A non-leap year has $D = 365$ days. Annual volume:

$$
S_{annual} = N_{day} \times D \times S_{on-disk}
$$

$$
S_{annual} = 120 \times 365 \times 49.7 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
S_{annual} = 120 \times 365 = 43800 \text{ exams}
$$

$$
S_{annual} = 43800 \times 49.7 \approx 2{,}176{,}860 \text{ MB}
$$

Convert to Terabytes ($1 \text{ TB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}$):

$$
S_{annual} \approx \frac{2{,}176{,}860}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 2.08 \text{ TB/year}
$$

### Step 5 — 5-Year retention projection (regulatory norm)

$$
S_{5yr} = 5 \times S_{annual} \approx 5 \times 2.08 \approx 10.4 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The hospital needs **≈ 2.08 TB/year** of PACS storage, growing to **≈ 10.4 TB over 5 years**. Hence a **RAID-6 NAS of ≥ 15 TB usable** is recommended (accounting for parity overhead).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — System Availability & Allowed Downtime

**Given:** MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) = **5000 hours**, MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) = **2 hours**.

### Step 1 — Apply availability formula

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 2} = \frac{5000}{5002}
$$

$$
A \approx 0.9996 = 99.96\%
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to annual downtime

Total hours in a year = $24 \times 365 = 8760$ h.

$$
DT_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = (1 - 0.9996) \times 8760
$$

$$
DT_{yr} = 0.0004 \times 8760 \approx 3.5 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This **99.96 %** ("four-nines") availability exceeds the KTU-stated benchmark of **99.9 %**, allowing only **~ 3.5 hours** of downtime per year — appropriate for a **critical-care HIS**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HIS Sub-Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **comparative matrix** is a high-yield KTU 14-mark answer template. Memorise the *columns*.

| Feature | EMR | EHR | HIS | RIS | PACS | LIS | CPOE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single facility | Cross-facility | Hospital-wide | Radiology dept. | Imaging archive | Laboratory | Ordering |
| **Data Type** | Clinical text | Interoperable clinical | All (admin+clinical) | Radiology workflow | DICOM images | Lab results | Drug/test orders |
| **Primary User** | Doctors | Multiple providers | All staff | Radiologists, techs | Radiologists | Pathologists | Physicians |
| **Standard** | HL7 FHIR | HL7 FHIR | HL7 + DICOM | HL7 + DICOM | DICOM | HL7 + ASTM E1394 | HL7 |
| **Patient Focus** | Yes | Yes (longitudinal) | Yes | Episode-based | Episode-based | Episode-based | Order-based |
| **Interoperability** | Limited | High | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Regulatory Driver** | HIPAA | Meaningful Use (US) | DISHA | AERB (India) | AERB | NABL | Patient Safety |
| **Storage Need** | GB | GB-TB | TB | GB | **TB-PB** | GB | GB |
| **Criticality** | High | High | **Mission-Critical** | High | High | High | High |

---

## 3.4 HL7 v2 Message Anatomy (Conceptual Derivation)

A HL7 v2 message is built **hierarchically** — the examiner often asks students to "draw" or "label" this in 7 marks.

$$
\text{Message} = \underbrace{MSH}_{\text{header}} \;+\; \underbrace{\{ \text{PID, PV1, ORC, OBR, OBX} \}}_{\text{segments}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{...}}_{\text{optional}}
$$

- **MSH** = Message Header (sender, receiver, timestamp, message type).
- **PID** = Patient Identification (name, DOB, MRN).
- **PV1** = Patient Visit (attending doctor, location).
- **ORC** = Common Order.
- **OBR** = Observation Request (which test was ordered).
- **OBX** = Observation/Result (the actual value).

Each segment contains **fields separated by `$\vert$`**, **components separated by `^`**, **sub-components by `&`**, and **repetition by `~`**. (Symbols rendered with LaTeX to preserve table integrity.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: node IDs are alphanumeric, labels use plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 HIS Three-Tier Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PresentationLayer["PRESENTATION LAYER CLIENTS"]
        nodeA["DOCTOR WORKSTATION"]
        nodeB["NURSE TABLET"]
        nodeC["PATIENT KIOSK"]
        nodeD["ADMIN DESK"]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LOGIC MIDDLEWARE"]
        nodeE["HL7 FHIR ENGINE"]
        nodeF["DICOM ROUTER"]
        nodeG["CDSS RULES ENGINE"]
        nodeH["WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATOR"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["DATA LAYER BACKEND"]
        nodeI["PATIENT DB ORACLE"]
        nodeJ["PACS DICOM ARCHIVE"]
        nodeK["DATA WAREHOUSE OLAP"]
        nodeL["AUDIT LOG STORE"]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeL
```

---

## 4.2 HIS-RIS-PACS Workflow Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DOC as DOCTOR
    participant HIS as HIS SERVER
    participant RIS as RIS SERVER
    participant MOD as CT MRI MODALITY
    participant PACS as PACS ARCHIVE
    participant RAD as RADIOLOGIST

    DOC->>HIS: LOGIN AND OPEN EMR
    DOC->>HIS: ORDER CT SCAN HL7 ORM
    HIS->>RIS: FORWARD ORDER HL7 ORM
    RIS->>RIS: SCHEDULE AND ASSIGN ACCESSION
    RIS->>MOD: PUSH DICOM MODALITY WORKLIST
    MOD->>PACS: C STORE IMAGES DICOM
    MOD->>RIS: EXAM COMPLETE HL7
    RAD->>PACS: QUERY RETRIEVE STUDY
    RAD->>PACS: DICTATE REPORT
    PACS->>HIS: REPORT HL7 ORU
    HIS->>DOC: DISPLAY REPORT IN EMR
```

---

## 4.3 HL7-DICOM Functional Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TEXTDATA["TEXTUAL DATA HL7 DOMAIN"]
        nodeM["PATIENT DEMOGRAPHICS PID"]
        nodeN["ORDER DETAILS OBR"]
        nodeO["REPORT TEXT OBX"]
    end
    subgraph IMGDATA["BINARY DATA DICOM DOMAIN"]
        nodeP["IMAGE PIXELS 7FE0"]
        nodeQ["DICOM HEADER METADATA"]
        nodeR["MODALITY WORKLIST C FIND"]
    end
    subgraph IHE["IHE PROFILE BRIDGE"]
        nodeS["SCHEDULED WORKFLOW SWF"]
        nodeT["PATIENT INFORMATION RECONCILIATION PIR"]
    end
    nodeM <--> nodeS
    nodeN <--> nodeS
    nodeO <--> nodeS
    nodeQ <--> nodeS
    nodeP <--> nodeS
    nodeR <--> nodeS
    nodeS <--> nodeT
```

---

## 4.4 HIS Security CIA Triad (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CONF["CONFIDENTIALITY"]
        nodeU["AES 256 AT REST"]
        nodeV["TLS 1.3 IN TRANSIT"]
    end
    subgraph INT["INTEGRITY"]
        nodeW["DICOM CHECKSUM HASH"]
        nodeX["IMMUTABLE AUDIT TRAIL"]
    end
    subgraph AVAIL["AVAILABILITY"]
        nodeY["RAID 6 STORAGE"]
        nodeZ["OFFSITE DR SITE"]
        nodeAA["LOAD BALANCER REDUNDANCY"]
    end
    nodeU --> PHIDATA
    nodeV --> PHIDATA
    nodeW --> PHIDATA
    nodeX --> PHIDATA
    nodeY --> PHIDATA
    nodeZ --> PHIDATA
    nodeAA --> PHIDATA
    PHIDATA["PROTECTED HEALTH INFORMATION PHI CENTRAL REPOSITORY"]
```
