<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between HL7 and DICOM standards. Why are both required in a Hospital Information System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HL7 | DICOM |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Health Level Seven | Digital Imaging & Communications in Medicine |
| OSI Layer | Layer 7 (Application) | Layer 7 (Application) + uses TCP/IP |
| Data Type | Textual/clinical data | Binary imaging data |
| Typical Message | Patient demographics, orders, reports | CT, MR, CR images + metadata |
| File Extension | N/A (stream-based) | `.dcm` |

**Why both are needed (1 mark):** HL7 cannot transport pixel data efficiently; DICOM cannot carry free-text clinical reports natively. A HIS uses **HL7 for textual workflow** and **DICOM for image archive (PACS)** — together they enable complete clinical interoperability.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of a Radiology Information System (RIS) in a hospital? List any four functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RIS is the workflow-management sub-system dedicated to the radiology department. Its **four primary functions** are:
1. **Patient scheduling** for imaging procedures.
2. **Exam tracking** — from arrival to report delivery.
3. **Report generation and storage** of radiologist interpretations.
4. **Billing integration** with the HIS billing module based on procedure codes.
5. *(optional)* Generates Modality Worklist (MWL) for DICOM-compliant modalities.
6. *(optional)* Tracks film/CD burning and image distribution.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Both Question A and Question B are provided below; the student answers **any ONE**.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Hospital Information System (HIS)**. Explain the functions of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **integration of HIS with RIS and PACS** using HL7 and DICOM standards. Illustrate with an example of a CT examination workflow. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

A modern HIS follows a **3-tier client–server architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Presentation Layer:** Includes the *Doctor Workstation*, *Nurse Tablet*, *Reception Kiosk* and *Admin Desk*. It interacts with the user through web/mobile interfaces using **HTML5, React, Angular** and **REST APIs**. Its function is to render the HIS screens, capture user input and display results.

**Layer 2 — Application / Logic Layer:** Houses the business rules — **HL7/FHIR message router**, **DICOM router**, **Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS)** and the **workflow orchestrator**. It validates orders, routes HL7 ORM/ORU messages, executes CDSS rules and synchronises state across modules.

**Layer 3 — Data Layer:** Stores the persistent data. The **Patient Database** (Oracle/PostgreSQL) holds demographics, orders and reports. The **PACS DICOM Archive** stores imaging studies. A **Data Warehouse** (OLAP) is used for analytics, and an **Audit Log Store** ensures tamper-evident records.

The layers communicate via secure TCP/IP, with **TLS 1.3** encryption. The architecture supports **horizontal scaling** — load balancers distribute requests across multiple application servers.

#### Model Solution — (b)

The HIS, RIS and PACS collaborate through a defined **workflow**:

1. Patient visits OPD; HIS registers and assigns a unique **MRN (Medical Record Number)**.
2. Doctor orders a CT scan → HIS sends **HL7 ORM (Order)** message to RIS.
3. RIS schedules the slot, generates an **Accession Number** and pushes the patient details into the **DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL)** of the CT scanner.
4. The CT scanner retrieves the worklist via **DICOM C-FIND** — eliminating manual entry errors.
5. After acquisition, the scanner sends images to PACS using **DICOM C-STORE**.
6. The radiologist opens the study in the PACS viewer via **DICOM WADO (Web Access to DICOM Objects)**.
7. The dictated report is sent back to HIS via **HL7 ORU (Observation Result)** and appears inline with the patient's EMR.

**Standards Bridge:** HL7 carries *textual* clinical data; DICOM carries *binary* image data. **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** profiles — particularly *Scheduled Workflow (SWF)* — formalise how these standards interoperate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **EMR with EHR** (EMR = single facility, EHR = interoperable). Lose 1 mark.
> 2. Saying *"HL7 stores images"* — **HL7 CANNOT store images**. Lose 2 marks.
> 3. Skipping the **Accession Number** linkage between RIS and PACS — examiners explicitly test this. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. Not mentioning **IHE profiles** — KTU 2024 syllabus lists them. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. Drawing PACS *outside* HIS — PACS is a **sub-system of HIS**, not a competitor.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** What is **HL7**? Explain the structure of an HL7 v2 message with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security challenges** in a Hospital Information System. Explain the **CIA Triad** and any four technical controls used to enforce it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**HL7 (Health Level Seven)** is an international standard (ISO/HL7 27931) for exchanging **clinical and administrative healthcare data** between heterogeneous information systems. It operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model.

**Structure of an HL7 v2 message:**
- A message is a **sequence of segments** separated by carriage-return `\r`.
- Each segment begins with a **3-character segment ID** (e.g., MSH, PID, OBR).
- Within a segment, **fields** are separated by the pipe delimiter `$\vert$`.
- **Components** within a field are separated by `^`.
- **Sub-components** by `&` and **repetitions** by `~`.

**Example — Patient Admission (ADT^A01):**

$$
\texttt{MSH$\vert$^~\&$\vert$EPR$\vert$HIS$\vert$RIS$\vert$PACS$\vert$20240520$\vert$1430$\vert$ADT^A01$\vert$MSG00001$\vert$P$\vert$2.5}
$$

$$
\texttt{PID$\vert$1$\vert$$\vert$$\vert$MRN12345$\vert$$\vert$RAJESH^KUMAR^M$\vert$$\vert$19850312$\vert$M}
$$

$$
\texttt{PV1$\vert$1$\vert$I$\vert$ICU^201^1$\vert$3$\vert$1234^DR^SMITH^A$\vert$$\vert$$\vert$$\vert$$\vert$3}
$$

Here:
- **MSH** = Message Header (sender HIS, receiver RIS, timestamp, message type).
- **PID** = Patient ID (MRN12345, name Rajesh Kumar, DOB 1985-03-12, gender M).
- **PV1** = Patient Visit (admitted to ICU, attending Dr. A. Smith).

This single message allows HIS to inform RIS that Mr. Rajesh has been admitted, triggering downstream workflow.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Security Challenges in HIS:**
- **Patient Privacy** — PHI must be protected from unauthorized access.
- **Cyber-Attacks** — Ransomware on hospitals has increased 300 % post-2020.
- **Insider Threats** — Curious employees snooping on celebrity records.
- **Interoperability Risk** — More integration points = larger attack surface.
- **Regulatory Compliance** — HIPAA, DISHA, GDPR fines can be enormous.

**The CIA Triad:**
1. **Confidentiality** — Only authorised users see PHI.
2. **Integrity** — Data is not altered maliciously or accidentally.
3. **Availability** — Systems are accessible 24×7.

**Four Technical Controls:**
1. **AES-256 encryption at rest** — protects databases and PACS archives even if disks are stolen.
2. **TLS 1.3 in transit** — protects HL7/DICOM traffic over the network.
3. **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — every clinician sees only the records relevant to their department.
4. **Audit Logs** — immutable, time-stamped trail of every PHI access — enables forensic investigation after a breach.

**Bonus Controls:** Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), DICOM file integrity checksums, intrusion detection systems (IDS), and a geographically separate **Disaster Recovery (DR) site**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Writing *"HIS security = antivirus"* — too superficial. Lose 2 marks.
> 2. Confusing **CIA Triad** with *confidentiality-insurance-availability*. The third is **Integrity**, not Insurance. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. Not providing **Indian context** (DISHA Act) when discussing regulations — KTU examiners prefer India-relevant examples. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. Writing HL7 segments without delimiters clearly labelled — examiners want the **pipe `$\vert$`, caret `^`, ampersand `&` and tilde `~`** hierarchy. Lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Information Systems in a Hospital**

- ☐ **HIS = Hospital-wide information system** integrating admin + clinical + financial modules.
- ☐ Know the **3-tier architecture** (Presentation / Application / Data) — guaranteed diagram question.
- ☐ **EMR ≠ EHR**: EMR is single-facility clinical; EHR is interoperable across providers.
- ☐ **RIS** manages radiology workflow; **PACS** stores DICOM images; they integrate via Accession Number.
- ☐ **HL7** = textual clinical data (Layer 7). **DICOM** = binary imaging data (Layer 7 + TCP/IP port 104).
- ☐ Remember **5 common HL7 message types**: ADT^A01, ORM^O01, ORU^R01, SIU^S12, MDM^T02.
- ☐ **DICOM C-STORE** sends images; **C-FIND** queries; **C-MOVE** retrieves; **WADO** is web-based.
- ☐ **IHE Profiles** (SWF, PIR) bridge HL7 & DICOM — mention them for full marks.
- ☐ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — never forget Integrity.
- ☐ Storage sizing formula: $S_{annual} = N_{day} \times D \times \bar{S}_{exam} \times k / CR$.
- ☐ Availability formula: $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$; **99.9 %** → 8.76 h downtime/year.
- ☐ Indian regulations to mention: **DISHA / DPDP Act 2023**, **AERB** (for radiation), **NABL** (for labs).
- ☐ Always conclude with **benefits**: paperless workflow, reduced errors, faster TAT, evidence-based care.
- ☐ Always mention **challenges**: cost, training, interoperability, cyber-security, change-management.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The DICOM Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The DICOM Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is a globally accepted, vendor-independent standard (developed by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association - NEMA**, currently maintained by the **DICOM Standards Committee**) for the storage, transmission, printing, processing, display, querying, and retrieval of medical imaging information. It is formally published as **NEMA Standard PS3** and is also recognized as **ISO 12052**.

The standard defines both:
1. A **file format** (for offline storage of medical images and related data).
2. A **network communication protocol** (for the exchange of medical images and associated information between devices, workstations, PACS servers, and modalities).

DICOM integrates patient demographic and study information with the image data itself into a single, self-contained object — meaning the image is no longer "anonymous" but travels with its complete clinical context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Universal Passport + Postal System" for Medical Images**

Imagine that hospitals all over the world take photographs (X-rays, MRIs, CT scans), but every camera brand (Siemens, GE, Philips, Toshiba) used to take pictures in a completely private, incompatible format — much like how early mobile phones couldn't text each other across different networks.

**DICOM is the "universal passport and language"** for medical images. Just like English serves as a common business language worldwide:

- Every DICOM image carries a **"passport"** (header tags) with the patient's name, ID, study date, modality, hospital, doctor, and image acquisition parameters.
- Every DICOM device speaks a **"common postal protocol"** so that a CT scanner from one company can reliably send images to a PACS server or workstation from another company.
- The image pixel data is wrapped in a structured **"envelope"** (the DICOM File Format or Network Message) that any compliant system can open.

Without DICOM, integrating a single patient's imaging history across multiple hospital systems would be nearly impossible. With DICOM, **interoperability** is guaranteed.

## 1.3 Standard Bodies and Governing Metrics

- **Standard Issuing Body:** NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association, USA)
- **International Recognition:** **ISO 12052:2017** (Health informatics — Digital imaging and communication in medicine (DICOM) including workflow and data management)
- **Current DICOM Version (as of recent publications):** PS3.x (continuously updated, with annual revisions)
- **File Extension:** `.dcm`
- **Default Port for Network Communication:** **Port 104** (TCP/IP, well-known DICOM port)
- **Earlier Name:** ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985) and ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988) — DICOM 3.0 was released in **1993**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to know *why* DICOM exists (interoperability), *what* it standardizes (file format + network protocol), and *how* it structures data (Information Object Definitions — IODs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Why DICOM Was Needed — The Problem of Interoperability

Before DICOM, medical imaging was essentially **vendor-locked**:
- Each manufacturer had proprietary image formats and transfer mechanisms.
- Cross-vendor integration (e.g., GE scanner to Siemens PACS) required expensive custom interfaces.
- Image data was often separated from patient demographic and clinical context.

DICOM solves this by providing:
- A **standardized file format** (`.dcm` file)
- A **standardized network protocol** (TCP/IP-based application-layer protocol)
- A **standardized information model** (patient → study → series → image hierarchy)
- A **standardized set of services** (Storage, Query/Retrieve, Print, Modality Worklist, etc.)
- A **standardized method of claiming compliance** (the DICOM Conformance Statement)

## 2.2 The DICOM Information Model — Patient/Study/Series/Image Hierarchy

DICOM organizes all medical imaging data in a strict **4-level real-world information model**:

1. **Patient Level** — Top of the hierarchy. Contains demographic information (Name, ID, Birth Date, Sex).
2. **Study Level** — A single diagnostic procedure performed on a patient (e.g., one CT chest exam). Contains Study UID, Study Date, Referring Physician.
3. **Series Level** — A logical grouping of images acquired under a common protocol (e.g., axial slices of a CT). Contains Modality, Series UID, Series Number.
4. **Image (Instance / SOP Instance) Level** — The individual image or frame. Contains the pixel data and the SOP Instance UID.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**P**lease **S**how **S**ome **I**mages" → **P**atient → **S**tudy → **S**eries → **I**mage.

This hierarchical model is enforced through **DICOM unique identifiers (UIDs)** which are unique strings of digits separated by periods, e.g., `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` (the standard CT Image Storage SOP Class UID).

## 2.3 Core Components of the DICOM Standard

The DICOM standard is divided into multiple parts (PS3.1 through PS3.22+). The major functional components are:

### 2.3.1 Information Object Definition (IOD)
An **IOD** is a data model describing the real-world entity involved in the information exchange. There are two types:
- **Normalized IOD** — Represents a single entity (e.g., a printed film, a results report).
- **Composite IOD** — Represents multiple related entities bundled together (e.g., a CT Image IOD bundles patient, study, series, equipment, and image data).

### 2.3.2 Service Classes
A **Service Class** defines the operations that may be performed on IODs. Examples:
- **Storage Service Class** — Push images from modality to PACS.
- **Query/Retrieve Service Class** — Search for and pull studies from PACS.
- **Modality Worklist (MWL) Service Class** — Modality queries the RIS/HIS for scheduled procedures.
- **Print Management Service Class** — Send images to a DICOM printer.
- **Storage Commitment Service Class** — PACS confirms it has taken ownership of stored images.

### 2.3.3 Service-Object Pair (SOP) Class
The combination of an **IOD** and a **Service Class** creates a **SOP Class**. It is the fundamental unit of DICOM interoperability. Each SOP Class is identified by a unique **SOP Class UID**.

### 2.3.4 Association and Presentation Contexts
Two DICOM Application Entities (AEs) negotiate communication through an **Association**. Within an association, **Presentation Contexts** specify which Transfer Syntaxes and SOP Classes are supported.

### 2.3.5 Transfer Syntax
A **Transfer Syntax** defines the encoding rules for the data, including:
- **Byte ordering** (Little Endian or Big Endian)
- **Compression** applied to pixel data (e.g., JPEG Lossless, JPEG 2000, RLE)

The default DICOM Transfer Syntax is **Implicit VR Little Endian** (UID `1.2.840.10008.1.2`).

## 2.4 The DICOM File Format

A DICOM file consists of:
1. **128-byte File Preamble** — Reserved, set to zeros (historically used to allow application-specific headers).
2. **4-byte "DICM" Magic Number** — Identifies the file as a valid DICOM file.
3. **File Meta Information** — Group 0002 elements (transfer syntax UID, media storage SOP class UID, etc.).
4. **Dataset** — Contains the actual data elements (Patient Name, Study Date, Pixel Data, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point:** If the first 4 bytes after the 128-byte preamble are not `"DICM"`, the file is **not** a DICOM file. This is the universal signature.

## 2.5 DICOM Network Communication — Protocol Stack

DICOM operates as an **Application Layer protocol** on top of the standard TCP/IP stack. Communication is initiated by the **Service Class User (SCU)** and the **Service Class Provider (SCP)** exchanging messages. The DICOM Upper Layer Protocol uses the **Association Control Service Element (ACSE)** and the **DICOM Message Service Element (DIMSE)** services.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet

| **Component** | **Description** | **Key Value / Identifier** |
|---|---|---|
| File Extension | DICOM file suffix | `.dcm` |
| Magic Number | 4-byte signature after preamble | `"DICM"` |
| File Preamble | Reserved header | **128 bytes** (zeros) |
| Default TCP Port | DICOM network port | **Port 104** |
| Default Transfer Syntax | Implicit VR Little Endian UID | `1.2.840.10008.1.2` |
| Explicit VR Little Endian UID | Common alternative | `1.2.840.10008.1.2.1` |
| Root UID for DICOM | Standard UID prefix | `1.2.840.10008` |
| Information Model Levels | Hierarchy of data | Patient → Study → Series → Image |
| Standard Body | NEMA / ISO | ISO 12052 |
| DICOM Version Origin | DICOM 3.0 released | **1993** |
| Conformance Proof | Vendor's capability declaration | DICOM Conformance Statement |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

DICOM is the **backbone of modern PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems)**, **RIS (Radiology Information Systems)**, **HIS (Hospital Information Systems)**, **telemedicine**, and **AI-driven diagnostic platforms**. Without DICOM, integrated electronic health records containing imaging would be infeasible. Engineers working in medical imaging device design, hospital IT, or AI healthcare must deeply understand DICOM to ensure that their systems can interoperate with the global ecosystem of compliant devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Structural Derivations

## 3.1 DICOM Data Element Structure — The Fundamental Building Block

Every piece of information in a DICOM dataset is encoded as a **Data Element**. Each Data Element has the following structure:

**Data Element = (Tag, Value Representation, Value Length, Value Field)**

### 3.1.1 Tag (Group Number, Element Number)
- A 32-bit identifier split into two 16-bit parts.
- **Group Number** (bits 1–16) — Categorizes the data (e.g., Group `0010` = Patient, Group `0028` = Image Presentation, Group `7FE0` = Pixel Data).
- **Element Number** (bits 17–32) — Identifies a specific attribute within the group.
- Written in hex as `(0010,0010)` for **Patient's Name**.

### 3.1.2 Value Representation (VR)
The **VR** specifies the data type and format of the value. Two encoding schemes exist:
- **Implicit VR** — VR is omitted from the file; the dictionary is consulted.
- **Explicit VR** — VR is included in the file as a 2-letter code.

Common VRs include:
- `PN` — Person Name
- `LO` — Long String
- `DA` — Date (YYYYMMDD)
- `TM` — Time (HHMMSS)
- `US` — Unsigned Short (2 bytes)
- `OW` — Other Word (used for pixel data)
- `OB` — Other Byte (used for encapsulated pixel data)

### 3.1.3 Value Length (VL)
The number of bytes in the Value Field. Encoded as either a 16-bit or 32-bit unsigned integer depending on the VR.

### 3.1.4 Value Field
The actual data of that attribute. The number of bytes is given by VL.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Decoding a DICOM Data Element

Consider the data element for **Patient's Name** (`(0010,0010)`) with the value `"DOE^JOHN"` using the **Explicit VR Little Endian** transfer syntax.

**Step 1 — Identify the Tag:**

$$\text{Tag} = (0010,0010)_{16} = \text{Patient's Name}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Value Representation:**

For Explicit VR, the VR for Patient's Name is `PN` (Person Name), encoded as the ASCII characters `'P'` and `'N'`.

$$\text{VR} = \text{`PN'}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Value Length:**

The string `"DOE^JOHN"` consists of 8 characters. In Explicit VR, the VL field is a 16-bit unsigned integer (2 bytes) for short VRs:

$$\text{VL} = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Encode the Value Field:**

Each character is stored in its ASCII byte representation:

$$\text{Value Field} = \{0x44, 0x4F, 0x45, 0x5E, 0x4A, 0x4F, 0x48, 0x4E\}$$

which corresponds to ASCII: `D` `O` `E` `^` `J` `O` `H` `N`.

**Step 5 — Assemble the Full Data Element in Little Endian byte order:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Full Data Element} &= \underbrace{0x10, 0x00}_{\text{Group (LE)}} , \underbrace{0x10, 0x00}_{\text{Element (LE)}}, \\
&\quad \underbrace{0x50, 0x4E}_{\text{VR = 'P','N'}}, \\
&\quad \underbrace{0x08, 0x00}_{\text{VL = 8 (LE)}}, \\
&\quad \underbrace{0x44, 0x4F, 0x45, 0x5E, 0x4A, 0x4F, 0x48, 0x4E}_{\text{Value = "DOE\^JOHN"}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total bytes = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 8 = 16 bytes.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Little-Endian byte ordering means multi-byte numeric fields (Group, Element, VL) are stored with the **least significant byte first**. This is the dominant byte order in DICOM (matching x86 architecture).

## 3.3 Pixel Data Encoding — The Bulk of the Image

The most important and largest data element in a DICOM image is `(7FE0,0010)` — **Pixel Data**. This element is encoded with VR `OW` (Other Word) for native pixel data, or `OB` (Other Byte) for encapsulated (compressed) pixel data.

### 3.3.1 Bits Allocated, Bits Stored, and Pixel Representation

These critical Image-related data elements determine how to interpret the pixel data:

- **(0028,0100) Bits Allocated** — Number of bits used to store each pixel (typically **8** or **16**).
- **(0028,0101) Bits Stored** — Number of bits actually used to represent the pixel value (e.g., 12 for CT).
- **(0028,0103) Pixel Representation** — `0` for unsigned, `1` for signed (2's complement).
- **(0028,0002) Samples per Pixel** — 1 for grayscale, 3 for color (RGB).

### 3.3.2 Memory Footprint Calculation for a DICOM Image

For a typical CT slice, the data footprint is:

$$\text{Memory} = \text{Rows} \times \text{Columns} \times \text{Bits Allocated} \times \text{Samples per Pixel}$$

**Example Calculation:** A CT slice with 512 × 512 pixels, 16 bits allocated, 1 sample per pixel:

$$\text{Memory} = 512 \times 512 \times 16 \times 1 \text{ bits}$$

Converting to bytes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory (bytes)} &= \frac{512 \times 512 \times 16 \times 1}{8} \\
&= \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{8} \\
&= 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 512 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

For a 500-slice CT series, the total raw image memory is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Series Memory} &= 500 \times 512 \text{ KB} \\
&= 256{,}000 \text{ KB} \\
&= 250 \text{ MB (approximately)}
\end{aligned}$$

This illustrates why **compression** (JPEG Lossless, JPEG 2000, etc.) is critical in DICOM and why **Storage Commitment** and **Query/Retrieve** services must be engineered for high throughput.

## 3.4 SOP Class UID Hierarchy — Identifying the Image Type

DICOM uses globally unique **SOP Class UIDs** to identify what kind of image is being transmitted. Each UID follows the ISO 8824 OID (Object Identifier) structure.

**Examples of standard SOP Class UIDs:**

| **SOP Class** | **SOP Class UID** |
|---|---|
| Computed Radiography Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.1` |
| CT Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` |
| MR Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4` |
| Ultrasound Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.6.1` |
| Secondary Capture Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.7` |
| PET Image Storage | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.128` |
| Verification SOP Class (Echo) | `1.2.840.10008.1.1` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The **Verification SOP Class** (`1.2.840.10008.1.1`) is used as a simple "ping" to test DICOM connectivity between two Application Entities — analogous to the ICMP echo (ping) in TCP/IP networking.

## 3.5 DICOM Association Negotiation — A Pseudo-Protocol Trace

When two DICOM devices wish to communicate, the following exchange occurs:

**Step 1 — A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Association Request)**
The SCU proposes:
- Its **Application Entity Title (AET)**
- A list of **Presentation Contexts**, each containing a **SOP Class UID** and a list of **supported Transfer Syntax UIDs**

**Step 2 — A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Association Accept) or A-ASSOCIATE-RJ (Reject)**
The SCP accepts or rejects each presentation context.

**Step 3 — DIMSE Messages (e.g., C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-ECHO)**
Data exchange occurs.

**Step 4 — A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP**
Graceful teardown of the association.

This protocol design ensures that **only mutually understood SOP Classes and Transfer Syntaxes** are used, preserving interoperability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DICOM Information Model — Real-World Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Patient → Study → Series → Image** information hierarchy that forms the backbone of the DICOM data model.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Patient Level] --> B[Study Level]
    B --> C1[Series Level 1]
    B --> C2[Series Level 2]
    B --> C3[Series Level N]
    C1 --> D1[Image Instance 1]
    C1 --> D2[Image Instance 2]
    C1 --> D3[Image Instance N]
    C2 --> E1[Image Instance 1]
    C2 --> E2[Image Instance 2]
    C3 --> F1[Image Instance 1]

    A --- A1[Patient ID]
    A --- A2[Patient Name]
    A --- A3[Birth Date]
    A --- A4[Sex]

    B --- B1[Study UID]
    B --- B2[Study Date]
    B --- B3[Referring Physician]
    B --- B4[Study Description]

    C1 --- C1A[Modality CT]
    C1 --- C1B[Series UID]
    C1 --- C1C[Series Number]

    D1 --- D1A[SOP Instance UID]
    D1 --- D1B[Pixel Data 7FE0 0010]
    D1 --- D1C[Rows and Columns]
    D1 --- D1D[Bits Allocated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each `Image Instance` is uniquely identified by its **SOP Instance UID** and contains the actual pixel data (the largest data element). All instances within a series share a common acquisition protocol.

## 4.2 DICOM Network Communication Architecture

The following block diagram depicts how two DICOM Application Entities (e.g., a CT scanner and a PACS server) establish communication and exchange medical images.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SCU[Application Entity SCU CT Scanner] --> ASSOC1[A-ASSOCIATE-RQ]
    ASSOC1 --> ASSOC2[A-ASSOCIATE-AC]
    ASSOC2 --> DIMSE[DIMSE Message Exchange]
    DIMSE --> DIMSE1[C-ECHO Verification]
    DIMSE --> DIMSE2[C-STORE Image Push]
    DIMSE --> DIMSE3[C-FIND Query]
    DIMSE --> DIMSE4[C-MOVE Retrieve]
    DIMSE1 --> RELEASE[A-RELEASE-RQ]
    DIMSE2 --> RELEASE
    DIMSE3 --> RELEASE
    DIMSE4 --> RELEASE
    RELEASE --> RELEASERESP[A-RELEASE-RP]
    RELEASERESP --> DONE[Connection Closed]

    SCP[Application Entity SCP PACS Server] -.-> ASSOC1
    SCP -.-> ASSOC2
    SCP -.-> DIMSE
    SCP -.-> RELEASE
    SCP -.-> RELEASERESP
    SCP -.-> DONE
```

## 4.3 DICOM File Format — Block Architecture

The following block diagram shows the layered architecture of a DICOM file on disk.

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[128-Byte File Preamble] --> F2[4-Byte DICM Magic Number]
    F2 --> F3[File Meta Information Group 0002]
    F3 --> F4[Dataset Group 0008 onwards]
    F4 --> D1[Patient Module 0010]
    F4 --> D2[Study Module 0020]
    F4 --> D3[Series Module 0028]
    F4 --> D4[Image Module 0028 and 7FE0]
    D4 --> P1[Pixel Data 7FE0 0010]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Detail:** If the bytes at offset 128 to 131 do **not** spell `"DICM"`, the file does not comply with the DICOM standard — regardless of any `.dcm` extension. This is the first thing a DICOM parser checks.

## 4.4 DICOM Service Class Ecosystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    SC[Service Classes] --> ST[Storage Class]
    SC --> QR[Query Retrieve Class]
    SC --> MWL[Modality Worklist Class]
    SC --> PM[Print Management Class]
    SC --> SC2[Storage Commitment Class]
    SC --> MPPS[Modality Performed Procedure Step]

    ST --> SOP1[SOP Class: CT Image Storage UID 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2]
    QR --> SOP2[SOP Class: Patient Root Query Retrieve Information Model]
    MWL --> SOP3[SOP Class: Modality Worklist Information Model FIND]
    PM --> SOP4[SOP Class: Basic Grayscale Print Management Meta SOP]
    SC2 --> SOP5[SOP Class: Storage Commitment Push Model]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions expect crisp, definition-based answers. Include key UIDs, ports, or standard values wherever applicable to score full marks.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define DICOM. Mention its full form and the standard body responsible for its development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DICOM stands for **Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine**. It is a globally accepted, vendor-independent standard for the storage, transmission, printing, and retrieval of medical imaging information. It is developed and maintained by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)**, and is also recognized internationally as **ISO 12052**. The current widely deployed version, DICOM 3.0, was released in **1993** and unifies the earlier ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985) and ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988) standards. DICOM standardizes both a file format and a network communication protocol, ensuring interoperability between imaging devices from different manufacturers.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]
**List the four levels of the DICOM Information Model in order, and state the default TCP port for DICOM network communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four levels of the DICOM Information Model, in hierarchical order from top to bottom, are:

1. **Patient Level** — Contains demographic data (name, ID, sex, birth date).
2. **Study Level** — Represents a single diagnostic imaging procedure.
3. **Series Level** — A logical group of related images from one acquisition.
4. **Image (SOP Instance) Level** — The individual image containing pixel data.

The default **TCP port** for DICOM network communication is **port 104**. This well-known port is registered with IANA for DICOM Upper Layer Protocol traffic.

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Structure your answer with clear headings, diagrams, and tabular comparisons to score full marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**Explain the DICOM standard in detail. Include in your answer:**
- **(a) The motivation and historical evolution of DICOM (7 marks).**
- **(b) The DICOM Information Model with a neat block diagram (7 marks).**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Motivation and Historical Evolution of DICOM (7 marks):**

Before DICOM, medical imaging faced a critical **interoperability crisis**. Each imaging modality manufacturer — Siemens, GE, Philips, Toshiba, Hitachi — used proprietary image formats, storage media, and transfer protocols. This led to:

- **Vendor lock-in** — Hospitals were forced to purchase all equipment from a single vendor to ensure compatibility.
- **Isolated image data** — Patient imaging data was separated from demographic and clinical context.
- **Expensive custom interfaces** — Integrating devices from multiple vendors required bespoke, costly software development.

To address this, the **American College of Radiology (ACR)** and the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** jointly developed the original standards:

- **ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985)** — Defined a point-to-point hardware interface and a 16-bit data dictionary; the first attempt at standardization.
- **ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988)** — Improved the data dictionary and added more command support; still used a proprietary network layer.
- **DICOM 3.0 (1993)** — A major architectural overhaul. It introduced:
  - Independence from specific network hardware (adopted **TCP/IP** as the transport).
  - Explicit object-oriented **Information Object Definitions (IODs)**.
  - Service-based architecture with **Service Classes**.
  - **Composite IODs** combining image and patient data.
  - Conformance Statements to declare a vendor's level of compliance.

Today, **DICOM 3.0 is the de facto global standard**, recognized as **ISO 12052:2017**, and is continuously maintained by the **DICOM Standards Committee** with annual public releases that incorporate new modalities (e.g., digital pathology, ophthalmology), compression schemes (e.g., JPEG XL), and workflows (e.g., DICOMweb for RESTful services).

**(b) DICOM Information Model (7 marks):**

The DICOM Information Model is a hierarchical data model that organizes all medical imaging information into four real-world levels, illustrated in the block diagram below:

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Patient Level Group 0010] --> L2[Study Level Group 0020]
    L2 --> L3a[Series Level Group 0028 etc.]
    L2 --> L3b[Series Level Group 0028 etc.]
    L3a --> L4a[Image Instance SOP Instance]
    L3a --> L4b[Image Instance SOP Instance]
    L3b --> L4c[Image Instance SOP Instance]
```

- **Patient Level (Group 0010):** Contains demographic information. Key tags include `(0010,0010)` Patient Name, `(0010,0020)` Patient ID, `(0010,0030)` Birth Date, `(0010,0040)` Sex.
- **Study Level (Group 0020):** Represents a single diagnostic imaging procedure. Key tags include `(0020,000D)` Study Instance UID, `(0008,0020)` Study Date, `(0008,0090)` Referring Physician's Name.
- **Series Level (Group 0020, 0028, etc.):** A logical group of images acquired under one protocol. Key tags include `(0020,000E)` Series Instance UID, `(0008,0060)` Modality, `(0020,0011)` Series Number.
- **Image / Instance Level (Group 0028, 7FE0, etc.):** The individual image. Key tags include `(0008,0018)` SOP Instance UID, `(0028,0010)` Rows, `(0028,0011)` Columns, `(0028,0100)` Bits Allocated, and `(7FE0,0010)` Pixel Data.

This hierarchy is enforced by **Unique Identifiers (UIDs)** that are globally unique strings of digits. For example, a CT Image Storage SOP Instance has a UID like `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2.2024.1.1`.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**
**(a) Describe the architecture of the DICOM File Format with a neat block diagram. Mention the role of the DICM magic number. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the DICOM Network Protocol. Describe the role of SOP Classes, Transfer Syntaxes, and the Association Negotiation process. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) DICOM File Format Architecture (7 marks):**

The DICOM file format is the standardized structure used to store medical images and related information on disk. Its architecture consists of the following parts in sequence:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[128-Byte File Preamble] --> B[4-Byte DICM Magic]
    B --> C[File Meta Information Group 0002]
    C --> D[Dataset Header Attributes]
    D --> E[Patient Module Group 0010]
    D --> F[Study and Series Modules Groups 0020 0028]
    D --> G[Image and Pixel Data Module Groups 0028 and 7FE0]
    G --> H[Pixel Data 7FE0 0010 with VR OW or OB]
```

- **128-Byte File Preamble:** Reserved area, traditionally zero-filled. Allows applications to embed application-specific headers without breaking DICOM compliance (though the 4-byte DICM must still be present).
- **4-Byte "DICM" Magic Number:** Located at byte offset 128. The bytes must literally be the ASCII characters `'D'`, `'I'`, `'C'`, `'M'`. This is the **signature** that identifies the file as a valid DICOM file. Any DICOM parser first checks for this signature before attempting to read the rest of the file.
- **File Meta Information (Group 0002):** Encoded with **Explicit VR Little Endian** transfer syntax, regardless of the dataset's transfer syntax. Contains essential metadata like `(0002,0000)` File Meta Information Group Length, `(0002,0010)` Transfer Syntax UID (which then specifies how the remaining dataset is encoded), and `(0002,0002)` Media Storage SOP Class UID.
- **Dataset (Group 0008 onwards):** Contains the actual data elements — patient, study, series, image attributes, and the pixel data. The encoding of the dataset is governed by the Transfer Syntax declared in the File Meta Information.

The **role of the DICM magic number** is to:
1. Serve as a **definitive signature** distinguishing DICOM files from arbitrary binary data or other medical formats.
2. Indicate the **byte offset** at which the File Meta Information begins.
3. Enable **parsers** to reject non-DICOM files quickly and reliably.

**(b) DICOM Network Protocol (7 marks):**

The DICOM network protocol operates as an **Application Layer protocol** over **TCP/IP** on **port 104** (well-known port registered with IANA). It enables any two DICOM-compliant devices (e.g., a CT scanner and a PACS server) to exchange medical images and related data.

**Core Concepts:**

1. **Application Entity (AE):** A software process running on a DICOM device. It is identified by an **Application Entity Title (AET)**, e.g., `"CT_SCANNER_01"`. Each AE can act as a **Service Class User (SCU)** (the requester) or **Service Class Provider (SCP)** (the responder) for various services.

2. **SOP Class (Service-Object Pair Class):** A SOP Class is the **fundamental unit of DICOM interoperability**. It is the unique combination of:
   - An **Information Object Definition (IOD)** — describing the type of data (e.g., CT Image, MR Image, Print Job).
   - A **Service Class** — describing what can be done with the data (e.g., Store, Query, Retrieve).
   - Each SOP Class is identified by a globally unique **SOP Class UID** (e.g., CT Image Storage = `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`).

3. **Transfer Syntax:** Specifies the **byte ordering** and **compression** of the data being exchanged. Common transfer syntaxes include Implicit VR Little Endian (`1.2.840.10008.1.2`), Explicit VR Little Endian (`1.2.840.10008.1.2.1`), and various JPEG / JPEG 2000 compressed syntaxes.

4. **Association Negotiation Process:** Before any data exchange occurs, the SCU and SCP must establish a **DICOM Association** through the following exchange:

   - **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Request):** The SCU proposes its own AET, the SCP's AET, the application context name, and a list of **Presentation Contexts**. Each Presentation Context proposes one SOP Class and a list of acceptable Transfer Syntaxes.
   - **A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Accept) or A-ASSOCIATE-RJ (Reject):** The SCP either accepts (choosing exactly one Transfer Syntax per accepted Presentation Context) or rejects the association.
   - **DIMSE Message Exchange:** With the association established, the SCU and SCP exchange **DIMSE messages** such as C-ECHO (verification), C-STORE (image push), C-FIND (query), and C-MOVE (retrieve).
   - **A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP:** The association is gracefully closed.

This negotiation ensures that **only mutually understood data types and encodings** are used, guaranteeing interoperability across vendors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse ACR-NEMA versions with DICOM 3.0 — Examiners deduct marks if you say "DICOM was released in 1985." The correct date is **1993** for DICOM 3.0.
> 2. **Always** mention the **DICM magic number** and its byte offset (128) in file format questions.
> 3. **Always** state the **default TCP port 104** in network-related questions.
> 4. When drawing the Information Model, label all four levels **Patient → Study → Series → Image** in the correct order.
> 5. In SOP Class questions, **explicitly cite the UID** (e.g., `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` for CT) — examiners award 1–2 marks for this precision.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DICOM** = **Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine**
- **Standard Body:** NEMA (USA), recognized as **ISO 12052**
- **DICOM 3.0 released in 1993** (succeeding ACR-NEMA 1.0 [1985] and 2.0 [1988])
- **File extension:** `.dcm`
- **Default TCP port:** **104**
- **File structure:** 128-byte preamble + 4-byte **`"DICM"`** magic + File Meta Information (Group 0002) + Dataset
- **Default Transfer Syntax:** **Implicit VR Little Endian** (`1.2.840.10008.1.2`)
- **Information Model Hierarchy (Top → Bottom):** **Patient → Study → Series → Image**
- **SOP Class** = IOD + Service Class (the fundamental unit of DICOM interoperability)
- **CT Image Storage SOP Class UID:** `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`
- **MR Image Storage SOP Class UID:** `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4`
- **Verification (C-ECHO) SOP Class UID:** `1.2.840.10008.1.1` (used for connectivity testing)
- **Association** = negotiated communication session between SCU and SCP
- **Presentation Contexts** = list of SOP Classes + Transfer Syntaxes proposed during association
- **DIMSE Services:** C-ECHO, C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET
- **Pixel Data tag:** `(7FE0,0010)`, VR `OW` (native) or `OB` (encapsulated/compressed)
- **VR** = Value Representation (data type, e.g., `PN`, `DA`, `US`, `OW`)
- **Conformance Statement** = vendor document declaring DICOM compliance features
- **Real-world utility:** PACS, RIS, HIS integration, telemedicine, AI-based imaging
- **Common Pitfall:** Always differentiate between **Composite IOD** (multi-entity, e.g., image with patient info) and **Normalized IOD** (single-entity, e.g., print queue).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Establishing DICOM Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT745 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING & IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Establishing DICOM Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is the international standard (ISO 12052 / NEMA Standard PS3) for transmitting, storing, retrieving, printing, processing, and displaying medical imaging information. It defines a **network communication protocol** based on the TCP/IP stack, ensuring interoperability between medical imaging devices manufactured by different vendors.

**DICOM Connectivity** refers to the complete process of configuring, negotiating, and establishing a logical communication channel (called an **Association**) between two **Application Entities (AE)** so that they can exchange standardized medical imaging data over a network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> "Establishing DICOM Connectivity involves the verification, configuration, and operational linking of DICOM-compliant nodes (modalities, PACS, workstations) using Application Entity Titles, Hostnames/IP addresses, and Port numbers through the A-ASSOCIATE protocol."

## 1.2 Fundamental Components of DICOM Connectivity

| Component | Description | Standard Value |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Application Entity (AE)** | A software process on a DICOM device that acts as a client or server | Vendor-defined name |
| **AE Title** | Unique 16-character identifier for an Application Entity | Max **16 uppercase characters** |
| **IP Address** | Network address of the host running the AE | IPv4 (e.g., 192.168.1.10) |
| **Port Number** | TCP port used for DICOM communication | **104** (well-known DICOM port) |
| **Presentation Context** | Proposed combination of SOP Class + Transfer Syntax | Abstract Syntax + Transfer Syntax UID |
| **Transfer Syntax** | Encoding rules (JPEG, JPEG-LS, RLE, Implicit/Explicit VR) | Defined by UID |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy: The "International Phone Call"**

Imagine you want to call a colleague in Japan, but you both speak different languages:

- **AE Title** → The colleague's **name registered in the phonebook** (unique identifier)
- **IP Address + Port** → The colleague's **phone number and extension**
- **Association Negotiation** → A short conversation: *"Can you speak English? Yes. Can you accept PDF files? Yes."* (Agreeing on a common language and document format)
- **Presentation Context** → The **agreed-upon format** for the conversation
- **C-ECHO Service** → A **"Can you hear me?"** test ping
- **C-STORE Service** → **Sending the actual document (image)** over the line
- **Association Release** → Both parties saying **"Goodbye, call ended"** (A-RELEASE)

Just as both parties must agree on language before speaking, two DICOM devices must negotiate **Presentation Contexts** (what image type and compression) before exchanging data. Without successful negotiation, **no data transfer can occur**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams):**
> - **Well-Known DICOM TCP Port:** **104** (IANA registered)
> - **Default Maximum PDU Length:** **16,384 bytes** (negotiable up to **2^32 - 1**)
> - **AE Title Max Length:** **16 characters** (case-sensitive)
> - **Default Implicit Transfer Syntax:** **1.2.840.10008.1.2**
> - **Default Explicit VR Little Endian:** **1.2.840.10008.1.2.1**
> - **DICOM Standard Version (Current):** **PS3-2024a**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The DICOM Protocol Stack

DICOM operates as an **Upper Layer Protocol (ULP)** riding on top of the standard **TCP/IP** stack. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests understanding of this layered architecture.

**Layer-wise breakdown:**

- **Layer 7 – Application Layer (DICOM Upper Layer / DUL)**
  - Defines Application Entities, Services, and Information Objects
  - Handles Association establishment, maintenance, and termination
  - Encodes messages using DIMSE (DICOM Message Service Element) protocol
- **Layer 6 – Presentation Layer (DICOM)**
  - Handles Abstract Syntax (SOP Class UID) and Transfer Syntax
  - Negotiates Presentation Contexts
  - Implements SOP (Service-Object Pair) Classes
- **Layer 5 – Session Layer (DICOM Upper Layer State Machine)**
  - Manages DICOM Association State Machine
  - States: Idle → Awaiting Association → Association Established → Awaiting Release
- **Layer 4 – Transport Layer (TCP)**
  - Provides reliable, connection-oriented byte stream
  - Default port **104**
  - Uses the **ARTIM (Association Request/Reject/Release Timer)** for timeouts
- **Layer 3 – Network Layer (IP)**
  - Standard IPv4/IPv6 addressing
  - No special DICOM-specific packet structure
- **Layer 2 – Data Link Layer (Ethernet)**
  - Standard Ethernet framing
  - No DICOM-specific modifications
- **Layer 1 – Physical Layer**
  - Cat5e/Cat6/Fiber optic cables
  - Wireless (Wi-Fi 802.11ac/ax) supported

## 2.2 Operational Steps for Establishing DICOM Connectivity

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6-Phase DICOM Association Lifecycle (High-Yield for KTU 2024):**

### Phase 1 — **Device Configuration (Pre-Association)**
- Both devices must have the DICOM software installed and running
- Each device is assigned a unique **AE Title**
- Network parameters configured: **IP Address, Subnet Mask, Default Gateway, Port 104**
- **DICOM Conformance Statement** of both devices must be cross-checked for compatibility

### Phase 2 — **TCP Connection Establishment (Transport Handshake)**
- The **Requesting AE (SCU – Service Class User)** initiates a TCP SYN to the **Responding AE (SCP – Service Class Provider)** on port **104**
- Standard **3-way TCP handshake**: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK
- After successful TCP connection, the DICOM Upper Layer takes over

### Phase 3 — **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Association Request)**
- The SCU sends an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU** containing:
  - Protocol Version (**1**)
  - Called AE Title (Destination)
  - Calling AE Title (Source)
  - Application Context Name: **1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1** (always fixed)
  - One or more **Presentation Contexts** (Abstract Syntax UID + list of Transfer Syntaxes)
  - **Maximum Length** sub-item
  - **User Information** sub-item (Implementation UID, Version, etc.)

### Phase 4 — **A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Association Accept) or A-ASSOCIATE-RJ (Reject)**
- The SCP evaluates the request
- **If accepted** → sends A-ASSOCIATE-AC with agreed Presentation Contexts
- **If rejected** → sends A-ASSOCIATE-RJ with one of the following reject reasons:
  - **Permanent** (no retry): 1 (no-reason), 2 (application-context-name-not-supported), 3 (calling-AE-title-not-recognized), 4-7 (various)
  - **Transient** (retry possible): 1-2 (temporary congestion, local limit exceeded)

### Phase 5 — **Data Exchange (DIMSE Messages)**
- Once Association is established, DIMSE messages flow:
  - **C-ECHO-RQ / C-ECHO-RSP** → Verification (ping)
  - **C-STORE-RQ / C-STORE-RSP** → Send image
  - **C-FIND-RQ / C-FIND-RSP** → Query
  - **C-MOVE-RQ / C-MOVE-RSP** → Retrieve
  - **C-GET-RQ / C-GET-RSP** → Pull image

### Phase 6 — **A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP (Graceful Termination)**
- Either side sends **A-RELEASE-RQ**
- The other side responds with **A-RELEASE-RP**
- TCP connection is closed via standard **FIN handshake**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield DICOM Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 University Exams**

| **Parameter** | **Equation / Value** | **Unit** | **Significance** |
|---------------|----------------------|----------|------------------|
| Default DICOM TCP Port | $P_{DICOM} = 104$ | (port number) | IANA-assigned well-known port |
| Maximum PDU Length | $L_{max} \in [16384,\ 2^{32}-1]$ | bytes | Negotiable per Association |
| AE Title Length | $L_{AE} \le 16$ | characters | Uppercase, no spaces recommended |
| Maximum Presentation Contexts per Association | $N_{PC} \le 255$ | contexts | 1-byte ID: 1–255 |
| Number of Transfer Syntaxes per Context | $N_{TS} \le 127$ | (count) | Listed in order of preference |
| TCP Timeout (Default) | $T_{ARTIM} = 30$ s | seconds | Adjustable |
| **Data Throughput Estimate** | $\Theta = \dfrac{L_{max}}{RTT}$ | bytes/second | RTT = Round-Trip Time |
| PDU Header Size | $H_{PDU} = 6$ | bytes | 1 byte type + 1 byte reserved + 4 bytes length |
| **Application Context UID** | 1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1 | UID | Always constant, non-negotiable |
| **Explicit VR Little Endian UID** | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 | UID | Most commonly negotiated |

**Real-world engineering utility:** This protocol stack is the **backbone of every hospital PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)**, enabling a CT scanner from Siemens to seamlessly send images to a PACS server from GE, which can then be viewed on a diagnostic workstation from Philips or Agfa.

## 2.4 Why "Why" and "How" — The Deep Reasoning

> [!TIP]
> **Why does DICOM need its own Upper Layer on top of TCP?**
> - TCP provides a **byte stream** but no concept of "messages" or "commands"
> - DICOM needs structured request/response semantics (DIMSE)
> - DICOM requires vendor-independent negotiation of **what data** and **how it is encoded**
> - TCP alone cannot enforce the state machine required for medical workflows

> [!TIP]
> **How is reliability guaranteed?**
> - TCP ensures byte-level reliability
> - DIMSE adds **message-level acknowledgment** (C-STORE-RSP)
> - The **ARTIM timer** prevents indefinite waiting for a non-responsive peer
> - **P-DATA-TF PDUs** carry the actual image data with proper framing

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: Establishing a DICOM Association (C-ECHO Example)

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 examination scenario** — the "DICOM Ping" using the C-ECHO Verification SOP Class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario:** A CT scanner (AE Title = `CT_SCANNER_01`, IP = `192.168.1.10`) wants to verify connectivity with a PACS server (AE Title = `PACS_MAIN`, IP = `192.168.1.50`, Port = `104`).

### Step 1: TCP 3-Way Handshake

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CT Scanner} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN, Seq=x}} \text{PACS} \\
\text{PACS} &\xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, Seq=y, Ack=x+1}} \text{CT Scanner} \\
\text{CT Scanner} &\xrightarrow{\text{ACK, Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1}} \text{PACS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- The CT scanner (client) sends a SYN packet with an initial sequence number $x$
- The PACS server replies with SYN-ACK, acknowledging $x+1$ and sending its own sequence $y$
- The CT scanner confirms with an ACK, completing the connection
- The TCP connection is now **ESTABLISHED** at the transport level

### Step 2: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU Construction

The CT scanner now constructs an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU** with the following structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDU} &= \{ \text{Type}=0x01,\ \text{Reserved}=0x00,\ \text{Length}=N \} \\
\text{Body} &= \{ \text{ProtocolVer}=1,\ \text{CalledAE}=\text{"PACS\_MAIN"},\ \text{CallingAE}=\text{"CT\_SCANNER\_01"}, \\
&\quad \text{AppContext}=1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1,\ \text{PresentationContexts} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- PDU Type = 0x01 (binary value for A-ASSOCIATE-RQ)
- The **Called AE Title** identifies the destination (`PACS_MAIN`)
- The **Calling AE Title** identifies the source (`CT_SCANNER_01`)
- The **Application Context Name** is the fixed UID `1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1`
- For C-ECHO, exactly **one Presentation Context** is proposed with:
  - **Abstract Syntax (SOP Class):** `1.2.840.10008.1.1` (Verification SOP Class)
  - **Transfer Syntaxes proposed:** `1.2.840.10008.1.2` (Implicit VR Little Endian), `1.2.840.10008.1.2.1` (Explicit VR Little Endian)

### Step 3: A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Accept) by PACS

The PACS server evaluates the request and sends an **A-ASSOCIATE-AC PDU**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDU} &= \{ \text{Type}=0x02,\ \text{Length}=M \} \\
\text{Body} &= \{ \text{AppContext}=\text{accepted},\ \text{PresentationContext}[1]=\text{accepted}, \\
&\quad \text{MaxLength}=16384,\ \text{ImplementationUID}=\text{"1.2.840.x.y"} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- PDU Type = 0x02 (A-ASSOCIATE-AC)
- The PACS accepts the proposed Presentation Context
- It selects **one of the proposed Transfer Syntaxes** (usually Explicit VR Little Endian)
- It declares its **Maximum Length** (typically 16,384 bytes)
- Both AEs are now in the **"Association Established"** state

### Step 4: C-ECHO-RQ Message Exchange

The CT scanner now sends a **C-ECHO-RQ** DIMSE message:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Command Set} &= \{ \text{Group}=0x0000,\ \text{CommandField}=0x0030,\ \text{MessageID}=1, \\
&\quad \text{CommandDataSetType}=0x0101\ (\text{no dataset}),\ \text{AffectedSOPClassUID}=1.2.840.10008.1.1 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- **Group 0000** identifies this as a Command element
- **CommandField 0x0030** identifies it as a C-ECHO-RQ
- **MessageID = 1** is a unique identifier (correlates request/response)
- **AffectedSOPClassUID** = Verification SOP Class UID

The PACS responds with **C-ECHO-RSP** (CommandField = 0x8030) and **Status = 0x0000** (Success).

### Step 5: A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP (Termination)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CT Scanner} &\xrightarrow{A\text{-}RELEASE\text{-}RQ\ (\text{Type}=0x05)} \text{PACS} \\
\text{PACS} &\xrightarrow{A\text{-}RELEASE\text{-}RP\ (\text{Type}=0x06)} \text{CT Scanner}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- Type 0x05 = A-RELEASE-RQ
- Type 0x06 = A-RELEASE-RP
- TCP connection is then closed via standard FIN handshake

## 3.2 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix: DICOM PDU Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU 2024 — All 7 PDU Types You Must Know**

| **PDU Type Code** | **PDU Name** | **Hex Code** | **Purpose** | **Initiator** |
|-------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| A-ASSOCIATE-RQ | Association Request | **0x01** | Initiate connection | SCU |
| A-ASSOCIATE-AC | Association Accept | **0x02** | Accept connection | SCP |
| A-ASSOCIATE-RJ | Association Reject | **0x03** | Reject with reason | SCP |
| P-DATA-TF | Data Transfer | **0x04** | Carry DIMSE messages + image data | Both |
| A-RELEASE-RQ | Release Request | **0x05** | Graceful termination | Both |
| A-RELEASE-RP | Release Response | **0x06** | Confirm release | Both |
| A-ABORT | Abort | **0x07** | Ungraceful termination | Both |

## 3.3 Analytical Computation Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU Numerical Question Style:** Suppose a PACS server has a Maximum PDU Length of **65,536 bytes** and a network RTT of **10 ms**. Calculate the theoretical maximum throughput for a single Association.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Theta_{max} &= \frac{L_{max}}{RTT} \\
&= \frac{65{,}536 \text{ bytes}}{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} \\
&= 6{,}553{,}600 \text{ bytes/second} \\
&= 6.25 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With a 10 ms RTT and 64 KB PDUs, the upper-layer DICOM protocol can theoretically push **6.25 MB/s** of medical image data per Association. For a typical 512 × 512 × 16-bit CT slice (512 KB), this means roughly **12 slices/second** of throughput — a critical metric for multi-slice CT workflows.

## 4.2 DICOM Association Establishment Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SCU as Application Entity SCU (CT Scanner)
    participant SCP as Application Entity SCP (PACS Server)

    SCP-->>SCU: TCP SYN-ACK
    SCU->>SCP: TCP ACK
    Note over SCU,SCP: TCP Connection Established

    Note over SCU,SCP: Phase 2 - DICOM Association Negotiation
    SCU->>SCP: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Called AE, Calling AE, Presentation Contexts)
    SCP-->>SCU: A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Accepted Contexts, Max Length)
    Note over SCU,SCP: Association Established - Data Transfer Possible

    Note over SCU,SCP: Phase 3 - DIMSE Service Exchange
    SCU->>SCP: C-ECHO-RQ (Message ID = 1)
    SCP-->>SCU: C-ECHO-RSP (Status = 0x0000 Success)

    Note over SCU,SCP: Phase 4 - Graceful Termination
    SCU->>SCP: A-RELEASE-RQ
    SCP-->>SCU: A-RELEASE-RP
    Note over SCU,SCP: TCP Connection Closed
```
